source_reference.source_text: If a master property insurance policy excludes or limits coverage of any of the required perils, the HOA or co-op corporation must obtain an acceptable stand-alone property insurance policy which provides adequate coverage for the limited or excluded peril

examples: Master policy excludes sinkhole collapse; HOA must add a stand-alone sinkhole policy with adequate limits.
Master policy caps water damage at a low sublimit for the building; HOA must add a stand-alone policy/endorsement that adequately covers water damage.
Co-op master policy excludes windstorm; co-op must obtain stand-alone wind coverage before the loan can be delivered.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery / potential repurchase demand

examples: One-unit single-family detached primary residence
Condominium unit in an eligible project
Property affected by a declared disaster requiring additional review

dependencies.rationale: A stand-alone peril policy (BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_PROCESS_023_003) is a cure for coverage gaps, not a cure for an unacceptable settlement basis (actual cash value). Therefore, workflows generally confirm the master policy meets replacement-cost requirements first before deciding whether a stand-alone policy is needed for exclusions/limitations.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If replacement-cost acceptability is not confirmed first, lenders may incorrectly attempt to cure an unacceptable master policy with add-on coverage, delaying remediation and risking closing/delivery timelines.

business_rules.rule_name: Unit Interior Insurance Required When Master Does Not Cover
business_rules.description: If the master policy does not cover the interior or improvements of the unit in a project development, the borrower must maintain an individual property insurance policy. The lender/servicer must verify the coverage amount is sufficient to restore the unit to its pre-loss condition.
business_rules.conditions: When master policy lacks unit interior/improvements coverage for project unit
business_rules.consequences: Meets: adequate unit coverage verified; Violates: insurance deficiency; require borrower policy update before delivery
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: B7-3-04, Determining the Required Coverage Amount
source_reference.source_text: To the extent the master property insurance policy does not cover the interior or improvements of a unit in a project development, the borrower must maintain an individual property insurance policy. The lender or servicer must verify the coverage amount is suﬃcient to restore the unit to its condition prior to a loss event.

examples: Condo master policy is ‘bare walls’; borrower must obtain HO-6 coverage for interior finishes and improvements; lender verifies limit supports restoration.
Co-op master policy excludes unit upgrades; borrower provides individual policy/endorsement covering improvements; lender confirms adequate coverage.
PUD master policy covers common elements only; borrower must maintain individual policy for the unit and improvements as needed.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery / potential repurchase demand

examples: One-unit single-family detached primary residence
Condominium unit in an eligible project
Property affected by a declared disaster requiring additional review

dependencies.rationale: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_VALIDATION_023_004 addresses an interior-coverage gap scenario. If the master policy is unacceptable overall (e.g., ACV settlement), the lender must resolve the master policy defect before relying on borrower-level interior policies for delivery acceptability.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the master policy is unacceptable and this is not detected early, the lender may incorrectly focus on borrower HO-6 coverage while the project remains ineligible, causing rework and delays.

business_rules.rule_name: Parties in possession rights limited to 2 years, no ROFR
business_rules.description: For a conventional loan, title is acceptable with rights of lawful parties in possession only if those rights do not include a right of first refusal to purchase the property, and the duration of any such rights (including a tenant lease term) is not more than two years. If the possession rights include a right of first refusal or exceed two years, the title condition is not acceptable under the listed minor impediments and must be cleared/mitigated to meet Fannie Mae purchase/securitization standards.
business_rules.conditions: Conventional loan; title review finds “rights of parties in possession”
business_rules.consequences: If within limits, title acceptable; if exceeded, loan not eligible until cured
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: B7-2-05, Minor Impediments to Title for Conventional Loans
source_reference.source_text: rights of lawful parties in possession, as long as such rights do not include the right of ﬁrst refusal to purchase the property. (No rights of parties in possession, including the term of a tenant’s lease, may have a duration of more than two years.);

examples: Purchase SFR: existing month-to-month tenant with no purchase option; acceptable if lender ensures no ROFR exists.
Investment property conversion attempt: lease shows 36-month term; not acceptable under minor impediments and must be modified/cleared before delivery.
Title exception shows a right of first refusal in favor of occupant; prohibited—loan ineligible for delivery until removed.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit numeric (2 years) and explicit prohibition (no right of first refusal) stated as condition for acceptable minor title impediment.
business_rules.enforcement_action: loan ineligible for purchase or securitization

examples: One-unit single-family detached primary residence
Condominium unit in an eligible project
Property affected by a declared disaster requiring additional review

business_rules.rule_name: Security property must be 1 to 4 units and located in US, PR, VI, or Guam
business_rules.description: Fannie Mae purchases or securitizes first-lien mortgages secured by residential properties when the dwelling consists of one to four units. The security property must be located in the United States (including the District of Columbia), Puerto Rico, the U.S. Virgin Islands, or Guam.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to any mortgage loan intended for sale or securitization to Fannie Mae
business_rules.consequences: If met, property location/units are eligible; if not, mortgage is ineligible for purchase/securitization
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: B2-3-01, Number of Units; Property Location
source_reference.source_text: of Units Fannie Mae purchases or securitizes ﬁrst-lien mortgages that are secured by residential properties when the dwelling consists of one to four units. Under some circumstances, Fannie Mae limits the number of dwelling units

examples: 3-unit property in California; meets unit count and location eligibility.
5-unit dwelling in Florida; fails because dwelling exceeds four units.
2-unit property in Canada; fails because property is not in the permitted locations.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery

examples: One-unit single-family detached primary residence
Condominium unit in an eligible project
Property affected by a declared disaster requiring additional review

dependencies.rationale: Unit-count eligibility is foundational for delivery eligibility. The ADU rule determines whether the subject is properly treated as 1-unit-with-ADU (still within 1–4) or is misclassified (e.g., multiple ADUs or ADU with 2–4 unit dwelling). That classification directly feeds the 1–4 unit eligibility gate.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If ADU classification fails or is misapplied, the lender can deliver a loan with an incorrect unit count (e.g., actually a 2-unit or illegal second unit), creating eligibility defects, appraisal/underwriting errors, and potential purchase/securitization rejection.

business_rules.rule_name: Property Insurer Must Meet AM Best B or Equivalent BBB/A Ratings
business_rules.description: For any first-mortgage property insurance policy (including master policies for projects), the insurer must meet at least one of Fannie Mae’s rating thresholds: AM Best Financial Strength Rating 'B' or better, Demotech 'A' or better, Kroll 'BBB' or better, or S&P Global 'BBB' or better. Meeting one agency’s threshold is sufficient even if the insurer has multiple ratings.
business_rules.conditions: Property secures any first mortgage; lender/servicer reviews insurer acceptability
business_rules.consequences: If insurer does not meet at least one rating threshold, coverage is noncompliant and must be corrected/replaced
business_rules.exceptions: Only one rating agency threshold must be met (per note)

source_reference.source_text: The property insurance policy for the property securing any ﬁrst mortgage, including master policies for project developments, must be written by an insurer that meets one of the rating requirements in the following table. AM Best Company “B” or better Financial Strength Rating. Demotech, Inc. “A” or better Insurance Financial Stability Rating. Kroll Bond Rating Agency “BBB” or better Insurance Financial Strength Rating (IFSR). S&P Global “BBB” or better Insurer Financial Strength Rating. Note: An insurer is only required to meet the rating category requirement for one of the rating agencies

examples: Hazard insurer is rated AM Best 'A-' → meets 'B or better' threshold; policy acceptable.
Insurer has no AM Best rating but has S&P Global IFSR of 'BBB+' → acceptable because it meets one listed threshold.
Insurer is rated below 'B' by AM Best and has no other qualifying rating → lender must replace insurer/policy to comply.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Suspension of delivery until corrected / servicing corrective action

examples: One-unit single-family detached primary residence
Condominium unit in an eligible project
Property affected by a declared disaster requiring additional review

dependencies.rationale: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_VALIDATION_033_009 requires an identified insurer and policy context. BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_PROHIBITION_023_002 defines acceptability criteria for the master policy; the rating check is executed against that same master policy’s insurer as part of a complete acceptability decision.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the master policy isn’t properly identified/validated first, the lender might validate the wrong insurer (e.g., borrower HO-6 carrier instead of HOA master carrier) and miss a noncompliant master insurer rating.

dependencies.rationale: When the master policy lacks unit interior coverage, a borrower-level policy becomes required. Once that policy becomes required, it becomes subject to insurer acceptability validation (rating thresholds).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If insurer rating validation is skipped for the required borrower policy, the file may contain a required policy from a non-qualifying carrier, resulting in delivery defects and last-minute re-shopping of insurance.

business_rules.rule_name: Private transfer fee covenants created on/after Feb 8 2011 barred
business_rules.description: Except as permitted by the FHFA Private Transfer Fee Regulation (12 CFR Part 1228), Fannie Mae will not purchase or securitize loans secured by properties encumbered by private transfer fee covenants created on or after February 8, 2011. Lenders must maintain policies and procedures/controls to ensure delivered loans are not secured by properties with private transfer fee covenants that are unacceptable under the regulation. If an unacceptable covenant exists, the loan is ineligible for purchase/securitization.
business_rules.conditions: Property has private transfer fee covenant created on/after 2011-02-08
business_rules.consequences: If unacceptable covenant, loan is not eligible for purchase/securitization
business_rules.exceptions: Excepted transfer fee covenants as defined in 12 CFR Part 1228

source_reference.source_text: Private Transfer Fee Covenants In accordance with a regulation issued by the Federal Housing Finance Agency on March 16, 2012, as amended, and codiﬁed at 12 CFR Part 1228 (the “Private Transfer Fee Regulation”), except as provided below, Fannie Mae will not purchase or securitize loans on properties encumbered by private transfer fee covenants if those covenants were created on or after February 8, 2011, unless permitted by the Private Transfer Fee Regulation. The lender must establish policies and/or procedures to ensure that the loans it delivers to Fannie Mae... are not secured by properties encumbered with a private transfer fee that is unacceptable under the Private Transfer Fee Regulation.

Fannie Mae will not purchase or securitize loans on properties encumbered by private transfer fee covenants if those covenants were created on or after February 8, 2011, unless permitted by the Private Transfer Fee Regulation. The lender must establish policies and/or procedures to ensure that the loans it delivers to Fannie Mae, whether or not the loans were originated by the lender, are not secured by properties encumbered with a private transfer fee that is unacceptable under the Private Transfer Fee Regulation.

examples: Property deed includes a private transfer fee covenant recorded in 2013 that is not an excepted covenant; loan is ineligible for Fannie Mae purchase.
Property has an excepted transfer fee covenant under 12 CFR Part 1228 (e.g., shared equity exception); loan may be eligible if all other criteria met.
Lender lacks procedures to screen title for private transfer fee covenants; operational review may cite deficiency and loans may be rejected.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for purchase by Fannie Mae

examples: One-unit single-family detached primary residence
Condominium unit in an eligible project
Property affected by a declared disaster requiring additional review

deduplication_info.rationale: Both rules implement the same business logic: except as permitted by the FHFA Private Transfer Fee Regulation (12 CFR Part 1228), Fannie Mae will not purchase/securitize loans secured by properties encumbered by private transfer fee covenants created on or after 2011-02-08, and lenders must maintain controls to avoid delivering such loans. Numeric trigger date and the regulatory carve-out are identical; context (property-level eligibility for Fannie Mae acquisition) and consequence (loan ineligible) align. Differences are only phrasing and category label (compliance vs prohibition).
deduplication_info.primary_selection_reason: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_PROHIBITION_035_008 selected as primary because it is more complete: it includes the same prohibition plus clarifying that certain covenants may be permitted/excepted under the FHFA regulation, and it frames the requirement directly as a purchase/securitization prohibition (most actionable).

dependencies.rationale: Private transfer fee covenants are discovered through title search, title commitment exceptions, and recorded covenant review. Obtaining and reviewing the title package (including policy/commitment) is a practical prerequisite to reliably determine whether the property is encumbered by a prohibited covenant.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If title materials are not obtained early, prohibited private transfer fee covenants may be discovered late, forcing contract/closing delays or making the loan undeliverable.

business_rules.rule_name: Condo full review replacement reserves must be at least 10% of budgeted income
business_rules.description: For condo projects evaluated under the Full Review process, the HOA projected budget must provide for funding replacement reserves for capital expenditures and deferred maintenance of at least 10% of the budget. To determine if the minimum 10% reserve allocation is met, the lender must divide the annual budgeted replacement reserve allocation by the association’s annual budgeted assessment income (including regular common expense fees). Certain income may be excluded from the reserve calculation: incidental income not relied on for ongoing operations/maintenance/capital improvements; utility income typically paid by unit owners; income allocated to reserve accounts; and special assessment income. A reserve study may be used in lieu of the 10% calculation if the stated conditions are met (obtain/retain study and analysis; equivalency to standard requirements; funded reserves meet/exceed study recommendations; and study meets Fannie Mae requirements).
business_rules.conditions: Project_type is condo and lender uses Full Review project eligibility process
business_rules.consequences: If ≥10% (or reserve study acceptable), project may pass; if not, project fails review
business_rules.exceptions: Reserve study may replace 10% calc if all listed reserve study conditions are met

source_reference.source_text: Lenders must review the HOA projected budget to determine that it • is adequate (that is, it includes allocations for line items pertinent to the type of condo project), and • provides for the funding of replacement reserves for capital expenditures and deferred maintenance that is at least 10% of the budget. To determine whether the association has a minimum annual budgeted replacement reserve allocation of 10%, the lender must divide the annual budgeted replacement reserve allocation by the association’s annual budgeted assessment income (which includes regular common expense fees). The following types of income may be excluded from the reserve calculation: • incidental income on which the project does not rely for ongoing operations, maintenance, or capital improvements; • income collected for utilities that would typically be paid by individual unit owners, such as cable TV or Internet access; • income allocated to reserve accounts; and • special assessment income. The lender may use a reserve study in lieu of calculating the replacement reserve of 10% provided the following conditions are met:

examples: HOA reserves $90,000 annually and assessment income is $800,000; ratio is 11.25%, meeting the 10% requirement (after excluding permitted income items).
HOA includes $50,000 incidental rental income in assessment income; lender excludes it if not relied upon for operations, potentially increasing the reserve ratio to meet 10%.

HOA reserve ratio calculates to 7%, but an acceptable reserve study shows funded reserves equivalent to standard requirements and meeting/exceeding study recommendations; lender may use reserve study instead.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery due to project ineligibility

examples: One-unit single-family detached primary residence
Condominium unit in an eligible project
Property affected by a declared disaster requiring additional review

dependencies.rationale: In practice, lenders determine condo project classification (new vs established) early to route the file into the correct review workflow. That routing decision often precedes the 10% reserve allocation calculation required under Full Review.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If classification is skipped or wrong, the file may be routed to the wrong review process, and the lender may omit or misapply the 10% reserve calculation, creating project eligibility defects.

dependencies.rationale: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_CONSTRAINT_020_008 governs sales/conveyance and phasing constraints; BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_CALCULATION_036_008 governs HOA reserve funding adequacy. In an automated condo review workflow, both must be executed to reach an overall project eligibility result.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If conveyance/phasing is satisfied but reserves fail (or vice versa), the project still fails Full Review and the loan becomes ineligible for delivery based on project standards.

business_rules.rule_name: Established condo requires 90% sold and 100% complete
business_rules.description: A condominium project is classified as an established condo project only if all criteria are met: (1) at least 90% of total units conveyed to unit purchasers, (2) project is 100% complete (all units and common elements), (3) not subject to additional phasing or annexation, and (4) HOA control turned over to unit owners. A project may still be treated as established with less than 90% sold if the shortfall is due to developer rental holdback and additional requirements are satisfied, including developer holdback ≤ 20% of total units and no active or pending special assessments.
business_rules.conditions: When subject property is in a condominium project under project standards
business_rules.consequences: If classified as established, established-project eligibility criteria apply; otherwise treat as new condo
business_rules.exceptions: May treat as established if rental holdback causes <90% sold and holdback ≤20%

source_reference.source_text: Established condo projectA project for which all of the following are true: • at least 90% of the total units in the project have been conveyed to unit purchasers; • the project is 100% complete, including all units and common elements; • the project is not subject to additional phasing or annexation; and • control of the HOA has been turned over to the unit owners. A project may also be treated as an established project with less than 90% of the units sold to unit purchasers, provided the deﬁcit is the result of the developer holding back units for rent... the developer’s share of the units held back for rental is no more than 20% of the project’s total units; ... there are no active or pending special assessments in the project.

examples: 92% of units sold; project 100% complete; no phasing; HOA owner-controlled → classify as established condo.
85% sold because developer holds 15% as rentals; HOA fees current on held units; no special assessments → may treat as established condo.
85% sold with developer holding 25% as rentals → cannot use established exception; treat as new condo project.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery until correct project type/eligibility applied

examples: One-unit single-family detached primary residence
Condominium unit in an eligible project
Property affected by a declared disaster requiring additional review

business_rules.rule_name: New condo project when under 90% sold or phased
business_rules.description: A condominium project is classified as a new condo project when any of the following are true: (1) fewer than 90% of total units conveyed to unit purchasers (or fewer than 80% if the project meets the established-project rental holdback exception), (2) project not fully completed (proposed/new construction or proposed/incomplete conversion), (3) newly converted, (4) subject to additional phasing or annexation, or (5) HOA still in developer control.
business_rules.conditions: When determining condo project type for subject property eligibility review
business_rules.consequences: If any condition is true, apply new-condo eligibility criteria and related review requirements
business_rules.exceptions: 80% threshold applies only if established-project exception criteria are met

source_reference.source_text: New condo projectA project for which one or more of the following is true: • fewer than 90% of the total units in the project have been conveyed to unit purchasers (or 80% if it meets the exception noted in the row above); • the project is not fully completed, such as proposed construction, new construction, or the proposed or incomplete conversion of an existing building to a condo; • the project is newly converted; • the project is subject to additional phasing or annexation; or • HOA still in the developer’s control.

examples: Project is 100% complete but only 88% conveyed to purchasers → classify as new condo project.
Project is 82% conveyed and developer holds 18% rentals, but HOA still developer-controlled → new condo (HOA control triggers).
Project is 79% conveyed even with rental holdback scenario → new condo (below 80% exception threshold).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery if wrong project standards applied

examples: One-unit single-family detached primary residence
Condominium unit in an eligible project
Property affected by a declared disaster requiring additional review

business_rules.rule_name: Waste site checks: 1 mile, 30 years, Phase II trigger
business_rules.description: For waste site contamination screening in a Phase I environmental hazard assessment format, if any answer to questions 2 through 7 is “yes” or “don’t know,” the property either fails or requires a Phase II assessment. If all answers to questions 2 through 7 are “no,” the property is acceptable for underwriting purposes. The screening questions include whether the site was used for research/industrial/military purposes during the last 30 years and whether the property or any site within one mile appears on state or federal hazardous waste lists.
business_rules.conditions: When completing Phase I waste site contamination screening questions
business_rules.consequences: All “no” answers 2-7 = acceptable; any “yes/DK” 2-7 = fail or Phase II required
business_rules.exceptions: If Q1=yes and Q2=no, stop: acceptable from waste site contamination standpoint

source_reference.source_text: Note: If the answer to question 1 is “yes” and the answer to question 2 is “no,” stop here because, for underwriting purposes, the property will be acceptable from the standpoint of waste site contamination. Otherwise, answer the questions below. 3. Was the site ever used for research, industrial, or military purposes during the last 30 years? ... 6. Does the property or any site within one mile of the property appear on any state or federal list of hazardous waste sites?

examples: Underwriter answers Q2-7 all “no”; the property passes waste site contamination screening and is acceptable for underwriting purposes.
Q6 is “yes” because a neighboring site within 0.7 miles is on a federal hazardous list; the property must fail or proceed to Phase II assessment before acceptability can be concluded.
Q3 is “don’t know” regarding industrial use in the last 30 years; the property triggers fail/Phase II per the note.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Ineligible for underwriting approval until remediated/cleared

examples: One-unit single-family detached primary residence
Condominium unit in an eligible project
Property affected by a declared disaster requiring additional review

business_rules.rule_name: PCB concentration 50 ppm or more triggers Phase II or fail
business_rules.description: For PCB contamination screening, if the answer to any question is “don’t know,” a Phase II assessment is required (stop). Additionally, if PCB concentrations of 50 parts per million or greater have been found in contaminated soils or groundwater, the property either fails or needs a Phase II assessment.
business_rules.conditions: When conducting Phase I PCB screening for subject property
business_rules.consequences: DK answer = Phase II required; PCB ≥50 ppm = fail or Phase II required
business_rules.exceptions: If initial PCB questions answered “no”, stop: acceptable from PCB standpoint

source_reference.source_text: Note: If the answers to the above questions are “no,” stop here because, for underwriting purposes, the property will be acceptable from the standpoint of PCB contamination. If the answer to a question is “don’t know,” stop here since a Phase II assessment is required. ... 6. If the answer to question 2 above is “yes,” have PCB concentrations of 50 parts per million or greater been found in contaminated soils or groundwater?

examples: Underwriter selects “don’t know” for whether transformers contain PCBs; the file must obtain a Phase II assessment before acceptability can be concluded.
Environmental report documents PCB in soil at 55 ppm; the property must fail or proceed to Phase II per the screening logic.
All PCB screening questions (including 3-6) are answered “no”; the property is acceptable from the standpoint of PCB contamination.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Ineligible for underwriting approval until Phase II completed

examples: One-unit single-family detached primary residence
Condominium unit in an eligible project
Property affected by a declared disaster requiring additional review

dependencies.rationale: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_VALIDATION_038_003 is executed in the context of a Phase I environmental hazard screening workflow. When that workflow is initiated (BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_ELIGIBILITY_020_009 context), PCB-specific answers can independently force Phase II or failure and thus condition the overall environmental acceptability.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If PCB screening is omitted when applicable, the lender may fail to obtain a required Phase II assessment even when 'don’t know' responses exist, elevating environmental risk and delivery defensibility concerns.

business_rules.rule_name: Radon test within 6 months must be at or below 4 pCi/L
business_rules.description: For a subject property that is a conversion of an existing building, an EPA-approved short-term radon test performed in the below-grade area of each subject building within the last six months must be at or below four picocuries per liter (4 pCi/L). If the answer is “no” or “don’t know,” a Phase II assessment is required.
business_rules.conditions: When property is conversion of existing building and radon screening applies
business_rules.consequences: Test ≤4 pCi/L within 6 months = acceptable; otherwise Phase II required
business_rules.exceptions: If radon questions 2 and 3 are “no,” property acceptable from radon standpoint

source_reference.source_text: Note: If the answers to questions 2 and 3 are “no,” the property, for underwriting purposes, will be acceptable from the standpoint of radon contamination. If the subject property is a conversion of an existing building, go to question 4. 4. Were the results of an EPA-approved short-term radon test that was performed in the below-grade area of each of the subject buildings within the last six months at or below four picocuries per liter? Note: If the answer to this question is “no” or “don’t know,” a Phase II assessment is required.

examples: A condo conversion building has a radon test from 5 months ago showing 3.2 pCi/L in the below-grade area; it satisfies the requirement and does not trigger Phase II.
A conversion property has a radon test performed 9 months ago (even if 3.0 pCi/L); it fails the “within the last six months” condition and triggers Phase II.
Underwriter cannot confirm whether an EPA-approved test was performed (“don’t know”); Phase II assessment is required.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Ineligible for underwriting approval until Phase II completed

examples: One-unit single-family detached primary residence
Condominium unit in an eligible project
Property affected by a declared disaster requiring additional review

dependencies.rationale: Within the Phase I environmental hazard assessment workflow, conversions introduce radon screening requirements. If radon test results are missing/unknown or above threshold, the rules require Phase II, which directly blocks environmental clearance for underwriting.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If this conditional screening is not executed for conversions, loans may proceed without required testing/Phase II, exposing lender to health/safety risk and potential ineligibility if discovered during QC or post-purchase review.

business_rules.rule_name: Hawaii Island lava zones 1 and 2 are ineligible; only zones 3 through 9 allowed
business_rules.description: Mortgage loans secured by properties located on the island of Hawaii are eligible for purchase/securitization by Fannie Mae only if the property is located in lava zones 3 through 9. Properties in lava zones 1 and 2 are not eligible due to increased risk of destruction from lava flows.
business_rules.conditions: Subject property is on the island of Hawaii; lava zone classification is known/verified
business_rules.consequences: If lava zone is 1 or 2: property/loan is not eligible for purchase or securitization
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: Fannie Mae will only purchase or securitize mortgage loans secured by properties that are located within lava zones 3 through 9 on the island of Hawaii. Properties in lava zones 1 and 2 are not eligible due to the increased risk of property destruction from lava ﬂows within these areas.

Hawaiian Lava Zones Fannie Mae will only purchase or securitize mortgage loans secured by properties that are located within lava zones 3 through 9 on the island of Hawaii. Properties in lava zones 1 and 2 are not eligible due to the increased risk of property destruction from lava ﬂows within these areas.

examples: Property in lava zone 3 on Hawaii Island: eligible (subject to all other requirements).
Property in lava zone 2 on Hawaii Island: ineligible for Fannie Mae delivery.
Property on another Hawaiian island (not Hawaii Island): this lava-zone rule does not apply (other property eligibility rules still apply).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery rejection

examples: One-unit single-family detached primary residence
Condominium unit in an eligible project
Property affected by a declared disaster requiring additional review

deduplication_info.rationale: Both rules state the identical geographic hazard eligibility requirement for the island of Hawaii: properties in lava zones 3–9 are eligible; lava zones 1–2 are ineligible due to heightened lava-flow destruction risk. All numeric zone thresholds and context match; consequences match (loan/property ineligible). The wording differences do not change the business logic.
deduplication_info.primary_selection_reason: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_ELIGIBILITY_039_009 selected as primary because it is slightly clearer/cleaner: it explicitly states the eligibility range (zones 3–9) and the ineligibility trigger (zones 1–2) in a single concise rule statement.

business_rules.rule_name: TBA UMBS nonstandard loans capped 10% and 15%
business_rules.description: In a TBA-eligible UMBS pool, each category of nonstandard loans is limited to 10% of the pool’s issue-date UPB. If loans with more than one nonstandard characteristic are included in the same TBA-eligible UMBS pool, the combined issue-date UPB of two or more nonstandard categories may not exceed 15% of the pool’s total issue-date UPB; however, the 15% cumulative limitation does not apply to high-balance loans.
business_rules.conditions: When pooling nonstandard loans in a TBA-eligible UMBS pool based on issue-date UPB
business_rules.consequences: If limits exceeded, pool is not compliant for TBA-eligible UMBS standard parameters
business_rules.exceptions: 15% cumulative limitation does not apply to high-balance loans

source_reference.source_text: Nonstandard Loans “Nonstandard loans” (or loans with nonstandard characteristics) may be pooled into MBS, but depending on the concentration in the pool, may only be pooled as negotiated rather than standard transactions. Nonstandard loans are: co-op share loans, relocation loans (deﬁned below), loans with signiﬁcant interest rate buydowns, and high-balance loans. In a TBA-eligible UMBS pool, nonstandard loans are each limited to 10% of the issue date UPB of a TBA-eligible UMBS pool. If loans with more than one of the nonstandard characteristics are included in the same TBA-eligible UMBS pool, the sum of the issue date UPB of two or more of the loans with nonstandard characteristics may not exceed 15% of the total issue date UPB of the pool. The 15% cumulative limitation, however, does not apply to high-balance loans.

examples: Pool issue-date UPB is $10,000,000: co-op share loans must be ≤ $1,000,000 (10%).
Pool includes $900,000 co-op loans (9%) and $800,000 significant buydown loans (8%): combined $1,700,000 (17%) violates the 15% cumulative cap.
Pool includes $900,000 co-op loans (9%) and $1,200,000 high-balance loans (12%): combined 21% may be permitted because high-balance loans are excluded from the 15% cumulative limitation.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Delivery rejection or negotiated pooling required

examples: Loan eligibility profile meeting baseline conforming loan limit requirements
Loan eligibility profile flagged due to ARM feature noncompliance
Loan eligibility profile requiring escrow account per policy

dependencies.rationale: BR_MORTGAGE_LOAN_DEFINITION_006_010 explicitly flags that significant buydown loans are subject to pooling composition restrictions. BR_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROFILE_CONSTRAINT_003_010 governs concentration limits for nonstandard categories in TBA UMBS pools. When a lender pools SFC 014 loans as a nonstandard category, the pooling limits rule becomes a required downstream check.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the pooling concentration limits are not applied, a lender may create a noncompliant TBA-eligible UMBS pool, requiring re-pooling, delayed issuance, or moving loans to a different execution channel.

business_rules.rule_name: High LTV Refinance Requires Note Date 10-01-2017 and 15-Month Seasoning
business_rules.description: To be eligible for the high LTV refinance option, the existing loan being refinanced must be a first-lien conventional mortgage loan owned/securitized by Fannie Mae, must have a note date on or after October 1, 2017, and must have seasoning of at least 15 months between the existing note date and the new note date (example provided: existing note date 01/01/2018 → new note date no earlier than 04/01/2019).
business_rules.conditions: Evaluating eligibility for high LTV refinance option based on existing loan data
business_rules.consequences: If met, existing loan meets base eligibility; if not, high LTV option ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited eligibility bullets

source_reference.source_text: ✓ The existing loan must.... be a ﬁrst lien, conventional mortgage loan owned or securitized by Fannie Mae. have a note date on or after October 1, 2017. have seasoning of at least 15 months - meaning at least 15 months have passed from the note date of the existing loan to the note date of the new loan. For example, if the note date on the existing loan is January 1, 2018, the note date of the new loan must be no earlier than April 1, 2019.

examples: Existing note date 2017-09-30: ineligible because note date is before 10/01/2017.
Existing note date 2018-01-01 and new note date 2019-03-01: ineligible because seasoning is <15 months.
Existing note date 2018-01-01 and new note date 2019-04-01: meets 15-month seasoning example.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery ineligible; repurchase demand risk

examples: Loan eligibility profile meeting baseline conforming loan limit requirements
Loan eligibility profile flagged due to ARM feature noncompliance
Loan eligibility profile requiring escrow account per policy

business_rules.rule_name: High LTV Refinance Limits Points to $5,000 and Cash Back to $250
business_rules.description: For high LTV refinance transactions, the standard limited cash-out refinance requirements are modified: the new loan amount is limited to (i) payoff of the UPB of the existing first mortgage being refinanced (including accrued interest), (ii) financing of closing costs, prepaid items, and points up to $5,000 total, and (iii) cash back to the borrower up to $250. Excess proceeds may be applied as a curtailment on the new loan.
business_rules.conditions: High LTV refinance option; determining maximum new loan amount components
business_rules.consequences: If within caps, loan amount eligible; if exceeded, must reduce loan amount/curtail
business_rules.exceptions: None stated (excess proceeds may be applied as curtailment)

source_reference.source_text: The standard limited cash-out reﬁnance requirements are modiﬁed for high LTV loan transactions. The new loan amount is limited to the payoﬀ of the UPB of the existing ﬁrst mortgage loan being reﬁnanced (including accrued interest); the ﬁnancing of closing costs, prepaid items, and points up to $5,000 total for the new loan; and cash back to the borrower up to $250. Excess proceeds may be applied as a curtailment on the new loan.

examples: Closing costs/prepaids/points total $6,200: only $5,000 may be financed; remainder must be paid outside the loan or loan amount reduced.
Borrower receives $600 cash back at closing: reduce to $250 max and apply $350 excess as curtailment or adjust loan amount.
Payoff includes accrued interest: accrued interest may be included in the payoff component of the new loan amount.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery ineligible; repurchase demand risk

examples: Loan eligibility profile meeting baseline conforming loan limit requirements
Loan eligibility profile flagged due to ARM feature noncompliance
Loan eligibility profile requiring escrow account per policy

dependencies.rationale: The max new loan amount components and caps in BR_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROFILE_CONSTRAINT_007_010 are specific to high LTV refi option processing. If the transaction is not eligible for the option, standard limited cash-out requirements apply instead.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the base program eligibility is not checked first, the lender may incorrectly apply high-LTV refi loan amount caps to a non-high-LTV refi transaction (causing incorrect denials or mis-disclosures), or fail to apply them to an eligible transaction (causing delivery defects).

dependencies.rationale: To ensure the final new loan amount is compliant, the lender must incorporate mandatory adjustments that reduce the maximum available loan amount (unpaid special assessments), otherwise the calculated loan amount under the high-LTV refi caps may still exceed what is permissible after required reductions.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If special assessments are not accounted for, the delivered loan amount can exceed allowable limits, causing eligibility defects, required curtailments, or forced rebalancing of funds at closing.

dependencies.rationale: If the borrower fails the high-LTV payment history gate, there is no need to apply the option-specific loan amount caps and curtailment logic. Sequencing the payment history gate earlier reduces rework and prevents structuring a loan under a program the borrower cannot use.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If sequencing is not followed, the lender may spend time calculating compliant loan amount components for a borrower who is ineligible due to payment history, delaying requalification into another product.

business_rules.rule_name: Limited cash-out refi 95.01–97% must be fixed 30-year DU only and no ARM
business_rules.description: For a limited cash-out refinance where the LTV/CLTV/HCLTV exceeds 95% (95.01% to 97%), the loan must be a fixed-rate loan with a term up to 30 years, must be underwritten through DU only, and must be secured by a one-unit principal residence that all borrowers occupy. High-balance and ARM loans are not permitted for this high-LTV limited cash-out refinance band. The CLTV may be up to 105% only if the subordinate lien is a Community Seconds loan (per the note in this section).
business_rules.conditions: Limited cash-out refinance; LTV/CLTV/HCLTV in 95.01–97% band
business_rules.consequences: If any criteria not met, transaction is ineligible for this high-LTV LCOR policy
business_rules.exceptions: CLTV up to 105% allowed only with Community Seconds subordinate lien

source_reference.source_text: If the LTV, CLTV, or HCLTV ratio exceeds 95% for a limited cash-out transaction, the following requirements also apply. LTV, CLTV, or HCLTV Ratio 95.01 to 97% Note : The CLTV ratio can be up to 105% if the subordinate lien is a Community Seconds loan. Loan Type Fixed-rate loans with terms up to 30 years. Note : High-balance and ARM loans are not permitted. Property and Occupancy One-unit principal residence. All borrowers must occupy the property. Underwriting Method DU only

examples: LCOR at 96% LTV, fixed-rate 30-year, DU underwritten, 1-unit principal residence: eligible under the 95.01–97% criteria (subject to other requirements).
LCOR at 96% LTV, 5/6 ARM: ineligible because ARM loans are not permitted in this 95.01–97% band.
LCOR at 97% LTV with a Community Seconds subordinate lien and 103% CLTV: allowed because CLTV can be up to 105% with Community Seconds.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery rejection or repurchase demand

examples: Loan eligibility profile meeting baseline conforming loan limit requirements
Loan eligibility profile flagged due to ARM feature noncompliance
Loan eligibility profile requiring escrow account per policy

dependencies.rationale: The high-LTV limited cash-out refinance band has strict product and collateral constraints and only allows CLTV above typical limits (up to 105%) when Community Seconds rules are met. Therefore, if the file uses the CLTV 105% allowance, it is dependent on satisfying Community Seconds constraints (eligible transaction type, terms, and resubordination recording for LCOR).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If Community Seconds constraints are not met, the loan may be ineligible for the 95.01–97% policy band or may require restructuring (reduce CLTV, change subordinate financing, or change transaction type).

dependencies.rationale: If the property is misclassified due to multiple ADUs or an ADU combined with a 2–4 unit dwelling, the loan fails the 'one-unit principal residence' requirement for this specific high-LTV LCOR band. Therefore, unit classification is an input prerequisite for eligibility testing under BR_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROFILE_ELIGIBILITY_013_009.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If ADU classification fails, the loan cannot be delivered under this high-LTV LCOR policy band and may require program change, restructure, or denial.

dependencies.rationale: The high-LTV LCOR rule is a program overlay that still sits within Fannie Mae’s base collateral eligibility. Base unit/location eligibility must be satisfied before program-specific constraints can be applied.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the property fails base eligibility (units/location), the high-LTV LCOR policy band cannot be used and the loan is ineligible for delivery.

business_rules.rule_name: Community Seconds Maximum 105% CLTV and 5-Year ARM
business_rules.description: When a first mortgage is originated with Community Seconds subordinate financing, the first mortgage must be fixed-rate or an ARM with an initial fixed-rate period of at least 5 years, the maximum CLTV is 105% (unless the first mortgage has an independent CLTV cap), and the transaction must be a purchase or limited cash-out refinance. For limited cash-out refinance, the Community Seconds holder must execute a resubordination agreement and it must be recorded to ensure enforceability.
business_rules.conditions: When underwriting a first mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae with Community Seconds financing
business_rules.consequences: If constraints not met, loan is ineligible for delivery/purchase by Fannie Mae
business_rules.exceptions: 105% CLTV may be limited by an independent first-mortgage CLTV cap

source_reference.source_text: First mortgage eligibilityThe ﬁrst mortgage must be ﬁxed-rate or an ARM with an initial ﬁxed-rate period of ﬁve years or more, and otherwise comply with this Guide. Maximum LTV and CLTV ratiosThe maximum LTV is based on the ﬁrst mortgage LTV requirement. The maximum CLTV is 105% with a Community Seconds loan, unless the ﬁrst mortgage has an independent CLTV cap (such as the CLTV cap for manufactured housing that does not meet MH Advantage requirements). Refer to the   Eligibility Matrix  for additional information. Loan purposeThe transaction is limited to a purchase or limited cash-out reﬁnance. For a limited cash-out reﬁnance, the Community Seconds mortgage holder must acknowledge their lien position by executing a resubordination agreement. The agreement must be recorded to ensure enforceability.

examples: Purchase loan with Community Seconds: first mortgage is 7/6 ARM (≥5-year initial fixed) and CLTV is 103%; loan meets this rule.
Purchase loan CLTV is 107%; even if first mortgage LTV meets matrix, CLTV violates the 105% cap and loan is ineligible.
Limited cash-out refinance with Community Seconds: resubordination agreement signed but not recorded; loan violates enforceability requirement until recorded.
business_rules.enforcement_action: N/A

examples: Loan eligibility profile meeting baseline conforming loan limit requirements
Loan eligibility profile flagged due to ARM feature noncompliance
Loan eligibility profile requiring escrow account per policy

dependencies.rationale: Before applying structural constraints on a first mortgage with Community Seconds (rate/CLTV caps/allowed transactions/resubordination requirements), the lender must first confirm the subordinate lien qualifies as Community Seconds (eligible provider and not an interested party). Otherwise, the transaction cannot be treated as Community Seconds and becomes ineligible.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If provider eligibility is not validated first, a lender may incorrectly allow CLTV up to 105% or permit a transaction structure based on Community Seconds rules, creating a delivery ineligibility.

business_rules.rule_name: Community Seconds Provider Cannot Be Seller or Interested Party
business_rules.description: A Community Seconds loan must be funded only by an eligible provider type (e.g., government entity, qualifying nonprofit, FHLB affordable housing program, eligible employer assistance, eligible lender in employer-guaranteed structure, or eligible Indian tribe). The Community Seconds provider must not be the property seller or other interested party in the transaction, except when the provider is the provider of a shared equity program or sweat equity program.
business_rules.conditions: When determining whether Community Seconds subordinate financing is eligible
business_rules.consequences: If provider is ineligible or interested party, first mortgage becomes ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: Allowed if provider is shared equity or sweat equity program provider

source_reference.source_text: A Community Seconds loan must only be funded by one of the following entities: a federal agency, state, county, or similar political subdivision of a state; any city, town, village, or borough of a state that has a local government and that has been created by a special legislative act, has been otherwise individually incorporated or chartered pursuant to state law, or is recognized as such under the constitution or by the laws of the state in which it is located; a housing ﬁnance agency as deﬁned in 24 C.F.R. §266.5; a nonproﬁt organization exempt from taxation under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code; a regional Federal Home Loan Bank under one of its aﬀordable housing programs; an employer where a borrower is an employee (see  B3-4.3-08, Employer Assistance ); a lender, only in connection with an employer-guaranteed Community Seconds loan as part of its aﬀordable housing program; or an Indian tribe on the most current list published by the Secretary of the Interior pursuant to 25 U.S.C. §5131. A Community Seconds provider must not be the property seller or other interested party in the transaction, except when they are the provider of a shared equity program or sweat equity program.

examples: Subordinate loan is funded by a 501(c)(3) nonprofit and is not the property seller; provider eligibility is met.
Subordinate loan is funded by the property seller as part of a non-shared-equity arrangement; this violates the interested party prohibition.
Subordinate financing is provided by the seller under a documented shared equity program; exception applies and provider may be eligible.
business_rules.enforcement_action: N/A

examples: Loan eligibility profile meeting baseline conforming loan limit requirements
Loan eligibility profile flagged due to ARM feature noncompliance
Loan eligibility profile requiring escrow account per policy

business_rules.rule_name: Employment-related assets require max 70% LTV or 80% at 62
business_rules.description: When employment-related assets are used as qualifying income under B3-3.4-06, the maximum LTV/CLTV/HCLTV is 70%. The maximum may be 80% only if the owner of the asset(s) used to qualify is at least 62 years old at closing; if the asset is jointly owned, all owners must be borrowers on the loan and the borrower using the income to qualify must be at least 62 at closing.
business_rules.conditions: Employment-related assets are used to qualify and LTV/CLTV/HCLTV is evaluated
business_rules.consequences: If ratios exceed limits, income method not eligible; loan may be ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: 80% cap allowed only with age 62 and joint-ownership borrower conditions

source_reference.source_text: Max LTV, CLTV, and HCLTV Ratio • 70%, or • 80% if the owner of the asset(s) being used to qualify is at least 62 years old at the time of closing. If the asset is jointly owned, all owners must be a borrower on the loan and the borrower using the income to qualify must be at least 62 years old at the time of closing.

examples: Borrower age 45 uses severance assets as income at 75% LTV → not permitted; must reduce LTV to 70% or use another income guideline.
Borrower age 63 uses IRA assets as income at 80% LTV → permitted if all other parameters are met.
Assets jointly owned by borrower (63) and non-borrower spouse → not permitted at 80%; co-owner must be a co-borrower to use jointly owned assets.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery ineligibility / repurchase exposure

examples: Loan eligibility profile meeting baseline conforming loan limit requirements
Loan eligibility profile flagged due to ARM feature noncompliance
Loan eligibility profile requiring escrow account per policy

business_rules.rule_name: Affordable LTV uses appraised value; example 80% and DU entry required
business_rules.description: For shared equity transactions where resale restrictions terminate automatically upon foreclosure (or redemption period expiration) or upon recordation of a deed-in-lieu, the lender must use the Affordable LTV calculation for LTV/CLTV/HCLTV: divide the loan amount by the appraised value (not the lesser of sales price or appraised value). For DU loans, the lender must enter “Affordable LTV” in the Product Description field to ensure DU calculates ratios based solely on the appraised value for purchase transactions. The lender must also use the appraised value (under Affordable LTV) to determine minimum down payment, any required borrower contribution, and required mortgage insurance level.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to shared equity transactions where resale restrictions terminate at foreclosure/deed-in-lieu
business_rules.consequences: If standard LTV is used, LTV/MI/down payment may be wrong and loan may be ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: If resale restrictions survive foreclosure, standard lesser-of calculation applies instead

source_reference.source_text: Accordingly, for these types of transactions, lenders must use the "Aﬀordable LTV" calculation to determine LTV, CLTV, and HCLTV ratios. The Aﬀordable LTV calculation divides the loan amount by the appraised value of the property, rather than the lesser of the sales price or the appraised value. ... Appraised value $200,000 ... First mortgage loan amount $160,000 Aﬀordable LTV Ratio ... 80% When using the Aﬀordable LTV calculation, the lender must for DU loans, enter "Aﬀordable LTV" in the Product Description ﬁeld

examples: Shared equity purchase: appraised value $200,000, subsidized sales price $160,000, loan amount $160,000; Affordable LTV = $160,000 / $200,000 = 80% (not 100%).

DU loan in shared equity program: lender forgets to enter “Affordable LTV” in Product Description; DU calculates LTV using lesser-of sales price/appraised value, causing ratio mismatch and delivery defects.
If resale restrictions survive foreclosure, lender must revert to standard lesser-of calculation; using Affordable LTV would overstate collateral value for foreclosure recovery.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan repurchase demand

examples: Loan eligibility profile meeting baseline conforming loan limit requirements
Loan eligibility profile flagged due to ARM feature noncompliance
Loan eligibility profile requiring escrow account per policy

business_rules.rule_name: Delivery requires no 30-day delinquencies in lookback
business_rules.description: To be eligible for delivery to Fannie Mae, borrower payment status must meet the applicable “no 30-day delinquency” requirement based on loan age at delivery: (1) If the loan is 12 or fewer months old at the time of delivery, the borrower must not have had any 30-day delinquencies since origination. (2) If the loan is more than one year old at the time of delivery, the borrower must not have had any 30-day delinquencies in the 12-month period preceding delivery. (3) For assumed loans, the requirement applies to the current borrower; if seasoned and current borrower has owned the property less than 12 months, the lookback is reduced to the months owned.
business_rules.conditions: When a loan is being delivered to Fannie Mae under a whole loan commitment
business_rules.consequences: If delinquency requirement not met, loan is not eligible for delivery/purchase
business_rules.exceptions: Assumed loan lookback reduced to months owned if <12 months

source_reference.source_text: Borrower Payment Status Requirements The table below provides the requirements for payment status for loans delivered to Fannie Mae. 12 or fewer months old at the time of delivery The borrower must not have had any 30-day delinquencies since the loan was originated. More than one year old at the time of delivery The borrower must not have any 30-day delinquencies in the 12-month period that precedes the lender’s delivery of the loan to Fannie Mae. Assumed The payment status requirement applies to the current borrower. However, for a seasoned loan, if the current borrower has owned the property less than 12 months, the time period is reduced to the number of months that they have owned the property.

examples: Loan delivered 10 months after origination with a 30-day delinquency in month 6: ineligible for delivery.
Loan delivered 26 months after origination; borrower had a 30-day delinquency 18 months ago but none in last 12 months: eligible under payment-status rule.
Assumed loan: current borrower has owned 8 months and had a 30-day delinquency 7 months ago: ineligible because lookback is 8 months owned.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery rejection / repurchase remedy if delivered with breach

examples: Loan eligibility profile meeting baseline conforming loan limit requirements
Loan eligibility profile flagged due to ARM feature noncompliance
Loan eligibility profile requiring escrow account per policy

business_rules.rule_name: Flow loan age maximum 6 months from first payment date
business_rules.description: To be eligible for purchase by Fannie Mae on a flow basis, a loan must be no more than six months old measured from the first payment date to (i) the Purchase Ready date for whole loans or (ii) the MBS pool issue date for MBS loans. Example: if a whole loan is Purchase Ready in May 2023 (or an MBS loan is in a May 1, 2023 issued pool), the first payment date can be no earlier than December 1, 2022. Exception: HomeStyle Renovation loans delivered after renovation completion with SFC 279 may be sold up to 15 months after the note date. If the loan exceeds the applicable age limit, it is not flow-eligible and may require a negotiated transaction.
business_rules.conditions: Flow purchase eligibility assessment at delivery to Fannie Mae
business_rules.consequences: If within limits, eligible for flow purchase; if exceeded, ineligible for flow
business_rules.exceptions: HomeStyle Renovation w/ SFC 279: up to 15 months after note date

source_reference.source_text: Age of Loan To be eligible for purchase by Fannie Mae on a ﬂow basis, a loan must be no more than six months old measured from the ﬁrst payment date to the "Purchase Ready" date (whole loans) or the MBS pool issue date (MBS loans). For example, if a whole loan is in "Purchase Ready" status in May 2023 or an MBS loan is in a May 1, 2023 issued pool, the ﬁrst payment date can be no earlier than December 1, 2022. NOTE: HomeStyle Renovation loans that are not delivered until renovation is complete (and delivered with SFC 279) can be sold up to 15 months after the note date.

To be eligible for purchase by Fannie Mae on a ﬂow basis, a loan must be no more than six months old measured from the ﬁrst payment date to the "Purchase Ready" date (whole loans) or the MBS pool issue date (MBS loans). ... NOTE: HomeStyle Renovation loans that are not delivered until renovation is complete (and delivered with SFC 279) can be sold up to 15 months after the note date.

examples: If a whole loan is Purchase Ready in May 2023 (or an MBS loan is in a May 1, 2023 issued pool), the first payment date can be no earlier than December 1, 2022.
HomeStyle Renovation delivered after renovation completion with SFC 279 may be sold up to 15 months after the note date.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for purchase by Fannie Mae

examples: Loan eligibility profile meeting baseline conforming loan limit requirements
Loan eligibility profile flagged due to ARM feature noncompliance
Loan eligibility profile requiring escrow account per policy

deduplication_info.rationale: Both rules define the same flow purchase eligibility aging constraint: loan age must be ≤ 6 months, measured from first payment date to Purchase Ready date (whole loans) or to MBS pool issue date (MBS loans), with the same explicit HomeStyle Renovation exception permitting sale up to 15 months after note date when delivered after renovation completion with SFC 279. Numeric thresholds (6 months, 15 months) and context (flow purchase eligibility) are identical; consequences align (flow-ineligible if exceeded).
deduplication_info.primary_selection_reason: BR_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROFILE_CONSTRAINT_035_005 selected as primary because it is the most complete: it includes the same six-month age test, explicitly states the measurement points for whole loans vs MBS, includes an illustrative example, and includes the HomeStyle Renovation exception with SFC 279 and the extended timing.

business_rules.rule_name: Unpaid special assessments reduce max loan amount dollar-for-dollar
business_rules.description: If special assessments have been levied against the property and are not paid before or at closing, the maximum loan amount otherwise available must be reduced by the amount of the unpaid special assessments, unless sufficient deposits to pay them will be collected as part of the loan payment. The lender must document that current installments of taxes and assessments (or future installments of levied special assessments) have been paid or that sufficient deposits are being collected to pay them.
business_rules.conditions: Property has levied special assessments not paid before/at closing
business_rules.consequences: If reduced or deposits collected, compliant; if not, max loan exceeded/ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: No reduction if sufficient deposits to pay are collected as part of loan payment

source_reference.source_text: Impact of Special Assessments on Maximum Loan Amount If special assessments have been levied against the property and they are not paid before or at closing, the maximum loan amount otherwise available must be reduced by the amount of the unpaid special assessments (unless suﬃcient deposits to pay them will be collected as part of the loan payment). The lender must provide documentation to show that the current installments of taxes and assessments (or future installments of special assessments that have been levied) - including those which may have been attached as prior liens, but which are not now in arrears - have been paid or that suﬃcient deposits are being collected to pay them.

examples: Unpaid special assessment is $6,000 at closing; max loan amount must be reduced by $6,000 if not escrowed/collected.
Special assessment installments will be collected monthly via escrow deposits; no dollar-for-dollar reduction required.
Lender does not document payment or deposits for levied special assessment; loan is noncompliant and may be ineligible.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for purchase by Fannie Mae

examples: Loan eligibility profile meeting baseline conforming loan limit requirements
Loan eligibility profile flagged due to ARM feature noncompliance
Loan eligibility profile requiring escrow account per policy

business_rules.rule_name: If sold > 4 months after note date, current property value cannot be lower
business_rules.description: For loans sold to Fannie Mae more than four months from the note date, the lender must be able to warrant that the current property value is not less than the original value. If the lender cannot provide this warranty, the loan is not eligible for sale except on a negotiated basis.
business_rules.conditions: When note date to sale date exceeds four months for delivery to Fannie Mae
business_rules.consequences: If current value is lower or unwarranted, loan becomes negotiated-only/ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: Negotiated basis permitted when lender cannot provide the value warranty

source_reference.section_id: B2-1.5-02 (06/05/2024), Property Value for Loans Sold More than Four Months from Note Date
source_reference.source_text: For loans that are more than four months old from the date of the note to the date the loan is sold to Fannie Mae, the current value of the property cannot be less than the original value. If the lender is unable to warrant that the current value of the property is not less than the original value of the property, the loan is not eligible for sale to Fannie Mae by the lender except on a negotiated basis.

examples: Loan note date Jan 1; sold May 15 (>4 months); lender obtains evidence current value ≥ original and can warrant—eligible.
Loan note date Jan 10; sold Jun 1; updated data shows value decreased—loan not eligible for flow sale.
Lender cannot obtain adequate support for current value; delivers only via negotiated transaction after Fannie Mae agreement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Ineligible for standard sale; negotiated-only delivery

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: Flood coverage minimum is lesser of 100% RCV or UPB
business_rules.description: For one- to four-unit properties, the minimum amount of flood insurance required for first mortgages must equal the lesser of (1) 100% of the replacement cost value of the improvements, (2) the maximum coverage amount available from NFIP, or (3) the unpaid principal balance (UPB) of the loan (or loan amount at origination).
business_rules.conditions: First mortgage secured by 1-4 unit property where flood insurance is required
business_rules.consequences: Meets: flood policy satisfies minimum coverage; Violated: ineligible/delivery defect
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: B7-3-06, Requirements for One- to Four-Unit Properties
source_reference.source_text: Requirements for One- to Four-Unit Properties The minimum amount of ﬂood insurance required for ﬁrst mortgages must be equal to the lesser of 100% of the replacement cost value of the improvements, the maximum coverage amount available from NFIP, or the unpaid principal balance (UPB) of the loan (or loan amount at the time of origination).

examples: RCV $350,000; NFIP max $250,000; UPB $300,000 → minimum required flood coverage is $250,000 (lesser-of).
RCV $200,000; NFIP max $250,000; UPB $180,000 → minimum required is $180,000.
RCV $500,000; NFIP max $250,000; UPB $260,000 → minimum required is $250,000.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit lesser-of rule with three quantified coverage bases.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase if delivered

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: High LTV refi requires 12-month payment history limits
business_rules.description: To be eligible for the high LTV refinance option, the existing loan payment history for the most recent 12 months must show: (1) no delinquencies in the most recent 6 months; and (2) during months 7-12, no more than one 30-day delinquency and no delinquency greater than 30 days.
business_rules.conditions: Loan is evaluated for high LTV refinance option; 12-month payment history reviewed
business_rules.consequences: Meets: eligible for high LTV refi option; Violated: ineligible for the option
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in this excerpt

source_reference.section_id: B5-7-02, Underwriting and Documentation Requirements (Payment History)
source_reference.source_text: Payment History To be eligible for the high LTV reﬁnance option, the payment history for the existing loan for the most recent 12 months must reﬂect • no delinquencies in the most recent 6 months, and • in months 7-12, no more than one, 30-day delinquency and no delinquency greater than 30 days.

examples: One 30-day late 9 months ago; no lates in last 6 months → passes payment history requirement.
Two separate 30-day lates in months 7-12 → fails (more than one 30-day delinquency).
One 60-day delinquency 10 months ago → fails (delinquency greater than 30 days).
business_rules.extraction_notes: Clear quantified delinquency limits for eligibility.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery under high LTV refinance option

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: BR_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROFILE_ELIGIBILITY_007_009 establishes the foundational program eligibility (existing loan owned by Fannie, note date threshold, and seasoning). If those gating criteria are not met, the payment history test is irrelevant to program eligibility and the file should exit the high LTV refi workflow.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If base eligibility is not checked first, lenders may spend time collecting/verifying 12-month payment history for a loan that is categorically ineligible for the high LTV refinance option.

business_rules.rule_name: Title Insurance Coverage Minimum 100% of Loan Amount
business_rules.description: The lender must obtain title insurance coverage with an amount of coverage that is at least equal to the loan’s original principal amount (i.e., coverage amount must be >= original principal balance at origination).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a title insurance policy is required for the mortgage loan.
business_rules.consequences: If met: title coverage satisfies minimum amount. If violated: loan not eligible for delivery until corrected.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.source_text: Amount of Coverage The amount of title insurance coverage must at least equal the original principal amount of the loan. Other Requirements If a mortgage is registered with MERS and is originated naming MERS as original mortgagee of record, solely as nominee for the lender named in the security instrument and the note, and the lender's successors and assigns,

examples: Original loan amount is $350,000; title policy coverage must be at least $350,000.
Original loan amount is $726,200; title policy coverage of $700,000 fails and must be increased before delivery.
Original loan amount is $500,000; coverage of $500,000 passes even if current UPB later declines.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery rejection / corrective title policy endorsement required

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_PROHIBITION_026_010 defines when possession rights create an unacceptable title condition that must be cleared. A title policy that meets the dollar-amount requirement (BR_MORTGAGE_LOAN_CONSTRAINT_005_001) does not resolve an unacceptable underlying title condition; therefore, curing the title defect is a prerequisite to a deliverable title insurance posture.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If possession-right issues are not resolved, the lender may be unable to deliver an insurable, acceptable title condition to Fannie Mae, blocking delivery or requiring negotiated sale/curative actions.

business_rules.rule_name: Title Policy Must Include ALTA 8.1 Environmental Lien Endorsement
business_rules.description: The title insurance coverage must include an environmental protection lien endorsement (ALTA 8.1 Environmental Protection Lien Endorsement or an equivalent state form providing required coverage). As an alternative, the protections may be incorporated into the policy. For loans originated prior to 2008-01-01, endorsement forms that met requirements at origination are acceptable.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to mortgage loans requiring title insurance coverage for delivery to Fannie Mae.
business_rules.consequences: If met: title coverage acceptable. If violated: must obtain endorsement/policy revision; delivery risk increases.
business_rules.exceptions: Loans originated before 2008-01-01 may use then-acceptable endorsement forms.

source_reference.source_text: The title insurance coverage must include an environmental protection lien endorsement (ALTA 8.1 Environmental Protection Lien Endorsement or equivalent state form that provides the required coverage). References are to the ALTA form of endorsement, but state forms may be used as described in Terms of Coverage. As an alternative to endorsements, the requisite protections may be incorporated into the policy. For loans originated prior to January 1, 2008, endorsement forms that meet Fannie Mae’s requirements at the time of origination are acceptable.

examples: A 2026 purchase loan includes ALTA 8.1 endorsement attached to the lender’s policy; requirement satisfied.
A state-specific equivalent environmental lien form is used instead of ALTA 8.1; acceptable if it provides the required coverage.
A 2007-originated loan being delivered includes an older endorsement form; acceptable if it met Fannie Mae requirements at origination.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery rejection / corrective endorsement required

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: The environmental protection lien endorsement is a requirement within the title insurance package. In practice, lenders validate baseline title policy terms (including coverage amount) before validating required endorsements/forms, to avoid rework if the base policy is noncompliant.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If baseline policy requirements are not satisfied first, endorsement work may be wasted, and delivery can be delayed while reissuing the policy to correct coverage amount.

business_rules.rule_name: Title Policy Cannot Include ALTA 1990 Creditors’ Rights Exclusion
business_rules.description: Title policies for Fannie Mae-delivered loans must not include the creditors’ rights exclusion language adopted by ALTA in 1990.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to title insurance policies obtained for mortgage loans delivered to Fannie Mae.
business_rules.consequences: If violated: title policy must be revised/endorsed; loan delivery may be blocked until cured.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.source_text: For loans originated prior to January 1, 2008, endorsement forms that meet Fannie Mae’s requirements at the time of origination are acceptable. Title policies may not include the creditors’ rights exclusion language that ALTA adopted in 1990.

examples: A title commitment includes ALTA 1990 creditors’ rights exclusion language; lender must obtain removal/acceptable modification before delivery.
A title policy issued without any creditors’ rights exclusion language passes this requirement.
A policy uses alternative language that functions as a creditors’ rights exclusion; it should be treated as noncompliant and cured.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery rejection / title policy revision required

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: Both rules govern title policy acceptability; the minimum coverage amount check and prohibited language check are typically executed in a single title-policy compliance sequence.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If minimum requirements are not confirmed first, the prohibited language remediation may be duplicated when policy is reissued for amount corrections.

business_rules.rule_name: ARM Loans Require ALTA Endorsement 6 in Title Policy
business_rules.description: For an ARM mortgage loan, the title insurance policy must include ALTA Endorsement 6.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the mortgage loan has adjustable-rate features (ARM).
business_rules.consequences: If met: title coverage meets ARM endorsement requirement. If violated: obtain endorsement before delivery.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.source_text: Other Mortgages The table below provides the title insurance coverage requirements or endorsements for other types of loans. Transaction Type Title Insurance Requirements ARM loan The policy must include ALTA Endorsement 6.

examples: A 7/6 ARM includes ALTA Endorsement 6 in the lender’s title policy; compliant.
A 5/1 ARM policy is issued without ALTA Endorsement 6; lender must add endorsement prior to delivery.
A fixed-rate loan is not an ARM; this ALTA 6 requirement does not apply.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery rejection / endorsement addition required

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: The ARM-specific endorsement is part of a compliant title policy package. Establishing that the title policy meets baseline minimum coverage is a practical gating step before checking loan-feature endorsements like ALTA 6.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the baseline title policy is deficient, adding ALTA 6 does not cure the deficiency; delivery remains blocked and rework is required.

business_rules.rule_name: Manufactured Home Loans Require ALTA Endorsement 7.2 or Equivalent
