dependencies.impact_if_fails: If MH appraisal documentation is incomplete/incorrect, the loan may be misclassified and the wrong MI coverage/eligibility table applied, creating delivery ineligibility risk (e.g., erroneously allowing 95–97% LTV MI on standard MH).

dependencies.rationale: BR_MORTGAGE_LOAN_CONSTRAINT_039_002 makes certain LTV bands ineligible for MI (95.01–97.00% not applicable) and sets minimum/standard coverage in other bands. BR_SUBMITS_TO_UCDP_VALIDATION_013_002 ensures the appraised value at sale matches the UCDP appraisal, preventing shifts into/out of the ineligible LTV band due to mismatched values.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: A value mismatch can move a standard manufactured home loan into the ineligible 95.01–97.00% band or change the minimum coverage requirement, creating delivery ineligibility or pricing/MI LLPA errors.

business_rules.rule_name: Lender-paid MI prohibited for convertible ARMs except limited MBS case
business_rules.description: Lender-purchased mortgage insurance (LPMI) is accepted for all loan types except adjustable-rate mortgages that can be converted to fixed-rate mortgages. Fannie Mae may consider accepting LPMI for convertible ARMs that are in MBS pools only if the lender uses the 'market rate' post-conversion disposition option and assumes all interest rate risk.
business_rules.conditions: Loan uses lender-purchased MI; evaluate whether ARM is convertible to fixed-rate
business_rules.consequences: If convertible ARM without stated MBS conditions: LPMI not acceptable for delivery
business_rules.exceptions: Convertible ARMs in MBS may be considered if market-rate option used and lender assumes IR risk

source_reference.source_text: Fannie Mae accepts lender-purchased mortgage insurance coverage for all loan types except adjustable-rate mortgages that can be converted to ﬁxed-rate mortgages. Fannie Mae will consider accepting lender-purchased mortgage insurance for convertible adjustable-rate mortgages that are in MBS pools if the lender uses the “market rate” post-conversion disposition option and assumes all interest rate risk.

examples: 7/1 ARM with a conversion option using LPMI: prohibited for delivery as LPMI is not accepted for convertible ARMs.
Convertible ARM delivered into an MBS pool: may be considered only if lender elects the market-rate post-conversion disposition option and assumes all interest rate risk.
30-year fixed-rate loan using LPMI: acceptable (subject to LPMI lender requirements).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery rejection

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: BR_039_003 addresses when LPMI is accepted (and when it is not, e.g., certain convertible ARMs). BR_039_004 specifies mandatory LPMI administration and delivery attributes (corporate obligation premiums, servicing comp adjustments, continuous coverage, SFC 019). To satisfy ‘accepted’ LPMI delivery, the lender must comply with these operational requirements.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_039_004 fails, the loan can be rejected at delivery (missing SFC), become a servicing defect, or trigger contractual remedies due to lapsed/incorrect MI coverage—even if the loan type would otherwise allow LPMI under BR_039_003.

dependencies.rationale: BR_039_003 states Fannie Mae "may consider accepting" LPMI for convertible ARMs in MBS pools only if the lender uses the "market rate" post-conversion disposition option. BR_009_009 defines eligibility criteria specifically for converted ARMs delivered under the "market rate" option (12 months seasoned at conversion; current at delivery). Therefore, to rely on the BR_039_003 exception pathway, the loan must meet BR_009_009’s market-rate-option eligibility.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_009_009 is not satisfied, the lender cannot credibly use the market-rate post-conversion exception referenced in BR_039_003; LPMI acceptance for the convertible ARM should be denied, potentially forcing loan exclusion from MBS delivery or MI restructuring.

business_rules.rule_name: Lender-paid MI must be kept until paid in full and delivered with SFC 019
business_rules.description: When providing lender-purchased mortgage insurance (LPMI), the lender must: (i) pay for MI as a corporate obligation with an initial premium and renewal premiums (or acceptable lump-sum life-of-loan plan); (ii) increase servicing compensation by at least the MI renewal premium amount (not required for lump-sum life-of-loan plans); (iii) keep MI coverage in effect until the mortgage is paid in full; and (iv) deliver the loan with Special Feature Code (SFC) 019.
business_rules.conditions: Loan uses lender-purchased MI; applies through delivery and ongoing coverage maintenance
business_rules.consequences: Failure can cause delivery issues, servicing transfer defects, and contractual remedies
business_rules.exceptions: Servicing comp increase not required for lump-sum life-of-loan MI plans

source_reference.source_text: When providing lender-purchased mortgage insurance, the lender must: pay for the mortgage insurance coverage as a corporate obligation with an initial premium and renewal premiums for each subsequent period of coverage... Lump-sum premium plans that provide coverage for the life-of-the-mortgage loan also are acceptable; increase the servicing compensation... by at least the amount of the mortgage insurance renewal premium. (This is not required for lump-sum premium plans...); keep the mortgage insurance coverage in eﬀect until the mortgage is paid in full; deliver the loan with SFC 019;

examples: Monthly-renewal LPMI: lender pays initial premium + monthly renewals, increases servicing comp by at least the renewal premium, and delivers loan with SFC 019.
Single-premium life-of-loan LPMI: lender does not need to increase servicing compensation but still must deliver with SFC 019 and keep coverage in effect until payoff.
Lender delivers an LPMI loan without SFC 019: delivery data defect requiring correction and potential remedy exposure.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery rejection or repurchase demand

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: Credit card early fees capped at 2% of loan amount
business_rules.description: Credit card financing may be allowed for payment of common and customary fees paid outside of closing, but only up to a maximum of 2% of the loan amount. To permit this, the lender must either (1) confirm the borrower has sufficient liquid funds (financial reserves) to cover these charges in addition to other closing costs and down payment, or (2) recalculate the credit card payment to account for the new charges and include the updated payment in the qualifying ratio calculation.
business_rules.conditions: When borrower pays common/customary pre-closing fees by credit card
business_rules.consequences: If >2% or requirements unmet, fees must be re-sourced or loan re-underwritten/denied
business_rules.exceptions: None

source_reference.source_text: Lenders may allow credit card ﬁnancing for the payment of common and customary fees paid outside of closing up to a maximum of 2% of the loan amount if the lender: conﬁrms that the borrower has suﬃcient liquid funds (ﬁnancial reserves) to cover these charges (in addition to funds needed for other closing costs and the down payment that they will be paying); or recalculates the credit card payment, per B3-6-05, Monthly Debt Obligations , to account for the new charges and includes the updated payment in the qualifying ratio calculation.

examples: Loan amount $400,000: max eligible credit-card financed early fees = $8,000; borrower charged $7,500 in origination/lock fees—allowed if reserves cover $7,500 plus other funds-to-close.
Loan amount $250,000: borrower charged $6,000 early fees (2.4%); lender must treat as not permitted and require different payment method or reduce fees.
Borrower charges $3,000 early fees but has limited reserves; lender may still allow if lender recalculates the credit card minimum payment to reflect the new balance and includes it in DTI.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase demand if delivered

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: When borrowers use a credit card for pre-closing fees, underwriting must decide whether to (a) include the increased payment in DTI or (b) exclude it if the balance will be paid off. BR_032_002 provides the payoff-based exclusion logic that conditions how BR_040_007 is satisfied.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_032_002 is ignored, the lender may unnecessarily increase DTI using recalculated card payments (causing avoidable declines), or improperly exclude payments without proof of payoff (understating DTI).

dependencies.rationale: BR_040_007’s option (2) explicitly requires updating the credit card payment included in qualifying ratios. BR_035_009 provides the required calculation approach when the credit report lacks a stated minimum payment and no supplemental documentation supports a lower amount.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_035_009 is not applied in those cases, DTI can be understated, potentially generating incorrect approvals and post-close QC findings.

dependencies.rationale: A lender cannot ‘confirm sufficient liquid funds (financial reserves)’ without first excluding ineligible reserve sources (IPCs, lender contributions) as mandated by BR_023_007. Otherwise, the sufficiency determination is unreliable.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_023_007 is not applied, the lender may allow credit card financing of fees based on inflated/invalid reserves, leading to insufficient verified funds and underwriting defects.

dependencies.rationale: BR_MORTGAGE_LOAN_CONSTRAINT_040_007 allows credit card financing of certain fees only if the lender either confirms sufficient liquid funds (reserves) or recalculates credit card payment into qualifying ratios. When the lender uses the 'sufficient liquid funds' option, it generally needs documented depository assets, which—when DU requires depository asset verification—must meet BR_DESKTOP_UNDERWRITER_DOCUMENTATION_025_005 requirements.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 025_005 documentation is missing/deficient, the lender may be unable to substantiate sufficient liquid funds, forcing the lender to use the alternate branch (recalculate payment) or re-source fees. This can trigger re-underwriting and delays.

dependencies.rationale: BR_040_007’s two compliance paths depend on credible reserve verification (assets) or recalculated payments included in qualifying ratios (income/debts). BR_036_007 imposes a recency requirement for those same underwriting documents when the property is in a FEMA-declared disaster area eligible for Individual Assistance.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_036_007 fails, the lender must re-verify assets/income/credit; until updated, BR_040_007 cannot be satisfied reliably, delaying underwriting and potentially requiring re-underwrite/denial.

business_rules.rule_name: De minimis correction limited to $500 and barred if it reduces minimum contribution
business_rules.description: Under the remedies framework, a de minimis correction is a minor amount not to exceed $500 (or a higher agreed amount) that can resolve an identified significant defect when remitted/refunded/otherwise provided, but it cannot be used if the correction would cause a specific required minimum borrower contribution to not be met or to correct a violation of Fannie Mae’s Charter requirements.
business_rules.conditions: When lender proposes a correction to resolve an identified significant defect
business_rules.consequences: If allowed, defect may be treated as corrected; if barred, other remedy applies
business_rules.exceptions: May exceed $500 only if lender and Fannie Mae agree; barred cases apply

source_reference.section_id: D2-1-04 (08/30/2016), Definitions of Remedy and Correction
source_reference.source_text: One example of a correction is a “de minimis correction,” which is a minor amount not to exceed $500 (or such higher amount as the lender and Fannie Mae may agree) that, when remitted, refunded, or otherwise provided, corrects or otherwise resolves an identiﬁed signiﬁcant defect. However, a “de minimis correction” cannot be made when the correction would result in a speciﬁc required minimum borrower contribution not being met, or to correct a violation of Fannie Mae’s Charter requirements.

examples: Fee cure of $250 is remitted to resolve a minor disclosed-fee defect; qualifies as de minimis correction.
A $400 correction would reduce borrower’s required minimum contribution below the required level; de minimis correction is not permitted.
Lender and Fannie Mae agree in writing to a higher de minimis cap (e.g., $750) for a specific defect type; allowed only per agreement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Repurchase demand or repurchase alternative if defect remains significant

examples: Fannie Mae post-purchase QC review identifying an appraisal documentation defect
Lender post-closing QC audit sampling loans and documenting findings
QC review resulting in remediation and updated mortgage file records

dependencies.rationale: BR_014_007 can trigger defect identification (e.g., data inconsistencies, missed derogatory items). Once a significant defect exists, BR_001_009 constrains whether a de minimis correction is allowed. Thus, defect identification/triage precedes remedy selection.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_014_007 is not executed, defects may not be detected early and BR_001_009 may never be considered until post-close QC—raising costs and timelines (e.g., repurchase demands vs minor corrections).

dependencies.rationale: BR_001_009 governs when a de minimis correction may be used to resolve a significant defect. Identifying and substantiating a defect (and whether borrower contribution minimums/Charter requirements are impacted) typically requires the complete file governed by BR_011_009. Thus, robust documentation is a practical prerequisite to applying BR_001_009 correctly.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_011_009 fails (incomplete file), the lender may be unable to prove a defect is minor/eligible for de minimis correction, forcing harsher remedies or causing delays while reconstructing documentation.

dependencies.rationale: BR_013_004 defines a minimum 3% borrower own-funds contribution for certain HomeReady purchase scenarios. BR_001_009 bars using a de minimis correction if it would cause a required minimum borrower contribution to not be met. Thus, QC remedy selection under BR_001_009 depends on identifying whether BR_013_004 applies and whether the correction would reduce the borrower’s qualifying contribution below 3%.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_013_004 is not considered during QC remedy selection, the lender might improperly apply a $500 de minimis correction that effectively reduces the borrower’s own-funds contribution below the minimum, leaving the significant defect unremedied and increasing repurchase/remedy exposure.

dependencies.rationale: BR_001_009 states a de minimis correction cannot be used "to correct a violation of Fannie Mae’s Charter requirements." BR_008_010 describes the MI/credit enhancement requirement as "charter-compliant" and requires MI absent another charter-compliant enhancement for LTV > 80%. In a QC/remedies workflow, if the significant defect is missing required MI/credit enhancement under BR_008_010, the defect cannot be cured via de minimis correction under BR_001_009.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If this dependency is missed, QC may incorrectly close a charter-level defect via a small refund/correction, leading to unresolved charter noncompliance and heightened remedy exposure (including repurchase).

dependencies.rationale: BR_023_009 noncompliance leads to appraisal defects and potential value/report corrections. When QC identifies that defect as significant, BR_001_009 governs whether a small monetary correction can resolve it or whether other remedies are required.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_001_009 bars de minimis correction, the lender must pursue alternative remediation (e.g., revised appraisal, repurchase, indemnification) instead of a simple refund/credit approach.

dependencies.rationale: BR_030_008 failures (endorsement chain break, MIN not registered) produce delivery/documentation defects. When those are classified as significant defects, BR_001_009 controls de minimis correction availability and prohibits using de minimis to fix certain minimum contribution or Charter-related issues.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_001_009 disallows de minimis correction, the lender must cure the document defect substantively (correct endorsements/MERS registration) or face stronger remedies.

dependencies.rationale: BR_039_007 sets enforceability-critical endorsement requirements. When QC identifies a breach, BR_001_009 determines whether a de minimis correction is an allowable remedy or whether the lender must pursue a different resolution path.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Incorrectly applying de minimis corrections to endorsement defects can leave the defect unresolved and expose the lender to stronger contractual remedies.

business_rules.rule_name: Mandatory delivery commitment shortfall limited to $10,000 or 2.5%
business_rules.description: A mandatory delivery commitment generally requires the lender to deliver eligible mortgages equal to at least the minimum required delivery amount by the commitment expiration date. The minimum required delivery amount cannot be less than the original commitment amount by more than $10,000 or 2.5% of the original amount.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when lender enters a mandatory delivery commitment for whole loans.
business_rules.consequences: If delivery falls below minimum, lender fails commitment and may need pair-off actions.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in this glossary definition.

source_reference.source_text: mandatory delivery commitment A whole loan commitment that generally requires the lender to deliver eligible mortgages equal to at least the minimum required delivery amount (which is an amount that will not be less than the original commitment amount by more than $10,000 or 2.5% of the original amount) by the expiration date of the commitment.

examples: Original commitment $2,000,000: minimum required delivery is $1,950,000 (2.5% shortfall) not $1,900,000.
Original commitment $300,000: 2.5% is $7,500, so the tighter $10,000 cap does not apply; minimum is $292,500.
Original commitment $350,000: 2.5% is $8,750; minimum is $341,250 (not $340,000).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Pair-off fee or other commitment remedy

examples: Seller/Servicer contract with representations and warranties for delivered mortgages
Contractual record retention and examination provisions for mortgage files
Contract remedy section enabling repurchase or make-whole payments

dependencies.rationale: BR_005_010 prohibits delivery of OFAC-listed borrowers and requires cessation/blocking upon matches. BR_002_007 requires meeting minimum delivery amount by expiration. OFAC events override planned deliveries and can force substitution/pair-off actions.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If OFAC screening is not integrated, the lender may plan to satisfy a commitment with loans that later become blocked, leading to last-minute substitutions, operational delays, and increased likelihood of failing minimum required delivery amounts.

dependencies.rationale: BR_010_002 defines property utilities eligibility for purchase/securitization. Loans failing collateral eligibility cannot be delivered and therefore cannot be relied upon to satisfy BR_002_007 minimum delivery requirements.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If utilities rights (well/septic access) are not confirmed, the loan may be ineligible and must be replaced in the commitment delivery plan.

dependencies.rationale: BR_010_003 is a collateral legality/marketability eligibility requirement. BR_002_007 requires delivery of eligible mortgages. Therefore, zoning/legal use must be cleared before loans are counted toward mandatory delivery fulfillment forecasts.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_010_003 fails after commitment planning, a planned delivery cannot be executed, risking pair-off or substitution under time pressure.

dependencies.rationale: BR_010_004 creates a delivery bar. BR_002_007 measures fulfillment via delivery of eligible mortgages. Therefore, the land-use reconstruction/maintenance screen must be run before using the loan in commitment fulfillment calculations.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_010_004 is discovered late (e.g., during title/legal review), the loan is removed from deliverable inventory, potentially creating a commitment shortfall.

dependencies.rationale: BR_020_008 defines acceptability thresholds for new/newly converted condo projects under Full Review. A loan secured by a non-acceptable project is ineligible for delivery; thus it cannot be counted toward BR_002_007’s eligible delivery minimum.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If presale percentage falls below 50% or phase/building rules are violated, the loan must be excluded from delivery allocation and replaced to avoid a commitment shortfall.

dependencies.rationale: BR_020_009 defines when a property is acceptable from a waste-site contamination standpoint, and when additional evaluation/Phase II is required. Loans with unresolved or failing environmental conditions are not reliably deliverable; thus this screen should occur before using the loan to meet BR_002_007 minimums.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If a Phase II is required and not completed by the commitment expiration, the loan may miss delivery timing, creating a commitment shortfall and pair-off exposure.

dependencies.rationale: BR_009_008 renders loans with payment abatements ineligible for sale. Because BR_002_007 measures required delivery amounts based on eligible loans delivered by expiration, BR_009_008 must be applied before commitment allocation and delivery forecasting.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If abatement-ineligible loans remain in the commitment set, the lender may fail minimum delivery and incur pair-off or substitution costs.

dependencies.rationale: BR_COOP_UNIT_PROHIBITION_019_006 makes certain condo/co-op project loans ineligible for purchase. BR_LENDER_CONTRACT_CONSTRAINT_002_007 requires delivery of eligible mortgages. Thus, project status eligibility is a prerequisite determinant for whether a loan can be delivered under a commitment.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If project status is Unavailable, the associated loans cannot be delivered, potentially creating delivery shortfalls and financial consequences under the commitment framework.

dependencies.rationale: BR_COOP_UNIT_CONSTRAINT_019_007 defines an ineligible project characteristic (commercial space >35%). BR_LENDER_CONTRACT_CONSTRAINT_002_007 requires delivering eligible mortgages. Therefore, passing 019_007 is a prerequisite for condo/co-op loans to be deliverable/countable under the commitment.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the 35% threshold is exceeded, loans in that project must be excluded from delivery, potentially impacting commitment minimums and requiring substitution/pair-off handling.

dependencies.rationale: Mandatory delivery commitments rely on acceptance of delivered loans. Allonge defects commonly produce delivery suspense/cure requirements; unresolved defects can prevent acceptance by custodian/Fannie, affecting BR_002_007.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Custodial rejection/suspense delays delivery and may cause commitment expiration shortfalls and pair-off exposure.

dependencies.rationale: BR_030_008 governs core custodial deliverables (blank endorsement chain, MIN registered prior to delivery for MERS). If not met, delivery is commonly suspended pending cure, affecting BR_002_007 delivery minimums.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Delivery suspense, cure cycles, or ultimate ineligibility reduces delivered UPB and increases pair-off risk.

dependencies.rationale: BR_031_010 sets transaction eligibility limits (purchase/limited cash-out refi) and POA documentation validity requirements. A POA-signed note/security instrument failing these requirements typically cannot be delivered, directly affecting BR_002_007 outcomes.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: POA defects require cure or render the loan undeliverable; this can cause commitment delivery shortfalls and pair-off.

dependencies.rationale: BR_036_007 imposes documentation dating constraints in FEMA disaster areas. If the loan cannot be cleared for delivery due to outdated verifications, it cannot reliably be delivered within the commitment window governed by BR_002_007.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Outdated docs trigger re-verification and re-underwrite, causing delays that can miss commitment expiration.

dependencies.rationale: BR_028_009 governs acceptable RIN execution standards. If not satisfied, the loan file may be documentation-defective and may not be accepted for delivery in time to satisfy BR_002_007.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Defective RIN documentation can trigger curative actions (re-notarization where possible, additional evidence gathering) causing delivery delays.

dependencies.rationale: BR_020_007 requires long-term retention and the ability to provide audit trail files. In practice, inability to evidence execution/identity can lead to loan file defects and delivery/audit exceptions that can delay or reduce deliverable loans under BR_002_007 timelines.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If retention/audit trail cannot be provided, the lender may face delivery suspense, post-delivery remedies, or exclusion from delivery pools, impacting meeting the commitment minimum.

dependencies.rationale: BR_002_007 measures fulfillment as delivery of eligible mortgages by an expiration date. BR_002_006 states HOEPA loans are "not eligible for delivery" and must be blocked/removed. Therefore, HOEPA screening must occur before a loan is allocated to satisfy a mandatory commitment or before counting it toward minimum required delivery.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If HOEPA status is not evaluated first, the lender may allocate prohibited loans to a commitment, later remove them at delivery, and fall below minimum delivery, triggering pair-off actions and potential financial penalties.

dependencies.rationale: BR_002_001 makes loans older than six months ineligible for purchase on a flow basis. BR_002_007 requires delivery of eligible mortgages by a deadline. Therefore, commitment allocation and delivery forecasting depends on BR_002_001 being satisfied for any loan expected to be delivered under the commitment.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_002_001 fails, the loan cannot be delivered as planned; commitment delivery may fall short, triggering pair-off actions or requiring urgent loan substitution.

dependencies.rationale: BR_002_005 states loans outside term bounds are "not eligible for purchase/securitization." BR_002_007 requires delivery of eligible mortgages by expiration. Therefore, term eligibility must be checked before commitment allocation and before counting loans toward minimum required delivery.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_002_005 fails for loans in a commitment bucket, the lender must substitute loans or risk failing minimum delivery and incurring pair-off exposure.

dependencies.rationale: BR_LENDER_CONTRACT_CONSTRAINT_002_007 requires delivery of 'eligible mortgages' by the expiration date. BR_SUBMITS_TO_UCDP_PROCESS_031_005 is a pre-sale requirement ('Successful' prior to sale). A loan that cannot be sold due to UCDP failure cannot be delivered to meet the commitment minimum, creating pair-off/shortfall risk.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If UCDP is not Successful, loans are held in suspense and may miss the commitment expiration date, causing the lender to fail minimum delivery requirements and incur pair-off costs or other contractual remedies.

business_rules.rule_name: DU limited waiver invalid if file contains undisclosed contradictory data
business_rules.description: For loans submitted to DU, the lender must review the entire underwriting file for data or information not submitted to DU or inconsistent with what was submitted. If derogatory or contradictory information is found, the lender must take appropriate action (e.g., further investigate to determine whether it would change the DU recommendation, or set aside the DU recommendation if grounds exist for a different decision). If such information exists and is not appropriately addressed, the limited waiver of representations and warranties is no longer valid even for an Approve/Eligible recommendation.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to loans submitted to DU for evaluation and potential limited waiver
business_rules.consequences: If acted on, lender can re-evaluate; if not, limited waiver becomes invalid
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: For loans submitted to DU for evaluation, the lender must review the entire underwriting ﬁle to determine whether it includes any data or other information that was either not submitted to DU, or is inconsistent with any data or information that was in fact submitted to DU. If any such information (especially of a derogatory or contradictory nature) is found, the lender must take appropriate action, such as further investigating the information, to see if it would change the DU recommendation, or setting aside the DU recommendation

examples: DU casefile shows no delinquent housing history, but file later reveals recent 60-day mortgage delinquency not entered into DU: lender must re-evaluate; waiver is invalid if not addressed.
Borrower employment in file conflicts with DU-submitted employer and indicates job loss prior to closing: lender must investigate and potentially set aside DU recommendation.
Lender discovers new undisclosed liability post-submission but before closing: must determine whether DU recommendation changes; waiver depends on proper action.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan repurchase demand

examples: Seller/Servicer contract with representations and warranties for delivered mortgages
Contractual record retention and examination provisions for mortgage files
Contract remedy section enabling repurchase or make-whole payments

business_rules.rule_name: Enforcement relief requires acquisition on or after July 1, 2014
business_rules.description: To be eligible for representation and warranty enforcement relief, a loan must have been acquired by Fannie Mae as either (1) a whole loan purchased on or after July 1, 2014, or (2) a mortgage loan delivered into MBS with a pool issue date on or after July 1, 2014.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when determining whether a loan can obtain enforcement relief framework
business_rules.consequences: If date test met, loan may qualify for relief subject to other criteria
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: Loans must have been acquired by Fannie Mae as either: whole loans purchased on or after July 1, 2014; or mortgage loans delivered into MBS with pool issue dates on or after July 1, 2014.

examples: Whole loan acquired June 15, 2014: fails acquisition-date eligibility and cannot obtain enforcement relief under this framework.
Loan delivered into MBS with pool issue date July 1, 2014: meets acquisition-date criterion (other criteria still apply).
Loan delivered into MBS with pool issue date August 2020: meets acquisition-date criterion and can proceed to payment history/QC review tests.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan repurchase demand

examples: Seller/Servicer contract with representations and warranties for delivered mortgages
Contractual record retention and examination provisions for mortgage files
Contract remedy section enabling repurchase or make-whole payments

business_rules.rule_name: Enforcement relief after 36 payments with max two 30-day lates and no 60+
business_rules.description: For all loans other than Fannie Mae high LTV refinance loans, when enforcement relief is based on acceptable payment history, relief occurs after the borrower pays the first 36 monthly payments due following the loan acquisition date, provided the borrower had no more than two 30-day delinquencies, had no 60-day or greater delinquencies, and is not 30 or more days delinquent on the 36th monthly payment.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to non–high LTV refinance loans seeking relief based on payment history
business_rules.consequences: If thresholds met, lender is relieved from remedies for covered breaches; else not
business_rules.exceptions: High LTV refinance loans follow separate 12/36-month payment history test

source_reference.source_text: For all loans other than Fannie Mae high LTV reﬁnance loans: To be eligible for relief under the framework, if the relief is based on the borrower’s acceptable payment history, the relief will occur upon payment by the borrower of the ﬁrst 36 monthly payments due following the loan acquisition date, provided that the borrower had no more than two 30-day delinquencies, had no 60-day or greater delinquencies, and is not 30 or more days delinquent with respect to the 36th monthly payment.

examples: Borrower has one 30-day late in month 10 and one 30-day late in month 22, no 60+ delinquencies, and month 36 is current: meets payment history thresholds for relief.
Borrower has three 30-day delinquencies over 36 months: fails relief threshold (more than two 30-day delinquencies).
Borrower is 30 days delinquent on the 36th payment: fails relief requirement even if prior months were acceptable.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan repurchase demand

examples: Seller/Servicer contract with representations and warranties for delivered mortgages
Contractual record retention and examination provisions for mortgage files
Contract remedy section enabling repurchase or make-whole payments

dependencies.rationale: BR_014_008 defines the eligibility window (whole loan purchased on/after 7/1/2014 or MBS pool issue date on/after 7/1/2014). BR_014_009 then describes when relief occurs after 36 payments for eligible loans (non–high LTV refi). The eligibility gate must be checked first to avoid incorrect relief determinations.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_014_008 fails and BR_014_009 is applied anyway, the lender may incorrectly assume representation & warranty relief has attached, causing improper remedy responses, mispriced risk, and disputes in defect resolution.

business_rules.rule_name: PMI lapse requires reinstate or repurchase within 30 days; reimburse in 15 days
business_rules.description: If failure of a Private Mortgage Insurance policy to be in force on or after the Purchase Date is due to Seller negligence, Seller must, no later than 30 days after receiving notification, either take actions necessary to cause the policy to be in force/reinstated or repurchase the related Servicing Rights at the Servicing Rights Repurchase Price. If Seller fails to comply, Servicer must notify Fannie Mae to initiate the repurchase process. If a rescission is notified and the policy cannot be reinstated, Seller must reimburse Servicer within 15 business days of Servicer’s reimbursement request.
business_rules.conditions: PMI policy not in force/rescinded post-Purchase Date due to Seller negligence
business_rules.consequences: If cured timely, servicing continues; if not, repurchase process is initiated
business_rules.exceptions: Servicer not required to advance funds to reinstate PMI

source_reference.source_text: Seller shall, no later than thirty (30) days after Seller’s receipt of notiﬁcation thereof, either (a) take all such actions as are necessary to cause such policy to be in force or to be reinstated by the applicable insurer, or (b) repurchase the related Servicing Rights from Servicer at the Servicing Rights Repurchase Price. If Seller fails to comply with the preceding sentence with respect to a Mortgage Loan, Servicer shall notify Fannie Mae, to initiate the repurchase process with respect to such Mortgage Loan. At no time shall Servicer be made to advance funds to reinstate any Private Mortgage Insurance Policy... Seller shall reimburse Servicer for any amounts due within ﬁfteen (15) Business Days

examples: Seller receives notice on May 1 that PMI lapsed due to Seller error → by May 31 Seller must reinstate PMI or repurchase servicing rights.
Seller does not act within 30 days → Servicer notifies Fannie Mae to initiate repurchase process for the affected mortgage loan.
PMI policy is rescinded and cannot be reinstated; Servicer requests reimbursement on June 10 → Seller must reimburse by 15 business days after June 10.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Initiate repurchase process / servicing rights repurchase

examples: Seller/Servicer contract with representations and warranties for delivered mortgages
Contractual record retention and examination provisions for mortgage files
Contract remedy section enabling repurchase or make-whole payments

dependencies.rationale: BR_018_010 is a post-purchase cure/repurchase pathway when a PMI policy is not in force due to seller negligence. BR_039_001 establishes when MI coverage is required by LTV/loan attributes, making PMI existence and continuity a compliance-critical element. Thus, MI coverage requirements conditionally trigger the operational risk controlled by BR_018_010.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_039_001-required MI is not properly maintained and then lapses post-purchase, BR_018_010 escalates into cure within 30 days or repurchase/servicing-rights repurchase. Failure increases repurchase exposure and servicing disruption.

business_rules.rule_name: SRP refund 100% if paid off in 120 days and $1,500 EPD fee
business_rules.description: Under the Servicing Marketplace Mortgage Loan Servicing Purchase and Sale Agreement: (a) If a mortgage loan is paid in full within 120 days of the Purchase Date, Seller must refund 100% of the price paid by Servicer for the servicing rights, plus any applicable associated interest loss and any applicable fees due to Fannie Mae. (b) If the first, second, or third monthly payment due Servicer becomes 60 or more days delinquent after the Purchase Date (an EPD Mortgage Loan), Seller must reimburse Servicer for the original purchase price paid for that EPD Mortgage Loan and pay an early payment default fee of $1,500 per EPD Mortgage Loan; Seller must wire the applicable amount within seven (7) Business Days from Servicer’s request date. Failure to pay/refund as required constitutes a breach of the servicing rights purchase/sale agreement.
business_rules.conditions: When servicing rights were purchased and payoff/EPD conditions occur after Purchase Date
business_rules.consequences: Seller must refund SRP and/or pay EPD fee within specified timeframes; nonpayment breaches agreement
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in excerpt

source_reference.source_text: 7.4 Refund of the SRP and Repurchase of Mortgage Loan . (a) Mortgage Loan Paid in Full . If the Mortgage Loan is paid in full within 120 days of the Purchase Date, Seller shall refund one hundred percent (100%) of the price paid by Servicer for such Servicing Rights, plus any applicable associated interest loss and any applicable fees due to Fannie Mae... (iii) ...the ﬁrst, second or third monthly payment due Servicer becomes sixty (60) or more days delinquent... Seller shall reimburse Servicer for the original purchase price paid... along with an early payment default fee of $1,500 per EPD Mortgage Loan. Seller shall wire to Servicer the applicable amount within seven (7) Business Days from the date of Servicer’s request.

(a) Mortgage Loan Paid in Full . If the Mortgage Loan is paid in full within 120 days of the Purchase Date, Seller shall refund one hundred percent (100%) of the price paid by Servicer for such Servicing Rights, plus any applicable associated interest loss and any applicable fees due to Fannie Mae.

(iii) In the event that for any Mortgage Loan for which Servicer has purchased the Servicing Rights, the ﬁrst, second or third monthly payment due Servicer becomes sixty (60) or more days delinquent (each such Mortgage Loan, an “ EPD Mortgage Loan ”) after the applicable Purchase Date, Seller shall reimburse Servicer for the original purchase price paid for said EPD Mortgage Loan, along with an early payment default fee of $1,500 per EPD Mortgage Loan. Seller shall wire to Servicer the applicable amount within seven (7) Business Days from the date of Servicer’s request.

examples: Servicing rights purchased on Jan 1; borrower refinances and pays off Mar 15 (within 120 days) → seller refunds 100% SRP plus applicable interest loss/fees.
First payment due becomes 65 days delinquent after purchase date → seller pays original SRP amount plus $1,500 EPD fee within 7 business days of servicer request.
Third payment becomes 60+ days delinquent on two separate loans → seller owes $1,500 per loan (2 × $1,500) plus reimbursement of both purchase prices.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Contractual reimbursement demand and fees; potential formal dispute/remedies

examples: Seller/Servicer contract with representations and warranties for delivered mortgages
Contractual record retention and examination provisions for mortgage files
Contract remedy section enabling repurchase or make-whole payments

deduplication_info.rationale: BR_GOVERNS_RELATIONSHIP_WITH_CONSTRAINT_003_005 is a direct restatement of clause (a) within BR_LENDER_CONTRACT_CONSTRAINT_037_007: refund 100% of servicing rights price plus associated interest loss/fees if paid in full within 120 days of Purchase Date. BR_GOVERNS_RELATIONSHIP_WITH_CONSTRAINT_003_006 is a direct restatement of the EPD clause portion: if the 1st/2nd/3rd payment becomes 60+ days delinquent after Purchase Date, seller must reimburse the servicing-rights purchase price and pay a $1,500 EPD fee, wired within 7 business days of request. All thresholds and consequences align; differences are only that the duplicates were extracted as separate rules rather than combined in one.
deduplication_info.primary_selection_reason: BR_LENDER_CONTRACT_CONSTRAINT_037_007 selected as primary because it is the most comprehensive: it includes both (a) the payoff-within-120-days servicing-rights refund obligation and (b) the early payment default (EPD) reimbursement/fee obligation, whereas the duplicates each capture only one sub-clause.

dependencies.rationale: To detect an EPD mortgage loan (e.g., first three payments becoming 60+ days delinquent), servicer systems rely on the established first payment due date and cadence. BR_038_009 governs compliant setup of that first payment date for delivered whole loans, which downstream supports correct EPD surveillance timelines.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_038_009 is wrong, EPD monitoring windows can be misaligned, causing late detection, incorrect fee/refund demands, or disputes over whether a loan meets the EPD definition.

business_rules.rule_name: DU Ineligible if 60+ day mortgage delinquency within 12 months
business_rules.description: If any borrower’s credit report contains a mortgage tradeline that is 60 or more days past due when last reported, and that tradeline was reported within the 12 months prior to the credit report date, the loan casefile will receive an Ineligible recommendation and is ineligible for delivery to Fannie Mae. This policy applies to all mortgage tradelines, including first liens, second liens, home improvement loans, HELOCs, and manufactured home loans.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when DU evaluates a casefile and credit report shows mortgage delinquency.
business_rules.consequences: DU returns Ineligible; loan becomes ineligible for delivery to Fannie Mae.
business_rules.exceptions: Limited discretion if documented delinquency not borrower’s fault (e.g., servicer error).

source_reference.source_text: If any borrower’s credit report contains a mortgage tradeline that is 60 or more days past due when the account was last reported by the creditor and the account was reported within the 12 months prior to the credit report date, the loan caseﬁle will receive an Ineligible recommendation and will be ineligible for delivery to Fannie Mae. ... Borrowers may not bring past-due mortgage accounts current prior to closing in order to circumvent Fannie Mae’s policy regarding past-due mortgages. However, the lender may apply some discretion ... if it determines and documents that the past-due account status was not the fault of the borrower

examples: Credit report dated 06/01 shows a first mortgage 60 days past due as of 05/15; tradeline reported within prior 12 months → DU Ineligible.
Borrower has a HELOC with a 90-day delinquency reported 8 months ago → DU Ineligible due to prior mortgage delinquency policy.
Servicer misapplied payments causing a 60-day delinquency; lender documents servicer error and account current → lender may apply discretion per guide text.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Delivery ineligible (DU Ineligible recommendation)

examples: Approve/Eligible with standard documentation conditions
Approve/Ineligible requiring changes to become eligible
Refer with Caution requiring manual underwriting and additional conditions

business_rules.rule_name: Collections payoff thresholds: $5,000 owner-occ; $250/$1,000 investment
business_rules.description: Non-mortgage accounts reported as past due (not collection accounts) must be brought current. Medical collection accounts are excluded from the stated payoff limits and are not required to be paid in full at or prior to closing. For one-unit principal residence properties, borrowers are not required to pay off outstanding collections or non-mortgage charge-offs regardless of amount. For two- to four-unit owner-occupied and second home properties, collections and non-mortgage charge-offs totaling more than $5,000 must be paid in full prior to or at closing. For investment properties, individual collection and non-mortgage charge-off accounts ≥ $250 and accounts that total more than $1,000 must be paid in full prior to or at closing.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when underwriting evaluates collections/charge-offs for qualifying and closing.
business_rules.consequences: If required payoffs not completed, loan fails DU conditions and may be ineligible.
business_rules.exceptions: Medical collections excluded; 1-unit primary has no payoff requirement per guide.

source_reference.section_id: B3-5.3-09 (02/05/2025), Past-Due, Collection, and Charge-Off of Non-Mortgage Accounts
source_reference.source_text: Medical collection accounts are excluded from the limits below and are not required to be paid in full at or prior to closing. For one-unit, principal residence properties, borrowers are not required to pay oﬀ outstanding collections or non-mortgage charge-oﬀs—regardless of the amount. ... For two- to four-unit owner-occupied and second home properties, collections and non-mortgage charge-oﬀs totaling more than $5,000 must be paid in full prior to or at closing. For investment properties, individual collection and non-mortgage charge-oﬀ accounts equal to or greater than $250 and accounts that total more than $1,000 must be paid in full prior to or at closing.

examples: 1-unit primary residence: borrower has $18,000 non-medical collections → no payoff required by this guide section.
2-unit owner-occupied: borrower has $5,200 total collections → must pay in full prior to or at closing.
Investment property: three collections of $200, $260, $600 (total $1,060) → must pay $260 and $600 (≥$250) and because total >$1,000, payoff required prior to/at closing.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Underwriting condition failure; potential delivery ineligibility

examples: Approve/Eligible with standard documentation conditions
Approve/Ineligible requiring changes to become eligible
Refer with Caution requiring manual underwriting and additional conditions

dependencies.rationale: BR_002_009 governs how delinquent non-mortgage accounts/collections/charge-offs are treated for qualifying/closing. BR_014_007 mandates the lender reconcile the full underwriting file against DU-submitted data; if new derogatory info exists, the lender must investigate/resubmit DU or set aside DU. This creates a validation layer over the application of BR_002_009 in DU workflows.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_014_007 is not performed, the lender may rely on DU findings that are incomplete or contradicted by the file, invalidating limited waiver protections and increasing defect/remedy exposure.

business_rules.rule_name: Buydown loans must be qualified at the note rate not the reduced rate
business_rules.description: When underwriting a loan with a temporary interest rate buydown, the lender must qualify the borrower based on the note rate, without considering the bought-down rate. The underwriting decision must therefore reflect qualifying payment calculations at the permanent note rate rather than the temporary reduced payment.
business_rules.conditions: When underwriting a mortgage loan that includes a temporary interest rate buydown
business_rules.consequences: If qualified at reduced rate, underwriting is noncompliant and may be remedied
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: Qualifying the Borrower When underwriting loans that have a temporary interest rate buydown, the lender must qualify the borrower based on the note rate without consideration of the bought-down rate.

examples: Note rate 6.50% with 2-1 buydown; lender uses 6.50% P&I to qualify: compliant.
Note rate 6.50% with buydown to 4.50% year 1; lender qualifies using 4.50% payment: noncompliant underwriting.
DU approval: underwriter ensures qualifying payment aligns to note rate even though initial payment is lower: compliant.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Post-purchase underwriting defect remedy / repurchase exposure

examples: Approve/Eligible with standard documentation conditions
Approve/Ineligible requiring changes to become eligible
Refer with Caution requiring manual underwriting and additional conditions

business_rules.rule_name: Manual UW collections exempt if under $250 or $1,000 total
business_rules.description: For manually underwritten loans, non-medical collection accounts and charge-offs on non-mortgage accounts do not have to be paid off at or prior to closing when (a) the balance of an individual account is less than $250 OR (b) the total balance of all such accounts is $1,000 or less. Amounts exceeding these limits must be paid off at or prior to closing.
business_rules.conditions: When decision_type indicates manual underwriting and collections/charge-offs are non-medical
business_rules.consequences: If within thresholds, payoff not required; if over thresholds, payoff required or loan is noncompliant
business_rules.exceptions: Applies only to manually underwritten loans and non-medical accounts

source_reference.section_id: B3-6-07, Collections, Charge-Offs, Judgments, and Liens
source_reference.source_text: For manually underwritten loans, non-medical collection accounts and charge-oﬀs on non-mortgage accounts do not have to be paid oﬀ at or prior to closing if the balance of an individual account is less than $250 or the total balance of all accounts is $1,000 or less. Non-medical collection accounts and charge-oﬀs on non-mortgage accounts that exceed these limits must be paid oﬀ at or prior to closing.

examples: Manual UW loan: borrower has one $200 non-medical collection; payoff is not required by this policy.
Manual UW loan: borrower has five collections totaling $950; payoff is not required by this policy.
Manual UW loan: borrower has collections totaling $1,250; payoff is required at or prior to closing.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery rejection/repurchase demand

examples: Approve/Eligible with standard documentation conditions
Approve/Ineligible requiring changes to become eligible
Refer with Caution requiring manual underwriting and additional conditions

dependencies.rationale: BR_002_009 sets payoff requirements for collections/charge-offs based on property type/occupancy. BR_032_005 states that for manually underwritten loans, certain non-medical collections/charge-offs do not need to be paid off below specified thresholds—altering the baseline requirement. Therefore, payoff decision logic must first determine underwriting method and apply the exception where applicable.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the override is missed, the lender may impose unnecessary payoffs (delaying closing, harming borrower liquidity) or, if misapplied, fail to require payoff when required (creating underwriting defects and delivery risk).

business_rules.rule_name: ADU rental income limited to 30% of total qualifying income
business_rules.description: For a one-unit principal residence with an accessory dwelling unit (ADU), rental income used in qualifying is limited to 30% of the total qualifying income. When the borrower does not have a current primary housing payment, no rental income can be used in qualifying. When the borrower has a housing payment but no property management experience, rental income used in qualifying remains limited to 30% and cannot exceed the PITIA.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when borrower uses ADU rental income to qualify on a 1-unit primary home.
business_rules.consequences: If rental income exceeds cap, qualifying income must be reduced; DTI must be recalculated.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated for the 30% cap in the provided table.

source_reference.section_id: B3-3.8-01 (10/08/2025), Subject Property rental income restrictions table
source_reference.source_text: 1 Unit Primary Residence with ADU Yes Yes Rental income used in qualifying is limited to 30% of the total qualifying income No Rental income used in qualifying is limited to 30% of the total qualifying income and cannot exceed PITIA No NA No rental income can be used in qualifying

examples: Borrower total qualifying income (before cap) would be $10,000/month; max ADU rental income usable is $3,000/month (30%).

Borrower has housing payment and ADU rent of $2,500/month, but PITIA is $2,200 and no property management experience → usable ADU rental income is capped at $2,200 and also must be ≤30% of total qualifying income.
Borrower reports no current primary housing payment (e.g., living rent-free) → no ADU rental income can be used for qualifying.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Underwriting income adjustment; potential delivery defect if exceeded

examples: U.S. citizen borrower purchasing an owner-occupied home
Permanent resident borrower applying for a conforming loan
Borrower with multiple financed properties seeking an additional mortgage

business_rules.rule_name: Support or Alimony Obligations Over 10 Months Must Be Counted
business_rules.description: If the borrower is required to pay alimony, child support, equalization payments, or separate maintenance under a divorce decree/separation agreement/other written legal agreement and the payments must continue for more than ten months, the payments must be treated as recurring monthly debt obligations. The lender must obtain and retain documentation confirming the obligation amount. For alimony, equalization, and separate maintenance, the lender may reduce qualifying income by the obligation amount instead of including it as a monthly DTI payment (with DU entry as negative income when applicable).
business_rules.conditions: Borrower has court-ordered support obligation continuing more than 10 months
business_rules.consequences: If included/reduced correctly, DTI is compliant; if omitted, underwriting defect risk
business_rules.exceptions: Voluntary payments excluded; special exception language noted for alimony

source_reference.source_text: 505When the borrower is required to pay alimony, child support, equalization payments, or separate maintenance payments under a divorce decree, separation agreement, or any other written legal agreement—and those payments must continue to be made for more than ten months—the payments must be considered as part of the borrower’s recurring monthly debt obligations. However, voluntary payments do not need to be taken into consideration and an exception is allowed for alimony. A copy of the divorce decree, separation agreement, court order, or equivalent documentation conﬁrming the amount of the obligation must be obtained and retained in the loan ﬁle. For alimony, equalization payments, and separate maintenance obligations, the lender has the option to reduce the qualifying income by the amount of the obligation in lieu of including it as a monthly payment in the calculation of the DTI ratio.

examples: Child support $600/month for 18 more months: must be included in recurring monthly debts for DTI.
Alimony $1,200/month for 24 more months: lender may either include $1,200 as a debt or reduce qualifying income by $1,200 per policy.
Voluntary support payment without legal agreement: does not have to be counted under this section.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan repurchase demand

examples: U.S. citizen borrower purchasing an owner-occupied home
Permanent resident borrower applying for a conforming loan
Borrower with multiple financed properties seeking an additional mortgage

business_rules.rule_name: Virtual currency income qualifies for $0 borrower income
business_rules.description: Any income paid to or earned by the borrower in the form of virtual currency (including cryptocurrencies) is not eligible to be used to qualify for the loan; it must be excluded from qualifying income calculations.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when borrower income source includes virtual currency (crypto) payments/earnings.
business_rules.consequences: Meets: income excluded and loan recalculated; Violated: loan not compliant/eligible.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: B3-3.1-02, Income Paid in Virtual Currency
source_reference.source_text: Income Paid in Virtual Currency Any income paid to or earned by the borrower in the form of virtual currency, such as cryptocurrencies, is not eligible to be used to qualify for the loan. Reduced Income Documentation Requirements for High LTV Reﬁnance Loans For certain high LTV reﬁnance loans, the lender is not required to follow the income documentation requirements described in this Chapter.

examples: Borrower receives salary in cryptocurrency; lender must exclude this income and resubmit DU using only eligible income sources.
Borrower reports freelance income paid in virtual currency; lender cannot use it for qualifying even if tax returns show the income.
Borrower has virtual currency holdings as assets (not income); this rule only prohibits using virtual currency as qualifying income.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan repurchase demand (if used to qualify and not eligible)
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit ineligibility statement; numeric in title expressed as $0 eligible for qualifying.

examples: U.S. citizen borrower purchasing an owner-occupied home
Permanent resident borrower applying for a conforming loan
Borrower with multiple financed properties seeking an additional mortgage

business_rules.rule_name: IRS Form 4506-C valid 120 days and up to 4 years
business_rules.description: IRS Form 4506-C may be used to obtain tax transcripts for up to four years/tax periods and is valid for 120 days after completion (including signature) by the borrower. The lender must have each borrower whose income is used in qualifying complete and sign a separate IRS Form 4506-C at or before closing. If all of a borrower’s income is validated by the DU validation service, the lender is not required to obtain a signed IRS Form 4506-C for that borrower.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when borrower income is used for qualifying and not fully validated by DU validation.
business_rules.consequences: Meets: transcript authorization compliant; Violated: income verification/QC defect.
business_rules.exceptions: No 4506-C needed when all income validated by DU validation service.

source_reference.section_id: B3-3.1-02, Use of IRS Form 4506-C to Verify Borrower Income Documentation
source_reference.source_text: IRS Form 4506-C can be used to obtain tax transcripts for up to four years or tax periods and is valid for 120 days after completion (including signature) by the borrower. The lender must have each borrower whose income is used in qualifying (regardless of income source) complete and sign a separate IRS Form 4506-C at or before closing. An alternative form or process is also acceptable if it authorizes the release of comparable tax information from the IRS. Note: When all of a borrower’s income is validated by the DU validation service, the lender is not required to obtain a signed IRS Form 4506–C for that borrower.

examples: Two W-2 borrowers use paystubs/W-2s (not DU validation); each signs their own 4506-C at closing dated the same day they sign.
Self-employed borrower needs personal and business transcripts; lender obtains separate 4506-Cs per tax form type and within 120-day validity.
Borrower’s income fully validated by DU validation service; lender does not obtain 4506-C for that borrower under the stated exception.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Indemnification or repurchase demand (if income not properly verified)
business_rules.extraction_notes: Contains explicit must language and numeric limits (120 days; up to four years).

examples: U.S. citizen borrower purchasing an owner-occupied home
Permanent resident borrower applying for a conforming loan
Borrower with multiple financed properties seeking an additional mortgage

business_rules.rule_name: Self-employment status triggers at 25% ownership
business_rules.description: A borrower is considered self-employed if they have a 25% or greater ownership interest in a business. If the borrower meets this ownership threshold, self-employment income underwriting and documentation requirements apply to income used for qualifying.
business_rules.conditions: When borrower has ownership interest in any business reported for income
business_rules.consequences: If ≥25% owned, apply self-employment analysis; if <25%, not self-employed
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: B3-3.5-01, Factors to Consider for a Self-Employed Borrower
source_reference.source_text: Any individual who has a 25% or greater ownership interest in a business is considered to be self-employed.
source_reference.source_text_note: Verbatim excerpt is a single sentence; shorter than 30 words due to source brevity.

examples: Borrower owns 30% of an LLC: treat as self-employed and underwrite per B3-3.5 requirements.
Borrower owns 24% of an S-corp and receives W-2 wages only: do not classify as self-employed based on ownership threshold.
Borrower owns 50% of a partnership: self-employment income analysis applies to qualifying income from the business.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for sale to Fannie Mae

examples: U.S. citizen borrower purchasing an owner-occupied home
Permanent resident borrower applying for a conforming loan
Borrower with multiple financed properties seeking an additional mortgage

business_rules.rule_name: Boarder income capped at 30% with 12-month history and proof
business_rules.description: When boarder income is used to qualify a HomeReady borrower on a one-unit property, the boarder income may be counted up to 30% of the total gross income used to qualify, only if the boarder is not obligated on the loan and has no ownership interest, has lived with the borrower for the last 12 months, provides documentation of shared residency, and demonstrates rental payments to the borrower for the last 12 months or at least 9 of the most recent 12 months (averaged over 12 months). Rent paid by the boarder directly to a third party is not acceptable.
business_rules.conditions: HomeReady; 1-unit property; borrower uses boarder income for qualifying
business_rules.consequences: If requirements not met, boarder income must be excluded from qualifying
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited passage

source_reference.source_text: The rental payments that any borrower receives from one or more individuals who reside with the borrower (who may or may not be related to the borrower) may be considered as acceptable stable income. This applies for a one-unit property in an amount up to 30% of the total gross income that is used to qualify the borrower for the mortgage if the boarder is not obligated on the mortgage loan and does not have an ownership interest in the property; has lived with the borrower for the last 12 months; can provide appropriate documentation to demonstrate a history of shared residency (such as a copy of a driver’s license, bill, or bank statement that shows the boarder’s address as being the same as the borrower’s address); and can demonstrate the payment of rental payments (such as with copies of canceled checks) to the borrower for the last 12 months, or at least 9 of the most recent 12 months provided the rental income is averaged over a 12–month period. Payment of rent by the boarder directly to a third party is not acceptable.

examples: Borrower qualifies with $1,000/month boarder rent; total gross income used to qualify is $3,000/month: only $900 (30%) may be used; remaining $100 must be excluded.
Boarder has lived with borrower 10 months: boarder income is not eligible because 12-month shared residency is required.
Boarder paid rent directly to the landlord/HOA instead of the borrower: payments are not acceptable for boarder income.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Underwriting denial or condition to remove income

examples: U.S. citizen borrower purchasing an owner-occupied home
Permanent resident borrower applying for a conforming loan
Borrower with multiple financed properties seeking an additional mortgage

business_rules.rule_name: HomeReady Max 2 Financed Properties and DU Max 10
business_rules.description: Limits apply to the borrower’s maximum number of financed properties: principal residence transactions other than HomeReady loans have no limit; principal residence HomeReady loans (DU and manually underwritten) are limited to 2 financed properties; and second home or investment property transactions underwritten in DU are limited to 10 financed properties. High LTV refinance loans are exempt from multiple financed property policies.
business_rules.conditions: When evaluating loan eligibility based on financed property count and occupancy
business_rules.consequences: If within limits, loan may be eligible; if exceeded, loan is ineligible unless exempt
business_rules.exceptions: High LTV refinance loans are exempt from this policy

source_reference.section_id: B2-2-03, Limits on the Number of Financed Properties
source_reference.source_text:
Limits on the Number of Financed Properties
The following table describes the limits that apply to the number of financed properties a borrower may have.

Financed Properties

HomeReady loansNo limit
Principal residence HomeReady loansDU and manually underwritten -
2

propertyAll DU - 10
Exception: High LTV refinance loans are exempt from the multiple financed property policies.

examples: HomeReady purchase of principal residence: borrower has 1 financed investment property now; with subject property becomes 2 (eligible).
DU underwritten second home: borrower already has 10 financed properties; adding another would be 11 (ineligible).
High LTV refinance: borrower has 12 financed properties; policy is exempt, so financed property count limit does not apply.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase exposure if delivered

examples: U.S. citizen borrower purchasing an owner-occupied home
Permanent resident borrower applying for a conforming loan
Borrower with multiple financed properties seeking an additional mortgage

business_rules.rule_name: Multiple Property Reserves 2%, 4%, or 6% of Aggregate UPB
business_rules.description: When the borrower owns other financed properties, the lender must calculate and document additional reserves for financed properties other than the subject property and the borrower’s principal residence by applying a percentage to the aggregate outstanding UPB of mortgages and HELOCs on those other financed properties: 2% (one to four financed properties), 4% (five to six financed properties), or 6% (seven to ten financed properties; DU only). The aggregate UPB excludes loans/HELOCs on the subject property, the borrower’s principal residence, properties sold or pending sale, and accounts paid by closing (or omitted in DU).
business_rules.conditions: When borrower owns other financed properties and subject is second home/investment
business_rules.consequences: Meets: additional reserve requirement satisfied; Violates: insufficient reserves; underwriting conditions unmet
business_rules.exceptions: 6% tier applies to DU only; exclusions listed must be applied

source_reference.section_id: B3-4.1-01, Calculation of Reserves for Multiple Financed Properties
source_reference.source_text: If the borrower owns other ﬁnanced properties (determined in accordance with B2-2-03, Multiple Financed Properties for the Same Borrower ), additional reserves must be calculated and documented for ﬁnanced properties other than the subject property and the borrower’s principal residence. The other ﬁnanced properties reserves amount must be determined by applying a speciﬁc percentage to the aggregate of the outstanding unpaid principal balance (UPB) for mortgages and HELOCs on these other ﬁnanced properties. The percentages are based on the number of ﬁnanced properties: 2% of the aggregate UPB if the borrower has one to four ﬁnanced properties, 4% of the aggregate UPB if the borrower has ﬁve to six ﬁnanced properties, or 6% of the aggregate UPB if the borrower has seven to ten ﬁnanced properties (DU only). The aggregate UPB calculation does not include the mortgages and HELOCs that are on the subject property, the borrower’s principal residence, properties that are sold or pending sale, and accounts that will be paid by closing (or omitted in DU on the online loan application).

examples: Borrower has 3 financed properties; aggregate UPB on other financed properties (excluding subject and principal) is $230,050: additional reserves = 2% × $230,050 = $4,601.
Borrower has 6 financed properties; eligible aggregate UPB is $345,030: additional reserves = 4% × $345,030 = $13,801.
Borrower has 8 financed properties in DU: eligible aggregate UPB $500,000: additional reserves = 6% × $500,000 = $30,000 (DU-only tier).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery / potential repurchase demand

examples: U.S. citizen borrower purchasing an owner-occupied home
Permanent resident borrower applying for a conforming loan
Borrower with multiple financed properties seeking an additional mortgage

dependencies.rationale: To compute additional reserves correctly, the lender must determine financed property count and ensure the transaction type is within allowed limits. BR_021_004 sets the governing tiers/limits by occupancy/product/underwriting method; BR_023_006 then applies tiered reserve percentages based on that count for second home/investment scenarios.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_021_004 is not applied first, the lender can (a) compute the wrong reserve tier, or (b) compute reserves for a scenario that is ineligible due to exceeding financed property limits—wasting processing time and increasing fallout risk.

dependencies.rationale: BR_023_006 produces a required reserve amount; BR_023_007 governs eligibility of assets counted toward meeting that requirement. Therefore, reserve-source exclusion must be applied to the verified assets before concluding that BR_023_006 is satisfied.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_023_007 is not enforced, the lender may pass reserves using ineligible funds, causing insufficient reserves findings in QC, underwriting defects, and potential repurchase/remedy exposure.

dependencies.rationale: Reserve validation must first check whether an exception applies. If the transaction is a 1-unit principal residence (no minimum reserves) or a high LTV refinance (exempt), reserve requirement checks should be bypassed to avoid false conditions and unnecessary document requests.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_023_008 is not applied, the lender may impose unnecessary reserve conditions, delay closing, and generate incorrect underwriting findings; conversely, misapplying the exception could cause under-reserving on transactions where reserves are required.

business_rules.rule_name: Unacceptable Reserves Include IPCs and Lender Contributions at $0
business_rules.description: Certain funds cannot be counted as borrower reserves, including interested party contributions (IPCs) and any amount of a lender contribution. These funds must be excluded from the verified reserves calculation used to satisfy minimum reserve requirements.
business_rules.conditions: When determining and documenting eligible borrower reserve sources
business_rules.consequences: Meets: reserves calculated using eligible assets only; Violates: reserves overstated; underwriting defect risk
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in this list

source_reference.source_text: The following cannot be counted as part of the borrower’s reserves: funds that have not been vested; funds that cannot be withdrawn under circumstances other than the account owner’s retirement, employment termination, or death; stock held in an unlisted corporation; non-vested stock options and non-vested restricted stock; personal unsecured loans; rent-back credit; interested party contributions (IPCs) (see B3-4.1-02, Interested Party Contributions (IPCs) ); any amount of a lender contribution (see B3-4.3-06, Grants and Lender Contributions ); and cash proceeds from a cash-out reﬁnance transaction on the subject property.

examples: Borrower needs $10,000 reserves; lender shows $8,000 in savings plus $2,500 lender credit—credit must be excluded, so borrower only has $8,000 eligible reserves.
Borrower’s cash-out refinance nets $20,000 cash back; those proceeds cannot be counted as reserves for the same subject property cash-out transaction.
Seller pays $5,000 IPC toward closing; IPC cannot be credited as reserves even if funds remain after closing.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan repurchase demand

examples: U.S. citizen borrower purchasing an owner-occupied home
Permanent resident borrower applying for a conforming loan
Borrower with multiple financed properties seeking an additional mortgage

business_rules.rule_name: No Reserves Required for 1-Unit Primary and High LTV Refi Loans
business_rules.description: There is no minimum reserve requirement for one-unit principal residence transactions. High LTV refinance loans are exempt from the minimum reserve requirements.
business_rules.conditions: When determining minimum required reserves for 1-unit primary or high LTV refi
business_rules.consequences: Meets: reserve minimum not required; Violates: N/A (exception removes minimum reserve test)
business_rules.exceptions: Applies only to stated scenarios; DU may still require additional reserves by risk

source_reference.source_text: Note : There is no minimum reserve requirement for one-unit principal residence transactions. High LTV reﬁnance loans are exempt from the minimum reserve requirements.

examples: One-unit primary residence purchase: lender does not need to document a minimum reserve amount (unless DU conditions it).
High LTV refinance: minimum reserve requirement is waived under this policy; lender applies other underwriting conditions normally.
Two-unit primary residence purchase: this exception does not apply; DU may require 6 months reserves.
business_rules.enforcement_action: N/A

examples: U.S. citizen borrower purchasing an owner-occupied home
Permanent resident borrower applying for a conforming loan
Borrower with multiple financed properties seeking an additional mortgage

business_rules.rule_name: Nontraditional housing payments must be documented for 12 consecutive months
business_rules.description: When underwriting a borrower using nontraditional credit history, the borrower’s housing payment history must be documented for the most recent consecutive 12-month period. Acceptable documentation includes canceled checks or, in lieu of canceled checks, bank statements, copies of money orders, or other reasonable methods that clearly show payee and amount and reflect consistent payment; or direct verification of rent from the landlord (individual or professional management company).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when borrower’s credit history is evaluated using nontraditional credit references
business_rules.consequences: If documented, satisfies housing payment history requirement; if not, nontraditional credit profile is incomplete and loan may be ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: If at least one borrower documents housing payment, others need not; shared lease counts for each

source_reference.source_text: The borrower’s housing payment history must be documented for the most recent consecutive 12-month period. The following documentation is acceptable: Canceled checks can be provided. In lieu of canceled checks, the lender may use the borrower’s bank statements, copies of money orders, or other reasonable methods for documenting timely housing payments. The documentation must clearly indicate the payee and amount being paid, and reﬂect that payments were made on a consistent basis. Direct veriﬁcation of the payment of rent from the landlord.

examples: Borrower provides 12 months of bank statements showing monthly rent ACH debits to the landlord with consistent amounts → requirement met.
Borrower provides only 10 months of canceled checks for rent → requirement not met because it is not the most recent consecutive 12-month period.
Two borrowers are on the lease; only one pays rent, but the lease/rent verification covers the household → counts as one nontraditional credit reference for each borrower.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase demand

examples: U.S. citizen borrower purchasing an owner-occupied home
Permanent resident borrower applying for a conforming loan
Borrower with multiple financed properties seeking an additional mortgage

business_rules.rule_name: Nontraditional creditor references must include fields and 0 x 30, 0 x 60, 0 x 90 format
