business_rules.rule_name: Asset verification report must cover 30 or 60 days minimum
business_rules.description: When a third-party asset verification report is used, the mortgage loan file must retain the report covering the minimum time period required for the purpose of the assessment. For cash flow assessment, the file must contain a minimum of 30 days for limited cash-out refinances and 60 days for purchase transactions. For rent payment history, the file must contain a minimum of 60 days for purchase transactions. When income or employment is validated using an asset verification report and the full report containing all data submitted to DU is not retained, the lender must have access to the full report at origination and retain ongoing access (no shorter than the lender’s full loan file documentation retention period) with the ability to reproduce it.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a third-party asset verification report is used for DU/underwriting purposes.
business_rules.consequences: Meets: file is QC-ready; Violated: documentation defect and QC findings risk.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated; different minimum periods apply by assessment purpose.

source_reference.section_id: A2-4.1-01, Retention of Third-Party Asset Verification Reports
source_reference.source_text: Cash ﬂow assessmentA minimum of • 30 days (limited cash-out reﬁnances); • 60 days (purchase transactions) Rent payment history A minimum of 60 days (purchase transactions) Income or employment validation using an asset veriﬁcation report• The full asset veriﬁcation report containing the entirety of the data submitted to DU, or • The greater of ⚬ the period of time (30- or 60 -days) required for asset veriﬁcation for the transaction, or ⚬ 30 -days if no asset documentation is required for the transaction When income or employment is validated using an asset veriﬁcation report and the full asset veriﬁcation report containing the entirety of the data submitted to DU is not retained in the loan ﬁle, the lender must • have access to the full asset veriﬁcation report at the time of origination; • retain on-going access to the full asset veriﬁcation report.

examples: Purchase loan uses rent history via asset verification; file retains at least 60 days of report data—meets requirement.
Limited cash-out refinance uses cash-flow assessment; file retains at least 30 days—meets requirement.
Lender keeps only a shortened asset report excerpt; to comply, it must retain ongoing access to the full report and be able to reproduce it for QC.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Compensatory fee, indemnification, or repurchase demand (if material defects)
business_rules.extraction_notes: Strong numeric specificity (30/60 days) and explicit retention/access requirements.

examples: Mortgage file retained with appraisal, DU findings, and closing package
Mortgage file requested for examination during Fannie Mae review
Mortgage file missing required exhibit leading to defect findings

dependencies.rationale: BR_010_010 requires retaining third-party asset verification reports for the minimum time period required for the purpose (cash flow, rent history, income/employment validation). BR_017_002 defines when an asset verification report is eligible to be used for DU validation of income/employment. When BR_017_002 is satisfied and the report is used for that purpose, BR_010_010 retention becomes operationally required to evidence DU validation inputs.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_010_010 is not met, the lender may pass underwriting initially but fail post-close QC due to missing third-party report artifacts supporting DU validation reliance.

dependencies.rationale: BR_014_005 requires confirming borrower ownership/name on the asset verification report over the relevant period. BR_010_010 requires retaining the asset verification report that was used. In practice, the workflow is: obtain report → perform ownership/name confirmation → retain report in file. Thus retention is sequentially dependent on (and supports evidencing) the BR_014_005 review.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the report is not retained, the lender cannot evidence the BR_014_005 confirmation in audit/QC, increasing post-close findings risk.

business_rules.rule_name: Form 1084 cash flow analysis must be in permanent loan file
business_rules.description: When qualifying self-employment income under the one-year returns option, the lender must complete Fannie Mae’s Cash Flow Analysis (Form 1084) or another cash flow analysis form applying the same principles, and a copy of the written analysis must be included in the permanent loan file.
business_rules.conditions: When lender documents self-employed income using the one-year returns option
business_rules.consequences: If written cash flow analysis is in file, requirement met; if missing, file is deficient
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: B3-3.5-01, Verification of Income (cash flow analysis requirement)
source_reference.source_text: when there are multiple self- employment income sources. the lender must complete Fannie Mae’s Cash Flow Analysis (Form 1084 ) or any other type of cash ﬂow analysis form that applies the same principles. A copy of the written analysis must be included in the permanent loan ﬁle. Published

examples: Lender uses one year returns based on 5-year business/ownership: file includes completed Form 1084 and meets the requirement.
Lender uses a third-party cash flow worksheet applying the same principles as Form 1084 and retains it in the permanent file: acceptable if equivalent.
Lender qualifies income but fails to retain any written cash flow analysis in the file: documentation defect that can trigger post-purchase findings.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan repurchase demand

examples: Mortgage file retained with appraisal, DU findings, and closing package
Mortgage file requested for examination during Fannie Mae review
Mortgage file missing required exhibit leading to defect findings

dependencies.rationale: BR_012_007 requires a written cash flow analysis (Form 1084 or equivalent) to be included in the file for the one-year returns option. BR_012_008 states that if the lender uses Income Calculator, the Findings Report may be used to satisfy the written analysis requirement (including Form 1084). Thus, using Income Calculator overrides the need to separately produce/retain Form 1084 as long as the Findings Report is retained.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_012_008 is not recognized, lender may create duplicative analyses; if it is misapplied (Findings Report not retained), the file fails the written-analysis requirement and becomes deficient.

business_rules.rule_name: Income Calculator findings report must be kept in loan file
business_rules.description: If the lender uses Income Calculator, the Income Calculator Findings Report may be used to satisfy the requirement to prepare a written analysis of qualifying income calculation (e.g., Form 1084, Form 1088, or similar). A copy of the Findings Report must be maintained in the loan file.
business_rules.conditions: When lender uses Income Calculator to calculate qualifying income for the loan
business_rules.consequences: If report is retained, written-analysis requirement may be satisfied; if not, file deficient
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: The Income Calculator Findings Report can be used to satisfy the requirements for the lender to prepare a written analysis of their evaluation regarding the calculation of qualifying income, such as Fannie Mae's Cash Flow Analysis (Form 1084), Comparative Income Analysis (Form 1088) or a similar form. A copy of the Findings Report must be maintained in the loan ﬁle.

examples: Lender runs Income Calculator and retains the Findings Report in the file: satisfies the written analysis retention requirement.
Lender uses Income Calculator but only retains screenshots, not the Findings Report: fails the “must be maintained” requirement.
Manual underwrite using Income Calculator output: Findings Report retained supports written analysis documentation.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan repurchase demand

examples: Mortgage file retained with appraisal, DU findings, and closing package
Mortgage file requested for examination during Fannie Mae review
Mortgage file missing required exhibit leading to defect findings

business_rules.rule_name: Trust note allonge must identify note with 4 elements and be affixed
business_rules.description: When a lender uses a signature addendum (allonge) for trustee signatures on a mortgage note for an inter vivos revocable trust, (1) all individual borrowers must sign the note itself, (2) the addendum must be permanently affixed to the note, (3) the addendum must clearly identify the note by referencing at least the borrower name, the date of the note, the amount of the note, and the address of the security property, and (4) the note must clearly and accurately reference the attached addendum. The lender must indemnify Fannie Mae for losses resulting from the use of a signature addendum.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a signature addendum/allonge is used for trustee signatures.
business_rules.consequences: If not met: signature may be unenforceable; lender indemnifies Fannie Mae losses.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated; lender must modify forms for non-CA states as needed.

source_reference.section_id: E-2-04 (02/27/2018), Use of a Signature Addendum to Note
source_reference.source_text: In these situations, the lender may use a separate attachment or addendum (sometimes referred to as an allonge) for the borrower signatures, so long as the following requirements are met: All individual borrower(s) must sign the note itself; lenders may use a signature addendum for trustee signatures. The addendum must be permanently aﬃxed to the related note and must clearly identify the note by referencing at least the name of the borrower, the date of the note, the amount of the note, and the address of the security property. The note must clearly and accurately reference the attached signature addendum... The lender must indemnify Fannie Mae...

examples: Borrower signs the note; two trustees sign on an allonge permanently affixed that lists borrower name, note date, note amount, and property address; note references the allonge.
Allonge is stapled loosely and does not include the note amount; this fails the requirement and creates enforceability/indemnification risk.
Lender originates in a non-CA state and updates terminology and signature format to meet local law; retains compliant allonge and note references in file.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Indemnification for losses

examples: Mortgage file retained with appraisal, DU findings, and closing package
Mortgage file requested for examination during Fannie Mae review
Mortgage file missing required exhibit leading to defect findings

business_rules.rule_name: eNote Must Use Uniform Form and 18-Digit MIN at Execution
business_rules.description: To be eligible for sale to Fannie Mae, eMortgages must be originated using the Uniform Fannie Mae/Freddie Mac form of eNote (modified per the eNote header/footer/clause requirements, including the electronic-note provision referencing the MERS eRegistry). Immediately upon execution by the borrower, all eNotes must be registered on the MERS eRegistry and contain a valid, unique 18-digit Mortgage Identification Number (MIN). Fannie Mae requires creation of eNotes in the MISMO SMART Doc® format.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the loan is an eMortgage intended for sale to Fannie Mae.
business_rules.consequences: If not Uniform eNote/18-digit MIN/SMART Doc, loan is ineligible for sale.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.source_text: To be eligible for sale to Fannie Mae, eMortgages must be originated using the Uniform Fannie Mae/Freddie Mac form of eNote... Such modification includes addition of a specific provision (Section 11, or Section 12 in the case of ARM eNotes) that addresses the electronic nature of the note. This special provision also includes reference to the MERS eRegistry. Immediately upon execution by the borrower, all eNotes must be registered on the MERS eRegistry and contain a valid, unique 18-digit Mortgage Identification Number (MIN). Fannie Mae requires creation of eNotes in the MISMO SMART Doc® format.

examples: Borrower signs an eNote in SMART Doc format; MIN is 18 digits and registration occurs immediately → meets rule.
Lender uses a proprietary eNote template instead of Uniform eNote → ineligible for sale to Fannie Mae.
eNote is Uniform but MIN is missing or not 18 digits → ineligible until corrected/re-executed as required.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for sale to Fannie Mae.

examples: Mortgage file retained with appraisal, DU findings, and closing package
Mortgage file requested for examination during Fannie Mae review
Mortgage file missing required exhibit leading to defect findings

business_rules.rule_name: eMortgage Records Retained for Life of Loan Plus 7 Years
business_rules.description: For eMortgages, the lender must retain documents and system records supporting eSignature attribution and identity for the life of the loan plus seven years. Retained files must include, at minimum: the name of the person (and related entity if not a natural person) who signed each document, the signature date, the signing method, and relevant identifying information to verify the electronic signature and its attribution. Lenders must provide Fannie Mae requested audit trail files in a format compatible with Fannie Mae systems, and upon servicing transfer must provide the new servicer with all attribution evidence and audit trails for transferred loans.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to eMortgages sold or to be sold to Fannie Mae.
business_rules.consequences: If retention/audit trail not available, evidentiary support is deficient and noncompliant.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.source_text: To preserve the lender's ability to attribute the signer's signature to their identity... the lender must retain evidence of a borrower's identity in the loan file... the lender must collect and retain systems logs and audit trails... Additionally, the lender must collect evidence of a borrower's agreement to conduct the transaction electronically... All files must be retained for the life of the loan plus seven years. Any files that a lender maintains must include the following: the name of the person... the date of the signature, the method by which the document was signed, and any relevant identifying... information... Lenders must provide Fannie Mae with any audit trail files requested... In the event of a servicing transfer, the lender is required to provide the new servicer with all attribution evidence and audit trails

examples: Loan paid off after 6 years; lender retains eClosing audit logs until 13 years after payoff (life + 7) → compliant.
Fannie Mae requests audit trail showing ID presentation and eSignature event; lender cannot produce logs → noncompliant retention.
Servicing transfers to new servicer; lender provides complete attribution evidence package with audit trails → compliant.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for sale; potential repurchase/indemnification exposure.

examples: Mortgage file retained with appraisal, DU findings, and closing package
Mortgage file requested for examination during Fannie Mae review
Mortgage file missing required exhibit leading to defect findings

business_rules.rule_name: Remote ink-signed notarization needs 2-ID or 2-factor and 10-year recording
business_rules.description: For audio-visual aided remote ink-signed notarization (RIN), the lender must ensure the audio-visual method meets minimum standards including identity verification via either (a) review of two forms of government-issued photo ID with signature via two-way audio-video and capture of the IDs, or (b) two-factor identity authentication using at least two of: remote presentation of a government-issued photo ID with signature, credential analysis, or identity-proofing (knowledge-based authentication). The system must be able to record the audio-video portion and retain that recording for the minimum period required by applicable law, or if none is specified, for 10 years; the lender must retain for the greater of 10 years or the required legal period. Loans closed using RIN must be delivered with SFC 920.
business_rules.conditions: When closing and delivering a loan that used audio-visual aided remote ink-signed notarization
business_rules.consequences: If standards/retention/SFC missing, loan may be ineligible or documentation-defective
business_rules.exceptions: Retention period follows applicable law if longer than 10 years

source_reference.section_id: A2-4.1-04 (RIN requirements; Minimum Standards; Additional Requirements)
source_reference.source_text: All loans delivered to Fannie Mae that have been closed using audio-visual aided ink-signed notarization must be delivered with special feature code (SFC) 920 in addition to any other special feature codes that may apply. ... It must include a review of two forms of government-issued photo ID that have a signature via two-way audio-video technology and capture of such photo ID[s] via a photocopy or other electronic image; or two-factor identity authentication... Recording capability It must have the ability to record... with storage for the minimum period required by applicable law, or if no period is speciﬁed... for 10 years. ... The lender must maintain... the recording... for the greater of 10 years or the minimum period required by applicable law.

examples: RIN closing in a state requiring 5-year retention: lender retains recording 10 years and delivers with SFC 920; compliant.
RIN closing where lender reviewed only one government ID and did not capture an image: noncompliant identity standard.
State law requires 12-year retention: lender retains recording 12 years (greater than 10) and includes SFC 920; compliant.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery rejection / repurchase for legal enforceability defect

Mortgage file missing required exhibit leading to defect findings

business_rules.rule_name: MERS MIN must be registered before delivery and on note
business_rules.description: For all mortgages delivered to Fannie Mae (when applicable), the original note must be endorsed “in blank” and without recourse with no break in the chain of endorsements. For MERS registered loans, the MIN must be added to the upper right-hand corner of the note and must be part of the electronic data delivery, and the MIN must be registered prior to delivery.
business_rules.conditions: Loans delivered to Fannie Mae requiring custodial documents; MERS loans
business_rules.consequences: If note endorsement chain/MIN requirements fail, delivery may be suspended/cured
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: Note The original note endorsed “in blank” and without recourse and there is no break in the chain of endorsements. For MERS registered loans, the MIN should be added to the upper right-hand corner of the note and must be part of the electronic data delivery. The MIN must be registered prior to delivery.

examples: MERS loan delivered with MIN printed on the note, MIN registered, and MIN included in electronic delivery—meets requirement.
MERS loan MIN is on the note but not registered prior to delivery—must cure (register) before delivery acceptance.
Non-MERS loan has a missing endorsement creating a break in chain—custodian rejects package until corrected.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit custodial document requirement; includes a clear pre-delivery registration condition for MIN.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Delivery suspense; potential repurchase demand if not cured

examples: Mortgage file retained with appraisal, DU findings, and closing package
Mortgage file requested for examination during Fannie Mae review
Mortgage file missing required exhibit leading to defect findings

dependencies.rationale: BR_030_008 requires the original note be endorsed “in blank” and without recourse with no break in the chain of endorsements. BR_039_007 defines the required ownership/payee/endorsement chain mechanics (original payee, endorsements to subsequent owners, seller endorsement in blank). In practice, BR_039_007 supplies the detailed chain logic needed to satisfy BR_030_008’s ‘no break in chain’ expectation.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_039_007 is not satisfied (e.g., originating lender not original payee, missing intermediate endorsements), BR_030_008 cannot be met in substance even if the final endorsement appears “in blank.” Delivery may be suspended pending cure; enforceability risk increases; custodian may reject collateral.

dependencies.rationale: BR_030_008 requires an unbroken endorsement chain and final delivery-ready endorsement status. When endorsements are executed using an allonge, the allonge becomes part of the legal endorsement instrument. A noncompliant allonge breaks enforceability and therefore breaks the endorsement-chain integrity required by BR_030_008.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_039_008 fails, BR_030_008 fails (delivery suspended/cure required). Potential outcomes include custodian rejection, delayed purchase, or repurchase/indemnification risk if undetected.

business_rules.rule_name: B8-5-05 POA Allowed for Purchase and Limited Cash-Out
business_rules.description: Loans with documents executed by an agent under a power of attorney (POA) are eligible for delivery only for the eligible transactions listed (Purchase and Limited cash-out refinance) and only if the lender meets the POA documentation requirements: obtain a copy of the POA, ensure names on the POA match names on the relevant loan document, ensure the POA date shows it was valid when the loan document was executed, ensure the POA is notarized, and ensure the POA references the subject property address.
business_rules.conditions: When note and/or security instrument is signed by an agent under POA
business_rules.consequences: If met: POA-signed docs acceptable; if violated: document defect may block delivery
business_rules.exceptions: None in this excerpt (other guide provisions may apply)

source_reference.section_id: B8-5-05 (10/05/2022), Eligibility Requirements for Using a POA
source_reference.source_text: Underwriting and Documentation Requirements Eligible Transactions• Purchase • Limited cash-out reﬁnance Documentation RequirementsAn agent under a POA may sign the note and/or security instrument on behalf of a borrower (as deﬁned in the Glossary) if all of the following requirements are met: • The lender obtains a copy of the POA. • The name(s) on the POA match the name(s) of the person on the relevant loan document. • The POA is dated such that it was valid at the time the relevant loan document was executed. • The POA is notarized. • The POA must reference the address of the subject property.

examples: Purchase loan: borrower is overseas; attorney-in-fact signs note; file contains notarized POA referencing the subject property address and matching borrower name.
Limited cash-out refinance: POA copy in file but POA does not reference subject property address; lender must cure (new POA) or loan becomes ineligible.
Cash-out refinance: even with a compliant POA, transaction type is not listed as eligible here; lender must not rely on POA under this section.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Delivery/custodian certification failure; potential repurchase demand

business_rules.rule_name: FEMA disaster area underwriting docs must be dated within 180 days of note date
business_rules.description: When the loan is secured by a property located in a FEMA-Declared Disaster Area eligible for Individual Assistance, the underwriting documentation (including credit reports and verifications of income and assets) must be dated no more than 180 days before the note date.
business_rules.conditions: Property is in FEMA-Declared Disaster Area eligible for Individual Assistance
business_rules.consequences: If docs ≤180 days, documentation is acceptable; if >180 days, updated docs required
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt

source_reference.source_text: When a loan is secured by a property located in a FEMA-Declared Disaster Area eligible for individual assistance, Fannie Mae provides additional ﬂexibilities. The following requirements apply: The underwriting documentation, including credit reports and veriﬁcations of income and assets, must be dated no more than 180 days before the note date.

examples: Credit report dated 200 days prior to note date for a FEMA IA ZIP; lender must refresh the credit report.
VOE and bank statements dated 150 days prior to note date in FEMA IA ZIP; documentation meets the 180-day requirement.
Income verification dated 181 days before note date; lender must obtain an updated verification dated within 180 days.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Delivery defect; potential repurchase demand if delivered

business_rules.rule_name: Note chain of endorsements must end with seller blank endorsement without recourse
business_rules.description: The originating lender must be the original payee on the note. The note must be endorsed to each subsequent owner of the mortgage unless one or more owners endorsed the note in blank. The last endorsement on the note should be that of the mortgage seller, and the mortgage seller must endorse the note in blank and without recourse.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to the promissory note included in the closing/delivery file for Fannie Mae loans
business_rules.consequences: If endorsement chain is defective: enforceability/delivery defect and remedy exposure
business_rules.exceptions: Subsequent endorsements not required when note was endorsed in blank earlier

source_reference.source_text: The originating lender must be the original payee on the note, even when MERS is named as nominee for the beneﬁciary in the security instrument. The note must be endorsed to each subsequent owner of the mortgage unless one or more of the owners endorsed the note in blank. The last endorsement on the note should be that of the mortgage seller. The mortgage seller must endorse the note in blank and without recourse.

examples: Originator is payee, then endorsed to aggregator, then seller endorses in blank without recourse: acceptable chain for delivery.
Note endorsed only to aggregator and not further to seller/blank: endorsement chain defect requiring correction before/after delivery.
If an intermediate owner endorsed in blank, later endorsements are not required (but seller still should be last endorsement).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery rejection or repurchase demand

business_rules.rule_name: Allonge must be permanently affixed and identify borrower, date, amount, and property address
business_rules.description: If an allonge is used for endorsement, the endorsement must appear on the note and the allonge must comply with law, be permanently affixed to the note, clearly identify the note by referencing at least borrower name(s), note date, note amount, and security property address, and the note must clearly reference the attached allonge. The lender must indemnify Fannie Mae for losses incurred as a result of using an allonge for note endorsement(s).
business_rules.conditions: Allonge used to endorse note delivered to Fannie Mae; enforceability must be preserved
business_rules.consequences: If allonge not compliant/affixed/referenced: endorsement defect; indemnification for losses applies
business_rules.exceptions: Subsequent endorsements may be placed on allonge but are not required

source_reference.section_id: B8-3-04, Using an Allonge for the Endorsement
source_reference.source_text: An allonge may be used for the endorsement as long as the following requirements are met: The allonge must be permanently aﬃxed to the related note and must clearly identify the note by referencing at least the name of the borrower(s), the date of the note, the amount of the note, and the address of the security property. The note must clearly reference the attached allonge... The lender must indemnify Fannie Mae... for any losses incurred by Fannie Mae as a result of the use of an allonge for the note endorsement(s).

examples: Allonge is stapled/glued to the note and references borrower names, note date, note amount, and property address: acceptable.
Allonge is loose in the file and note does not reference it: fails permanent affix/reference requirements and creates enforceability risk.
Allonge used and later causes loss due to improper endorsement: lender must indemnify Fannie Mae for losses tied to allonge use.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Indemnification

examples: Mortgage file retained with appraisal, DU findings, and closing package
Mortgage file requested for examination during Fannie Mae review
Mortgage file missing required exhibit leading to defect findings

dependencies.rationale: BR_039_008 is an allonge-specific rule that only matters when the note endorsement(s) required by BR_039_007 are placed on an allonge. Therefore BR_039_007’s endorsement requirement is the trigger/context that makes BR_039_008 applicable.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_039_007 endorsements are implemented through an allonge but BR_039_008 requirements are not met (not affixed, not referenced, incomplete identification), the endorsement chain is defective and the note may be rejected by custodian/Fannie Mae; indemnification exposure may apply.

business_rules.rule_name: Square Footage Must Follow ANSI Z765-2021 for Hybrid Appraisals
business_rules.description: Appraisers must follow the ANSI Z765-2021 standard when measuring, calculating, and reporting above- and below-grade square footage for attached and detached single-family dwellings, including manufactured homes. This applies to all hybrid appraisals and appraisals requiring interior and exterior inspections. If state law or another regulatory requirement mandates a different standard, the appraisal report must note the required standard and explain how it was applied.
business_rules.conditions: When reporting above-/below-grade square footage for eligible SFR appraisals
business_rules.consequences: Meets: measurement compliant; Violates: appraisal defect; may require revision/replacement and impact deliverability
business_rules.exceptions: If state/reg mandates different standard, it must be noted and explained

source_reference.source_text: Appraisers must follow the American National Standard Institute (ANSI) Square Footage-Method for Calculating : ANSI® Z765-2021 ("ANSI standard") when measuring, calculating and reporting the above- and below-grade square footage(s). This includes ﬁnished and unﬁnished area(s) of the subject for attached and detached single- family dwellings, including manufactured homes. This is applicable to all hybrid appraisals and appraisals requiring interior and exterior inspections. If state law or a regulatory requirement mandates adherence to a diﬀerent measurement standard, the appraisal report must note the standard required and explain how it was applied.

examples: Hybrid appraisal on a manufactured home: appraiser reports GLA using ANSI Z765-2021; lender accepts as compliant.
Appraiser uses a non-ANSI local method without noting a state requirement; lender must require correction/revision to meet ANSI requirement.
State law mandates a different measurement standard; appraiser states the mandated standard in the report and explains application—acceptable under the exception.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Appraisal revision/replacement; potential repurchase demand if not cured

examples: State-certified residential appraiser engaged for a purchase transaction
Appraiser meeting lender selection criteria and independence requirements
Appraiser rejected due to unacceptable appraisal practice indicators

business_rules.rule_name: CLT appraisal must use 3-step leasehold valuation method
business_rules.description: For a community land trust (CLT) ground lease, the appraiser must develop the value opinion using a required three-step process: (1) determine fee simple value using Sales Comparison; (2) determine a market-derived capitalization rate and convert annual ground rent into leased fee value; (3) determine leasehold value by reducing fee simple value by leased fee value. When using this technique, the appraiser does not need to document actual land value of the security property.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when subject property is under CLT ground lease/leasehold estate
business_rules.consequences: If followed, valuation method is Guide-compliant; if not, appraisal is deficient
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: The appraiser must use a three-step process to develop an opinion of value. Step The appraiser must determine 1 the fee simple value of the property by using the sales comparison analysis approach to value, 2the applicable capitalization rate and convert the income from the ground lease into a leased fee value by using the market-derived capitalization rate, and 3the leasehold value by reducing the fee simple value by the lease fee value.

examples: CLT purchase: appraiser first estimates fee simple value at $250,000 using fee simple comps, then capitalizes ground rent to leased fee value, then subtracts to reach leasehold value.
CLT loan file where appraiser only reports contract price without fee simple analysis: fails required 3-step process and is a material appraisal defect.
CLT appraisal where appraiser documents land value but skips leased fee calculation: noncompliant because step 2/3 are required for leasehold valuation.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan repurchase demand

examples: Uniform Residential Appraisal Report (URAR) with UAD-compliant fields
Appraisal report reused within permitted age and use requirements
Appraisal report flagged for unacceptable practices or missing exhibits

dependencies.rationale: BR_014_001 mandates determining fee simple value using Sales Comparison. BR_014_002 specifies which comparables may/may not be used for that fee simple value (must use similar fee simple properties; CLT leasehold sales must not be used as comps). Therefore, compliant execution of BR_014_001’s Step 1 requires BR_014_002 compliance.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_014_002 is violated, the fee simple value conclusion in BR_014_001 is noncompliant, contaminating the entire 3-step CLT valuation. Appraisal is deficient and may require revision/replacement; delivery may be blocked.

dependencies.rationale: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_ELIGIBILITY_011_001 establishes that leasehold collateral can be eligible only under specific conditions (market acceptance and eligible property types). When the leasehold is specifically a CLT ground lease, the appraisal must be developed using the CLT three-step process in BR_APPRAISAL_REPORT_PROCESS_014_001. In practice, the eligibility determination and underwriting acceptance for leasehold/CLT is highly dependent on having a Guide-compliant value opinion that reflects the correct property interest (leasehold vs fee simple).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If leasehold/CLT status is identified but the CLT appraisal process is not followed: (1) appraisal is deficient, (2) underwriter may be unable to establish acceptable collateral value/interest, (3) delivery to Fannie Mae may be suspended pending appraisal correction, (4) higher repurchase/indemnification risk if delivered with a defective appraisal.

business_rules.rule_name: CLT appraisal must not use CLT leasehold sales as comps
business_rules.description: When determining fee simple value for a CLT subject property, the appraiser must use comparable sales of similar fee simple properties. If fee simple comps are not available, the appraiser may use other (non-CLT) leasehold estate sales with appropriate lease-term adjustments to reflect fee simple interest. If the community has sales of CLT leasehold estates, the appraiser must discuss them but must not use them as comparables because CLT restrictions likely limit sales prices and are not comparable to the hypothetical condition required for valuation.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to CLT appraisals when selecting comps for fee simple value estimate
business_rules.consequences: Using CLT-restricted sales as comps creates a noncompliant appraisal and defect
business_rules.exceptions: None stated; non-CLT leasehold sales allowed with adjustments if needed

source_reference.source_text: In determining the fee simple value of the subject property, the appraiser must use comparable sales of similar properties that are owned as fee simple estates. If this is not possible, the appraiser may use sales of properties that are subject to other types of leasehold estates as long as they make appropriate adjustments, based on the terms of their leases, to reﬂect a fee simple interest. When the community or neighborhood has sales activity for other leasehold estates held by a community land trust, the appraiser must discuss them in the appraisal report, but must not use them as comparable sales

examples: Neighborhood has many CLT resales at restricted prices: appraiser lists them in narrative but selects fee simple sales as comps for fee simple value.
No fee simple sales exist but several non-CLT leasehold sales exist: appraiser uses non-CLT leasehold sales and adjusts for lease terms to reflect fee simple interest.
Appraiser uses CLT restricted resale as Comp 1 and 2: prohibited and triggers appraisal revision/QC defect.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan repurchase demand

examples: Uniform Residential Appraisal Report (URAR) with UAD-compliant fields
Appraisal report reused within permitted age and use requirements
Appraisal report flagged for unacceptable practices or missing exhibits

business_rules.rule_name: CLT capitalization rate equals rent divided by price difference
business_rules.description: When market activity includes both fee simple and non-CLT leasehold sales, the capitalization rate must be extracted from the market by dividing the annual ground rent for leasehold properties by the difference between (a) sales prices of fee simple comparable sales and (b) sales prices of leasehold comparable sales.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when CLT appraisal can extract cap rate from fee simple vs leasehold sales
business_rules.consequences: If computed per formula, cap rate is market-derived; wrong method is defect
business_rules.exceptions: If no non-CLT leasehold sales exist, use alternative low-risk rates method

source_reference.source_text: To extract the capitalization rate, the appraiser must divide the annual ground rent for the properties subject to leasehold estates by the diﬀerence in the sales prices for the comparable sales of properties owned as fee simple estates and the comparable sales of properties subject to leasehold estates.

examples: Annual ground rent is $1,200; fee simple sale is $300,000; similar leasehold sale is $285,000; cap rate = 1,200 / (300,000−285,000) = 8.00%.
Appraiser uses a cap rate taken from a generic survey despite available fee simple vs leasehold pairs: noncompliant with required direct extraction method.
No non-CLT leasehold sales exist: appraiser documents alternative low-risk rate comparison instead (allowed exception path).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan repurchase demand

examples: Uniform Residential Appraisal Report (URAR) with UAD-compliant fields
Appraisal report reused within permitted age and use requirements
Appraisal report flagged for unacceptable practices or missing exhibits

dependencies.rationale: BR_014_001 establishes the CLT three-step technique and requires fee simple value determination using Sales Comparison (Step 1). BR_014_003 requires fee simple comparable sales prices and leasehold comparable sales prices to extract a cap rate. Those market inputs and their selection occur within the framework established by BR_014_001.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Without completing BR_014_001’s required CLT method (and its sales comparison development), the cap rate extraction in BR_014_003 cannot be supported; appraisal becomes deficient and may not be acceptable for delivery.

business_rules.rule_name: CLT leasehold value equals fee simple minus leased fee value
business_rules.description: To determine leasehold value in a CLT appraisal, the appraiser must (1) convert annual ground lease income into leased fee value by dividing that income by the market-derived capitalization rate, and then (2) reduce the estimated fee simple value by the leased fee value to arrive at leasehold value.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when CLT appraisal is developing leasehold value using 3-step method
business_rules.consequences: Correct computation supports indicated value; incorrect math is appraisal defect
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: To determine the leasehold value of the subject property, the appraiser must ﬁrst convert the annual income from the community land trust’s ground lease into a leased fee value by dividing the income by the market-derived capitalization rate. The appraiser must then reduce the estimated fee simple value of the subject property by this leased fee value to arrive at their opinion of the leasehold value of the subject property.

examples: Annual rent $300 and cap rate 5.75%: leased fee value = 300/0.0575 = $5,217; fee simple $100,000; leasehold value ≈ $94,800 (as in Guide example).
Appraiser subtracts annual rent ($300) directly from fee simple value: incorrect, because leased fee value must be derived via capitalization.
Appraiser divides fee simple value by cap rate to compute leased fee value: incorrect input; numerator must be annual ground rent income.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan repurchase demand

examples: Uniform Residential Appraisal Report (URAR) with UAD-compliant fields
Appraisal report reused within permitted age and use requirements
Appraisal report flagged for unacceptable practices or missing exhibits

dependencies.rationale: BR_014_004 Step (1) requires dividing annual ground lease income by the market-derived capitalization rate. BR_014_003 defines how that capitalization rate must be extracted in the applicable market condition. Therefore BR_014_003 must be satisfied before BR_014_004 can be executed correctly.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_014_003 is not satisfied (wrong cap rate method or no cap rate), BR_014_004 leased fee and leasehold values are incorrect; appraisal is defective and could lead to ineligible delivery or repurchase risk due to value misstatement.

dependencies.rationale: BR_014_004 requires reducing the estimated fee simple value by the leased fee value to arrive at leasehold value. The fee simple value is produced under BR_014_001 Step 1 (Sales Comparison fee simple value). Without that fee simple value, BR_014_004 cannot be completed.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_014_001 Step 1 is missing/defective, leasehold value cannot be reliably determined; appraisal is deficient and may require revision, delaying underwriting and delivery.

business_rules.rule_name: Environmental hazard not measurable by comps means loan is not eligible for delivery
business_rules.description: If an environmental hazard has a significant effect but the effect is not measurable because the hazard is so serious or so recently discovered that the appraiser cannot arrive at a reliable opinion of market value due to lack of comparable market data (sales, contract sales, or active listings), the mortgage is not eligible for delivery to Fannie Mae.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an environmental hazard exists and no comparable data supports a reliable value opinion.
business_rules.consequences: If measurable through comparable data, loan may remain eligible with appraisal reflecting impacts; if not measurable, loan is ineligible for delivery.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.source_text: In rare situations, a particular environmental hazard may have a signiﬁcant eﬀect on the value of the subject property, although the actual eﬀect is not measurable because the hazard is so serious or so recently discovered that an appraiser cannot arrive at a reliable opinion of market value because there is no comparable market data available, such as sales, contract sales, or active listings that are available to reﬂect the eﬀect of the hazard. In such cases, the mortgage will not be eligible for delivery to Fannie Mae.

examples: Newly discovered toxic spill adjacent to property with no affected-area sales/listings: appraiser cannot support value; loan is ineligible for delivery.
Known radon issue with ample comparable sales showing no buyer resistance: appraiser analyzes comps; loan may be eligible if impacts addressed.
Serious hazard discovered weeks before appraisal with no market evidence yet: value impact not measurable; delivery ineligible.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Ineligible for delivery to Fannie Mae

examples: Uniform Residential Appraisal Report (URAR) with UAD-compliant fields
Appraisal report reused within permitted age and use requirements
Appraisal report flagged for unacceptable practices or missing exhibits

business_rules.rule_name: UAD Appendix D Required for Forms 1004 and 1073
business_rules.description: For conventional loans, Fannie Mae requires specified appraisal report forms to be completed using Appendix D of the Uniform Appraisal Dataset (UAD) Specification when reporting appraisal results. The required forms are: Form 1004, Form 1004 Desktop, Form 1004 Hybrid, Form 1073, and Form 1073 Hybrid.
business_rules.conditions: When reporting appraisal results for a conventional loan using listed forms
business_rules.consequences: If met: appraisal is UAD-compliant; if violated: UCDP/loan delivery defects likely
business_rules.exceptions: Other forms may use UAD to the extent applicable; not mandated for all forms

source_reference.source_text: Fannie Mae requires that the following appraisal report forms are completed utilizing Appendix D of the UAD Speciﬁcation when reporting the results of an appraisal for a conventional loan: Uniform Residential Appraisal Report ( Form 1004 ), Uniform Residential Appraisal Report (Desktop) (Form 1004 Desktop ) Uniform Residential Appraisal Report (Hybrid) (Form 1004 Hybrid ) Individual Condominium Unit Appraisal Report ( Form 1073 ), and Individual Condominium Unit Appraisal Report (Hybrid) (Form 1073 Hybrid ).

examples: Conventional purchase uses Form 1004; lender/appraiser completes UAD Appendix D fields as required.
Conventional condo appraisal uses Form 1073 Hybrid; report must follow UAD Appendix D for applicable fields.
Lender delivers a Form 1004 Desktop without UAD Appendix D formatting; UCDP edits/flags increase delivery risk.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery rejection or post-purchase remedy exposure

examples: Uniform Residential Appraisal Report (URAR) with UAD-compliant fields
Appraisal report reused within permitted age and use requirements
Appraisal report flagged for unacceptable practices or missing exhibits

business_rules.rule_name: Solar Panels Get No Value Unless UCC Search Confirms No Collateral Claim
business_rules.description: If documentation is insufficient and solar panel ownership status is unclear, the lender must ensure no value is attributed to the solar panels in the appraisal unless the lender obtains a UCC personal property search confirming the panels are not claimed as collateral by a non-mortgage lender.
business_rules.conditions: Solar ownership unclear due to insufficient documentation
business_rules.consequences: If UCC search not obtained, appraisal must not include solar value; violation risks value misstatement and delivery defects
business_rules.exceptions: If UCC search confirms no collateral claim, value may be considered

source_reference.source_text: If insuﬃcient documentation is available and the ownership status of the panels is unclear, no value for the panels may be attributed to the property value on the appraisal unless the lender obtains a Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) “personal property” search that conﬁrms the solar panels are not claimed as collateral by any non-mortgage lender.

examples: Borrower cannot provide solar financing/ownership documents; lender orders UCC personal property search showing no lien → appraiser may consider contributory value under standard requirements.
Borrower cannot document ownership and lender does not obtain UCC search → appraisal must show $0 contributory value for the solar array.
UCC search reveals a filed UCC-1 claiming the panels as collateral → appraiser must not include solar value and lender must underwrite debt accordingly.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan repurchase demand

examples: Uniform Residential Appraisal Report (URAR) with UAD-compliant fields
Appraisal report reused within permitted age and use requirements
Appraisal report flagged for unacceptable practices or missing exhibits

business_rules.rule_name: Special assessment district with no measurable market data is ineligible
business_rules.description: If the financial difficulty of a special assessment district is so severe that its effect on value and marketability is not measurable due to no comparable market data, the mortgage secured by a property in that district is not eligible for delivery to Fannie Mae until an active market develops that enables the appraiser to demonstrate value and marketability.
business_rules.conditions: Subject property in special assessment district; appraiser lacks comparable market data
business_rules.consequences: If active market data exists, appraisal may support delivery; if not, loan ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: Eligibility resumes when an active market develops enabling reliable valuation

source_reference.section_id: B4-1.4-09 (06/04/2025) - Appraisal Requirements for Special Assessment Districts
source_reference.source_text: There may be some instances in which the ﬁnancial diﬃculty of a special assessment district is so severe that its actual eﬀect on the value and marketability of a property is not measurable because there is no comparable market data available to enable the appraiser to arrive at a reliable opinion of market value. When this is the case, a mortgage secured by a property in that district will not be eligible for delivery to Fannie Mae until such time that an active market develops that will enable the appraiser to demonstrate the value and marketability of the subject property.

examples: Appraiser documents special assessment district distress and states no comparable sales/listings exist to measure impact; loan cannot be delivered to Fannie Mae.
Comparable closed sales within the district exist showing price impact; appraisal can quantify effect and loan may be eligible.
After market recovers and sufficient sales occur, a new appraisal can demonstrate value/marketability and restore eligibility.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for sale to Fannie Mae

examples: Uniform Residential Appraisal Report (URAR) with UAD-compliant fields
Appraisal report reused within permitted age and use requirements
Appraisal report flagged for unacceptable practices or missing exhibits

business_rules.rule_name: Non MH Advantage Manufactured Homes Require LLPA Pricing
business_rules.description: A loan-level price adjustment (LLPA) applies to all mortgages secured by manufactured homes that do not meet MH Advantage requirements, regardless of whether the loan is delivered for whole loan purchase or MBS issuance. The manufactured housing LLPA is in addition to any other price adjustments applicable to the transaction.
business_rules.conditions: Mortgage is secured by a manufactured home and is not MH Advantage eligible
business_rules.consequences: If met, apply manufactured housing LLPA in addition to other LLPAs; if missed, pricing defect risk
business_rules.exceptions: MH Advantage manufactured homes are excluded from this specific LLPA requirement

source_reference.source_text: An LLPA applies to all mortgages secured by manufactured homes that do not meet the MH Advantage requirements, whether delivered to Fannie Mae for whole loan purchase or MBS issuance. These LLPAs are in addition to any other price adjustments that are otherwise applicable to the particular transaction.

examples: Multi-width manufactured home not MH Advantage: lender applies the manufactured housing LLPA plus any standard credit score LLPA.
Single-width manufactured home not MH Advantage delivered into an MBS pool: manufactured housing LLPA still applies.
MH Advantage loan delivered with evidence of MH Advantage eligibility: manufactured housing non-MH Advantage LLPA does not apply.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Pricing adjustment / compensatory fee

examples: Doublewide manufactured home on a permanent foundation
Manufactured home with HUD label verification
Manufactured housing loan requiring specific eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: BR_015_007 applies an LLPA when the manufactured home does not meet MH Advantage requirements. BR_015_008 requires delivering SFCs that distinguish standard manufactured vs MH Advantage types. While the LLPA decision can be made without SFCs internally, delivery and audit alignment typically depend on consistent classification signals (SFCs) supporting the LLPA choice.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If SFCs are wrong/missing, pricing and delivery data may not reconcile (e.g., LLPA applied/not applied inconsistently with reported MH Advantage status), leading to purchase conditions, pricing defects, or post-purchase adjustments.

business_rules.rule_name: Manufactured Home Delivery Requires Special Feature Codes 235 791 859
business_rules.description: For manufactured home deliveries, the lender must deliver specific Special Feature Code(s) based on manufactured home type and width: Single-width Manufactured—codes 235 and 791; Multi-width—code 235; MH Advantage MultiWide—codes 235 and 859; MH Advantage SingleWide—codes 235, 791, and 859. These codes are in addition to any other special feature codes that apply.
business_rules.conditions: Loan secured by a manufactured home is being delivered and requires SFC identification
business_rules.consequences: If codes are delivered correctly, loan data is complete; if missing/incorrect, delivery defects may result
business_rules.exceptions: Codes listed are additive; other applicable SFCs may also be required

source_reference.source_text: Manufactured Home Type... Special Feature Code(s) Single-width Manufactured SingleWide 235 and 791 Multi-width MultiWide 235 MH Advantage MultiWide 235 and 859 MH Advantage SingleWide 235, 791, and 859 Note: These special feature codes are in addition to any other special feature codes that may apply.

examples: Single-wide manufactured home (not MH Advantage): lender delivers SFC 235 and 791 in delivery data.
MH Advantage multi-wide: lender delivers 235 and 859; omitting 859 would be a delivery data defect.
MH Advantage single-wide: lender delivers 235, 791, and 859 plus any additional SFCs applicable to the transaction.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan purchase delay / compensatory fee

examples: Doublewide manufactured home on a permanent foundation
Manufactured home with HUD label verification
Manufactured housing loan requiring specific eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: BR_015_008 requires delivering specific Special Feature Codes depending on whether the home is single-width or multi-width (and MH Advantage variants). BR_019_009 sets a minimum width requirement (≥12 feet) and is part of collateral eligibility evaluation. Determining and documenting width/type classification is needed to accurately assign SFCs.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_019_009 assessment (and underlying width determination) is wrong or missing, the lender may deliver incorrect SFCs, creating delivery data defects and potentially mispricing/misclassification.

dependencies.rationale: BR_015_008 applies to "deliveries" of manufactured home loans. BR_019_008 states that without HUD Data Plate/Certification Label evidence (or alternatives), the loan is not eligible for sale to Fannie Mae. Practically, eligibility confirmation (including HUD evidence) precedes or gates delivery data completion tasks like SFC population.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_019_008 fails, resources spent perfecting SFC delivery data are wasted because the loan cannot be delivered; if discovered late, it can cause pipeline fallout and investor delivery failure.

business_rules.rule_name: Manufactured home must have HUD plate or labels or loan is ineligible for sale
business_rules.description: For manufactured homes, compliance with Federal Manufactured Home Construction and Safety Standards (June 15, 1976, as amended and in force at time of manufacture) and HUD regulations at 24 C.F.R. Part 3280 must be evidenced by HUD Data Plate or HUD Certification Label(s). Lenders must obtain photos of the original HUD Data Plate or HUD Certification Label(s), or obtain specified alternative documentation (e.g., IBTS label verification letter or duplicate HUD Data Plate) for existing construction. If neither the Data Plate nor Certification Labels can be obtained, the loan is not eligible for sale to Fannie Mae. Both are required for new construction.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to loans secured by manufactured homes (existing or new construction).
business_rules.consequences: If HUD evidence cannot be obtained, loan is not eligible for sale to Fannie Mae.
business_rules.exceptions: Alternative documentation allowed for existing homes (e.g., IBTS verification).

source_reference.section_id: B2-3-02 (02/04/2026), Manufactured Home Property Eligibility Requirements
source_reference.source_text: Compliance with these standards will be evidenced by the presence of either a HUD Data Plate or the HUD Certiﬁcation Label(s) for each section of the home for existing construction. Lenders must obtain photos of the original HUD Data Plate or HUD Certiﬁcation Label(s) or provide the alternative documentation described below. If neither of these can be obtained, the loan is not eligible for sale to Fannie Mae. (Both are required for new construction).

examples: Existing doublewide: appraisal includes photo of HUD Certification Labels on each section; eligibility evidence satisfied.
Existing manufactured home: labels removed and Data Plate missing; lender cannot obtain IBTS verification; loan is ineligible for sale to Fannie Mae.
New construction manufactured home: lender must obtain both HUD Data Plate and Certification Labels documentation; missing either fails eligibility.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for sale to Fannie Mae

examples: Doublewide manufactured home on a permanent foundation
Manufactured home with HUD label verification
Manufactured housing loan requiring specific eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: BR_007_003 mandates manufactured home loans be underwritten through DU. BR_019_008 mandates HUD Data Plate/Certification Label evidence (or acceptable alternatives) and states lack of evidence makes the loan ineligible for sale. They are independent but jointly required for a manufactured home loan to be eligible for Fannie Mae delivery.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If either rule fails, the manufactured home loan is ineligible for delivery: (a) not DU-underwritten (BR_007_003 failure) or (b) no HUD compliance evidence (BR_019_008 failure). This blocks sale and creates significant remediation burden.

business_rules.rule_name: Manufactured home minimum size is 12 feet wide and 400 square feet
business_rules.description: A manufactured home securing a Fannie Mae-eligible loan must be at least 12 feet wide and have a minimum of 400 square feet of above-grade finished area.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to manufactured home collateral eligibility evaluation.
business_rules.consequences: If width <12 feet or area <400 sq ft, property fails eligibility requirements.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in excerpt (MH Advantage noted but not changing these minimums here).

source_reference.section_id: B2-3-02 (02/04/2026), Manufactured Home Property Eligibility Requirements
source_reference.source_text: The manufactured home must be at least 12 feet wide and have a minimum of 400 square feet of above-grade ﬁnished area.

examples: Singlewide is 14 feet wide with 780 sq ft; meets minimums.
Unit is 11 feet wide (regardless of sq ft); fails the 12-foot minimum width requirement.
Unit is 12 feet wide but only 380 sq ft above-grade finished area; fails the 400 sq ft minimum.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for sale to Fannie Mae

examples: Doublewide manufactured home on a permanent foundation
Manufactured home with HUD label verification
Manufactured housing loan requiring specific eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: BR_019_009 requires a manufactured home to have "a minimum of 400 square feet of above-grade finished area." BR_023_009 requires ANSI Z765-2021 when measuring/calculating/reporting above- and below-grade square footage (including manufactured homes). Therefore, correct evaluation of BR_019_009 depends on applying BR_023_009 measurement methodology to produce reliable area figures.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If ANSI measurement is not followed, the reported GLA/area may be unreliable, leading to incorrect eligibility determinations (false pass/fail). This can cause delivery ineligibility, repurchase exposure, or require appraisal revision/re-measurement.

business_rules.rule_name: Manufactured Home Chattel Lien Plus Land Real Property Lien Is Unacceptable
business_rules.description: Loans secured by a chattel lien on the manufactured home plus a separate real property lien on the land are unacceptable for Fannie Mae purchase. Acceptable structures described in the Guide require real property lien treatment for the home, not chattel financing combined with land lien.
business_rules.conditions: Manufactured home transaction uses chattel lien on the home and a real property lien on the land
business_rules.consequences: Loan is unacceptable/ineligible for purchase; lender must restructure liens to meet requirements
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: Note : Loans in which there is a chattel lien on the home plus a real property lien on the land are unacceptable.

examples: Borrower finances the manufactured home with a vehicle-style chattel lien and separately records a mortgage on the land → unacceptable for Fannie Mae purchase.
Lender converts/manages titling so the manufactured home is treated as real property and secures both home and land under real property lien → avoids chattel-plus-land structure.
Seller proposes chattel financing to speed closing while land is mortgaged traditionally → lender must not deliver this loan to Fannie Mae.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligibility for purchase / delivery rejection

examples: Doublewide manufactured home on a permanent foundation
Manufactured home with HUD label verification
Manufactured housing loan requiring specific eligibility checks

dependencies.rationale: BR_033_010 prohibits chattel lien on the home plus separate real property lien on the land. BR_036_005 requires lien searches in real and personal property records, including "cases with separate liens." This is direct validation evidence used to confirm whether the prohibited structure exists.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_036_005 is not performed, the lender may miss a prohibited lien structure and deliver an unacceptable loan, creating immediate ineligibility and repurchase exposure.

business_rules.rule_name: Manufactured home land equity uses 12-month lesser-of rule
business_rules.description: When borrower-owned land is credited toward the borrower’s minimum down payment/equity for a manufactured home that will be permanently attached, the land value that may be credited depends on the acquisition timing: (i) if the land was purchased more than 12 months before the loan application date, use the current appraised value; (ii) if the land was purchased 12 or fewer months before the application date, use the lesser of the sales price or the current appraised value and document the borrower’s cash investment in the land. If documentation/value requirements are not met, land equity may not be credited toward the minimum contribution/equity requirement.
business_rules.conditions: Loan secured by manufactured home; borrower’s land equity credited to down payment
business_rules.consequences: If values/docs meet rule, land equity may be credited; if not, credit is disallowed
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in table; other funds policies may apply to own-funds requirement

source_reference.section_id: B5-2-03 (10/08/2025) - Land value based on acquisition timing
source_reference.source_text: More than 12 months preceding the loan application.The current appraised value. None. 12 or fewer months preceding the date of the loan application.The lesser of the sales price or the current appraised value.The lender must document the borrower’s cash investment by obtaining: • a copy of the settlement statement, • a copy of the warranty deed that shows there are no outstanding liens against the property, or • a copy of the release of any prior liens(s). The borrower acquired the land at any time as a gift, inheritance, or other non-purchase transaction.The current appraised value.The lender must obtain appropriate documentation to verify the acquisition and transfer of ownership of the land.

More than 12 months preceding the loan application.The current appraised value. None. 12 or fewer months preceding the date of the loan application.The lesser of the sales price or the current appraised value.The lender must document the borrower’s cash investment by obtaining: • a copy of the settlement statement, • a copy of the warranty deed that shows there are no outstanding liens against the property, or • a copy of the release of any prior liens(s). The borrower acquired the land at any time as a gift, inheritance, or other non-purchase transaction.The current appraised value.The lender must obtain appropriate documentation to verify the acquisition and transfer of ownership of the land.

examples: Land bought 18 months before application; lender may credit current appraised land value toward down payment without the table’s additional lien documentation.
Land bought 6 months before application for $80,000 and appraises at $95,000; lender must use $80,000 and retain settlement statement plus deed showing no liens (or lien release).
Land inherited last year; lender may use current appraised value but must document inheritance/transfer into borrower ownership.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase demand if delivered

examples: Doublewide manufactured home on a permanent foundation
Manufactured home with HUD label verification
Manufactured housing loan requiring specific eligibility checks

deduplication_info.rationale: Both rules implement the identical manufactured home land-equity credit logic for borrower minimum down payment/equity: if land purchased >12 months prior to application, use current appraised value; if purchased ≤12 months, use lesser of sales price or current appraised value and document cash investment. Threshold (12 months) and context (manufactured home permanently attached; borrower-owned land counted) align; consequences align (land equity can/cannot be credited). Only wording differs.
deduplication_info.primary_selection_reason: BR_MANUFACTURED_HOME_VALIDATION_036_003 selected as primary because it is more clearly scoped to manufactured home guidance tables and more explicitly labels the rule as a manufactured home-specific validation requirement (improves graph placement under manufactured home domain).

business_rules.rule_name: Trade equity capped at 90% of NADA retail value with 12-month lookback
business_rules.description: Trade equity from the borrower’s existing manufactured home may be used toward the minimum down payment, but the maximum equity contribution is 90% of the traded home’s retail value per the NADA Manufactured Housing Appraisal Guide. If the borrower has owned the traded manufactured home for less than 12 months preceding the loan application date, the maximum equity contribution is the lesser of (i) 90% of NADA retail value or (ii) the lowest price at which the home was sold during that 12-month period. If the calculated trade equity exceeds the cap, the excess must be excluded and the transaction must be restructured/adjusted accordingly.
business_rules.conditions: Manufactured home transaction uses trade equity from borrower’s existing manufactured home
business_rules.consequences: If within cap, equity may count; if over cap, excess must be excluded from funds
business_rules.exceptions: Owned <12 months triggers lesser-of 90% NADA or lowest 12-month sales price

source_reference.section_id: B5-2-03 (10/08/2025) - Trade equity from existing manufactured home
source_reference.source_text: Trade equity from the borrower’s existing manufactured home may be used as part of the borrower’s minimum down payment requirement. The maximum equity contribution from the traded manufactured home is 90% of the retail value for the traded manufactured home based on the NADA Manufactured Housing Appraisal Guide except: If the borrower has owned the traded manufactured home for less than 12 months preceding the date of the loan application, the maximum equity contribution is the lesser of 90% of the retail value or the lowest price at which the home was sold during that 12 month period. Any costs associated with the removal of the traded home or any outstanding indebtedness secured by liens on the home must be deducted from the maximum equity contribution.

source_reference.section_id: B5-2-03 (10/08/2025) - Trade Equity from Existing Manufactured Home
source_reference.source_text: Trade equity from the borrower’s existing manufactured home may be used as part of the borrower’s minimum down payment requirement. The maximum equity contribution from the traded manufactured home is 90% of the retail value for the traded manufactured home based on the NADA Manufactured Housing Appraisal Guide except: If the borrower has owned the traded manufactured home for less than 12 months preceding the date of the loan application, the maximum equity contribution is the lesser of 90% of the retail value or the lowest price at which the home was sold during that 12 month period. Any costs associated with the removal of the traded home or any outstanding indebtedness secured by liens on the home must be deducted from the maximum equity contribution.

examples: NADA retail value is $50,000; borrower owned it 3 years; max equity allowed is $45,000 less removal costs and any lien payoff.
Borrower owned traded home 8 months; NADA retail is $60,000 (90%=$54,000) but lowest sale price in last 12 months was $48,000; max equity is $48,000 minus liens/removal.
Removal cost $5,000 and lien payoff $10,000 on a $45,000 max equity; allowable equity contribution becomes $30,000.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase demand if delivered

examples: Doublewide manufactured home on a permanent foundation
Manufactured home with HUD label verification
Manufactured housing loan requiring specific eligibility checks

deduplication_info.rationale: Both rules state the same cap on trade equity from an existing manufactured home used toward minimum down payment: maximum equity contribution is 90% of NADA retail value; if owned <12 months, the maximum is the lesser of 90% of retail value or the lowest sales price during that 12-month period. Numeric thresholds (90%, 12 months) and context align; consequences align (cap equity; adjust funds to close). Wording differences only.
deduplication_info.primary_selection_reason: BR_MANUFACTURED_HOME_CONSTRAINT_036_004 selected as primary because it is slightly more complete/cleanly scoped as a manufactured home constraint and is better positioned as the canonical manufactured-home trade-equity cap rule for the knowledge graph.

dependencies.rationale: BR_036_004 allows applying trade equity toward down payment subject to caps. BR_036_005 mandates lien searches and title/lien satisfaction proof for traded manufactured homes. Without verifying liens/ownership, the lender cannot determine whether trade equity is valid/available or whether proceeds are encumbered.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_036_005 fails, the trade equity cannot be safely credited; down payment sources become unsupported, potentially making the loan ineligible or requiring restructuring with additional borrower funds.

business_rules.rule_name: Traded manufactured home requires lien search and proof of lien satisfaction
business_rules.description: For traded manufactured homes, a lien search must be completed in the appropriate real property and personal property records to verify ownership and determine whether existing liens are present on the manufactured home and land (including cases with separate liens). The seller of the new manufactured home must provide proof of title transfer and satisfaction of any existing liens on the traded manufactured home.
business_rules.conditions: Manufactured home transaction includes trade-in (traded manufactured home)
business_rules.consequences: If lien search/proof obtained, trade equity may proceed; if not, trade cannot be relied on
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: For traded manufactured homes, Fannie Mae requires a lien search in the appropriate real property and personal property records to verify ownership and to determine whether there are any existing liens on the manufactured home and land, or on the home and the land if they are encumbered by separate liens. The seller of the new manufactured home must provide proof of title transfer and satisfaction of any existing liens on the traded manufactured home.

examples: Borrower trades in a home titled as personal property; lender must obtain UCC/personal property lien search results showing lien status.
Borrower trades in home and separately financed land; lender must verify liens on both the home and the land where separate liens exist.
Dealer cannot provide proof that prior lien was satisfied; lender must not treat the trade as clear equity for down payment.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase demand if delivered

examples: Doublewide manufactured home on a permanent foundation
Manufactured home with HUD label verification
Manufactured housing loan requiring specific eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: 401(k) or IRA qualifies only with unrestricted access to all funds
business_rules.description: A borrower may be considered to have unrestricted access to a 401(k) or IRA/SEP/Keogh retirement account only if, at the time of calculation, the borrower has the unqualified and unlimited right to request a distribution of all funds in the account, regardless of any possible tax withholding or applicable penalty.
business_rules.conditions: Retirement account funds are used to calculate employment-related asset income
business_rules.consequences: If access is unrestricted, account may be used; if restricted, exclude income
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: The borrower must only be considered to have unrestricted access to a 401(k) or IRA, SEP, Keogh retirement account if the borrower has, as of the time of calculation, the unqualiﬁed and unlimited right to request a distribution of all funds in the account (regardless of any possible tax withholding or applicable penalty applied to such distribution).

examples: Borrower can request full IRA distribution at any time (penalty applies) → eligible for asset-income calculation (penalty is handled in net assets).
Employer plan restricts distributions until termination of employment → not unrestricted; lender must not use the 401(k) for employment-related asset income.
Account allows only limited hardship withdrawals → not unlimited; exclude the account from the income stream calculation.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Underwriting finding; potential repurchase demand

examples: 401(k) with vested balance used to meet reserve requirements
IRA funds withdrawn for down payment with documented terms
Retirement account with outstanding loan affecting available funds

business_rules.rule_name: HomeStyle Refresh requires completion escrow and 180-day completion
business_rules.description: For HomeStyle Refresh loans with incomplete improvements, the lender must establish a completion escrow for the improvement funds, and the improvements must be completed no later than 180 days from the date of the mortgage note.
business_rules.conditions: HomeStyle Refresh loan has incomplete improvements at closing/delivery
business_rules.consequences: If escrow set and work completes by 180 days, loan stays compliant; else defect
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in excerpt

source_reference.source_text: The lender must establish a completion escrow for incomplete improvements. The improvements must be completed no later than 180 days from the date of the mortgage note.

examples: Loan closes on 2026-04-01 with $12,000 in incomplete improvements → completion escrow required; all work must be finished by 2026-09-28.
Lender fails to set up completion escrow at closing → noncompliance; subject to QC defect and remedies.
Escrow is set, but improvements remain incomplete after 180 days → violates requirement and may trigger repurchase exposure.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Underwriting/QC defect; potential repurchase demand

examples: Escrow account established for property taxes and homeowners insurance
Escrow waiver granted where permitted and documented
Escrow analysis identifying shortage and updated monthly payment

business_rules.rule_name: QC data discrepancies require DU resubmission or manual risk assessment
business_rules.description: When post-closing QC finds inconsistencies between DU (or manual underwriting) data and source documents, the lender must (1) determine if differences are within Selling Guide/DU tolerances, (2) resubmit the loan to DU with corrected data when outside DU tolerances, and (3) if DU resubmission is not possible, manually perform a comprehensive risk assessment using DU-required documentation (unless the loan is a DU-only product). If a loan exceeds DU tolerances and is not resubmitted to DU, the DU limited waiver of representations and warranties is invalidated.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when QC identifies data/source-document inconsistencies post-closing.
business_rules.consequences: If outside tolerances: must resubmit/assess; if not, DU waiver invalidated.
business_rules.exceptions: DU-only products are not eligible for manual comprehensive risk assessment.
