source_reference.source_text: When there are inconsistencies between the data and/or information submitted to DU (or used for manual underwriting) and the source documents, the lender must complete the steps in the following table. Step The lender must.... 1 determine whether discrepancies are within the tolerances permitted by the Selling Guide... 2 resubmit the loan to DU with the correct data, if discrepancies are outside the DU allowed tolerances... The DU limited waiver of representations and warranties is invalidated when loans that exceed DU tolerances are not resubmitted to DU... Note: DU-only products are not eligible for a manual comprehensive risk assessment.

examples: QC finds employment start date entered in DU is incorrect and outside DU tolerances; lender corrects and resubmits to DU, then re-determines eligibility.
QC finds asset balance mismatch outside tolerances; lender cannot resubmit DU due to closed casefile, so performs comprehensive manual risk assessment using DU-required docs (if not DU-only).
QC finds DTI error outside tolerances and lender does not resubmit to DU; DU limited waiver of representations and warranties is invalidated.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loss of DU limited waiver; potential repurchase/other remedies

examples: Lender QC program performing pre-funding and post-closing audits
QC program with documented defect categories and corrective action tracking
QC program updated to reflect new appraisal or DU policy changes

dependencies.rationale: BR_019_003 is scoped to "DU (or manual underwriting)" and directs DU resubmission when outside DU tolerances. Therefore, when the underwriting method is DU, BR_006_006 must have occurred to create a DU casefile and baseline data against which QC compares.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_006_006 was not satisfied for a DU-intended loan, QC cannot perform DU tolerance comparisons or resubmissions properly, and any DU waiver/tolerance reliance may be invalidated, increasing defect and remedy risk.

business_rules.rule_name: Ineligible-as-delivered loans must be self-reported in Loan Quality Connect
business_rules.description: If, after QC data verification and underwriting reassessment, the lender determines a loan was not eligible as delivered to Fannie Mae, the lender must advise Fannie Mae of the findings using the self-report functionality in Loan Quality Connect.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when QC reassessment determines the loan was not eligible as delivered.
business_rules.consequences: Must self-report via Loan Quality Connect; failure may worsen remedies/exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.source_text: 4 document the underwriting ﬁle to reﬂect its decision when the lender determines the loan remains eligible as delivered. 5 advise Fannie Mae of these ﬁndings using the self-report functionality in Loan Quality Connect when the lender determines the loan was not eligible as delivered.

examples: QC discovers undisclosed debt that changes eligibility; lender determines not eligible as delivered and self-reports via Loan Quality Connect.
QC identifies income documentation does not support DU inputs; after reassessment loan fails Selling Guide requirements, so lender self-reports.
QC finds appraisal documentation missing and cannot be cured; lender self-reports the delivery ineligibility through Loan Quality Connect.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Potential repurchase demand or other remedies following self-report

examples: Lender QC program performing pre-funding and post-closing audits
QC program with documented defect categories and corrective action tracking
QC program updated to reflect new appraisal or DU policy changes

dependencies.rationale: BR_019_004 begins "If, after QC data verification and underwriting reassessment..." which is the workflow defined in BR_019_003 (tolerance check, DU resubmission, or manual risk assessment). Therefore BR_019_003 is the prerequisite process that produces the determination that triggers BR_019_004.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_019_003 is not performed correctly, the lender may fail to identify ineligible delivered loans, leading to unreported defects, increased remedy severity, and heightened repurchase/indemnification exposure. If ineligibility is identified but BR_019_004 is not executed, reporting noncompliance may worsen outcomes.

business_rules.rule_name: Gift of equity must contribute $0 toward financial reserves
business_rules.description: A gift of equity may be used to fund all or part of the down payment and closing costs (including prepaid items), but it cannot be used towards financial reserves. This limits the gift of equity to funds-to-close purposes only, not post-closing reserve requirements.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a purchase transaction includes a seller-provided gift of equity credit
business_rules.consequences: If applied to reserves, reserves are unsupported and loan may be ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: A gift of equity is permitted for principal residence and second home purchase transactions; can be used to fund all or part of the down payment and closing costs (including prepaid items); and cannot be used towards ﬁnancial reserves.

examples: Purchase of a second home requires 6 months reserves; borrower cannot satisfy reserves using a $15,000 gift of equity credit.
Principal residence purchase: gift of equity covers down payment and prepaid taxes; borrower must still document separate assets for required reserves.
If underwriter credits gift of equity as reserves in DU, the loan must be corrected and reserves re-sourced before delivery.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan repurchase demand

examples: Seller-parent provides equity gift via below-market sales price
Gift of equity used to satisfy down payment requirement
Equity gift documented in purchase contract and appraisal

dependencies.rationale: BR_022_005 restricts how a gift of equity may be used (not for reserves). BR_022_006 requires retaining a signed gift letter and settlement statement listing the gift. Those documents are the primary evidence to identify the gift-of-equity credit and ensure it was applied only to down payment/closing costs, not reserves.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_022_006 fails, the lender may be unable to prove compliance with BR_022_005 during post-purchase review (even if the transaction was structured correctly), resulting in file defects and remedy exposure.

business_rules.rule_name: Gift of equity requires 2 documents: gift letter and settlement statement
business_rules.description: The lender must retain in the loan file (1) a signed gift letter and (2) the settlement statement listing the gift of equity. Both documents must be retained to evidence the gift and its application as a credit in the transaction.
business_rules.conditions: Applies whenever a gift of equity is used in the mortgage transaction
business_rules.consequences: Missing documents create file defects and may trigger repurchase/indemnification
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: Documentation Requirements The following documents must be retained in the loan ﬁle: a signed gift letter (see B3-4.3-04, Personal Gifts ), and the settlement statement listing the gift of equity.

examples: Loan file contains signed gift letter but no settlement statement showing the equity credit; lender must obtain and retain the settlement statement to cure.
Settlement statement shows seller credit labeled as gift of equity, but no signed gift letter is retained; lender must obtain a signed gift letter to cure.
Both documents are imaged and indexed to the Fannie Mae loan number in the mortgage file; documentation requirement satisfied.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan repurchase demand

examples: Seller-parent provides equity gift via below-market sales price
Gift of equity used to satisfy down payment requirement
Equity gift documented in purchase contract and appraisal

business_rules.rule_name: Grant donors must be eligible entities; exclude seller or interested party
business_rules.description: Grant funds must be funded by specified acceptable entities and the grant provider cannot be the property seller or other interested party in the transaction. Acceptable entities include governmental bodies, a housing finance agency (24 C.F.R. §266.5), a 501(c)(3) nonprofit, a regional Federal Home Loan Bank program, an employer (for an employee borrower), an Indian tribe (25 U.S.C. §5131 list), or a lender (subject to lender-funded grant rules).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when funds to close/reserves include donated funds treated as a grant
business_rules.consequences: Ineligible donor source makes grant funds unacceptable and may invalidate approval
business_rules.exceptions: Lender-funded grants permitted only under separate lender-funded grant criteria

source_reference.source_text: Grants must be funded by one of the following entities, provided they are not the property seller or other interested party in the transaction: a federal agency, state, county, or similar political subdivision of a state; any city, town, village, or borough of a state that has a local government ...; a housing ﬁnance agency as deﬁned in 24 C.F.R. §266.5; a nonproﬁt organization exempt from taxation under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code; a regional Federal Home Loan Bank under one of its aﬀordable housing programs; an employer where the borrower is an employee

examples: A 501(c)(3) nonprofit provides a $10,000 grant; donor is eligible if not the property seller or otherwise an interested party.
The property seller offers a 'grant' through an affiliated entity; it is ineligible because the provider is an interested party.
An employer provides a $3,000 housing grant to its employee borrower; donor is eligible under the employer category.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan repurchase demand

examples: State housing finance agency grant applied to closing costs
Nonprofit down payment assistance grant with recorded terms
Grant structured as a forgivable subordinate lien

dependencies.rationale: BR_022_007 includes "a lender (subject to lender-funded grant rules)." BR_022_009 defines those lender-funded grant rules (HomeReady purchase-only, contribution requirements, documented program). Thus, when the grant source is the lender, BR_022_009 governs and effectively overrides/conditions the general eligibility statement in BR_022_007.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_022_009 is not satisfied (e.g., not HomeReady purchase), the lender-funded 'grant' becomes ineligible even though 'lender' is listed generally in BR_022_007; this can invalidate funds-to-close/reserves and require restructuring.

business_rules.rule_name: Grants require 5% borrower funds for 2–4 unit when LTV over 80%
business_rules.description: For transactions that contain grants (excluding lender-funded grants), if the property is a two- to four-unit principal residence and the LTV/CLTV/HCLTV ratio is greater than 80%, the borrower must make a 5% minimum borrower contribution from their own funds. After the minimum contribution is met, grants can supplement down payment, closing costs, reserves, and energy-related improvements.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to principal residence 2–4 units with grant funds and LTV/CLTV/HCLTV > 80%
business_rules.consequences: If borrower funds < 5%, loan is not compliant and may be ineligible for delivery
business_rules.exceptions: Does not apply to lender-funded grants; other LTV/property cases may have no minimum

source_reference.source_text: Greater than 80% Two- to four-unit principal residence The borrower must make a 5% minimum borrower contribution from their own funds. After the minimum borrower contribution has been met, grants can be used to supplement the down payment, closing costs, reserves, and energy-related improvements.

examples: Two-unit principal residence purchase at 90% LTV with a $20,000 grant: borrower must contribute at least 5% of the value/price from own funds before applying the grant.
Three-unit principal residence at 75% LTV with a grant: no minimum borrower contribution required under the table for ≤80%.
Four-unit principal residence at 85% CLTV: borrower contributes only 3% own funds; loan must be restructured to meet 5% minimum or the grant cannot be used as structured.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan repurchase demand

examples: State housing finance agency grant applied to closing costs
Nonprofit down payment assistance grant with recorded terms
Grant structured as a forgivable subordinate lien

dependencies.rationale: BR_022_008 applies "For transactions that contain grants" and imposes borrower minimum contribution requirements at higher LTVs for 2–4 unit principal residences. BR_022_007 defines whether funds can be treated as grant funds at all (eligible donor; not seller/interested party). Therefore grant eligibility must be established before applying BR_022_008’s contribution logic.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_022_007 fails (e.g., seller-funded 'grant'), BR_022_008 calculations become irrelevant because the funds are not eligible. The loan may become ineligible unless restructured with acceptable funds and re-underwritten.

dependencies.rationale: BR_022_008 requires a 5% minimum borrower contribution in certain grant scenarios (2–4 unit PR, >80% LTV). BR_034_004 states disaster relief grants/loans may satisfy minimum borrower contribution and that "no borrower contribution from the borrower’s own funds is required" when using this assistance. This is a direct exception overriding the base constraint.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_034_004 is not applied when it should be, the lender may incorrectly require borrower funds, potentially denying eligible borrowers or delaying closing. If applied incorrectly, the lender may under-collect required contributions, risking ineligibility.

business_rules.rule_name: Lender-funded grants: HomeReady purchase with 3% or 5% borrower contribution
business_rules.description: A lender-funded grant is permitted only if the loan is a HomeReady loan used for a purchase transaction. Minimum borrower contribution requirements apply: (i) one-unit principal residences require 3% contribution from borrower’s own funds/other eligible sources/Community Seconds; (ii) two- to four-unit principal residences with LTV/CLTV/HCLTV ≤ 80% require 3%; and (iii) two- to four-unit principal residences with LTV/CLTV/HCLTV > 80% require 5%. Additionally, the lender must have a documented program providing grants for low- to moderate-income borrowers, community development, equitable housing initiatives, or similar initiatives.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the grant source is the lender (lender-funded grant) for a HomeReady purchase
business_rules.consequences: If not HomeReady purchase or contribution shortfall, lender-funded grant is ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: If only closing cost assistance, Lender Contributions policy may be applied instead

source_reference.source_text: A lender-funded grant is subject to the requirements described in this topic, but must also meet the requirements in the following table. The loan must be a HomeReady loan used for a purchase transaction. ... One-unit principal residences: The borrower(s) must make a 3% contribution ... Two- to four-unit principal residences with LTV, CLTV, or HCLTV ratios 80% or less: The borrower(s) must make a 3% contribution ... Two- to four-unit principal residences with LTV, CLTV, or HCLTV ratios greater than 80%: The borrower(s) must make a 5% minimum contribution from their own funds. The lender must have a documented program

examples: HomeReady 1-unit purchase: lender-funded grant used; borrower contributes 3% from own funds/eligible sources; rule satisfied.
Two-unit HomeReady purchase at 85% LTV: borrower must contribute 5% own funds; if only 3% is documented, the lender-funded grant cannot be used as structured.
Conventional (non-HomeReady) purchase: lender-funded grant is not eligible under this section; lender must remove it or restructure as allowable lender contribution if applicable.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan repurchase demand

examples: State housing finance agency grant applied to closing costs
Nonprofit down payment assistance grant with recorded terms
Grant structured as a forgivable subordinate lien

dependencies.rationale: BR_022_009 specifies minimum borrower contribution requirements for lender-funded grants. BR_034_004 states disaster relief lump sums may satisfy minimum contribution and that no borrower own-funds contribution is required when using this assistance. Therefore, in scenarios where both could apply, BR_034_004 overrides the borrower-funds requirement aspect of BR_022_009.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If not applied, lender may wrongly impose borrower contribution requirements, causing delays/denials. If misapplied, lender may fail to meet HomeReady/grant contribution rules when disaster relief does not qualify, risking ineligibility.

business_rules.rule_name: Hybrid appraisal report must enter required assignment fields including YYYY-MM-DD
business_rules.description: For a hybrid appraisal, the appraiser must provide required entries in the appraisal report’s Additional Comments section: Appraisal Assignment Type must be entered as "Hybrid"; Subject Property Data Collection Method must be "Physical"; Subject Property Data Collection Date must be entered in "YYYY-MM-DD" format (the date data collection was performed); and Subject Property Data Workforce must be one of: "Appraiser", "AppraiserTrainee", "RealEstateAgent", "HomeInspector", "InsInspector", or "Other".
business_rules.conditions: Applies when appraisal is completed as a hybrid appraisal on Form 1004 Hybrid or Form 1073 Hybrid
business_rules.consequences: If completed, appraisal meets required reporting fields; if missing/invalid, appraisal may be noncompliant and require correction or resubmission
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: B4-1.2-03, Entry of Appraisal Assignment Type
source_reference.source_text: Entry of Appraisal Assignment Type: The appraiser must provide the Appraisal Assignment Type and supporting details (located in the Additional Comments section of the appraisal report) as shown in the following table. Field Labels Required Entry Appraisal Assignment Type "Hybrid" Subject Property Data Collection Method "Physical" Subject Property Data Collection Date "YYYY-MM-DD" Subject Property Data Workforce "Appraiser" "AppraiserTrainee" "RealEstateAgent" "HomeInspector" "InsInspector" (*Insurance Inspector) "Other"

examples: Hybrid appraisal includes: Assignment Type “Hybrid”, Method “Physical”, Date “2026-01-15”, Workforce “HomeInspector” → compliant.
Hybrid appraisal enters data collection date as “01/15/2026” instead of “YYYY-MM-DD” → noncompliant format; must be corrected.
Hybrid appraisal uses Workforce value “Inspector” (not in allowed list) → invalid; must be replaced with an allowed enumeration (e.g., “HomeInspector”).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery delay; potential repurchase demand

examples: Hybrid appraisal with separate property data collector and appraiser analysis
Hybrid appraisal delivered with required data collection documentation
Hybrid appraisal declined due to insufficient data collection scope

dependencies.rationale: BR_031_004 requires specific appraisal forms (including Form 1004 Hybrid and Form 1073 Hybrid) be completed using Appendix D of the UAD Specification. BR_027_008 applies specifically to hybrid appraisals on those forms and requires specific field entries. In practice, the report must first be in the correct UAD form/spec context for the hybrid-required entries to be valid and accepted by downstream systems (e.g., UCDP).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_031_004 is not met (wrong form/spec), BR_027_008 entries may be rejected by UCDP or be nonstandard, causing appraisal submission failures, delivery defects, and rework delays.

dependencies.rationale: BR_023_009 applies to "all hybrid appraisals" and mandates ANSI Z765-2021 measurement for above-/below-grade reporting. BR_027_008 requires specific hybrid appraisal report fields. They don’t strictly require each other, but together they form the compliance completeness of a hybrid appraisal deliverable (content + measurement methodology).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_023_009 fails, the hybrid appraisal may be defective even if all BR_027_008 fields are populated. This can trigger appraisal revision/replacement, delaying underwriting/closing and risking delivery defects.

business_rules.rule_name: Desktop Appraisal Allowed Only for 1-Unit Purchase at 90% LTV with DU Approve
business_rules.description: A desktop appraisal (Form 1004 Desktop) is permitted only when the transaction is a one-unit property (including ADU and PUD units), principal residence, purchase (including new construction), the LTV ratio is less than or equal to 90%, and the DU loan casefile receives an Approve/Eligible recommendation.
business_rules.conditions: Lender elects Form 1004 Desktop option offered by DU
business_rules.consequences: If criteria met, desktop appraisal is eligible; if not met, desktop appraisal not permitted and another appraisal type is required
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: To be eligible for a desktop appraisal, transactions must meet the following criteria: one-unit property (including those with an ADU and units in a PUD), principal residence, purchase transaction (including new construction), LTV ratio less than or equal to 90%, and DU loan caseﬁle that receives an Approve/Eligible recommendation.

examples: Borrower purchases a one-unit primary residence at 90% LTV and DU returns Approve/Eligible → Form 1004 Desktop is permitted.
Borrower purchases a one-unit primary residence at 92% LTV with DU Approve/Eligible → desktop appraisal is not permitted due to LTV > 90%.
Borrower purchases a PUD unit as a principal residence at 80% LTV but DU returns Ineligible → desktop appraisal is not permitted.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Require different appraisal type; delivery defect if not corrected

examples: Desktop appraisal used for a low-risk refinance where permitted
Desktop appraisal completed with required data sources and exhibits
Desktop appraisal rejected because transaction/property type is ineligible

dependencies.rationale: BR_033_005 permits desktop appraisal using Form 1004 Desktop under certain eligibility criteria. BR_031_004 states Form 1004 Desktop is a required form that must be completed using UAD Appendix D when reporting appraisal results. Therefore, to validly use the desktop appraisal option, the appraisal must meet BR_031_004 form/spec requirements.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_031_004 fails, the lender may have an otherwise-eligible desktop appraisal transaction but cannot successfully submit/accept the appraisal in required systems, leading to rework and potential loss of the desktop appraisal pathway.

dependencies.rationale: BR_033_005 requires the DU loan casefile receive Approve/Eligible. BR_006_006 governs DU submission timing and ensures the underwriting DU run occurs before closing. Without DU submission/run, the Approve/Eligible condition cannot be satisfied or evidenced.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_006_006 fails (first DU submission after closing), the lender cannot substantiate desktop appraisal eligibility tied to DU outcomes, and the loan may be defective/ineligible for delivery as DU underwritten.

dependencies.rationale: BR_032_010 enforces DU resubmission under defined DTI change triggers after changes (rate increase, new debt, verified income lower). Since BR_033_005 requires DU Approve/Eligible, the valid DU outcome must reflect updated data when triggers occur; otherwise the desktop appraisal decision is based on stale/inaccurate DU findings.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_032_010 is not followed, the lender may incorrectly proceed with a desktop appraisal based on an outdated Approve/Eligible. This creates underwriting support defects and can lead to appraisal type ineligibility and delivery remedies.

business_rules.rule_name: Desktop Appraisal Cannot Be Made Subject To Condition Verification
business_rules.description: Desktop appraisals may not use extraordinary assumptions or be made 'subject to' verification of the subject property's condition, quality, or physical characteristics. If such assumptions are present, the appraisal is not permitted/acceptable for desktop appraisal use and must be corrected or replaced.
business_rules.conditions: Desktop appraisal relies on alternative data/photos or third-party information
business_rules.consequences: If extraordinary assumptions used, appraisal is unacceptable; lender must obtain a compliant appraisal report
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: Information provided by the homeowner, potential borrower, or a third party can be used to develop the description of the interior and exterior of the improvements. Extraordinary assumption(s) or appraisals made "subject to" veriﬁcation of the subject property's condition, quality, or physical characteristics are not permitted.

examples: Form 1004 Desktop states the value is 'subject to interior inspection to confirm condition' → prohibited; lender must cure or replace appraisal.
Appraiser uses borrower-provided photos but verifies key facts with a disinterested source and makes no 'subject to' condition statement → permitted.
Desktop report includes extraordinary assumption that 'all systems are functional' without verification → prohibited for desktop appraisal.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Require corrected/replacement appraisal; delivery defect if not resolved

examples: Desktop appraisal used for a low-risk refinance where permitted
Desktop appraisal completed with required data sources and exhibits
Desktop appraisal rejected because transaction/property type is ineligible

dependencies.rationale: BR_033_006 governs acceptability constraints unique to desktop appraisals (no extraordinary assumptions, not 'subject to' verification of condition/quality/characteristics). It functions as a validation gate on the use permitted by BR_033_005.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_033_006 fails, the desktop appraisal is unacceptable even if BR_033_005 eligibility criteria were met; lender must obtain a corrected/replacement appraisal, causing delays and potential pricing/lock impacts.

business_rules.rule_name: Disaster Relief Funds Allow 0% Borrower Contribution
business_rules.description: Borrowers may use lump-sum disaster relief grants or loans to satisfy Fannie Mae’s minimum borrower contribution requirement; no borrower contribution from the borrower’s own funds is required when using this assistance.
business_rules.conditions: Borrower uses a lump-sum disaster relief grant or loan in the transaction
business_rules.consequences: If used, minimum borrower contribution requirement is satisfied; otherwise follow standard rules
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: Borrowers may use lump-sum disaster relief grants or loans to satisfy Fannie Mae’s minimum borrower contribution requirement. No borrower contribution is required.

examples: Purchase: Borrower brings $15,000 FEMA grant; lender treats minimum borrower contribution requirement as met (0% required).
Refinance: Borrower receives lump-sum disaster relief loan used for closing costs; no borrower contribution is required under this topic.
If no disaster relief funds are used, lender applies the standard minimum borrower contribution requirements.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for purchase if standard contribution rules misapplied

examples: Disaster relief grant used to replenish savings for reserves
Disaster relief loan with repayment terms impacting DTI
Assistance tied to a declared disaster affecting the subject property area

business_rules.rule_name: High-Balance MI Required Over 80% LTV and Max 95%
business_rules.description: Mortgage insurance coverage is required for high-balance mortgage loans when the LTV ratio is greater than 80%. Financed borrower-purchased mortgage insurance is permitted, but the maximum gross LTV after including the financed premium cannot exceed 95%.
business_rules.conditions: Loan is a high-balance mortgage loan and LTV ratio > 80%
business_rules.consequences: If MI obtained and gross LTV ≤ 95%, eligible; if no MI or gross LTV > 95%, ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: Mortgage Insurance Requirements Mortgage insurance coverage is required for high-balance mortgage loans with LTV ratios greater than 80%. Financed borrower-purchased mortgage insurance is permitted; however, the maximum gross LTV (after the inclusion of the ﬁnanced premium) cannot exceed 95%.

examples: High-balance purchase at 85% LTV: borrower must have MI coverage; otherwise the loan is not eligible.
High-balance loan at 94% LTV plus financed MI premium that pushes gross LTV to 96%: not eligible (exceeds 95%).
High-balance loan at 79.9% LTV: MI not required under this specific requirement (other rules may still apply).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for purchase / potential repurchase demand

examples: High-balance loan in a designated high-cost county within permitted limits
High-balance loan requiring mortgage insurance due to LTV thresholds
High-balance loan with special delivery limitations and pricing requirements

business_rules.rule_name: ARM conversion disclosure fee cap $250 or $100
business_rules.description: If an ARM includes a conversion option to a fixed-rate loan, the lender’s conversion disclosures must include: (i) the instances when the conversion option may be exercised, (ii) the time frame within which conversion requests must be received, (iii) the time frame within which the borrower must return executed conversion documents, (iv) any processing fees. Fee cap: Fannie Mae allows a $250 fee for ARM plans that have a monthly conversion option and a $100 fee for other ARM plans.
business_rules.conditions: ARM includes a conversion option to a fixed-rate mortgage
business_rules.consequences: If met, disclosures/fees are compliant; if violated, delivery defect risk
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: Requirement: Conversion Disclosures Must Include The instances when the conversion option may be exercised. The time frame within which conversion requests must be received. The time frame within which the borrower must return executed conversion documents. Any fees that will be charged for processing the conversion. Note : Fannie Mae allows a $250 fee for ARM plans that have a monthly conversion option and a $100 fee for other ARM plans.

examples: 7/1 ARM with monthly conversion option discloses a $250 conversion processing fee (allowed).
10/6 ARM with a non-monthly conversion option discloses a $100 processing fee (allowed).
5/1 ARM discloses a $400 conversion processing fee; this exceeds the allowed cap and is a defect.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan repurchase demand

examples: 5/6 ARM with periodic and lifetime caps defined
Convertible ARM with conversion feature per product terms
ARM with temporary interest rate buydown applied at origination

business_rules.rule_name: Premium pricing lender credit cannot fund any down payment
business_rules.description: For premium pricing (borrower selects a higher interest rate in exchange for a lender credit), the lender credit cannot be used to fund any portion of the borrower’s down payment, and should not exceed the amount needed to offset the borrower’s closing costs. Any excess lender credit required to be returned to the borrower is considered an overpayment of fees and charges and may be applied as a principal curtailment or returned in cash to the borrower.
business_rules.conditions: Loan uses premium pricing to generate a lender credit
business_rules.consequences: If used only for eligible costs, compliant; if used for down payment, noncompliant
business_rules.exceptions: Excess credit may be principal curtailment or cash back per regulation

source_reference.source_text: Premium Pricing Premium pricing refers to situations when a borrower selects a higher interest rate on a loan in exchange for a lender credit. The lender credit cannot be used to fund any portion of the borrower’s down payment, and should not exceed the amount needed to oﬀset the borrower’s closing costs. Any excess lender credit required to be returned to the borrower in accordance with applicable regulatory requirements is considered an overpayment of fees and charges, and may be applied as a principal curtailment or returned in cash to the borrower.

examples: Borrower receives a $4,000 lender credit from premium pricing and applies it to title, appraisal, and prepaid taxes (compliant).
Borrower applies a premium pricing lender credit to meet minimum down payment funds-to-close (prohibited).
Closing costs total $3,000 but lender credit is $3,800; the $800 excess is treated as overpayment and applied as principal curtailment or returned in cash as allowed.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan repurchase demand

examples: Lender credit applied to borrower closing costs
Lender contribution structured as pricing credit (rate/points tradeoff)
Contribution limited by transaction caps and documented on closing disclosure

business_rules.rule_name: DU returns Ineligible for bankruptcy within 2/4 years or foreclosure within 7 years
business_rules.description: DU issues an Ineligible recommendation (loan ineligible for delivery) when bankruptcy/foreclosure seasoning thresholds are not met: Chapter 13 discharged within last 2 years, dismissed within last 4 years, or filed but neither discharged nor dismissed within last 4 years; non–Chapter 13 filed/discharged/dismissed within last 4 years; and foreclosure reported within 7 years prior to credit report date.
business_rules.conditions: When DU identifies bankruptcy or foreclosure in the credit report data for casefile
business_rules.consequences: DU returns Ineligible and loan casefile is ineligible for delivery to Fannie Mae
business_rules.exceptions: None in base rule; separate DU override instructions exist for inaccuracies/EC

source_reference.source_text: If a Chapter 13 bankruptcy was discharged within the last two years, dismissed within the last four years, or ﬁled but neither discharged nor dismissed within the last four years, the loan caseﬁle will receive an Ineligible recommendation and will be ineligible for delivery to Fannie Mae. If a non-Chapter 13 bankruptcy was ﬁled, discharged, or dismissed within the last four years, the loan caseﬁle will receive an Ineligible recommendation and will be ineligible for delivery to Fannie Mae. ... If a foreclosure was reported within the seven-year period prior to the credit report date, the loan caseﬁle will receive an Ineligible recommendation and will be ineligible for delivery to Fannie Mae.

examples: Chapter 13 discharged 18 months before new loan disbursement: DU returns Ineligible.
Chapter 7 discharged 3 years before disbursement: DU returns Ineligible.
Foreclosure tradeline shows foreclosure reported 6 years before credit report date: DU returns Ineligible.
business_rules.enforcement_action: DU Ineligible; loan ineligible for delivery

examples: DU evaluates an application and generates an Approve/Eligible recommendation.
DU evaluates an application and returns Refer with Caution requiring manual underwriting.

dependencies.rationale: BR_001_010 is a DU-issued Ineligible recommendation logic. BR_006_006 governs when DU is first submitted for underwriting. Operationally, you must run DU to receive and act on BR_001_010 outcomes before closing.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If DU is not submitted early, bankruptcy/foreclosure seasoning issues may be discovered late, causing denial after borrower has incurred costs or causing closing disruption.

business_rules.rule_name: First DU underwriting submission must occur before closing
business_rules.description: Loans may be submitted to DU before or after closing; however, the first submission to DU for underwriting purposes must occur before closing of the mortgage loan.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when underwriting method is DU for a mortgage loan application.
business_rules.consequences: If first DU submission occurs after closing, loan fails DU process requirement and may be ineligible for delivery.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.source_text:
Loans may be
submitted to DU before or after the closing of the mortgage loan; however, the ﬁrst submission to DU for
underwriting purposes must occur before closing of the mortgage loan.

examples: Loan is submitted to DU, receives Approve/Eligible, then closes: compliant (first submission pre-closing).
Loan closes first and is then submitted to DU for the first time: noncompliant with this DU timing requirement.
Loan is submitted to DU pre-closing, then resubmitted post-closing prior to delivery: allowed (first submission was pre-closing).
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit must requirement for first DU submission relative to closing.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery

examples: DU evaluates an application and generates an Approve/Eligible recommendation.
DU evaluates an application and returns Refer with Caution requiring manual underwriting.

business_rules.rule_name: Post-closing credit changes require updated application kept in file
business_rules.description: When a lender requests a new credit report after closing for a DU resubmission, and the new report contains information different from the information used to prepare the final loan application signed at closing, the loan application must be updated. Borrower signatures are not required due to the post-closing update. The lender must include both the final signed and the updated unsigned loan applications in the loan file.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a new post-closing credit report is obtained for DU resubmission.
business_rules.consequences: If not updated/retained, the file is incomplete and may be subject to delivery defects or remedies.
business_rules.exceptions: If new report contains no differences vs closing application data, update not triggered.

source_reference.source_text:
If the new credit report contains information that is diﬀerent than the information used to prepare the
ﬁnal loan application that was signed by the borrower at closing, the loan application must be updated.
(Borrower signature(s) are not required due to the update occurring post-closing.) The lender must include both
the ﬁnal signed and the updated unsigned loan applications in the loan ﬁle.

examples: Post-closing credit refresh adds a new auto loan not on the closing application: update URLA and retain both signed-at-closing and updated unsigned versions.
Post-closing credit refresh shows same tradelines and balances as closing: no application update required under this trigger.
Post-closing credit refresh shows a paid-off debt but lender fails to update the application and only keeps the signed copy: documentation defect.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Direct documentation retention requirement tied to post-closing credit refresh differences.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Suspension/purchase delay pending file cure

examples: DU evaluates an application and generates an Approve/Eligible recommendation.
DU evaluates an application and returns Refer with Caution requiring manual underwriting.

dependencies.rationale: BR_006_007 applies "when a lender requests a new credit report after closing for a DU resubmission." BR_032_010 mandates resubmission when specified DTI triggers occur due to changes. In many cases, resubmission requires updated credit inputs, which triggers BR_006_007’s application update/retention requirement.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_032_010-triggered resubmission occurs with a new credit report but BR_006_007 is not followed, the loan file lacks required documentation (final signed + updated unsigned application), creating delivery defects and post-purchase exposure.

dependencies.rationale: BR_019_003 mandates DU resubmission when discrepancies exceed tolerances. If that resubmission requires pulling a new credit report after closing (explicit trigger in BR_006_007), the lender must update and retain both the signed final and updated unsigned applications.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Failure to follow BR_006_007 after a QC-driven new credit report causes a documentation defect, even if the underwriting reassessment was done. This can lead to delivery defects and post-purchase findings.

business_rules.rule_name: Manufactured Home Loans Must Be Underwritten Through DU
business_rules.description: Loans secured by manufactured homes (including MH Advantage properties) must be underwritten through Desktop Underwriter (DU). If DU underwriting is not used, the loan does not meet this requirement.
business_rules.conditions: Subject property is a manufactured home, including MH Advantage
business_rules.consequences: If met, underwriting method is acceptable; if not, loan is ineligible for delivery
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: Underwriting and DU Requirements Loans secured by manufactured homes (including MH Advantage properties) must be underwritten through DU.

examples: Loan secured by manufactured home underwritten manually (no DU casefile): not permitted; must run through DU.
Loan secured by MH Advantage property: must be underwritten through DU even if lender otherwise uses manual underwriting.
Loan initially entered as site-built but determined to be manufactured: lender must treat as manufactured and ensure DU underwriting is used.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery ineligible; repurchase demand risk

examples: DU evaluates an application and generates an Approve/Eligible recommendation.
DU evaluates an application and returns Refer with Caution requiring manual underwriting.

business_rules.rule_name: Disputed tradeline accurate and borrower-responsible makes 0 DU eligibility
business_rules.description: When DU cannot issue an Approve recommendation using disputed tradelines but can issue an Approve recommendation when the disputed tradelines are excluded, the lender must investigate the disputed tradelines to determine borrower responsibility and whether the account information is accurate/complete. If the borrower is responsible and the account/tradeline information is accurate and complete, the loan is not eligible for delivery as a DU loan (though the lender may manually underwrite if the transaction is eligible for manual underwriting). If DU issues an Approve recommendation using the disputed tradelines, no further documentation or action is necessary.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when credit report includes borrower-disputed tradelines and DU reassesses risk.
business_rules.consequences: Meets: lender investigates and routes DU/manual correctly; Violated: DU delivery ineligible risk.
business_rules.exceptions: If DU Approve uses disputed tradelines, no further action is necessary.

source_reference.source_text: When the credit report contains tradelines disputed by the borrower, DU will ﬁrst assess the risk of the loan caseﬁle using all tradelines, including those disputed. If DU issues an Approve recommendation using the disputed tradelines, no further documentation or action is necessary. If DU does not issue an Approve recommendation when including the disputed tradelines, DU will re-assess the risk without using the disputed tradelines. If DU is then able to issue an Approve recommendation, the lender must investigate the tradelines to determine whether the borrower is responsible for the accounts or if the account information is accurate or complete. If the borrower is responsible for the disputed account and the account and tradeline information is accurate and complete, the loan is not eligible for delivery as a DU loan.

examples: DU Approve/Eligible with disputed tradelines included—lender takes no additional dispute action.

DU only approves when disputed tradeline excluded; lender confirms borrower is responsible and tradeline is accurate—loan cannot be delivered as DU; lender may proceed as manual underwrite if permitted.
DU only approves when disputed tradeline excluded; lender obtains documentation showing borrower not responsible—loan may be delivered as DU after documentation.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan repurchase demand (if delivered as DU when not eligible)
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit DU logic and ineligibility outcome; numeric precision limited to categorical outcomes.

examples: DU evaluates an application and generates an Approve/Eligible recommendation.
DU evaluates an application and returns Refer with Caution requiring manual underwriting.

dependencies.rationale: BR_010_007 applies "when DU cannot issue an Approve recommendation using disputed tradelines but can... when excluded." That state can only occur after a DU submission/run, governed by BR_006_006.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If DU isn’t run (or isn’t run timely), the lender cannot identify the disputed-tradeline issue and may proceed with incorrect underwriting path, risking DU delivery ineligibility.

business_rules.rule_name: DU Rent History Requires 12 Months Renting and $300 Minimum Rent
business_rules.description: When rent payment history is used in DU for certain loan casefiles, at least one borrower must have been renting for at least 12 months with monthly rent of $300 or more and must have no mortgage on credit report, limited credit history, or no credit score. If rent history is not on the credit report, the lender must enter monthly rent in the online loan application, obtain a 12-month asset verification report through an authorized DU validation service vendor, and confirm the borrower is an account holder and the account is used to pay rent.
business_rules.conditions: DU casefile uses rent payment history from asset verification or credit report
business_rules.consequences: If met, DU can use rent history; if violated, rent history cannot be credited in DU
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in excerpt

source_reference.source_text: The following requirements apply when using rent payment history in DU: At least one borrower must have been renting for at least 12 months with a monthly rent payment of $300 or more and one of the following: have no mortgage reported on their credit report, have a limited credit history, or have no credit score. When the rent payment history does not appear on the credit report, for DU to be able to identify rent payments using an asset veriﬁcation report, the lender must enter the monthly rent paid by the borrower in the online loan application, obtain an asset veriﬁcation report with 12 months of bank statement data through an authorized DU validation service asset veriﬁcation report vendor, and conﬁrm the borrower is an account holder and that the account provided in the asset veriﬁcation report is the one from which the borrower pays rent.

examples: Compliant: Borrower renting 18 months at $1,500/mo, no mortgage tradeline; lender inputs rent and obtains 12-month asset verification report → DU credits rent history.
Non-compliant: Borrower rent is $250/mo (below $300) → rent history cannot be used under this DU logic requirement.
Non-compliant: Rent not on credit report and lender fails to confirm borrower is account holder for the account used to pay rent → DU cannot identify rent payments from the report.
business_rules.enforcement_action: N/A

examples: DU evaluates an application and generates an Approve/Eligible recommendation.
DU evaluates an application and returns Refer with Caution requiring manual underwriting.

dependencies.rationale: BR_011_011 applies "when rent payment history is used in DU" and requires specific borrower/rent conditions and potentially an asset verification report through DU validation vendors. That assumes DU underwriting is in play per BR_006_006.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If DU is not run or DU data-entry requirements are not followed, rent history may be improperly credited, changing risk findings and potentially producing unsupported Approve/Eligible decisions.

dependencies.rationale: BR_011_011 requires obtaining a 12-month asset verification report through an authorized DU validation service vendor when rent history is not on credit report. BR_037_008 requires investigating and resolving conflicting/contradictory information and confirming borrower ownership/name when an asset verification report is used (for employment validation). When the same asset verification report (or vendor ecosystem) supports DU validations, applying BR_037_008-style conflict/ownership checks strengthens and standardizes compliance for BR_011_011 implementations.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_037_008-type conflict/ownership issues are not resolved, DU validation inputs (including rent history via asset verification) may be unreliable, increasing risk of incorrect DU findings and post-purchase validation failures.

business_rules.rule_name: DU value acceptance ineligible when price or value is at least $1,000,000
business_rules.description: DU will not consider a value acceptance offer for transactions where either the purchase price or the estimated value provided to DU is $1,000,000 or more. If the transaction meets this ineligible criterion, an appraisal will be required under DU’s standard appraisal messaging and the lender cannot rely on value acceptance.
business_rules.conditions: DU casefile is evaluated for value acceptance and price/value inputs are provided
business_rules.consequences: If ≥$1,000,000, value acceptance not offered; lender must obtain appraisal
business_rules.exceptions: None stated for the $1,000,000 threshold

source_reference.source_text: The following transactions are not eligible for a value acceptance oﬀer: two- to four-unit properties; co-op units and manufactured homes; proposed construction; construction-to-permanent loans (single-close and two-close); HomeStyle Renovation and HomeStyle Refresh loans; leasehold properties; Texas Section 50(a)(6) loans; community land trusts or other properties with resale price restrictions, which include loan caseﬁles using the Aﬀordable LTV feature; transactions where either the purchase price or estimated value provided to DU is $1,000,000 or more;

examples: Purchase price $1,250,000 with estimated value $1,260,000 → DU cannot offer value acceptance; appraisal required.
Purchase price $999,999 but estimated value entered as $1,000,000 → ineligible for value acceptance; appraisal required.
Purchase price $850,000 and estimated value $900,000 → this rule does not block value acceptance (other ineligibilities may still apply).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery ineligibility / repurchase exposure

examples: DU evaluates an application and generates an Approve/Eligible recommendation.
DU evaluates an application and returns Refer with Caution requiring manual underwriting.

dependencies.rationale: BR_018_006 describes when DU will not consider value acceptance (≥$1,000,000) and will require an appraisal. That determination is an output of the DU evaluation process, which necessarily follows DU submission governed by BR_006_006.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If DU is not run timely, the lender may proceed too far without knowing value acceptance is unavailable, causing late appraisal ordering, closing delays, or last-minute underwriting changes.

business_rules.rule_name: DU non-occupant max LTV 95% (CLTV 105% with CS)
business_rules.description: For DU-underwritten loan casefiles that include a guarantor, co-signer, or co-borrower who will not occupy the subject property, the maximum LTV, CLTV, and HCLTV ratios may not exceed 95%. If a Community Seconds is part of the transaction, the CLTV ratio may not exceed 105% where permitted in the Eligibility Matrix.
business_rules.conditions: DU loan casefile includes a non-occupant guarantor/co-signer/co-borrower
business_rules.consequences: If ratios within limits, loan may be delivered; if exceeded, ineligible as DU loan
business_rules.exceptions: Community Seconds allows CLTV up to 105% where Eligibility Matrix permits

source_reference.section_id: B2-2-04, LTV Ratio Requirements for Loan Casefiles Underwritten through DU
source_reference.source_text: For DU loan caseﬁles with a guarantor, co-signer, or co-borrower who will not occupy the subject property, the maximum LTV, CLTV, and HCLTV ratio may not exceed 95% (unless a Community Seconds is part of the transaction, in which case the CLTV ratio may not exceed 105% where permitted in the Eligibility Matrix .

examples: DU purchase with non-occupant co-borrower: LTV 97%; fails because maximum is 95% for DU casefiles with non-occupant borrower.
DU purchase with Community Seconds: LTV 95% and CLTV 105%; acceptable if Eligibility Matrix permits CLTV up to 105%.
DU refinance: HCLTV 96% with non-occupant co-borrower; fails because max HCLTV is 95% absent Community Seconds exception.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit 95% cap and explicit Community Seconds CLTV 105% exception are stated for DU casefiles.
business_rules.enforcement_action: loan ineligible for sale to Fannie Mae as a DU loan

examples: DU evaluates an application and generates an Approve/Eligible recommendation.
DU evaluates an application and returns Refer with Caution requiring manual underwriting.

dependencies.rationale: BR_026_007 begins "For DU-underwritten loan casefiles..." meaning its applicability and enforcement are tied to DU usage and casefile evaluation governed by BR_006_006.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the loan is treated as DU-underwritten without proper DU submission timing/compliance, the lender may incorrectly apply or overlook the LTV/CLTV/HCLTV constraints, leading to ineligible DU delivery.

business_rules.rule_name: DU resubmission required when DTI exceeds 45% or rises 3 points
business_rules.description: When certain changes occur (e.g., interest rate increase, new debts, verified income lower than submitted), the lender must resubmit the loan casefile to DU if the lender’s recalculated DTI would (1) exceed 45% OR (2) increase by 3 percentage points or more when the recalculated DTI is 50% or less.
business_rules.conditions: When post-submission changes require DTI recalculation based on verified/updated data
business_rules.consequences: If trigger met, DU resubmission is required; if not resubmitted, underwriting decision is unsupported and loan may be defective
business_rules.exceptions: None stated (applies to listed trigger events and DTI thresholds)

source_reference.section_id: B3-2-10, Ensuring DU Data and Delivery Information Accuracy
source_reference.source_text: DU loans — the result of these changes causes the DTI ratio recalculated by the lender to • now exceed 45%, or • increase by 3 percentage points or more (if the recalculated DTI ratio is 50% or less) * See resubmission examples below. Loan caseﬁle must be resubmitted to DU

examples: Original DTI 44%; new debt discovered increases recalculated DTI to 46% (>45%); lender must resubmit to DU.
Original DTI 35%; verified income lower increases recalculated DTI to 38% (increase 3 points and ≤50%); lender must resubmit.
Original DTI 46%; recalculated DTI becomes 48% (increase 2 points); resubmission is not required by this DTI trigger.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery rejection/repurchase demand

examples: DU evaluates an application and generates an Approve/Eligible recommendation.
DU evaluates an application and returns Refer with Caution requiring manual underwriting.

dependencies.rationale: BR_032_010 requires resubmitting the loan casefile to DU when certain changes occur and DTI thresholds trigger. This can only happen if the loan has already been submitted to DU under BR_006_006.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_006_006 is not satisfied, the resubmission control (BR_032_010) cannot be executed, causing stale underwriting findings and unsupported approval/delivery.

business_rules.rule_name: Employment via asset report needs owner match and 30 or 60-day review
business_rules.description: When DU validates employment and an asset verification report is used for that employment validation, the lender must investigate and resolve conflicting or contradictory information. The lender must confirm the borrower is listed as an owner of the asset account and that the report reflects the borrower’s name as account holder. For this purpose, the lender need only review the report covering the 30- or 60-day period required for asset verification for the transaction type; if no asset verification is required for the transaction, the lender must review account activity covering the most recent 30-day period.
business_rules.conditions: When DU employment validation relies on an asset verification report
business_rules.consequences: If owner/name mismatch or unresolved conflicts exist, employment validation is not satisfied
business_rules.exceptions: If no asset verification is required, use most recent 30-day account activity period

source_reference.source_text: The lender must review the veriﬁcation report relied upon by DU... investigate and resolve any conﬂicting or contradictory information, and when an asset veriﬁcation report is used to validate employment, the lender must conﬁrm that the borrower is listed as an owner of the asset account and that the report reﬂects the borrower's name as account holder. The lender need only review the report covering the period of time (30 or 60 days) required for asset veriﬁcation for the transaction type, or if no asset veriﬁcation is required for the transaction, the account activity covering the most recent 30-day period

examples: Asset verification report lists borrower as joint account holder and shows payroll deposits; lender reviews last 60 days (required for transaction) → acceptable if no conflicts.
Report shows payroll deposits but account holder name is spouse only → lender must not treat as validated employment until borrower ownership confirmed/resolved.
Transaction type requires no asset verification; lender reviews most recent 30-day activity to confirm borrower is account holder for employment validation purposes.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loss of validation relief; underwriting finding cannot be relied upon

examples: DU evaluates an application and generates an Approve/Eligible recommendation.
DU evaluates an application and returns Refer with Caution requiring manual underwriting.

business_rules.rule_name: Flow loan age must be 6 months or less from first payment date
business_rules.description: To be eligible for purchase by Fannie Mae on a flow basis, a loan must be no more than six months old. The loan age is measured from the first payment date to the “Purchase Ready” date for whole loans or to the MBS pool issue date for MBS loans.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to flow whole loans or MBS loans being delivered for purchase/securitization.
business_rules.consequences: If older than 6 months by the applicable measurement, loan is not eligible for purchase.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in this section.

source_reference.source_text: To be eligible for purchase by Fannie Mae on a ﬂow basis, a loan must be no more than six months old measured from the ﬁrst payment date to the "Purchase Ready" date (whole loans) or the MBS pool issue date (MBS loans).

examples: Whole loan: first payment date 01/01; Purchase Ready date 06/15 → within 6 months → eligible on age.
MBS loan: first payment date 01/01; pool issue date 07/01 → exceeds 6 months → ineligible on age.
Whole loan: first payment date 03/01; Purchase Ready date 08/31 → exceeds 6 months by 1 day → ineligible unless delivery date changes.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Delivery rejection (ineligible for purchase)

examples: Loan eligibility profile shows high-balance indicator true based on county limits.
Loan eligibility profile flags escrow required per policy and loan characteristics.

dependencies.rationale: BR_002_001’s age test is defined as a function of first payment date to Purchase Ready date (whole loans) or MBS pool issue date (MBS). Because BR_038_009 constrains the allowed first payment date timing for whole loan delivery and clarifies the start point for CTP loans, BR_038_009 must be satisfied/known before a reliable BR_002_001 age computation can be performed.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_038_009 is wrong or missing, BR_002_001 can incorrectly classify loans as older/newer than six months, causing wrongful delivery blocks or post-delivery repurchase/rejection exposure.

business_rules.rule_name: Modified loan limit uses original amount; over current limit is ineligible
business_rules.description: Loan limits for modified loans must be evaluated using the original loan amount, not the unpaid principal balance at the time of modification or acquisition. A modified loan with an original loan amount that exceeds the current applicable loan limit is not eligible for purchase, even if its balance at the time of modification is at or below the current applicable loan limit.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a loan delivered to Fannie Mae has been modified prior to acquisition.
business_rules.consequences: If original loan amount exceeds current loan limit, loan is not eligible for purchase.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text.

source_reference.section_id: B2-1.5-02 (06/05/2024), Modified Loans / Loan limits for modified loans
source_reference.source_text: Loan limits for modiﬁed loans are based on the original loan amount of the loan and not on the unpaid principal balance of the loan at the time of modiﬁcation or acquisition by Fannie Mae. A modiﬁed loan with an original loan amount exceeding the current loan limit is not eligible for purchase by Fannie Mae, even though the balance at the time of the modiﬁcation may be at or below the current applicable loan limit.

examples: Original loan amount $800,000 in a baseline area; later modified down to $720,000 UPB → still ineligible if current limit is $766,550 and original exceeded applicable limit at time of evaluation.
Original loan amount exceeds current local high-balance limit; modification reduces UPB below limit → still ineligible due to original amount test.
Original loan amount within current limit; modification changes rate/term only → loan may remain eligible if all other requirements are met.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Delivery rejection (ineligible for purchase)

examples: Loan eligibility profile shows high-balance indicator true based on county limits.
Loan eligibility profile flags escrow required per policy and loan characteristics.

business_rules.rule_name: Total points and fees cap: 3% ATR covered; 5% ATR exempt
business_rules.description: Total points and fees and total loan amount must be calculated in accordance with Regulation Z (12 CFR § 1026.32). For ATR Covered Loans, total points and fees may not exceed 3% of the total loan amount (or a different amount per QM provisions); a timely cure payment under 12 CFR § 1026.43(e)(3)(iii) satisfies the requirement. For ATR Exempt Loans, total points and fees may not exceed 5% of the total loan amount, with possible adjustments (permitted reduction under 12 CFR § 1026.31(h) or, for loans not subject to TILA, restitution to borrower of at least the portion exceeding 5% at closing).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to loans with application dates on/after 2014-01-10 under ATR framework.
business_rules.consequences: Exceeding the applicable cap makes loan ineligible unless cured/adjusted as permitted.
business_rules.exceptions: ATR covered: cure payment allowed; ATR exempt: reduction or restitution allowed.

source_reference.source_text: Points and Fees Limitations. For purposes of these requirements, “total points and fees” and “total loan amount” must be calculated in accordance with Regulation Z (12 CFR § 1026.32). ATR Covered Loans: Total points and fees may not exceed 3% of the total loan amount or such diﬀerent amount in accordance with the qualiﬁed mortgage provisions of Regulation Z (12 CFR § 1026.43(e)(3)(i)). If a lender makes a cure payment in the amount and by the time required by 12 CFR § 1026.43(e)(3)(iii), such loan satisﬁes this requirement. ATR Exempt Loans: Total points and fees may not exceed 5% of the total loan amount.

examples: ATR covered purchase, total loan amount $400,000: max points/fees = $12,000 (3%); $12,500 would violate unless cured per Reg Z.
ATR exempt loan, total loan amount $200,000: max points/fees = $10,000 (5%); $10,800 requires permitted reduction/restitution to meet cap.
ATR covered loan initially at 3.2% points/fees: lender makes cure payment per 12 CFR § 1026.43(e)(3)(iii) within required timeframe → satisfies this requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Delivery ineligible; potential repurchase demand for breach

examples: Loan eligibility profile shows high-balance indicator true based on county limits.
Loan eligibility profile flags escrow required per policy and loan characteristics.

business_rules.rule_name: APR cannot exceed APOR by more than 2.25% (ATR covered) or 6.5% (exempt)
business_rules.description: For loans with application dates on and after July 1, 2021, the APR-APOR spread is the maximum difference by which the loan’s APR cannot exceed the APOR. ATR Covered Loans: spread may not exceed 2.25% (or different amount per Revised General QM Rule) calculated per that rule; for ARMs with initial fixed periods of five years or less, APR must be calculated using the maximum interest rate that could apply during the first five years after the first payment is due. ATR Exempt Loans: spread may not exceed 6.5% (or more calculated per Revised General QM rule).
business_rules.conditions: Applies for application dates on/after 2021-07-01; evaluate APR/APOR per QM rule.
business_rules.consequences: If spread exceeds limit, loan fails ATR/QM eligibility for Fannie Mae acquisition.
business_rules.exceptions: Different amounts may apply if specified by the Revised General QM Rule.

source_reference.source_text: APR-APOR Spread Limitations. Eﬀective for loans with applications dates on and after July 1, 2021, the APR-APOR spread is the maximum diﬀerence by which the loan's APR cannot exceed the APOR. ... ATR Covered Loans: The spread may not exceed 2.25% or a diﬀerent amount as speciﬁed in the Revised General QM Rule, calculated in accordance with the provisions of that rule. Note: For ARMs with initial ﬁxed periods of ﬁve years or less, the APR must be calculated using the maximum interest rate that could apply during the ﬁrst ﬁve years after the ﬁrst payment is due. ATR Exempt Loans: The spread may not exceed 6.5% or more calculated in accordance with the Revised General QM rule.

examples: Fixed-rate ATR covered loan: APR 7.10%, APOR 5.00% → spread 2.10% ≤ 2.25% → passes spread test.
ATR covered ARM with initial fixed period 5 years: APR must be computed using max rate possible in first 5 years; if computed spread becomes 2.40% → fails 2.25% cap.
ATR exempt loan: APR 10.25%, APOR 3.50% → spread 6.75% > 6.5% → fails unless QM rule provides a different allowable amount.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Delivery ineligible; potential repurchase demand for ATR/QM breach

examples: Loan eligibility profile shows high-balance indicator true based on county limits.
Loan eligibility profile flags escrow required per policy and loan characteristics.

dependencies.rationale: Both rules are used to establish ATR/QM-related eligibility for delivery/purchase: BR_DETERMINES_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_CONSTRAINT_002_003 constrains points and fees, while BR_DETERMINES_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_CONSTRAINT_002_004 constrains pricing via APR/APOR spread. Many compliance workflows run both checks together and treat failure of either as a QM/ATR eligibility failure (and likely delivery ineligibility per investor policy).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If one of the two checks is omitted, the lender may mistakenly treat a loan as ATR/QM-compliant and proceed to delivery. This increases repurchase exposure and post-purchase compliance defect risk.

dependencies.rationale: BR_GENERATES_UNDERWRITING_DECISION_PROCESS_035_004 requires qualifying borrowers using an ARM qualifying rate for ARMs with initial fixed periods of five years or less. BR_DETERMINES_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_CONSTRAINT_002_004 requires APR calculation for such ARMs using the maximum interest rate that could apply during the first five years (for the APR/APOR spread test). For these products, underwriting qualification and QM pricing compliance must both be satisfied; workflows should navigate users through both checks for the same ARM feature set.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the lender performs only one of the two ARM-specific controls, it may understate risk (qualify too easily) or misclassify QM status (APR/APOR test incorrect), creating eligibility failures and post-purchase compliance findings.

dependencies.rationale: BR_DETERMINES_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_CONSTRAINT_002_004 states a special APR calculation rule for 'ARMs with initial fixed periods of five years or less'. Correctly establishing the initial fixed period in months/years (per BR_HAS_ARM_TERMS_CONSTRAINT_038_006) is necessary to decide whether that APR rule is triggered.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the ARM fixed period is mis-specified, the lender may compute APR using the wrong method for QM/APR-APOR testing, causing false QM compliance conclusions and potential ineligibility for Fannie Mae acquisition.

business_rules.rule_name: Original loan term max 30 years and minimum 85 months (with construction exception)
business_rules.description: Fannie Mae purchases or securitizes loans with original terms up to 30 years. The term of a first mortgage may not extend more than 30 years beyond the date that is one month prior to the date of the first payment. The minimum original term is 85 months (subject to applicable committing and delivery requirements). Exception/clarification: for single-closing construction-to-permanent loans, the loan term must not exceed 30 years after conversion to permanent financing (per applicable guidance). If the term is outside these limits, the loan is not eligible for purchase/securitization.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to first-lien mortgage original term evaluation for eligibility and delivery.
business_rules.consequences: If term exceeds maximum or is below 85 months, loan is not eligible for purchase/securitization.
business_rules.exceptions: Single-closing construction-to-permanent: ignore construction period; still ≤30 years post-conversion.

source_reference.source_text: Fannie Mae purchases or securitizes loans that have original terms up to 30 years. The term of a ﬁrst mortgage may not extend more than 30 years beyond the date that is one month prior to the date of the ﬁrst payment. The minimum original term is 85 months, subject to applicable committing and delivery requirements for whole loans and loans in MBS. Exception: The only exception to these requirements is for single-closing construction-to-permanent loans, which must have a loan term not exceeding 30 years after conversion to permanent ﬁnancing (disregarding the construction period).

Fannie Mae purchases or securitizes loans that have original terms up to 30 years. The term of a ﬁrst mortgage may not extend more than 30 years beyond the date that is one month prior to the date of the ﬁrst payment. The minimum original term is 85 months... Exception: The only exception to these requirements is for single-closing construction-to-permanent loans, which must have a loan term not exceeding 30 years after conversion to permanent ﬁnancing (disregarding the construction period).

examples: Standard purchase: first payment due 06/01; term cannot extend past 05/01 thirty years later → if maturity exceeds that, ineligible.
Loan with original term 60 months → below 85-month minimum → ineligible for delivery.
Single-closing construction-to-permanent: 12-month construction + 30-year permanent; permanent term is 30 years post-conversion → eligible on term under exception.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Delivery rejection (ineligible for purchase)

examples: Loan eligibility profile shows high-balance indicator true based on county limits.
Loan eligibility profile flags escrow required per policy and loan characteristics.

deduplication_info.rationale: Both rules enforce the same core loan term eligibility boundaries for Fannie Mae acquisition: original terms up to 30 years; the term may not extend more than 30 years beyond the date one month prior to the first payment date; and the minimum original term is 85 months. BR_DETERMINES_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_CONSTRAINT_002_005 includes an additional explicit construction-to-permanent (single-close) exception clarification. Because the added exception does not change the general rule but clarifies its application to a special case, the rules are treated as duplicates with a completeness variation (semantic not scored at full 40).
deduplication_info.primary_selection_reason: BR_DETERMINES_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_CONSTRAINT_002_005 selected as primary because it is more complete: it includes the same maximum and minimum term constraints and adds the explicit single-closing construction-to-permanent exception details, which increases testability and reduces ambiguity.

dependencies.rationale: BR_002_005 repeats the 30-year test relative to a date one month prior to the first payment and adds a construction-to-permanent clarification. BR_038_009 governs permissible first payment timing and defines the conversion start point for the 2‑month period. A compliant first payment date (and correct conversion timing) must exist before evaluating BR_002_005.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_038_009 is not satisfied, BR_002_005 eligibility may be determined incorrectly. This can create false eligibility (loan delivered but later rejected) or false ineligibility (loan unnecessarily removed from delivery pipeline).

business_rules.rule_name: HOEPA Section 32 loans are not eligible for delivery to Fannie Mae
business_rules.description: Any mortgage loan that is subject to the Home Ownership and Equity Protection Act of 1994 (HOEPA), as described in Section 32 of Regulation Z, is not eligible for delivery to Fannie Mae. This is an absolute delivery prohibition regardless of other underwriting or eligibility compliance.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the loan is determined to be HOEPA/Section 32 under Regulation Z.
business_rules.consequences: Loan is not eligible for delivery; delivery must be blocked or removed from pipeline.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text.

source_reference.section_id: B2-1.5-02 (06/05/2024), HOEPA and State Higher-Priced Loans
source_reference.source_text: A loan that is subject to the Home Ownership and Equity Protection Act of 1994 (HOEPA), as described in Section 32 of Regulation Z, is not eligible for delivery to Fannie Mae.

source_reference.section_id: B2-1.5-02, HOEPA and State Higher-Priced Loans
source_reference.source_text: A loan that is subject to the Home Ownership and Equity Protection Act of 1994 (HOEPA), as described in Section 32 of Regulation Z, is not eligible for delivery to Fannie Mae.

examples: Closed-end refinance triggers HOEPA high-cost coverage under Section 32 → loan must not be delivered to Fannie Mae.
Purchase loan later determined to be HOEPA-covered due to points/fees → lender must prevent delivery or remove from delivery batch.
If a delivered loan is later found to be HOEPA-covered, it may be treated as a material eligibility defect subject to remedy.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Delivery rejection; potential repurchase demand if delivered

examples: Loan eligibility profile shows high-balance indicator true based on county limits.
Loan eligibility profile flags escrow required per policy and loan characteristics.

deduplication_info.rationale: Both rules impose the same absolute prohibition: any loan that is a HOEPA/Regulation Z Section 32 covered transaction is not eligible for delivery to Fannie Mae. Context and consequences are identical (do not deliver / ineligible). There are no conflicting thresholds; differences are wording only.
deduplication_info.primary_selection_reason: BR_DETERMINES_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROHIBITION_002_006 selected as primary because it is more explicit about the control outcome: it frames HOEPA/Section 32 as an absolute delivery block regardless of other compliance, which is the clearest operational consequence.

dependencies.rationale: BR_DETERMINES_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROHIBITION_002_006 prohibits delivery of any loan that is determined to be HOEPA/Section 32 under Regulation Z. BR_DETERMINES_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_CONSTRAINT_002_003 requires calculating total points and fees and total loan amount in accordance with 12 CFR §1026.32 (Section 32), which is a core input to identifying whether a loan is Section 32/HOEPA.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If §1026.32-aligned points/fees calculations are not performed or are incorrect, the lender may (a) fail to identify a HOEPA loan and inappropriately deliver it (hard delivery defect), or (b) falsely flag a non-HOEPA loan as prohibited, causing avoidable fallout and delays.

dependencies.rationale: BR_DETERMINES_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_CONSTRAINT_002_004 governs how APR is calculated and compared to APOR for QM/ATR eligibility (including special ARM APR calculation). While BR_DETERMINES_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROHIBITION_002_006 references HOEPA/Section 32 status, APR accuracy is typically part of high-cost status determinations and pricing compliance checks. Therefore, APR/APOR computations are a meaningful upstream dependency for robust Section 32 screening workflows.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If APR is calculated incorrectly (especially for short-reset ARMs), the lender may misclassify loans during high-cost screening, leading to prohibited delivery risk or unnecessary delivery blocks pending re-calculation.

business_rules.rule_name: HCLTV Must Include 100% HELOC Line and UPBs in Numerator
business_rules.description: For first mortgages with subordinate financing under a HELOC, the lender must calculate HCLTV by dividing (i) original loan amount of the first mortgage + (ii) the full amount of any HELOCs (whether or not drawn) + (iii) the UPB of all closed-end subordinate financing by the lesser of the sales price or appraised value of the property.
business_rules.conditions: First mortgage has subordinate financing under a HELOC and HCLTV required
business_rules.consequences: If calculated correctly, eligibility ratios are accurate; if not, eligibility defect
business_rules.exceptions: None stated (other programs may have different HCLTV details via cross-refs)

source_reference.source_text:
