DU generates Approve/Ineligible decision requiring changes to achieve eligibility.

business_rules.rule_name: ARM fixed period 5 years or less must use ARM qualifying rate
business_rules.description: To limit potential payment shock, lenders must qualify borrowers using the ARM qualifying rate described in B3-6-04 for any ARM with an initial fixed-rate period of five years or less. This underwriting qualification requirement applies when generating the underwriting decision for such ARMs.
business_rules.conditions: ARM initial fixed-rate period is 5 years or less during underwriting
business_rules.consequences: If qualified per ARM qualifying rate, decision is compliant; if not, underwriting defect
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: Lenders must limit the impact of any potential payment shock on an ARM with an initial ﬁxed-rate period of ﬁve years or less by qualifying borrowers based on the qualifying rate described in B3-6-04, Qualifying Payment Requirements .

examples: Borrower applies for a 5/1 ARM; lender qualifies using the B3-6-04 ARM qualifying rate, not just the introductory rate.
Borrower applies for a 3/1 ARM; qualifying uses the ARM qualifying rate to assess post-intro payment increase risk.
Lender qualifies a 5/1 ARM using only the teaser rate payment; QC later flags a payment shock qualifying defect.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan repurchase demand

examples: DU generates Approve/Eligible decision with standard documentation conditions.
DU generates Approve/Ineligible decision requiring changes to achieve eligibility.

business_rules.rule_name: Bridge Loan Payment Included in DTI Unless Sale Contract Clears Contingencies
business_rules.description: A bridge (or swing) loan creates a contingent liability that must be included in the borrower’s recurring monthly debt obligations and the DTI ratio. Fannie Mae will waive inclusion in DTI only if the lender documents a fully executed sales contract for the current residence and confirmation that any financing contingencies have been cleared.
business_rules.conditions: Borrower obtains bridge/swing loan before selling current residence
business_rules.consequences: If docs provided, may exclude from DTI; if not, must include in DTI calculation
business_rules.exceptions: Waiver applies only with executed contract and cleared financing contingencies

source_reference.source_text: When a borrower obtains a bridge (or swing) loan, the funds from that loan can be used for closing on a new principal residence before the current residence is sold. This creates a contingent liability that must be considered part of the borrower’s recurring monthly debt obligations and included in the DTI ratio calculation. Fannie Mae will waive this requirement and not require the debt to be included in the DTI ratio if the following documentation is provided: a fully executed sales contract for the current residence, and conﬁrmation that any ﬁnancing contingencies have been cleared.

examples: Borrower has bridge loan with $800 monthly payment and no signed sales contract: must include $800 in DTI.
Borrower has fully executed contract but contingencies not cleared: still must include bridge loan payment in DTI.
Borrower provides executed sales contract and proof financing contingencies cleared: lender may exclude bridge loan payment from DTI.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan repurchase demand

examples: Loan uses a bridge loan secured by departing residence until that property sells.
Loan includes swing loan monthly payment in DTI until payoff.

business_rules.rule_name: Significant defect or warranty breach can trigger immediate repurchase or make-whole payment
business_rules.description: If Fannie Mae determines through review (or otherwise learns) that a mortgage loan did not meet requirements due to violation of the Lender Contract, or if the remedies framework applies and a significant defect is identified, Fannie Mae may require the lender to immediately repurchase the mortgage loan/acquired property (or Fannie Mae participation interest) or remit a make whole payment if the property has been liquidated. Fannie Mae may also require repurchase/make whole if any selling lender warranty is untrue and, if the remedies framework applies, qualifies as a significant defect, whether or not the lender had actual knowledge—except that no request will be made when the warranty specifically requires actual knowledge and the lender has no such knowledge.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when QC/loan review identifies contract violation, warranty breach, or significant defect
business_rules.consequences: If triggered, lender must repurchase immediately or remit make-whole payment
business_rules.exceptions: No request if warranty requires actual knowledge and lender had no knowledge

source_reference.source_text: If Fannie Mae's loan review determines (or Fannie Mae otherwise learns) that a mortgage loan did not meet Fannie Mae requirements due to violation of the Lender Contract or, if the “remedies framework” applies and a “signiﬁcant defect” is identiﬁed, Fannie Mae may require the lender to immediately repurchase the mortgage

examples: Post-purchase review discovers misrepresented occupancy that qualifies as a significant defect under the remedies framework; Fannie Mae issues an immediate repurchase demand.
A life-of-loan warranty is untrue; even if the lender lacked actual knowledge, Fannie Mae may require repurchase if it qualifies as a significant defect.
A warranty clause states a breach exists only with actual knowledge; lender proves it had no actual knowledge, so no repurchase request is made under that clause.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan repurchase demand

examples: Fannie Mae issues repurchase request due to ineligible loan attribute discovered in QC.
Fannie Mae issues make-whole request based on appraisal-related defect findings.

dependencies.rationale: BR_ISSUES_REPURCHASE_REQUEST_COMPLIANCE_008_012 establishes that Fannie Mae may require the lender to immediately repurchase or make-whole upon significant defects. BR_ISSUES_REPURCHASE_REQUEST_PROCESS_017_010 provides an exception/deferral when the demand relates to compliance-with-laws issues under pending litigation or active agency review, stating the Seller/Servicer is not required to repurchase until 30 days after resolution. Therefore, repurchase timing logic must check the override conditions to avoid enforcing an incorrect immediate repurchase deadline.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the override is not applied, the lender may unnecessarily repurchase early (liquidity and operational burden) or mis-handle the demand response process, potentially undermining legal strategy. Conversely, applying deferral when not allowed can cause breach/escalation for non-performance.

business_rules.rule_name: Repurchase Not Required Until 30 Days After Litigation Ends
business_rules.description: If Fannie Mae issues a repurchase demand for a failure to comply with laws while litigation on the same issue is pending, or while a government agency with authority to decide the issue has publicly stated it is reviewing it, the Seller/Servicer is not required to repurchase the loan until 30 days after the matter is resolved (dismissed/settled/concluded at trial/adjudication) or the agency issues a final determination. After resolution, the Seller/Servicer may request Fannie Mae to review appropriateness of the demand, and Fannie Mae will withdraw where appropriate.
business_rules.conditions: When repurchase demand is issued for compliance-with-laws issue with pending litigation/agency review
business_rules.consequences: Repurchase obligation is deferred; after resolution, demand may be reviewed/withdrawn
business_rules.exceptions: Applies only when the same issue is in litigation or under public agency review

source_reference.section_id: A3-2-01 (12/10/2025), Repurchase demands for compliance with laws violations
source_reference.source_text: If Fannie Mae issues a repurchase demand involving a failure to comply with laws when there is pending litigation underway for that same issue, or when a government agency with authority to make a determination regarding the issue has publicly stated that it is reviewing the issue, the seller/servicer is not required to repurchase the loan until 30 days after the litigation is • dismissed, • settled, or • concluded at trial in an adjudication, or • the government agency has made a ﬁnal determination (collectively, the “Resolution”). After the Resolution, the seller/servicer may request that Fannie Mae review the appropriateness of the repurchase demand in light of the Resolution. Fannie Mae will withdraw the repurchase demand where appropriate.

examples: Repurchase demand issued while state AG lawsuit is pending on the same practice; seller waits until 30 days after final settlement before repurchasing.
Agency publicly states it is reviewing the issue; seller defers repurchase until 30 days after the agency’s final determination is issued.
After litigation is dismissed, seller requests Fannie Mae review; Fannie Mae withdraws demand where appropriate based on the resolution.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan repurchase demand

examples: Fannie Mae issues repurchase request due to ineligible loan attribute discovered in QC.
Fannie Mae issues make-whole request based on appraisal-related defect findings.

business_rules.rule_name: Repurchase demand appeals: 60 days first, 15 days second, 30 days impasse
business_rules.description: A lender may submit a written appeal of a repurchase/make-whole “demand.” The lender must submit a first appeal in writing within 60 days of receiving the demand, and Fannie Mae must respond in writing within 60 days of receipt. If the first appeal is denied and the lender has additional material information, the lender may submit a second appeal in writing within 15 days of receiving the denial; Fannie Mae must respond within 60 days. If challenging the existence of the defect after the first or second appeal, the lender must initiate the impasse process in writing within 15 days of receiving Fannie Mae’s denial; then Fannie Mae and the lender have 30 days to attempt to resolve (unless mutually extended).
business_rules.conditions: After Fannie Mae issues a demand and lender elects appeal/impasse processes
business_rules.consequences: Meeting timelines preserves appeal rights; missing deadlines risks losing escalation options
business_rules.exceptions: Fannie may specify a shorter or longer first-appeal period in the demand

source_reference.source_text: First Appeal: A lender may submit a written appeal of a demand.The lender must submit an appeal in writing within 60 days of receiving a demand. Note: Fannie Mae, in its discretion, may identify a shorter or longer appeal period in the demand... Fannie Mae must respond in writing to the lender’s appeal within 60 days of its receipt. Second Appeal: ... The lender must submit a second appeal in writing within 15 days of receiving a denial of the ﬁrst appeal... Impasse: ... The lender must initiate the impasse process in writing within 15 days of receiving Fannie Mae’s denial... Fannie Mae and the lender will have 30 days in which to attempt to resolve the dispute...

examples: Demand received 2026-02-01; lender must submit first appeal by 2026-04-02 (60 days). Fannie Mae must respond within 60 days of receiving the appeal.
First appeal denied 2026-05-15; lender has additional material information and must file second appeal by 2026-05-30 (15 days).
After denial, lender initiates impasse on day 20; it misses the 15-day impasse initiation deadline and risks losing that challenge route.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan repurchase demand

examples: Fannie Mae issues repurchase request due to ineligible loan attribute discovered in QC.
Fannie Mae issues make-whole request based on appraisal-related defect findings.

dependencies.rationale: BR_ISSUES_REPURCHASE_REQUEST_PROCESS_038_010 defines timelines to appeal a repurchase/make-whole demand. Without the issuance of a demand under BR_ISSUES_REPURCHASE_REQUEST_COMPLIANCE_008_012, there is no appealable event and no appeal clock to start.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the system does not anchor the appeal workflow to an issued demand date, the lender may miss the 60-day/15-day deadlines and lose escalation rights, forcing repurchase/make-whole without further process.

business_rules.rule_name: Sales Comparison Approach Must Include 3 Closed Comparables
business_rules.description: The appraisal report sales comparison approach must report a minimum of three closed comparable sales. Additional comparable sales may be included for support. Contract offerings and current listings may be included as supporting data when appropriate, but the minimum three must be closed comparables.
business_rules.conditions: Appraisal report uses the sales comparison approach to develop value
business_rules.consequences: If fewer than 3 closed comps, lender must obtain revision/new appraisal
business_rules.exceptions: Exceptions referenced for new/converted projects; still requires settled sales

source_reference.section_id: B4-1.3-08, Minimum Number of Comparable Sales
source_reference.source_text: A minimum of three closed comparables must be reported in the sales comparison approach. Additional comparable sales may be reported to support the opinion of market value provided by the appraiser. The subject property can be used as a fourth comparable sale or as supporting data if it was previously closed. Contract oﬀerings and current listings can be used as supporting data, if appropriate.

examples: Appraisal includes only two closed sales and one active listing; lender requires a revision to add a third closed comparable.
Appraisal includes three closed sales plus two active listings as support; meets the minimum requirement.
Subject property was a recent resale and is included as a fourth comparable; still must have three other closed comparable sales.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit minimum count and acceptable supporting data are stated.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Appraisal revision request; loan delivery suspense until cured

examples: Appraiser produces a UAD-compliant URAR with required exhibits.
Appraiser produces an appraisal report with comparable sales and reconciliation.

dependencies.rationale: BR_IS_SECURED_BY_ELIGIBILITY_036_010 sets a delivery-stage expectation to assess post-disaster property condition changes and determine if a new appraisal is needed. When that determination results in ordering a new appraisal, the appraisal report must comply with the appraisal content requirements such as BR_PRODUCES_APPRAISAL_REPORT_CONSTRAINT_009_001 (minimum three closed comps) to be acceptable for delivery decisions.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the lender obtains a post-disaster appraisal that does not meet minimum report standards, the disaster-condition clearance cannot be completed, delaying delivery and potentially causing rate lock extension costs and purchase deadline misses.

business_rules.rule_name: Comparable Distance Must Be Miles with Direction and Straight Line
business_rules.description: When describing the proximity of each comparable sale to the subject, the appraisal report must specify the distance in miles and include a directional indicator (e.g., “1.75 miles NW”). The distance between the subject property and each comparable must be measured using a straight line between the properties.
business_rules.conditions: Appraisal report describes proximity/location of comparable sales
business_rules.consequences: If distance method/format missing, lender must cure via appraisal update
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: When describing the proximity of the comparable sale to the subject property, the appraiser must be speciﬁc with respect to the distance in terms of miles and include the applicable directional indicator (for example, “1.75 miles NW”). The distance between the subject property and each comparable property is to be measured using a straight line between the properties.

examples: Comparable described as “nearby” without mileage/direction; lender requests correction to include miles and direction.
Comparable distance shown as “2 miles” without direction; revised to “2.0 miles SE” to comply.
Comparable distance measured as driving miles; must be revised to straight-line measurement.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit format and measurement method requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Appraisal revision request

examples: Appraiser produces a UAD-compliant URAR with required exhibits.
Appraiser produces an appraisal report with comparable sales and reconciliation.

business_rules.rule_name: New Condo Subdivision PUD Requires 1 Inside and 1 Outside Sale
business_rules.description: For a subject property in a new (or recently converted) condo project, subdivision, or PUD, the appraisal report must include at minimum: (1) at least one settled comparable sale from the subject project/subdivision/PUD, (2) at least one settled comparable sale from outside the subject project/subdivision/PUD, and (3) a third settled comparable sale that may be inside or outside.
business_rules.conditions: Subject is in a new or recently converted condo/subdivision/PUD project
business_rules.consequences: If mix not met, lender must obtain appraisal revision or new appraisal
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in cited bullet list

source_reference.section_id: B4-1.3-08, Additional Requirements for New (or Recently Converted) Condos, Subdivisions, or PUDS
source_reference.source_text: At a minimum, the appraisal report for these properties must include the following: At least one settled comparable sale from the subject condo project, subdivision, or PUD. (A resale is preferable if it is veriﬁable and does not involve the subject builder or developer). At least one settled comparable sale from outside the subject condo project, subdivision, or PUD. A third settled comparable sale can be from inside or outside of the subject condo project, subdivision, or PUD.

examples: New condo: appraisal uses three internal builder sales only; lender requires at least one settled sale outside the project.
New PUD: appraisal includes one resale inside the PUD, one sale in competing neighborhood, and one more inside; meets the minimum mix.
Recently converted condo: appraisal includes one inside settled sale, two outside settled sales; meets the minimum mix.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit minimum composition requirement with counts and inside/outside criteria.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Appraisal revision request; loan delivery suspense until cured

examples: Appraiser produces a UAD-compliant URAR with required exhibits.
Appraiser produces an appraisal report with comparable sales and reconciliation.

business_rules.rule_name: Comparable Concessions Must Be Reported or Explained with Addendum
business_rules.description: For comparable sales that include sales or financing concessions, the appraisal must report the dollar amount of seller-paid concessions for the comparable if the information is reasonably available. If the information is not available due to legal restrictions or disclosure-related problems, the appraiser must explain why it is not available. If the appraisal form lacks enough space, the appraiser must include the explanation in an addendum.
business_rules.conditions: Comparable sales include sales/financing concessions and data is obtainable
business_rules.consequences: Missing concession reporting/explanation triggers appraisal deficiency cure
business_rules.exceptions: Legal restrictions/disclosure problems allow explanation for unavailability

source_reference.source_text: The dollar amount of sales or ﬁnancing concessions paid by the seller must be reported for the comparable sales if the information is reasonably available (see UAD Appendix D: Field–Speciﬁc Standardization Requirements , for data entry instructions). Sales or ﬁnancing data should be obtained from parties associated with the comparable transaction, such as the broker, buyer or seller, or a reliable data source. If information is not available because of legal restrictions or other disclosure-related problems, the appraiser must explain why the information is not available.

examples: MLS shows seller paid $8,000 in closing costs for a comparable; report must disclose the dollar amount for that comparable.
Local law prevents disclosure of concession details; appraiser documents the legal restriction and explains the unavailability.
Form field space insufficient to explain disclosure issue; appraiser includes the explanation in an addendum.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Mandatory documentation requirement with explicit exception condition; no numeric thresholds.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Appraisal revision request

examples: Appraiser produces a UAD-compliant URAR with required exhibits.
Appraiser produces an appraisal report with comparable sales and reconciliation.

business_rules.rule_name: Comparable Concession Adjustments Cannot Be Positive and Must Be Market Based
business_rules.description: Adjustments for sales or financing concessions on comparable sales must reflect market reaction. The negative dollar adjustment for each comparable should equal the increase in the comparable’s price attributable to the concessions (as determined by the appraiser). Positive adjustments for sales or financing concessions are not acceptable. Dollar-for-dollar adjustments are acceptable only if the appraiser’s analysis concludes the market reaction equals the full amount of the concession.
business_rules.conditions: Comparable sale includes sales/financing concessions requiring adjustment
business_rules.consequences: If positive or non-market-based, appraisal must be corrected or replaced
business_rules.exceptions: Dollar-for-dollar is acceptable only if market reaction is full amount

source_reference.source_text: adjustments for sales or ﬁnancing concessions and the amount of the adjustments to the comparable sales is not based on how typical the concessions might be for a segment of the market area. Large

examples: Comparable sold for $410,000 with a $10,000 seller credit; analysis shows market inflated price by $6,000, so concession adjustment is -$6,000 (not -$10,000 by default).
Appraiser applies a +$5,000 adjustment because the comparable had no concessions; this is prohibited as a positive concession adjustment.
Market analysis supports full pass-through of a $7,500 concession; a -$7,500 dollar-for-dollar adjustment is acceptable.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Strong prohibitive language; numeric thresholds are not fixed but method is explicit.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Appraisal revision request; loan delivery suspense until cured

examples: Appraiser produces a UAD-compliant URAR with required exhibits.
Appraiser produces an appraisal report with comparable sales and reconciliation.

dependencies.rationale: BR_009_005 requires correct identification and treatment of sales/financing concessions on comparable sales. In purchase files, BR_031_002 requires providing the complete ratified sales contract and addenda to the appraiser, which typically contains concession details that the appraiser must analyze. Without the contract, the appraiser may miss or mischaracterize concessions, increasing probability of noncompliant positive or non-market-based adjustments.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_031_002 fails, the appraisal may omit or mis-handle concessions, triggering appraisal correction/replacement, delaying underwriting and UCDP submission, and increasing delivery defect risk if an incorrect value is delivered.

dependencies.rationale: BR_031_003 requires the lender to disclose specific financial information (credits, buydowns, subordinate financing terms, etc.) that can affect value. BR_009_005 governs how concessions must be adjusted (negative, market-reaction based; no positive adjustments). The appraiser cannot reliably apply BR_009_005 if the lender fails to disclose key concession/financing items.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Failure leads to flawed comparable adjustments, appraisal revisions, potential underwriting rework, and delivery risk due to unsupported value conclusions.

business_rules.rule_name: Appraisal must use actual site size, not 5 of 40 acres
business_rules.description: The appraisal must include the actual size of the site for the subject property and cannot appraise a hypothetical portion of the site. If the property is an unsubdivided parcel, the appraised value must reflect the entire parcel area (e.g., may not appraise only 5 acres of an unsubdivided 40-acre parcel).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when subject property includes an unsubdivided parcel with larger total acreage.
business_rules.consequences: Meets: appraisal acceptable; Violated: appraisal revision/new appraisal required; delivery risk.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.source_text: The appraisal must include the actual size of the site and not a hypothetical portion of the site for the subject property. For example, the appraiser may not appraise only 5 acres of an unsubdivided 40–acre parcel. The appraised value must reﬂect the entire 40–acre parcel.

examples: Subject property is a single residence on an unsubdivided 12-acre parcel; appraisal must value the full 12 acres, not a 1-acre homesite.
Property includes 40 acres with no legal subdivision; appraiser values only 5 acres—lender must require correction because the value must reflect all 40 acres.
Property is legally subdivided and the loan is secured by a specific lot; appraisal uses the actual lot size for that legal parcel—requirement met.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery suspended until appraisal corrected; possible repurchase if delivered
business_rules.extraction_notes: Direct must language with explicit acreage example; strong validation rule for appraisal review.

examples: Appraiser produces a UAD-compliant URAR with required exhibits.
Appraiser produces an appraisal report with comparable sales and reconciliation.

business_rules.rule_name: Manufactured home appraisal must use Form 1004C and exclude non-realty
business_rules.description: The appraiser must report a manufactured home appraisal on the Manufactured Home Appraisal Report (Form 1004C) and include required manufactured home data elements (e.g., manufacturer name, model, year, serial number, HUD certification numbers, foundation/utility connections, cost approach, site value). The appraiser must base the value conclusion solely on the real property as completed (home, site improvements, land) and must not include non-realty items such as insurance, warranties, or furniture.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to appraisals for manufactured housing delivered to Fannie Mae
business_rules.consequences: If met: appraisal form/content compliant; if violated: appraisal not acceptable
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: B4-1.4-01, Manufactured Housing Appraisal Requirements and Standards
source_reference.source_text: The appraiser must report the results of a manufactured home appraisal on the Manufactured Home Appraisal Report (Form 1004C ). The use of Form 1004C will help to ensure that the appraiser inspected, considered, and reported the appropriate information including, but not limited to, the: manufacturer’s name, trade or model number, year of manufacture, serial number, Certiﬁcation number(s) from the HUD Data Plate or HUD Certiﬁcation Label(s)... The appraiser must indicate a value conclusion based solely on the real property as completed... The value conclusion cannot include any non-realty items including, but not limited to, insurance, warranties, and furniture.

examples: Appraiser uses Form 1004C and reports HUD certification numbers and foundation/utility connections: compliant.
Appraisal adds $2,000 for furniture included in the sale: noncompliant because value conclusion cannot include non-realty items.
Appraiser submits Form 1004 (not 1004C) for a manufactured home: noncompliant appraisal form for manufactured housing.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan repurchase demand

examples: Appraiser produces a UAD-compliant URAR with required exhibits.
Appraiser produces an appraisal report with comparable sales and reconciliation.

dependencies.rationale: BR_024_009 requires providing the executed contract(s) and manufacturer invoice (if new) for manufactured housing purchase money mortgages, and requires the appraiser to analyze and summarize them in the report. BR_024_010 requires the manufactured home appraisal to include required manufactured home data elements and reflect real property only. The contract/invoice analysis is a key documentation input for a compliant 1004C package and credible value conclusion.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Missing documents often triggers appraisal deficiency conditions, revision requests, or appraisal rejection for delivery.

business_rules.rule_name: Project Ineligible for Unfunded Repairs Over $10,000 per Unit Within 12 Months
business_rules.description: A project is considered in need of critical repairs (and therefore ineligible) when it has unfunded repairs costing more than $10,000 per unit that should be undertaken within the next 12 months. This definition excludes repairs made by unit owners and repairs funded through a special assessment. If damage or deferred maintenance is isolated to one or a few units and does not affect overall safety/soundness/structural integrity/habitability, these requirements do not apply.
business_rules.conditions: Subject property is in a project evaluated under physical condition/critical repair criteria
business_rules.consequences: If threshold met, project is ineligible until remediation; otherwise may proceed
business_rules.exceptions: Does not apply if isolated to few units and no overall impact

source_reference.section_id: B4-2.1-03, Projects in Need of Critical Repairs
source_reference.source_text: Projects in need of critical repairs are those needing repairs or replacements that signiﬁcantly impact the safety, soundness, structural integrity or habitability of the project's building(s), or the ﬁnancial viability or marketability of the project. Critical repairs include conditions such as: ... any unfunded repairs costing more than $10,000 per unit that should be undertaken within the next 12 months (does not include repairs made by the unit owner or repairs funded through a special assessment). If damage or deferred maintenance is isolated to one or a few units and does not aﬀect the overall safety, soundness, structural integrity, or habitability of the project, then these requirements do not apply.

examples: Ineligible: 40-unit project needs roof replacement in 6 months; no reserves/financing; cost equals $12,500 per unit → exceeds $10,000 threshold.
Eligible (on this rule): Repairs are funded via a special assessment already approved and within guidelines, so the ‘unfunded repairs’ trigger is not met.
Exception applies: Water damage isolated to one unit only, with no impact to common structures and no broader habitability concern.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery rejection

examples: Subject property identified as a detached PUD unit with HOA dues.
Subject property identified as an attached PUD unit in a planned development.

business_rules.rule_name: Inspection Reports Within 3 Years Must Show No Critical Repairs or Evacuation Orders
business_rules.description: If a structural and/or mechanical inspection was completed within 3 years of the lender's project review date, the lender must obtain and review the inspection report. The report cannot indicate that any critical repairs are needed, no evacuation orders are in effect, and no regulatory actions are required. If the report indicates unaddressed critical repairs, the project is ineligible until repairs are completed and documented; the lender must review an engineer’s report (or substantially similar document) to confirm repairs resolved safety/soundness/structural integrity/habitability concerns.
business_rules.conditions: Project review performed and an inspection exists dated within prior 3 years
business_rules.consequences: If report clean, proceed; if critical repairs noted, project ineligible until cured
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in excerpt

source_reference.source_text: If a structural and/or mechanical inspection was completed within 3 years of the lender's project review date, the lender must obtain and review the inspection report. The report cannot indicate that any critical repairs are needed, no evacuation orders are in eﬀect, and no regulatory actions are required. If the inspection report indicates there are unaddressed critical repairs, the project is ineligible until the required repairs have been completed and documented accordingly. The lender must review an engineer's report or substantially similar document to determine if the repairs completed have resolved the safety, soundness, structural integrity, or habitability concerns of the project.

examples: Compliant: Inspection dated 18 months before review shows no critical repairs and no evacuation orders → lender retains and relies on report.
Ineligible: Inspection dated 2 years before review cites structural spalling requiring immediate repair; repairs not done → project ineligible.
Cure: Repairs completed; lender obtains engineer report confirming structural integrity restored → project may become eligible.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery rejection

examples: Subject property identified as a detached PUD unit with HOA dues.
Subject property identified as an attached PUD unit in a planned development.

dependencies.rationale: BR_011_008 requires the lender to review an inspection report and confirm it cannot indicate that any critical repairs are needed; if unaddressed critical repairs exist, the project is ineligible until cured. BR_011_007 defines a project in need of critical repairs (unfunded repairs > $10,000 per unit within 12 months with stated exclusions). The lender cannot consistently interpret BR_011_008 outcomes without BR_011_007’s definition.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Misclassification can lead to approving an ineligible project (delivery defect) or unnecessarily declining an eligible project (lost production).

business_rules.rule_name: 3- and 5-year ARM initial rate must be within 3% and margin ≤300 bps
business_rules.description: For a Fannie Mae ARM, the fully indexed rate is calculated as the applicable index value plus the mortgage margin, using any index value in effect during the 90 days preceding the note date, and rounded to the nearest one-eighth percent. To determine acceptability for purchase by Fannie Mae, subtract the initial note rate from the fully indexed rate in effect at origination; for 3-year and 5-year ARMs, the difference must not exceed 3%. Separately, the mortgage margin must be no more than 300 basis points. If the spread exceeds 3% or the margin exceeds 300 bps, the ARM terms are not acceptable for delivery.
business_rules.conditions: When originating a 3- or 5-year ARM intended for Fannie Mae purchase/securitization
business_rules.consequences: If difference ≤3% and margin ≤300 bps, ARM acceptable; otherwise ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: B2-1.4-02, Calculating the Fully Indexed Rate; Determining ARM Acceptability; Mortgage Margin
source_reference.source_text: The fully indexed rate is the sum of the value of the applicable index and the mortgage margin, which is then rounded to the nearest one-eighth percent. The applicable index value that determines the fully indexed rate is any index value in eﬀect during the 90 days that precede the note date. Lenders must determine whether an ARM loan is acceptable for purchase by Fannie Mae by subtracting the initial note rate of the loan from the fully indexed rate in eﬀect when the loan originated. For 3- and 5-year ARMs, the diﬀerence must not exceed 3%. The maximum mortgage margin may be no more than 300 basis points.

source_reference.section_id: B2-1.4-02, Determining ARM Acceptability; Mortgage Margin
source_reference.source_text: Lenders must determine whether an ARM loan is acceptable for purchase by Fannie Mae by subtracting the initial note rate of the loan from the fully indexed rate in eﬀect when the loan originated. For 3- and 5-year ARMs, the diﬀerence must not exceed 3%. ... The maximum mortgage margin may be no more than 300 basis points.

examples: 5-year ARM: fully indexed 7.125%, initial note 4.500% → difference 2.625% (≤3%): acceptable if margin ≤300 bps.
3-year ARM: fully indexed 6.750%, initial note 3.500% → difference 3.250% (>3%): not acceptable for purchase.
5-year ARM with margin 3.25% (325 bps): fails margin cap even if initial-rate difference is ≤3%.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for sale to Fannie Mae

examples: ARM loan references index and margin and defines periodic and lifetime caps.
Convertible ARM includes conversion indicator and related parameters.

deduplication_info.rationale: Both rules define the same ARM acceptability constraints for 3- and 5-year ARMs intended for Fannie Mae: (i) subtract initial note rate from fully indexed rate at origination, and the difference must not exceed 3%; and (ii) the mortgage margin must be ≤ 300 bps. BR_HAS_ARM_TERMS_CONSTRAINT_012_010 additionally specifies how to compute fully indexed rate (index value in effect during the 90 days preceding the note date and rounding to nearest 1/8%). These are not conflicting requirements; the second rule is a restatement of the same constraint set.
deduplication_info.primary_selection_reason: BR_HAS_ARM_TERMS_CONSTRAINT_012_010 selected as primary because it is more complete: it includes both the acceptability test (spread and margin caps) and the method details for fully indexed rate calculation (index lookback window and rounding), making it more directly testable.

dependencies.rationale: BR_038_007 defines how to calculate the fully indexed rate (index + margin, rounding rules, index value timing window). BR_012_010 requires that the lender subtract the initial note rate from the fully indexed rate at origination and evaluate the 3% cap for 3/5-year ARMs and the 300 bps margin cap. Accurate and compliant calculation of fully indexed rate is a prerequisite input.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If fully indexed rate is miscalculated, ARM eligibility determination may be wrong, causing ineligible delivery or inappropriate denial/restructure.

business_rules.rule_name: ARM fixed period must match 3-year 36 months and 5-year 60 months
business_rules.description: For standard conventional ARMs, the initial adjustment period in months must align with the initial fixed-rate period in years. Example requirements: a 3-year ARM must have an initial fixed period of 36 months; a 5-year ARM must have an initial fixed period of 60 months.
business_rules.conditions: When setting initial_fixed_period_months for a standard conventional ARM plan
business_rules.consequences: If aligned, ARM characteristic is acceptable; if misaligned, ARM terms are noncompliant
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: The initial adjustment period in months must align with the initial ﬁxed-rate period in years. For example, a "3-year ARM" must have an initial ﬁxed period of 36 months, and a "5-year ARM" must be 60 months.

examples: Loan disclosed as a 3-year ARM with initial_fixed_period_months = 36; it satisfies the alignment requirement.
Loan disclosed as a 5-year ARM with initial_fixed_period_months = 54; it violates the required 60-month fixed period.
Loan disclosed as a 7-year ARM with initial_fixed_period_months = 84; it follows the same alignment principle (7 years × 12 months).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery until corrected

examples: ARM loan references index and margin and defines periodic and lifetime caps.
Convertible ARM includes conversion indicator and related parameters.

business_rules.rule_name: Fully indexed rate equals index plus margin rounded to 1/8 using 90-day index
business_rules.description: The fully indexed rate is calculated as the applicable index value plus the mortgage margin, rounded to the nearest one-eighth percent. If the index plus gross margin is exactly equidistant between the higher and lower one-eighth percent, Fannie Mae rounds down. The applicable index value used for the fully indexed rate is any index value in effect during the 90 days preceding the note date.
business_rules.conditions: When calculating fully indexed rate at origination for initial note rate comparisons
business_rules.consequences: If calculated per rule, compliant fully indexed rate; if not, noncompliant rate calculation
business_rules.exceptions: Unless specific product terms provide otherwise (rounding rule caveat)

source_reference.section_id: B2-1.4-02, Calculating the Fully Indexed Rate
source_reference.source_text: The fully indexed rate is the sum of the value of the applicable index and the mortgage margin, which is then rounded to the nearest one-eighth percent. Note : Unless speciﬁc product terms provide otherwise, if the index plus gross margin equals a number that is equidistant between the higher and lower one-eighth percent, Fannie Mae rounds down to the nearest one-eighth percent. The applicable index value that determines the fully indexed rate is any index value in eﬀect during the 90 days that precede the note date.

examples: Index 4.983% + margin 2.250% = 7.233%; rounded to nearest 1/8% (0.125) gives 7.250% fully indexed rate.
Index 5.000% + margin 2.250% = 7.250% exactly; fully indexed rate remains 7.250%.
If note date is 2026-06-30, the selected index value must be an index value that was in effect during the 90 days preceding 2026-06-30.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery until corrected

examples: ARM loan references index and margin and defines periodic and lifetime caps.
Convertible ARM includes conversion indicator and related parameters.

business_rules.rule_name: Revised appraisal must be 100% Successful in UCDP before loan sale
business_rules.description: If an appraisal report is revised after the original, the final version that was used to make the underwriting decision must be submitted through UCDP and must receive a “Successful” status prior to sale of the loan to Fannie Mae. When submitting through UCDP, the lender must ensure the appraisal is the unaltered report submitted by the identified appraiser.
business_rules.conditions: Appraisal has subsequent revisions; final version used for underwriting decision
business_rules.consequences: Without UCDP Successful final version, loan must not be sold to Fannie Mae
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited passage

source_reference.section_id: B4-1.2-01, UCDP submission requirement for revised appraisal
source_reference.source_text: If there are subsequent revisions to the appraisal report, the ﬁnal version of the report that was utilized in making the underwriting decision must be submitted through the UCDP and receive a “Successful” status from the UCDP prior to sale of the loan to Fannie Mae. When submitting an appraisal report through the UCDP, lenders must ensure it is the unaltered report submitted by the identiﬁed appraiser.

examples: Appraisal updated after reconsideration of value; lender sells loan without resubmitting final version to UCDP: violates requirement and risks delivery rejection/repurchase.
Appraisal revised and resubmitted; UCDP returns “Successful” before delivery: meets the condition.
Lender alters PDF after appraiser signs (e.g., edits commentary) and submits: violates “unaltered report” requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery rejection or repurchase demand

examples: Lender submits appraisal file to UCDP and receives hard-stop messages.
Lender resubmits corrected appraisal after resolving UAD formatting errors.

dependencies.rationale: BR_013_001 requires that the final revised appraisal used for underwriting be submitted through UCDP and receive Successful prior to sale. If the appraiser is not properly licensed/active (BR_021_009), the final appraisal cannot be relied upon for delivery even if UCDP is Successful.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Final revised appraisal may need replacement, causing rework and sale delays; possible delivery defect if sold without curing.

dependencies.rationale: BR_031_005 sets the baseline requirement to submit required appraisal forms through UCDP and obtain 'Successful' prior to sale. BR_013_001 adds a controlling rule for revised appraisals: the final version used for underwriting must be the one submitted and Successful. Thus, in revised-appraisal scenarios, BR_013_001 supersedes the generic 'submit the appraisal' approach by specifying the correct version control requirement.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_013_001 is not followed, the lender may have a Successful status for an outdated version, leading to delivery defects and potential inability to sell until corrected.

business_rules.rule_name: Appraised value must match 100% between UCDP appraisal and loan sale
business_rules.description: For loans that require an appraisal, the lender must ensure that the appraised value shown on the appraisal submitted to UCDP exactly matches the appraised value reported at sale of the loan. If the underwriting appraisal is a desk or field review of an existing appraisal (and thus cannot be submitted to UCDP), the appraised value at sale must reflect the value in the desk/field review; however, the original appraisal that was reviewed must have been submitted to UCDP.
business_rules.conditions: Loan requires appraisal; appraisal data submitted to UCDP; value reported at sale
business_rules.consequences: If values do not match (or missing required original UCDP submission), delivery defect
business_rules.exceptions: Desk/field review appraisals cannot be submitted; sale value reflects review value

source_reference.section_id: B4-1.2-01, Appraised value consistency at UCDP and sale
source_reference.source_text: Additionally, for loans that require an appraisal, lenders must ensure the appraised value as indicated on the appraisal submitted to UCDP matches the appraised value as reported at sale of the loan. An exception is allowed for this requirement when the appraisal used to underwrite the loan is a desk or ﬁeld review of an existing appraisal because those types of reports cannot be submitted through the UCDP. In those instances, the appraised value reported at sale of the loan will reﬂect the value as stated in the desk or ﬁeld review. However, the original appraisal that was the subject of review must have been submitted to UCDP.

examples: UCDP appraisal value is $400,000 but loan delivery data reports $410,000: mismatch violates requirement; must correct delivery or resubmit correct appraisal.
Desk review concludes $395,000; desk review cannot be submitted to UCDP, so loan sale reports $395,000, and the original appraisal being reviewed was submitted to UCDP: meets exception handling.
Desk review used for underwriting but original appraisal was never submitted to UCDP: violates the ‘original appraisal must have been submitted’ requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery rejection or repurchase demand

examples: Lender submits appraisal file to UCDP and receives hard-stop messages.
Lender resubmits corrected appraisal after resolving UAD formatting errors.

dependencies.rationale: BR_013_002 explicitly requires that the appraised value shown on the appraisal submitted to UCDP exactly matches the appraised value reported at sale. That validation is impossible unless the appraisal was submitted to UCDP with a Successful status per BR_031_005 (or required original submission where a desk/field review is used).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_031_005 fails, BR_013_002 cannot be satisfied; the loan sale/delivery will have a defect or be blocked until UCDP is Successful and values are aligned.

dependencies.rationale: In revised appraisal scenarios, BR_013_001 dictates that the final underwriting version must be submitted and Successful. BR_013_002 then requires the value at sale to match the UCDP-submitted appraisal value. Without BR_013_001, the UCDP record could reflect an earlier value, making BR_013_002 mismatches likely.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Mismatch between UCDP value and delivered value creates delivery defects, potential purchase delays, and required resubmissions/curative actions.

business_rules.rule_name: UCDP Successful Status Required Before Sale 06/04/2025
business_rules.description: For conventional loans sold to Fannie Mae, the lender must submit required appraisal report forms (including all exhibits, addenda, and photographs) through UCDP and the submission must receive a “Successful” status prior to sale of the loan to Fannie Mae. Required forms listed include Form 1004, Form 1004 Desktop, Form 1004 Hybrid, Form 1004C, Form 1025, Form 1073, Form 1073 Hybrid, and Form 2090.
business_rules.conditions: When selling a conventional loan to Fannie Mae that requires listed appraisal forms
business_rules.consequences: If met: loan may be sold; if violated: sale is blocked until UCDP Successful
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: Uniform Collateral Data Portal (UCDP) The UCDP is a portal through which lenders are required to electronically submit appraisal reports for conventional loans sold to Fannie Mae. The following appraisal report forms including all exhibits, addenda, and photographs, must be submitted through the UCDP and receive a “Successful” status from the UCDP prior to sale of the loan to Fannie Mae: Uniform Residential Appraisal Report ( Form 1004 ), Uniform Residential Appraisal Report (Desktop) (Form 1004 Desktop ), Uniform Residential Appraisal Report (Hybrid) (Form 1004 Hybrid ) Manufactured Home Appraisal Report ( Form 1004C ), Small Residential Income Property Appraisal Report (Form 1025 ), Individual Condominium Unit Appraisal Report ( Form 1073 ), Individual Condominium Unit Appraisal Report (Hybrid) ( Form 1073 Hybrid ), and Individual Cooperative Interest Appraisal Report ( Form 2090 ).

examples: One-unit SFR conventional loan uses Form 1004; lender uploads full report with photos/addenda and obtains Successful before sale.
Manufactured home loan uses Form 1004C; UCDP must show Successful prior to sale to Fannie Mae.
Co-op loan uses Form 2090; lender cannot sell the loan until UCDP status is Successful.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan sale blocked / delivery rejection until Successful status

examples: Lender submits appraisal file to UCDP and receives hard-stop messages.
Lender resubmits corrected appraisal after resolving UAD formatting errors.

dependencies.rationale: BR_021_009 requires state-licensed/state-certified appraisers with active credentials as of the appraisal effective date. BR_031_005 requires submitting appraisals through UCDP with a Successful status prior to sale. A UCDP 'Successful' status does not guarantee licensing compliance; therefore licensing validation should occur before (or at least alongside) UCDP submission to prevent submitting an appraisal that will later be deemed unacceptable.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If licensing is invalid, the appraisal becomes unacceptable for delivery even if UCDP is Successful, forcing appraisal replacement, re-underwriting, and delaying sale.

dependencies.rationale: BR_024_010 requires manufactured home appraisals to be reported on Form 1004C with specified data elements. BR_031_005 lists Form 1004C among required forms to submit through UCDP for conventional loans sold to Fannie Mae. Therefore, for manufactured housing, producing the appraisal on the correct form is a gating condition for a correct UCDP submission set.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Incorrect form/content leads to appraisal not acceptable for delivery, UCDP re-submission, and sale delays.

dependencies.rationale: BR_010_001 defines an appraisal acceptability requirement (actual site size; cannot appraise a hypothetical portion). BR_031_005 is the UCDP submission requirement prior to sale. In practice, lenders should validate BR_010_001 before finalizing the appraisal package for UCDP submission to avoid late-stage appraisal rejection/revision cycles.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_010_001 fails, lender may need appraisal revision/new appraisal, causing UCDP resubmission and sale delays.

dependencies.rationale: BR_031_005 requires UCDP submission with 'Successful' status prior to sale. BR_005_010 prohibits delivery of a loan if any borrower is on OFAC sanctions lists. While neither rule is a strict computational prerequisite for the other, an automated sale readiness workflow should require both checks before allowing delivery.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_005_010 is not checked alongside BR_031_005, a loan might satisfy appraisal/UCDP requirements but still be prohibited from delivery due to OFAC, leading to last-minute sale stoppage, repurchase risk, or sanctions exposure.

dependencies.rationale: BR_023_009 governs how square footage must be measured/reported (ANSI Z765-2021) and requires noting alternate standards if mandated. BR_031_005 requires submitting the appraisal report used for sale. A lender should not submit a report for delivery if it is known to be defective on a core property characteristic like GLA, because that triggers appraisal revision, underwriting changes, and potential delivery defects.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_023_009 fails, the lender may still submit to UCDP but later face appraisal defect findings, requiring revisions and re-submission, delaying sale and potentially changing LTV/eligibility.

business_rules.rule_name: Loan Delivery Purchase Prohibited Until Fatal Edits Cleared
business_rules.description: Loans may only be submitted and purchased in Loan Delivery if they are clear of all fatal edits. Loans remain subject to fatal edits throughout submission, documentation certification, and acquisition. If a fatal edit is encountered, it must be resolved for Fannie Mae to acquire the loan.
business_rules.conditions: Seller/Servicer submits loan data through Loan Delivery for purchase or securitization
business_rules.consequences: If clear, loan can proceed to acquisition; if fatal edits exist, Fannie Mae will not acquire the loan
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: C1-2-02, Electronic Submission via Loan Delivery
source_reference.source_text: Loans may only be submitted and purchased in Loan Delivery if they are clear of all fatal edits. Loans are subject to all fatal edits in Loan Delivery throughout the submission, loan documentation certiﬁcation and acquisition purchase process. If a loan encounters a fatal edit, the fatal edit must be resolved for Fannie Mae to acquire the loan.

examples: Seller submits ULDD data and receives a fatal edit for missing required field; loan cannot be purchased until corrected.
A loan passes initial edits but later triggers a fatal edit during documentation certification; seller must resolve before acquisition.
Seller resolves fatal edits and resubmits; loan becomes eligible for purchase in Loan Delivery.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan purchase rejection / acquisition hold

examples: Approved Seller/Servicer originates a fixed-rate purchase loan intended for delivery.
Seller/Servicer originates an ARM subject to ARM policy requirements.

dependencies.rationale: BR_015_009 requires that loans be clear of all fatal edits to be acquired through Loan Delivery. BR_031_005 requires UCDP Successful prior to sale. In operational delivery workflows, missing/failed UCDP submissions are a frequent source of delivery blocking conditions, meaning BR_031_005 is typically required to satisfy BR_015_009 for appraisal-required loans.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_031_005 fails, lender may hit fatal edits or delivery blocks; acquisition cannot proceed until cured.

business_rules.rule_name: Post Purchase LLPA Repricing Limited to 18 Months and $100
business_rules.description: For Seller/Servicer-initiated post-purchase adjustments, data corrections must be submitted within 18 months of the loan’s acquisition date to be considered for repricing of LLPAs. A minimum $100 per-loan threshold (credit or debit) is required. The 18-month lookback period does not apply to drafting or collection of repurchase alternative fees. Fannie Mae may adjust the minimum dollar amount and lookback period in its sole discretion.
business_rules.conditions: Seller/Servicer discovers discrepancies in delivered data and seeks LLPA repricing post-acquisition
business_rules.consequences: If within 18 months and ≥$100, repricing may be considered; otherwise, repricing not considered
business_rules.exceptions: 18-month lookback does not apply to repurchase alternative fees drafting/collection

source_reference.source_text: considered for repricing of loan-level price adjustments (LLPAs). A minimum $100 requirement per loan (credit or debit) is also required. The 18-month lookback period does not apply to the drafting or collection of repurchase alternative

examples: Seller discovers wrong occupancy code 9 months after acquisition causing LLPA impact of $250; submits correction—eligible for repricing consideration.
Seller submits a correction 20 months after acquisition for a $400 LLPA issue; beyond 18 months—repricing is not considered.
Seller submits a correction within 18 months but LLPA delta is $75; below $100 minimum—repricing not considered under the stated threshold.
business_rules.enforcement_action: LLPA repricing denial

examples: Approved Seller/Servicer originates a fixed-rate purchase loan intended for delivery.
Seller/Servicer originates an ARM subject to ARM policy requirements.

business_rules.rule_name: Student loan cash-out refinance cash back limit is greater of 1% or $2,000
business_rules.description: For a student loan cash-out refinance, borrower cash back at closing must not exceed the greater of (i) 1% of the new refinance loan amount or (ii) $2,000. The lender may also refund overpayment of fees/charges due to law/regulation or apply a principal curtailment.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the refinance is structured as a student loan cash-out refinance.
business_rules.consequences: If cash back exceeds the greater-of limit, the transaction fails program requirements and is not compliant for delivery as such.
business_rules.exceptions: Refunds of overpaid fees/charges due to law or principal curtailment are permitted.

source_reference.section_id: B2-1.3-04 (text preceding), Requirements for Student Loan Cash-out Refinances
source_reference.source_text: The borrower may receive cash back in an amount that does not exceed the greater of 1% of the new reﬁnance loan amount or $2,000. The lender may also refund the borrower for the overpayment of fees and charges due to federal or state laws or regulations, or apply a principal curtailment (see B2-1.3-02, Limited Cash-Out Reﬁnance Transactions for additional information). Unless otherwise stated, all other standard cash-out reﬁnance requirements apply.

examples: New refinance loan amount $300,000: 1% is $3,000, so max cash back is $3,000; $3,100 cash back would violate the rule.
New refinance loan amount $150,000: 1% is $1,500, so max cash back is $2,000; $2,000 cash back is compliant.
Borrower receives $1,800 cash back plus a $250 refund of overpaid recording fee required by state law: refund is permitted per the rule text.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Delivery ineligibility / purchase refusal

examples: Approved Seller/Servicer originates a fixed-rate purchase loan intended for delivery.
Seller/Servicer originates an ARM subject to ARM policy requirements.

business_rules.rule_name: Limited cash-out refinance is ineligible if prior cash-out note date is within 30 days
business_rules.description: A transaction is not eligible as a limited cash-out refinance if the borrower completed a cash-out refinance transaction with a note date 30 days or less prior to the application date of a new refinance secured by the same property. This is treated as circumvention of cash-out refinance policies and pricing.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to new refinance applications secured by the same property as a recent cash-out refinance.
business_rules.consequences: If prior cash-out note date is ≤ 30 days before new application date, the new refinance cannot be treated as limited cash-out (and is ineligible as such).
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.source_text: eligible as a limited cash-out reﬁnance if the borrower completed a cash-out reﬁnance transaction with a note date 30 days or less prior to the application date of a new reﬁnance secured by the same property. Published March 4, 2026

examples: Cash-out refinance note date is June 1; new refinance application date is June 25 on same property: 24 days, so limited cash-out is ineligible.
Cash-out refinance note date is June 1; new refinance application date is July 2 on same property: 31 days, so this specific prohibition does not apply.
Cash-out refinance note date is May 15; new refinance application date is June 14 (30 days) on same property: limited cash-out is ineligible.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Delivery ineligibility / repurchase risk if mischaracterized

examples: Approved Seller/Servicer originates a fixed-rate purchase loan intended for delivery.
Seller/Servicer originates an ARM subject to ARM policy requirements.

business_rules.rule_name: Payoff within 120 days can trigger reimbursement of premium or buyup proceeds
business_rules.description: For any mortgage loan that pays off within 120 days from the whole loan purchase date or the MBS issue date, Fannie Mae may require the seller/servicer to reimburse any premium paid or buyup proceeds paid in connection with the purchase. For loans repurchased by a seller/servicer, Fannie Mae may require reimbursement in its sole discretion without regard to the 120-day limitation.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to loans purchased/securitized by Fannie Mae that pay off within 120 days.
business_rules.consequences: If payoff occurs within 120 days, seller/servicer may owe reimbursement; repurchased loans may owe reimbursement even beyond 120 days.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: B2-1.3-04, Agreements to Advance Borrower Payments
source_reference.source_text: With respect to any mortgage loan that pays oﬀ within 120 days from the whole loan purchase date or the MBS issue date, Fannie Mae in its sole discretion may require reimbursements by the seller/servicer for any premium paid or buyup proceeds paid in connection with the purchase of the mortgage loan. (For mortgage loans repurchased by a seller/servicer, Fannie Mae may require reimbursement in its sole discretion, without regard to the 120-day limitation.)

examples: Loan purchased as a whole loan on Jan 1 pays off Apr 15 (104 days): Fannie Mae may require reimbursement of any premium/buyup proceeds.
MBS issued Feb 1; loan prepays May 25 (113 days): reimbursement may be required based on premium/buyup proceeds paid.
Loan is repurchased by seller 10 months after purchase: Fannie Mae may still require reimbursement regardless of the 120-day limitation.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Reimbursement demand for premium/buyup proceeds

examples: Approved Seller/Servicer originates a fixed-rate purchase loan intended for delivery.
Seller/Servicer originates an ARM subject to ARM policy requirements.

business_rules.rule_name: Whole Loan Funding Requires Clean Data by 9:00 p.m. ET
business_rules.description: For whole loan deliveries of regularly amortizing mortgages, Fannie Mae will fund purchase proceeds only after: (1) the lender has electronically submitted clean, error-free loan delivery data through Loan Delivery by 9:00 p.m. Eastern time; (2) the document custodian has received a complete and accurate mortgage document submission package by first morning delivery the following day; and (3) the document custodian has certified the package.
business_rules.conditions: Whole loan delivery of a regularly amortizing mortgage for purchase by Fannie Mae
business_rules.consequences: If all steps met, proceeds funded and status becomes Purchased and Funded; if not, funding delayed
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: C2-2-04 (Timing of Distribution of Proceeds for Regularly Amortizing Mortgages)
source_reference.source_text: Fannie Mae will fund the purchase proceeds for whole loan deliveries of regularly amortizing mortgages provided the following procedure has already occurred: lender has electronically submitted clean, error-free loan delivery data through Loan Delivery by 9:00 p.m. (Eastern time), the document custodian has received a complete and accurate mortgage document submission package from a lender by ﬁrst morning delivery the following day, and the document custodian has certiﬁed the mortgage document submission package.

examples: Seller submits clean delivery data at 8:45 p.m. ET, custodian receives and certifies package next morning; Fannie Mae funds purchase proceeds.
Seller submits delivery data at 9:05 p.m. ET; procedure not met for same-cycle funding and proceeds may be delayed.
Custodian receives package but cannot certify due to missing docs; proceeds are not funded until the package is complete and certified.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Funding delay / purchase processing delay

examples: Approved Seller/Servicer originates a fixed-rate purchase loan intended for delivery.
Seller/Servicer originates an ARM subject to ARM policy requirements.

dependencies.rationale: BR_034_007 requires 'clean, error-free loan delivery data' and custodian certification before funding. BR_015_009 states that fatal edits must be resolved for Fannie Mae to acquire the loan. If fatal edits exist, the delivery data cannot be considered clean/error-free and acquisition/funding is blocked.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Funding does not occur; purchase is delayed; rate lock/hedge exposure and operational escalations increase.

business_rules.rule_name: Late Delivery Data Delays Funding to Next Business Day
business_rules.description: If the required delivery data and documentation are not received by the applicable cutoff time, Fannie Mae will disburse whole loan purchase proceeds on the business day following the date it receives the required materials, assuming the materials are in order and the mortgage is eligible for purchase.
business_rules.conditions: Required delivery data/docs missed the cutoff time for whole loan funding
business_rules.consequences: Funding shifts to the next business day after receipt; unresolved issues can further delay purchase
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: C2-2-04 (Impact of Late Submissions of Required Data and Documentation)
source_reference.source_text: If the required delivery data and documentation are not received by the applicable cutoﬀ time, Fannie Mae will disburse the purchase proceeds on the business day following the date it receives these materials, assuming they are in order and the mortgage is eligible for purchase.

examples: Custodian package arrives one day late; Fannie Mae disburses proceeds the business day after receiving the complete, in-order package.
Data arrives after cutoff and contains errors; disbursement does not occur until corrected materials are received and in order.
Delivery materials arrive on Friday after cutoff; disbursement occurs Monday (next business day) if eligible and in order.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Funding delay / purchase processing delay

examples: Approved Seller/Servicer originates a fixed-rate purchase loan intended for delivery.
Seller/Servicer originates an ARM subject to ARM policy requirements.

dependencies.rationale: BR_034_008 only applies if required delivery data/documentation are not received by the cutoff described in BR_034_007. Therefore, the evaluation of BR_034_008 depends on the timing outcome relative to BR_034_007 conditions.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_034_007 cutoff is missed, funding shifts to the next business day after receipt; operational timelines and settlement expectations change.

business_rules.rule_name: Post-Purchase Adjustment Request Due Within 30 Days
business_rules.description: The lender must submit a post-purchase adjustment request to notify Fannie Mae of any error, omission, or irregularity in the transfer of funds or repetitive transfer processing within 30 days of the date of the Purchase Advice; otherwise, Fannie Mae will assume the Purchase Advice information is correct. The request must include the lender’s nine-digit seller/servicer number, the ten-digit Fannie Mae loan number, the Post-Purchase Adjustment Request form, and applicable documentation.
business_rules.conditions: Lender identifies a funds transfer error/omission after receiving Purchase Advice
business_rules.consequences: If request submitted within 30 days, Fannie reviews/adjusts; if late, Purchase Advice deemed correct
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: C2-2-05 (Reconciliation of Purchase Advices and After Sale Corrections)
source_reference.source_text: The lender must submit a post-purchase adjustment request to provide notice of any error, omission, or irregularity in the transfer of funds or in processing the request for the repetitive transfer within 30 days of the date of the Purchase Advice. Otherwise, Fannie Mae will assume that the information on its Purchase Advice is correct. The request must include the following: the lender’s nine-digit Fannie Mae seller/servicer number, the ten-digit Fannie Mae loan number, the Post-Purchase Adjustment Request form, and applicable documentation.

examples: Purchase Advice dated 06/01: lender discovers wire was credited to wrong internal account; submits adjustment request by 07/01 with required identifiers and docs.
Lender submits adjustment request 45 days after Purchase Advice date; Fannie Mae may assume Purchase Advice is correct due to missed 30-day window.
Lender submits within 30 days but omits the Post-Purchase Adjustment Request form; request is incomplete and may not be processed until corrected.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Adjustment denied due to timeliness / funding reconciliation risk

examples: Approved Seller/Servicer originates a fixed-rate purchase loan intended for delivery.
Seller/Servicer originates an ARM subject to ARM policy requirements.

dependencies.rationale: BR_034_009 requires action within 30 days of the Purchase Advice date to correct funds transfer errors/omissions. Purchase Advice is associated with a purchased/funded loan; BR_034_007 defines the conditions for reaching 'Purchased and Funded' status. Operationally, BR_034_007 must occur before BR_034_009 can be triggered.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If purchase/funding does not occur, there is no Purchase Advice timeline to act upon; if funding is delayed, the adjustment window shifts accordingly.

business_rules.rule_name: ARM delivery prohibited when adjustment litigation exists
business_rules.description: For ARM loans, the loan being delivered must not be subject to any current litigation with respect to how the interest rate and/or payment adjustments were calculated or implemented. Additionally, the lender must not be servicing other ARMs with adjustment provisions similar to the mortgage being sold to Fannie Mae that are the subject of current litigation related to the manner in which adjustments were made.
business_rules.conditions: Seller/Servicer delivers an ARM loan to Fannie Mae
business_rules.consequences: If no relevant litigation, delivery can proceed; if litigation exists, delivery barred
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: Requirements Regarding Interest Rate and Monthly Payment Adjustments The following requirements apply to interest rate and monthly payment adjustments for ARM loans: The loan being delivered must not be subject to any current litigation with respect to the manner in which the interest rate and/or payment adjustments were calculated or implemented. The lender must not be servicing other ARMs that include interest rate and payment adjustment provisions similar to those of the mortgage being sold to Fannie Mae that are the subject of current litigation related to the manner in which adjustments were made.

examples: ARM has an active lawsuit alleging improper index application for past adjustments; loan may not be delivered to Fannie Mae.
Lender is being sued on another ARM product with similar adjustment provisions; lender must not deliver a similar ARM to Fannie Mae while litigation is current.
No litigation exists regarding adjustment mechanics; ARM can be delivered if all other eligibility criteria are met.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for purchase by Fannie Mae

examples: Approved Seller/Servicer originates a fixed-rate purchase loan intended for delivery.
Seller/Servicer originates an ARM subject to ARM policy requirements.

dependencies.rationale: BR_039_010 is an ARM acceptability constraint for purchase by Fannie Mae. BR_035_003 prohibits delivery of ARM loans subject to relevant litigation (or similar ARM provisions in litigation in the servicer’s portfolio). For ARM delivery workflows, both must be checked; eligibility depends on satisfying both dimensions (terms and litigation status).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Loan may be structurally eligible but still undeliverable due to litigation exposure; delivery must stop or loan must be held/redirected.

business_rules.rule_name: Occupying borrower must fund first 5% down payment
business_rules.description: For manually underwritten loans, when income of a guarantor, co-signer, or non-occupant borrower is used for qualifying, the occupying borrower(s) must make the first 5% of the down payment from their own funds. This 5% own-funds minimum contribution is not required if (i) the LTV or CLTV ratio is ≤ 80%, or (ii) the occupying borrower is purchasing a one-unit principal residence and meets requirements to use gifts, donated grant funds, or employer funds for some or all of the borrower’s minimum contribution.
business_rules.conditions: Manual underwrite; non-occupant income used to qualify; principal residence
business_rules.consequences: If met, down payment source compliant; if not, loan ineligible for delivery
business_rules.exceptions: Exception if LTV/CLTV ≤80% or eligible 1-unit gift/grant/employer funds

source_reference.section_id: B2-2-04, Down Payment and Qualifying Ratio Requirements for Manually Underwritten Loans
source_reference.source_text: For manually underwritten loans, if the income of a guarantor, co-signer, or non-occupant borrower is used for qualifying purposes, the occupying borrower(s) must make the ﬁrst 5% of the down payment from their own funds unless: the LTV or CLTV ratio is less than or equal to 80%; or the occupying borrower is purchasing a one-unit principal residence and meets the requirements to use gifts, donated grant funds, or funds received from an employer to pay for some or all of the borrower’s minimum contribution.

examples: Purchase 95% LTV principal residence: non-occupant co-borrower income used; occupying borrower must contribute first 5% down payment from own verified funds.
Purchase 80% LTV: non-occupant co-borrower income used; 5% own-funds requirement does not apply due to LTV/CLTV ≤80%.

One-unit principal residence at 97% LTV where gifts are permitted and borrower meets gift requirements; gift/grant/employer funds may satisfy minimum contribution instead of occupying borrower own funds.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Clear 5% requirement with explicit exception conditions (≤80% LTV/CLTV, or 1-unit with gift/grant/employer assistance).
business_rules.enforcement_action: loan ineligible for sale to Fannie Mae

examples: Co-borrower is added to support qualifying income for DTI.
Non-occupant co-borrower joins the application to strengthen credit profile.

dependencies.rationale: Both rules apply to manually underwritten loans. BR_024_002 sets minimum credit score thresholds for eligibility (620 fixed/640 ARM). BR_026_004 imposes down payment own-funds requirements when using non-occupant income. In practice, lenders must first confirm the loan clears baseline manual underwriting eligibility, including credit score floors, before evaluating conditional overlays like down payment sourcing tied to non-occupant income.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_024_002 fails, any work to structure BR_026_004 compliance is wasted; loan is ineligible for delivery.

business_rules.rule_name: Manual underwrite DTI capped at 43% using occupier income
business_rules.description: For manually underwritten loans that use a guarantor, co-signer, or non-occupant borrower income for qualifying, the lender must calculate the DTI ratio using only the income of the occupying borrower(s). Based on that calculation, the maximum allowable DTI ratio is 43%, even if the combined qualifying ratios for all borrowers are below Fannie Mae’s standard qualifying ratio benchmark.
business_rules.conditions: Manual underwrite; non-occupant income present; DTI computed on occupier income only
business_rules.consequences: If DTI ≤43% (occupier-only), compliant; if >43%, loan ineligible for delivery
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: B2-2-04, Down Payment and Qualifying Ratio Requirements for Manually Underwritten Loans
source_reference.source_text: Using only the income of the occupying borrower(s) to calculate the DTI ratio, the maximum allowable DTI ratio is 43%. Note : This policy applies even if the combined qualifying ratios for the borrower and the guarantor, co-signer, or non-occupant borrower are well below Fannie Mae’s standard qualifying ratio benchmark.

examples: Occupying borrower income $6,000/month and debts $2,700/month: DTI = 45%; exceeds 43% cap and is not eligible under this manual underwriting policy.
Occupying borrower income $6,000/month and debts $2,400/month: DTI = 40%; compliant even if non-occupant has additional income.
Combined DTI including non-occupant income would be 30%, but occupier-only DTI is 44%; still fails because the rule requires occupier-only DTI ≤43%.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit DTI cap (43%) and explicit instruction to use only occupying borrower income are stated.
business_rules.enforcement_action: loan ineligible for sale to Fannie Mae

examples: Co-borrower is added to support qualifying income for DTI.
Non-occupant co-borrower joins the application to strengthen credit profile.

dependencies.rationale: BR_026_005 applies to manually underwritten loans using non-occupant/guarantor income and caps occupier-only DTI at 43%. Even if the DTI overlay is satisfied, the loan remains ineligible if BR_024_002 credit score floors are not met.
