dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_024_002 fails, loan is ineligible regardless of BR_026_005 DTI compliance; underwriting resources are misallocated.

dependencies.rationale: BR_026_004 governs minimum occupying-borrower own-funds contribution, while BR_026_005 governs DTI calculation and cap using occupier-only income. They are separate requirements triggered by the same fact pattern and jointly determine deliverability for that structure.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Failing either results in ineligibility; checking only one gives a false sense of compliance completeness.

business_rules.rule_name: Manual non-occupant max LTV 90% (CLTV 105% with CS)
business_rules.description: For manually underwritten loans, when income of a guarantor, co-signer, or co-borrower is used for qualifying and that party will not occupy the subject property, the maximum LTV, CLTV, and HCLTV ratios may not exceed 90%. If a Community Seconds is part of the transaction, the CLTV ratio may not exceed 105% where permitted in the Eligibility Matrix.
business_rules.conditions: Manual underwrite; non-occupant guarantor/co-signer/co-borrower income used
business_rules.consequences: If ratios within limits, eligible; if exceeded, loan not eligible for delivery
business_rules.exceptions: Community Seconds allows CLTV up to 105% where Eligibility Matrix permits

source_reference.section_id: B2-2-04, LTV Ratio Requirements for Manually Underwritten Loans
source_reference.source_text: For manually underwritten loans, if the income of a guarantor, co-signer, or co-borrower is used for qualifying purposes, and that guarantor, co-signer, or co-borrower will not occupy the subject property, the maximum LTV, CLTV, and HCLTV ratio may not exceed 90% (unless a Community Seconds is part of the transaction, in which case the CLTV ratio may not exceed 105% where permitted in the Eligibility Matrix .

examples: Manual underwrite purchase: LTV 92% with non-occupant co-borrower income used; fails because maximum is 90%.
Manual underwrite with Community Seconds: first mortgage 95% LTV and CLTV 104%; acceptable if Eligibility Matrix permits and CLTV ≤105%.
Manual underwrite refinance: CLTV 106% with Community Seconds; fails because CLTV may not exceed 105% even when Community Seconds is present.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit max 90% and explicit conditional exception to 105% CLTV with Community Seconds are stated.
business_rules.enforcement_action: loan ineligible for sale to Fannie Mae

examples: Co-borrower is added to support qualifying income for DTI.
Non-occupant co-borrower joins the application to strengthen credit profile.

dependencies.rationale: BR_026_006 caps LTV/CLTV/HCLTV for manual underwrite non-occupant income scenarios. BR_024_002 sets minimum credit score floors for manual underwriting eligibility. Baseline eligibility is required before ratio overlays can result in a deliverable loan.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_024_002 fails, loan is ineligible no matter how low the LTV is.

dependencies.rationale: BR_026_004 addresses minimum own-funds down payment contribution in certain scenarios; BR_026_006 imposes maximum LTV/CLTV/HCLTV caps (with Community Seconds exception). Together they form a combined eligibility test path for that loan structure.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Loan can fail due to leverage caps even if down payment sourcing passes, or vice versa; workflow should prompt both checks.

business_rules.rule_name: Appraiser License Must Be Active on Effective Date and Documented
business_rules.description: The lender (or its authorized agent) must use appraisers or supervisory appraisers that are state-licensed or state-certified, must document that the appraisers used are appropriately licensed/certified under state law, and must ensure the license/certification is active as of the effective date of the appraisal report. The appraiser must note their license or certification number on the appraisal report forms in compliance with UAD requirements.
business_rules.conditions: When selecting/using an appraiser for a Fannie Mae-eligible appraisal report
business_rules.consequences: If met, appraisal is compliant; if violated, appraisal is unacceptable for delivery
business_rules.exceptions: None stated (trainee rules addressed separately in same section)

source_reference.source_text: Fannie Mae requires a lender (or its authorized agent) to use appraisers or supervisory appraisers that are state-licensed or state-certified (in accordance with the provisions of Title XI of the Financial Institutions Reform, Recovery and Enforcement Act of 1989 and all applicable state laws). The lender (or its authorized agent) must document that the appraisers it uses are licensed or certified as appropriate under the applicable state law. The lender must ensure that the state license or state certification is active as of the effective date of the appraisal report. The appraiser must note their license or certification number on the individual appraisal report forms

examples: Appraisal effective date is 2026-04-01; appraiser license expired 2026-03-31—lender must not use this appraisal for delivery.
Lender’s file includes evidence of state certification status at engagement and confirms it was active on effective date—compliant.
Appraiser omits license number on the report; lender must obtain corrected report before delivery.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Appraisal rejection; potential repurchase exposure if delivered noncompliant

examples: Lender selects an appraiser from an approved panel meeting independence standards.
Lender reassigns an appraiser due to credential or independence concerns.

business_rules.rule_name: Manufactured home purchase: provide executed contracts and new invoice to appraiser
business_rules.description: For purchase money mortgages involving manufactured housing, the lender must provide the appraiser a complete copy of the executed contract for sale of the manufactured home and land (or separate executed contracts if purchased separately), and must provide a copy of the manufacturer’s invoice if the manufactured home is new. The appraiser must analyze these documents and summarize them in the appraisal report.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to purchase money mortgages secured by manufactured housing
business_rules.consequences: If met: appraiser can analyze contract/invoice; if violated: appraisal/file deficient
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: B4-1.4-01, Manufactured Housing Appraisal Requirements and Standards
source_reference.source_text: For purchase money mortgages, the lender must provide the appraiser with a complete copy of the executed contract for sale of the manufactured home and land; or a complete copy of the executed contract for both, if the manufactured home and land are purchased separately; and a copy of the manufacturer’s invoice if the manufactured home is new. The appraiser must analyze the contract(s) and the manufacturer’s invoice for new manufactured homes, and provide a summary in the appraisal report.

examples: Purchase of manufactured home and land in one contract: lender sends executed contract to appraiser before appraisal completion.
Home and land purchased under two contracts: lender provides both executed contracts to appraiser for analysis.
New manufactured home: lender provides manufacturer invoice; appraiser summarizes invoice details in the appraisal report.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery refusal

examples: Lender selects an appraiser from an approved panel meeting independence standards.
Lender reassigns an appraiser due to credential or independence concerns.

business_rules.rule_name: B4-1.1-05 Requires Full Property Disclosure to Appraiser
business_rules.description: The lender must disclose to the appraiser all information about the subject property that the lender is aware of. The appraiser must then determine whether the disclosed information could affect the marketability of the property or the opinion of market value.
business_rules.conditions: When ordering/engaging an appraisal for a subject property
business_rules.consequences: If met: appraiser can analyze impacts; if violated: appraisal credibility and delivery risk
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: All information about the subject property the lender is aware of must be disclosed to the appraiser and the details provided must comply with B4-1.1-04, Unacceptable Appraisal Practices . The appraiser must determine if the information could aﬀect either the marketability of the property or the opinion of the market value of the property.

examples: Lender learns from agent about an unpermitted addition; lender discloses it to the appraiser for marketability/value consideration.
Borrower reports prior water intrusion; lender discloses to appraiser so condition and stigma can be analyzed.
Lender is aware of nearby environmental hazard (e.g., known contamination) and must disclose to appraiser.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Post-purchase remedy (make-whole/repurchase) based on defect severity

examples: Lender selects an appraiser from an approved panel meeting independence standards.
Lender reassigns an appraiser due to credential or independence concerns.

business_rules.rule_name: B4-1.1-05 Provide Ratified Contract to Appraiser
business_rules.description: For purchase transactions, the lender must provide, or ensure the appraiser is provided with, a copy of the complete, ratified sales contract and all addenda for the property to be appraised, as the contract typically contains financing data and sales concessions that must be disclosed.
business_rules.conditions: Purchase transactions where a sales contract exists for the subject property
business_rules.consequences: If met: appraiser can analyze concessions/terms; if violated: appraisal review/delivery defects
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: All ﬁnancing data and sales concessions for the subject property that will be or have been granted by anyone associated with the transaction must be disclosed to the appraiser, as appropriate. Typically, this information is provided in the sales contract. Therefore, the lender must provide, or ensure that the appraiser is provided with, a copy of the complete, ratiﬁed sales contract and all addenda for the property that is to be appraised.

examples: Purchase contract shows $10,000 seller credit and personal property inclusion; lender provides full ratified contract and addenda to appraiser.
Contract has multiple addenda (price reduction, repair addendum); lender ensures appraiser receives all addenda.
Lender only sends a partially executed contract; appraisal review flags missing ratified contract/addenda and lender must cure.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Appraisal condition/cure; potential delivery ineligibility

examples: Lender selects an appraiser from an approved panel meeting independence standards.
Lender reassigns an appraiser due to credential or independence concerns.

business_rules.rule_name: B4-1.1-05 Disclose Buydowns and Subordinate Financing
business_rules.description: On purchase transactions, if applicable, the lender must disclose to the appraiser specific financial information items impacting value, including settlement charges, loan fees/charges, discounts to sales price, interest rate buydowns, below-market-rate financing, terms of subordinate financing provided by interested parties, credits/refunds of borrower expenses, absorption of monthly payments, assignment of rent payments, and any other information that impacts property value.
business_rules.conditions: Purchase transactions with applicable financing terms/concessions/credits affecting value
business_rules.consequences: If met: appraiser considers value impacts; if violated: value opinion may be flawed
business_rules.exceptions: Only items that are applicable to the transaction must be disclosed

source_reference.source_text: The list below includes items that must be disclosed to the appraiser on purchase transactions, if applicable: settlement charges, loan fees or charges, discounts to the sales price, interest rate buydowns, below-market-rate ﬁnancing, terms of any subordinate ﬁnancing provided by interested parties, credits or refunds of borrower expenses, absorption of monthly payments, assignment of rent payments, and any other information not listed above that impacts property value.

examples: Seller provides a 2-1 temporary buydown and pays $8,000 closing costs; lender discloses to appraiser for value/market reaction.
Interested party provides subordinate financing at below-market terms; lender discloses terms to appraiser.
Rent assignment is part of transaction structure; lender discloses to appraiser because it may affect value opinion.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Post-purchase remedy exposure if value impacted by undisclosed terms

examples: Lender selects an appraiser from an approved panel meeting independence standards.
Lender reassigns an appraiser due to credential or independence concerns.

business_rules.rule_name: Manual underwrite minimum 620 fixed and 640 ARM credit score
business_rules.description: For manually underwritten loans, the minimum credit score that applies for loan eligibility is 620 for fixed-rate loans and 640 for ARMs. To determine the loan-level credit score for eligibility: use the representative credit score for loans with one borrower; use the average median credit score for loans with more than one borrower.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to manually underwritten loans when determining eligibility credit score
business_rules.consequences: If met: meets minimum score floor; if violated: loan ineligible for delivery
business_rules.exceptions: Certain transactions not subject to minimum (see Guide exceptions section)

source_reference.source_text: To determine the credit score that applies for loan eligibility, use the following: • Loans with one borrower - representative credit score, • Loans with more than one borrower - average median credit score. The minimum credit score that applies for loan eligibility is: 620 — ﬁxed-rate loans 640 — ARMs

examples: One borrower, fixed-rate, representative score 621: meets minimum 620.
Two borrowers with median scores 680 and 620: average median is 650; meets ARM minimum 640 if product is ARM.
One borrower fixed-rate with representative score 615: fails minimum; loan ineligible unless a stated exception applies.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery refusal

examples: Application indicates non-U.S. citizen status, triggering specific eligibility documentation requirements.
Application indicates multiple financed properties, triggering additional eligibility and reserve considerations.

dependencies.rationale: BR_024_002 requires establishing the loan-level credit score (representative or average median) and meeting minimum scores for manual underwriting. BR_024_003 defines when a traditional credit report is acceptable even if 1-2 repositories have no data, provided a tri-merge was requested and at least one repo has data. Without BR_024_003, the credit score inputs could be rejected or misapplied.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If credit report acceptability is not established, credit score determination can be invalid, leading to incorrect eligibility outcomes or needing to shift to nontraditional credit requirements.

dependencies.rationale: BR_024_004 is an eligibility prohibition tied to credit report availability under freezes. BR_024_002 sets minimum credit score thresholds and scoring methodology for manual underwriting. If BR_024_004 renders the loan ineligible (two+ frozen repos), BR_024_002 cannot be meaningfully applied for eligibility. If one repo frozen, BR_024_004 dictates the data sufficiency (two repositories with data) needed to compute a valid loan-level score.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_024_004 is violated, the loan cannot be sold to Fannie Mae regardless of credit score; proceeding wastes underwriting resources and creates compliance risk if delivered.

business_rules.rule_name: Tri-merge credit report OK with 1 bureau data if requested
business_rules.description: For borrowers who have traditional credit, a credit report is acceptable even if one or two repositories do not contain any credit information, provided credit data is available from one repository and the lender requested a three in-file merged report.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when borrower has traditional credit but missing bureau data
business_rules.consequences: If met: credit report acceptable; if violated: follow nontraditional credit rules
business_rules.exceptions: If not met, refer to Nontraditional Credit History requirements

source_reference.source_text: If one or two of the credit repositories do not contain any credit information for the borrowers who have traditional credit, the credit report is still acceptable as long as credit data is available from one repository, and the lender requested a three in-ﬁle merged report. If the transaction does not meet the above requirements, refer to Section B3–5.4, Nontraditional Credit History...

examples: Borrower has credit at Experian only; lender requested tri-merge: report acceptable because 1 repository has credit data.
Borrower has credit at Equifax only; lender requested a single-bureau report: not acceptable under this rule; must meet nontraditional credit requirements.
Two borrowers: one has traditional credit at one bureau, other has full tri-merge; lender requested tri-merge: acceptable for the borrower with one bureau data.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery refusal

examples: Application indicates non-U.S. citizen status, triggering specific eligibility documentation requirements.
Application indicates multiple financed properties, triggering additional eligibility and reserve considerations.

business_rules.rule_name: Frozen credit at 2+ bureaus makes loan ineligible
business_rules.description: If a borrower’s credit is frozen at one repository (traditional credit), the credit report is acceptable only if credit data is available from two repositories and the lender requested a three in-file merged report. Loans for borrowers with credit data frozen at two or more repositories are not eligible whether underwritten manually or in DU.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when borrower has a credit freeze at one or more repositories
business_rules.consequences: If violated: loan is not eligible for sale to Fannie Mae (manual or DU)
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: If the borrower’s credit information is frozen at one of the credit repositories for borrowers who have traditional credit, the credit report is still acceptable as long as credit data is available from two repositories, and the lender requested a three in-ﬁle merged report. Loans for borrowers with credit data frozen at two or more of the credit repositories will not be eligible whether underwritten manually or in DU.

examples: Borrower freezes Experian; Equifax and TransUnion provide data; tri-merge requested: acceptable under this rule.
Borrower freezes Equifax and TransUnion: loan is ineligible whether manual underwrite or DU.
Two borrowers: one has freezes at two bureaus while the other has full credit; loan is still ineligible due to the frozen-credit borrower.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery refusal

examples: Application indicates non-U.S. citizen status, triggering specific eligibility documentation requirements.
Application indicates multiple financed properties, triggering additional eligibility and reserve considerations.

business_rules.rule_name: HomeStyle may escrow up to 6 PITIA payments for uninhabitable PR
business_rules.description: At the borrower’s request, up to six mortgage payments (PITIA) that become due during the renovation period may be included as part of total renovation costs for a principal residence when the property cannot be occupied during renovation. The lender/servicer/agent must hold the funds in a renovation escrow account (or separate escrow) and apply them only to payments due during the period the property cannot be occupied.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to HomeStyle principal residence when property cannot be occupied
business_rules.consequences: If met: PITIA funds controlled in escrow; if violated: misuse of funds; noncompliance
business_rules.exceptions: Separate escrow account may be used in lieu of renovation escrow account

source_reference.source_text: At the borrower’s request, up to six mortgage payments (PITIA) that will become due during the renovation period may be included as part of the total renovation costs for a principal residence property if the property cannot be occupied during the renovation period. The lender, servicer, or its agent must hold the funds in a renovation escrow account, and only apply them to payments that come due during the period in which the property cannot be occupied. Note: The lender may set up a separate escrow account for the mortgage payments...

examples: Borrower requests payment escrows for a 4-month renovation: lender escrows 4 PITIA payments and applies them only during non-occupancy.
Borrower requests 7 PITIA payments: lender limits escrow to 6 payments per Guide.
Lender holds PITIA funds in a separate escrow account instead of the renovation escrow account: permitted by Note.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan repurchase demand

examples: Loan requires escrow for property taxes and homeowners insurance.
Loan includes an escrow waiver only where permitted and properly documented.

business_rules.rule_name: Renovation draws only by joint check or consented wire each time
business_rules.description: Renovation escrow funds may only be disbursed by (1) a check issued jointly to the borrower and the contractor, or (2) a wire transfer to the contractor after the lender obtains written borrower consent prior to each disbursement. Each consent must specify borrower name, property address, amount, contractor, and date of consent.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to HomeStyle Renovation escrow disbursements of renovation funds
business_rules.consequences: If met: compliant draw controls; if violated: improper disbursement; delivery risk
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: Renovation funds may only be disbursed using the following processes: a check issued jointly to the borrower and the contractor; or • a wire transfer to the contractor after the lender has obtained written consent from the borrower to release the funds to the contractor. The written consent must be received prior to each disbursement. It must specify the borrower’s name, property address, amount of funds to be disbursed, contractor to which the funds are to be disbursed, and date of the consent.

examples: Draw #2 released via joint check payable to borrower and contractor after work completion verified: compliant.
Lender wires $15,000 to contractor after receiving a signed consent listing borrower name, property address, amount, contractor, and date: compliant.
Lender wires funds based on a single blanket authorization signed at closing (no per-draw consent): violates the rule.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan repurchase demand

examples: Loan requires escrow for property taxes and homeowners insurance.
Loan includes an escrow waiver only where permitted and properly documented.

dependencies.rationale: BR_024_006 requires that PITIA funds included in renovation costs be held in a renovation escrow account and applied only to payments due during non-occupancy. BR_024_007 then restricts how renovation escrow funds may be disbursed (joint check or contractor wire with borrower consent). Without establishing the escrow structure and use restrictions, the disbursement controls are not properly scoped or enforceable.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Improper escrow setup/use leads to noncompliance, potential misuse of funds, and delivery risk for HomeStyle transactions.

business_rules.rule_name: Prefunding QC Sample Minimum 10% or 750 Loans
business_rules.description: Each month, the lender’s prefunding QC monthly loan selection must be at least the lesser of (a) 10% of prior month total loans closed/acquired or 10% of current month projected loans closed/acquired (with reconciliation required if projections are used), or (b) 750 loans. If the lender did not close/acquire at least 10 loans in the prior month, it must select at least 1 loan for the monthly prefunding QC review.
business_rules.conditions: When establishing the monthly prefunding QC sample for loans prior to closing/acquisition
business_rules.consequences: If met, QC sampling minimum satisfied; if violated, QC plan is noncompliant
business_rules.exceptions: If prior month total loans <10, sample must include at least 1 loan

source_reference.source_text: Each month, the lender must complete a minimum number of prefunding QC reviews. The monthly loan selection must equal, at a minimum, the lesser of: 10% of the prior month's total number of loans closed or acquired, or 10% of the current month's projected total number of loans to be closed or acquired (if using projections, the lender must perform a reconciliation process to ensure the 10% requirement is met); or 750 loans. If the lender does not close or acquire at least 10 loans in the prior month, the lender must select at least one loan for its monthly prefunding QC review.

examples: Prior month closed 1,200 loans; current month projected 1,000. Minimum sample is lesser of 10% (120 or 100) vs 750 ⇒ 100 loans.
Prior month closed 9 loans; lender must review at least 1 loan in the current month prefunding QC sample.
Current month projected 10,000 loans; 10% is 1,000, but cap is lesser of 1,000 vs 750 ⇒ 750 loans must be reviewed.
business_rules.enforcement_action: N/A

examples: Seller/Servicer maintains a QC program with post-closing sampling and defect tracking.
Seller/Servicer updates QC procedures following policy changes in appraisal requirements.

business_rules.rule_name: QC Reverification Records Must Be Retained for 3 Years
business_rules.description: All post-closing QC reverification documentation must be retained for at least three years in the loan file or lender QC records. If a vendor performs reverification services, the lender must be provided or have access to written results and documentation for at least three years. The lender must provide reverification documentation to Fannie Mae upon request.
business_rules.conditions: Loans subject to post-closing QC reverification and documentation is obtained
business_rules.consequences: If retained, lender can evidence compliance; if not, QC defect and enforcement risk
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: All reverification documentation must be retained for at least three years in either the loan file or in the lender's QC records. If the lender uses a vendor to perform reverification services, the lender must be provided, or have access to, the written results and documentation, for at least three years. The lender must provide reverification documentation to Fannie Mae upon request.

examples: QC vendor performs VOE and transcript checks; lender stores vendor results in QC system for 3+ years and can retrieve on demand.
Lender deletes QC reverification results after 18 months; upon Fannie request, lender cannot produce them—noncompliant.
QC documentation is stored in the loan file rather than QC records; retention still satisfies the rule if kept 3+ years.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Potential repurchase/indemnification exposure; QC finding escalation

examples: Seller/Servicer maintains a QC program with post-closing sampling and defect tracking.
Seller/Servicer updates QC procedures following policy changes in appraisal requirements.

dependencies.rationale: BR_037_010 requires obtaining a new tri-merge credit report for QC reverification (when traditional credit used). BR_021_010 requires retaining post-closing QC reverification documentation for at least three years and providing it to Fannie Mae upon request. The retention obligation is triggered by the reverification activity and its outputs.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If retention fails, lender cannot evidence QC compliance during audits/reviews, increasing enforcement and remedies risk.

business_rules.rule_name: Post-closing QC cycle completed within 90 days
business_rules.description: Loans must be selected for post-closing QC reviews at least monthly. The entire post-closing QC cycle—selection, review, rebuttal, and reporting—must be completed within 90 days from the month of loan closing. If the QC cycle is in arrears by more than one 30-day cycle, the lender must provide written notice to its Fannie Mae customer account team or QC Specialist.
business_rules.conditions: Post-closing QC program for loans closed in a given month
business_rules.consequences: If timely, QC meets timing standard; if late, written notice is required
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: D1-3-01, Timing of QC Review Process
source_reference.source_text: Loans must be selected for post-closing QC reviews on at least a monthly basis. The entire QC cycle (selection, review, rebuttal, and reporting) must be completed within 90 days from the month of the loan closing. Note : If the lender's QC cycle is in arrears more than one 30-day cycle, the lender must provide written notice to its Fannie Mae customer account team or QC Specialist.

examples: Loans closed in January are selected monthly; selection/review/rebuttal/reporting are finished by April 30 to satisfy the 90-day completion requirement.
Lender finishes February QC cycle in June (more than one 30-day cycle in arrears); lender must send written notice to its Fannie Mae account team/QC Specialist.
Lender selects loans quarterly instead of monthly; violates the monthly selection requirement and triggers QC process noncompliance.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit monthly cadence, 90-day completion window, and arrears trigger language are directly stated.
business_rules.enforcement_action: N/A

examples: Seller/Servicer maintains a QC program with post-closing sampling and defect tracking.
Seller/Servicer updates QC procedures following policy changes in appraisal requirements.

dependencies.rationale: BR_026_001 requires monthly selection and completion of the post-closing QC cycle within 90 days. BR_026_002 specifies the required selection volume and methodology when the 10% method is used, including separate retail vs third-party sampling. Thus, for the 10% method, BR_026_002 is a gating input to the BR_026_001 cycle’s selection step.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If sampling is below minimums, the QC cycle can be timely but still noncompliant due to under-selection; lender may face QC findings and corrective action demands.

dependencies.rationale: BR_026_001 mandates monthly selection and completion timing of the QC cycle. BR_026_003 governs how statistical sampling must be computed (95% confidence, 2% precision, six-month statement) and documented. Therefore, for lenders using statistical sampling, BR_026_003 is prerequisite logic for compliant monthly selection under BR_026_001.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Noncompliant statistical sampling undermines the QC program even if reviews are performed; may require re-selection, re-review, and reporting corrections.

business_rules.rule_name: Random post-closing QC sample minimum 10% monthly
business_rules.description: If the lender uses the 10% random sampling method, it must select for post-closing QC review a minimum of 10% of the loans it originates using a random selection methodology. If the lender originates or acquires loans from a third party, it must conduct separate QC reviews for retail and third-party originations—sampling at least 10% of retail originations and at least 10% of third-party acquired loans.
business_rules.conditions: Post-closing QC random sampling method is “10% sample”
business_rules.consequences: If ≥10% sampled per channel, sampling meets standard; if <10%, QC noncompliance
business_rules.exceptions: If 10% < 1 loan, at least 1 must be selected (handled in related rule)

source_reference.source_text: The lender must select for its post-closing QC review a minimum of 10% of the loans that it originates using a random selection methodology. Lenders who originate or acquire loans from a third-party must conduct a separate review for both its retail and third-party originations. Speciﬁcally, the lender must sample at least 10% of its retail originations and sample at least 10% of loans acquired through third-party originations.

examples: Lender originated 200 retail loans in March and acquired 50 third-party loans; it must randomly sample at least 20 retail and at least 5 third-party loans.
Lender samples 15 of 200 retail loans (7.5%); fails the minimum 10% requirement and must increase the sample size.
Lender mixes retail and third-party into one 10% pool without separate channel sampling; violates the separate review requirement.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Multiple explicit 10% minimum requirements, including separate channel minimums, are directly stated.
business_rules.enforcement_action: N/A

examples: Seller/Servicer maintains a QC program with post-closing sampling and defect tracking.
Seller/Servicer updates QC procedures following policy changes in appraisal requirements.

business_rules.rule_name: Statistical QC sampling 95% confidence and 2% precision
business_rules.description: If the lender uses statistical sampling instead of the 10% method, the statistical sampling model variables must be calculated using at least a 95% confidence level with a 2% precision rate and a statistical statement of six months (Fannie Mae recommends three months). The lender must document the statistical sampling methodology and provide detailed written justification to Fannie Mae upon request; Fannie Mae may require adjustments based on its review.
business_rules.conditions: Post-closing QC uses statistical sampling methodology
business_rules.consequences: If model meets parameters, sampling acceptable; if not, must revise/justify and adjust
business_rules.exceptions: None stated (3-month statement is recommendation, not requirement)

source_reference.source_text: Statistical Sample If the lender uses statistical sampling for its selection process, at a minimum, the statistical sampling model (variables) must be calculated using a 95% conﬁdence level with a 2% precision rate and a statistical statement of six months. (Fannie Mae recommends

examples: Lender implements statistical sampling at 95% confidence, 2% precision, using a 6-month lookback; compliant with minimum requirements.
Lender uses a 90% confidence level and 5% precision; noncompliant and must recalibrate to at least 95%/2%.
Fannie Mae requests the lender’s sampling documentation; lender must provide written justification and may be required to modify the model.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit statistical parameters (95%, 2%, six months) and documentation/adjustment provisions are stated.
business_rules.enforcement_action: N/A

examples: Seller/Servicer maintains a QC program with post-closing sampling and defect tracking.
Seller/Servicer updates QC procedures following policy changes in appraisal requirements.

business_rules.rule_name: QC 4506-C Code 10 ends transcript attempts; Codes 1-9 require correction
business_rules.description: During post-closing QC, if the lender attempts to execute Form 4506-C but is unable to obtain a transcript due to IRS rejection Code 10 ('Due to Limitations, the IRS is unable to process this request'), the lender does not need to take further steps to attempt to obtain a transcript. For issues other than rejection Code 10 (including missing/incomplete/illegible/altered information Codes 1-9), the lender should attempt to obtain a corrected and signed Form 4506-C; if the lender chooses not to get the corrected form or is unable to obtain it, the lender must have a process to cite a moderate defect and implement a corrective action plan where appropriate.
business_rules.conditions: When post-closing QC includes tax transcript reverification via Form 4506-C
business_rules.consequences: Code 10 permits stopping; other codes require correction or moderate defect + corrective action
business_rules.exceptions: Code 10 specifically allows no further transcript attempt after rejection

source_reference.source_text: the transcript request is rejected by the IRS with a Code 10 indicating "Due to Limitations, the IRS is unable to process this request"; ... rejected because of missing, incomplete, illegible, or altered information on the Form 4506-C (Codes 1-9)... If the lender attempts to execute Form 4506-C with the IRS but is unable to obtain a transcript due to receiving a rejection Code 10, the lender does not need to take further steps to attempt to obtain a transcript. For issues other than rejection Code 10, the lender should attempt to obtain a corrected and signed Form 4506-C... it must have a process to cite a moderate defect and implement a corrective action plan, where appropriate.

examples: QC requests transcript and IRS returns Code 10 → lender documents result and stops further transcript attempts.
QC request rejected due to borrower name mismatch (Code 2) → lender obtains corrected/signed 4506-C and retries transcript request.
Code 5 rejection and borrower will not re-sign 4506-C → lender cites moderate defect and opens corrective action plan per QC process.
business_rules.enforcement_action: QC moderate defect citation and corrective action plan; potential sanctions if systemic

examples: Seller/Servicer maintains a QC program with post-closing sampling and defect tracking.
Seller/Servicer updates QC procedures following policy changes in appraisal requirements.

business_rules.rule_name: Post-closing QC requires new tri-merge 3-bureau credit report
business_rules.description: Post-closing QC must include reverification of the borrower’s credit history. If the borrower’s credit was evaluated using a traditional credit report, the lender must reverify by obtaining a new tri-merge credit report. The new report does not need to generate a new inquiry that will appear on future credit reports or include trended credit data, even if trended data was reflected on the underwriting report. The lender is not required to analyze trended credit data in the new report.
business_rules.conditions: When post-closing QC reviews a loan underwritten with a traditional credit report
business_rules.consequences: If new tri-merge is not obtained, QC reverification requirement is unmet and defect may be cited
business_rules.exceptions: No new inquiry required; no trended data required; no trended analysis required

source_reference.source_text: Credit History The post-closing QC review must include reveriﬁcation of the borrower's credit history. If a borrower's credit was evaluated by using a traditional credit report, the lender must reverify the borrower's credit history by obtaining a new tri-merge credit report. The new report does not need to generate a new inquiry... or include trended credit data... The lender is not required to analyze trended credit data in the new credit report.

examples: Underwriting used a tri-merge report dated pre-closing; QC obtains a new tri-merge report post-closing without a new inquiry → compliant.
QC file contains only the original underwriting credit report and no new tri-merge → reverification requirement not met; cite defect per QC taxonomy.
Original underwriting report included trended credit; QC’s new tri-merge omits trended data and lender does not analyze trends → compliant per rule.
business_rules.enforcement_action: QC defect citation; corrective action plan; potential additional reporting/sanctions

examples: Seller/Servicer maintains a QC program with post-closing sampling and defect tracking.
Seller/Servicer updates QC procedures following policy changes in appraisal requirements.

dependencies.rationale: BR_026_001 establishes that post-closing QC reviews occur monthly and must complete within 90 days. BR_037_010 specifies that post-closing QC must include reverification of borrower credit history (new tri-merge) when traditional credit was used. Thus, performing BR_037_010 depends on running the QC cycle that selects and reviews loans.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If QC cycle is not operating timely, required credit reverification may be missed, producing QC defects and potential enforcement actions.

business_rules.rule_name: QC Document Request Response Due Within 30 Days
business_rules.description: When Fannie Mae selects a loan for underwriting, servicing, or data validation review and requests documentation, the lender must send the requested documentation within 30 days after notification, unless Fannie Mae specifies a shorter or longer period based on circumstances. If the lender delays, Fannie Mae may require indemnification or repurchase; extensive patterns of delays/unresponsiveness may be considered a breach of contract leading up to termination.
business_rules.conditions: After Fannie Mae notifies lender that a loan is selected for QC review
business_rules.consequences: If met: review proceeds; if violated: indemnification/repurchase and possible termination
business_rules.exceptions: Fannie Mae may set shorter/longer timeline; may work with extenuating circumstances

source_reference.source_text: Lenders must send the requested documentation for an underwriting, servicing, or data validation review within 30 days after Fannie Mae notiﬁes the lender that it has selected a loan for review. Fannie Mae, in its sole discretion, may request the documentation in a shorter or longer period of time based upon circumstances at the time. However, if a lender delays in providing the requested information, Fannie Mae, in its sole discretion, may require indemniﬁcation or repurchase... up to and including termination.

examples: Fannie Mae requests the underwriting file on April 1; lender provides all requested documents by May 1 (within 30 days).
Fannie Mae specifies a 10-day response due to urgent review; lender must meet the shorter timeframe.
Lender repeatedly fails to respond within timelines across multiple QC selections; Fannie Mae treats as breach and may terminate.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Indemnification, repurchase demand, or termination

examples: Fannie Mae performs a post-purchase QC review and identifies an underwriting documentation defect.
Fannie Mae performs a review focused on appraisal compliance and UCDP submission results.

dependencies.rationale: BR_031_009 requires sending requested documentation within 30 days of notification. BR_021_010 requires retaining QC reverification documentation for three years and providing it upon request. While BR_031_009 can sometimes be met by reconstructing documents, retention under BR_021_010 materially enables compliance with the response deadline.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If retention fails, lender may miss the 30-day window, increasing indemnification/repurchase risk and potential breach-of-contract findings.

business_rules.rule_name: QC must confirm all DU verification messages are resolved and documented
business_rules.description: For loans underwritten through DU, the post-closing QC reviewer must confirm that all DU Verification Messages/Approval Conditions in the DU Underwriting Findings report were satisfactorily resolved and adequately supported by appropriate documentation. If DU returned an Ineligible recommendation, the reviewer must confirm the loan was eligible for delivery to Fannie Mae.
business_rules.conditions: Applies during post-closing QC file review of DU-underwritten loans.
business_rules.consequences: If conditions not supported/resolved, loan may be ineligible and subject to remedies.
business_rules.exceptions: Ineligible may be acceptable only under specified DU circumstances (see B3-2).

source_reference.section_id: D1-3-02 (06/04/2025), Review of DU Findings and Conditions
source_reference.source_text: For loans underwritten through DU, the lender must conﬁrm that all DU Veriﬁcation Messages/Approval Conditions that appear in the DU Underwriting Findings report were satisfactorily resolved and adequately supported by appropriate documentation. If DU returned an Ineligible recommendation, the reviewer must conﬁrm that the loan was eligible for delivery to Fannie Mae.

examples: DU requires verification of employment; QC file contains compliant VOE and condition is marked satisfied—rule met.
DU returns Ineligible due to data issue; QC reviewer confirms corrected eligibility basis and documentation supports delivery eligibility per applicable guidance.
QC finds DU verification message unresolved and no supporting documentation; loan is flagged for reassessment and potential self-report/remedy.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Potential repurchase demand or other remedies

examples: QC review examines file for required appraisal exhibits and UCDP acceptance.
QC review checks file for borrower eligibility documentation and DU findings.

dependencies.rationale: BR_019_001 applies during post-closing QC review of DU-underwritten loans (confirm DU conditions resolved/supported). BR_026_001 defines the monthly selection and 90-day completion requirements for the post-closing QC cycle. Without a functioning QC cycle, BR_019_001 cannot be systematically performed.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Missing QC file review results in QC findings, potential delivery exposure if DU conditions were unresolved, and possible repurchase/indemnification risk.

business_rules.rule_name: QC review must reassess underwriting when verified data differs from underwriting data
business_rules.description: During post-closing QC, the lender must review the final terms of the loan to ensure they align with the data used to support underwriting decisions. If QC verification identifies discrepancies between underwriting data and QC-verified data, the lender must reassess the underwriting decision using the newly verified information to determine whether the loan remains eligible as delivered to Fannie Mae.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when QC verifies data and finds discrepancies vs underwriting inputs/terms.
business_rules.consequences: Requires underwriting reassessment; may lead to ineligibility finding and self-report.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in excerpt.

source_reference.source_text: The lender must review the ﬁnal terms of the loan to ensure they align with the data used to support the underwriting decisions. When a lender's review identiﬁes discrepancies between the data used in underwriting and the data veriﬁed through the QC process, the lender must reassess the underwriting decision based on the newly veriﬁed information to determine whether the loan remains eligible as delivered to Fannie Mae.

examples: QC verifies a higher monthly debt than underwritten; lender reassesses DTI and confirms whether loan remains eligible as delivered.
QC verifies occupancy intent documentation differs from application; lender reassesses eligibility and delivery status based on verified facts.
QC finds final loan terms differ from those used in underwriting; lender reassesses the underwriting decision using the final verified terms.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Potential repurchase demand or other remedies

examples: QC review examines file for required appraisal exhibits and UCDP acceptance.
QC review checks file for borrower eligibility documentation and DU findings.

dependencies.rationale: BR_019_002 requires review of final terms vs underwriting data and reassessment if discrepancies are found. This is an activity inside the QC review process, which is organized by BR_026_001 (selection, review, rebuttal, reporting within 90 days).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If QC cycle is not run, discrepancies are not identified and underwriting is not reassessed, increasing undetected ineligible deliveries.

dependencies.rationale: In a DU post-closing QC review, confirming DU conditions are satisfied (BR_019_001) and confirming final terms align with underwriting inputs with reassessment if discrepancies exist (BR_019_002) together create a more complete QC validation. They should be navigated as linked checks within the same QC review workflow.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If only one is performed, QC may miss either unfulfilled DU conditions or post-closing data drift that changes eligibility.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_PROHIBITION_035_008 selected as primary because it is more complete: it includes the same prohibition plus clarifying that certain covenants may be permitted/excepted under the FHFA regulation, and it frames the requirement directly as a purchase/securitization prohibition (most actionable).
duplicate_groups.rationale: Both rules implement the same business logic: except as permitted by the FHFA Private Transfer Fee Regulation (12 CFR Part 1228), Fannie Mae will not purchase/securitize loans secured by properties encumbered by private transfer fee covenants created on or after 2011-02-08, and lenders must maintain controls to avoid delivering such loans. Numeric trigger date and the regulatory carve-out are identical; context (property-level eligibility for Fannie Mae acquisition) and consequence (loan ineligible) align. Differences are only phrasing and category label (compliance vs prohibition).
duplicate_groups.merged_description: Except as permitted by the FHFA Private Transfer Fee Regulation (12 CFR Part 1228), Fannie Mae will not purchase or securitize loans secured by properties encumbered by private transfer fee covenants created on or after February 8, 2011. Lenders must maintain policies and procedures/controls to ensure delivered loans are not secured by properties with private transfer fee covenants that are unacceptable under the regulation. If an unacceptable covenant exists, the loan is ineligible for purchase/securitization.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_ELIGIBILITY_039_009 selected as primary because it is slightly clearer/cleaner: it explicitly states the eligibility range (zones 3–9) and the ineligibility trigger (zones 1–2) in a single concise rule statement.
duplicate_groups.rationale: Both rules state the identical geographic hazard eligibility requirement for the island of Hawaii: properties in lava zones 3–9 are eligible; lava zones 1–2 are ineligible due to heightened lava-flow destruction risk. All numeric zone thresholds and context match; consequences match (loan/property ineligible). The wording differences do not change the business logic.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: Mortgage loans secured by properties located on the island of Hawaii are eligible for purchase/securitization by Fannie Mae only if the property is located in lava zones 3 through 9. Properties in lava zones 1 and 2 are not eligible due to increased risk of destruction from lava flows.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROFILE_CONSTRAINT_035_005 selected as primary because it is the most complete: it includes the same six-month age test, explicitly states the measurement points for whole loans vs MBS, includes an illustrative example, and includes the HomeStyle Renovation exception with SFC 279 and the extended timing.
duplicate_groups.rationale: Both rules define the same flow purchase eligibility aging constraint: loan age must be ≤ 6 months, measured from first payment date to Purchase Ready date (whole loans) or to MBS pool issue date (MBS loans), with the same explicit HomeStyle Renovation exception permitting sale up to 15 months after note date when delivered after renovation completion with SFC 279. Numeric thresholds (6 months, 15 months) and context (flow purchase eligibility) are identical; consequences align (flow-ineligible if exceeded).
duplicate_groups.merged_description: To be eligible for purchase by Fannie Mae on a flow basis, a loan must be no more than six months old measured from the first payment date to (i) the Purchase Ready date for whole loans or (ii) the MBS pool issue date for MBS loans. Example: if a whole loan is Purchase Ready in May 2023 (or an MBS loan is in a May 1, 2023 issued pool), the first payment date can be no earlier than December 1, 2022. Exception: HomeStyle Renovation loans delivered after renovation completion with SFC 279 may be sold up to 15 months after the note date. If the loan exceeds the applicable age limit, it is not flow-eligible and may require a negotiated transaction.
merged_examples: If a whole loan is Purchase Ready in May 2023 (or an MBS loan is in a May 1, 2023 issued pool), the first payment date can be no earlier than December 1, 2022.
HomeStyle Renovation delivered after renovation completion with SFC 279 may be sold up to 15 months after the note date.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_DETERMINES_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROHIBITION_002_006 selected as primary because it is more explicit about the control outcome: it frames HOEPA/Section 32 as an absolute delivery block regardless of other compliance, which is the clearest operational consequence.
duplicate_groups.rationale: Both rules impose the same absolute prohibition: any loan that is a HOEPA/Regulation Z Section 32 covered transaction is not eligible for delivery to Fannie Mae. Context and consequences are identical (do not deliver / ineligible). There are no conflicting thresholds; differences are wording only.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: Any mortgage loan that is subject to the Home Ownership and Equity Protection Act of 1994 (HOEPA), as described in Section 32 of Regulation Z, is not eligible for delivery to Fannie Mae. This is an absolute delivery prohibition regardless of other underwriting or eligibility compliance.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_DETERMINES_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_CONSTRAINT_002_005 selected as primary because it is more complete: it includes the same maximum and minimum term constraints and adds the explicit single-closing construction-to-permanent exception details, which increases testability and reduces ambiguity.
duplicate_groups.rationale: Both rules enforce the same core loan term eligibility boundaries for Fannie Mae acquisition: original terms up to 30 years; the term may not extend more than 30 years beyond the date one month prior to the first payment date; and the minimum original term is 85 months. BR_DETERMINES_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_CONSTRAINT_002_005 includes an additional explicit construction-to-permanent (single-close) exception clarification. Because the added exception does not change the general rule but clarifies its application to a special case, the rules are treated as duplicates with a completeness variation (semantic not scored at full 40).
duplicate_groups.merged_description: Fannie Mae purchases or securitizes loans with original terms up to 30 years. The term of a first mortgage may not extend more than 30 years beyond the date that is one month prior to the date of the first payment. The minimum original term is 85 months (subject to applicable committing and delivery requirements). Exception/clarification: for single-closing construction-to-permanent loans, the loan term must not exceed 30 years after conversion to permanent financing (per applicable guidance). If the term is outside these limits, the loan is not eligible for purchase/securitization.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_HAS_ARM_TERMS_CONSTRAINT_012_010 selected as primary because it is more complete: it includes both the acceptability test (spread and margin caps) and the method details for fully indexed rate calculation (index lookback window and rounding), making it more directly testable.
duplicate_groups.rationale: Both rules define the same ARM acceptability constraints for 3- and 5-year ARMs intended for Fannie Mae: (i) subtract initial note rate from fully indexed rate at origination, and the difference must not exceed 3%; and (ii) the mortgage margin must be ≤ 300 bps. BR_HAS_ARM_TERMS_CONSTRAINT_012_010 additionally specifies how to compute fully indexed rate (index value in effect during the 90 days preceding the note date and rounding to nearest 1/8%). These are not conflicting requirements; the second rule is a restatement of the same constraint set.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: For a Fannie Mae ARM, the fully indexed rate is calculated as the applicable index value plus the mortgage margin, using any index value in effect during the 90 days preceding the note date, and rounded to the nearest one-eighth percent. To determine acceptability for purchase by Fannie Mae, subtract the initial note rate from the fully indexed rate in effect at origination; for 3-year and 5-year ARMs, the difference must not exceed 3%. Separately, the mortgage margin must be no more than 300 basis points. If the spread exceeds 3% or the margin exceeds 300 bps, the ARM terms are not acceptable for delivery.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_MANUFACTURED_HOME_VALIDATION_036_003 selected as primary because it is more clearly scoped to manufactured home guidance tables and more explicitly labels the rule as a manufactured home-specific validation requirement (improves graph placement under manufactured home domain).
duplicate_groups.rationale: Both rules implement the identical manufactured home land-equity credit logic for borrower minimum down payment/equity: if land purchased >12 months prior to application, use current appraised value; if purchased ≤12 months, use lesser of sales price or current appraised value and document cash investment. Threshold (12 months) and context (manufactured home permanently attached; borrower-owned land counted) align; consequences align (land equity can/cannot be credited). Only wording differs.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: When borrower-owned land is credited toward the borrower’s minimum down payment/equity for a manufactured home that will be permanently attached, the land value that may be credited depends on the acquisition timing: (i) if the land was purchased more than 12 months before the loan application date, use the current appraised value; (ii) if the land was purchased 12 or fewer months before the application date, use the lesser of the sales price or the current appraised value and document the borrower’s cash investment in the land. If documentation/value requirements are not met, land equity may not be credited toward the minimum contribution/equity requirement.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_MANUFACTURED_HOME_CONSTRAINT_036_004 selected as primary because it is slightly more complete/cleanly scoped as a manufactured home constraint and is better positioned as the canonical manufactured-home trade-equity cap rule for the knowledge graph.
duplicate_groups.rationale: Both rules state the same cap on trade equity from an existing manufactured home used toward minimum down payment: maximum equity contribution is 90% of NADA retail value; if owned <12 months, the maximum is the lesser of 90% of retail value or the lowest sales price during that 12-month period. Numeric thresholds (90%, 12 months) and context align; consequences align (cap equity; adjust funds to close). Wording differences only.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: Trade equity from the borrower’s existing manufactured home may be used toward the minimum down payment, but the maximum equity contribution is 90% of the traded home’s retail value per the NADA Manufactured Housing Appraisal Guide. If the borrower has owned the traded manufactured home for less than 12 months preceding the loan application date, the maximum equity contribution is the lesser of (i) 90% of NADA retail value or (ii) the lowest price at which the home was sold during that 12-month period. If the calculated trade equity exceeds the cap, the excess must be excluded and the transaction must be restructured/adjusted accordingly.
duplicate_groups.merged_references: NADA Manufactured Housing Appraisal Guide (as referenced)

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_LENDER_CONTRACT_CONSTRAINT_037_007 selected as primary because it is the most comprehensive: it includes both (a) the payoff-within-120-days servicing-rights refund obligation and (b) the early payment default (EPD) reimbursement/fee obligation, whereas the duplicates each capture only one sub-clause.
duplicate_groups.rationale: BR_GOVERNS_RELATIONSHIP_WITH_CONSTRAINT_003_005 is a direct restatement of clause (a) within BR_LENDER_CONTRACT_CONSTRAINT_037_007: refund 100% of servicing rights price plus associated interest loss/fees if paid in full within 120 days of Purchase Date. BR_GOVERNS_RELATIONSHIP_WITH_CONSTRAINT_003_006 is a direct restatement of the EPD clause portion: if the 1st/2nd/3rd payment becomes 60+ days delinquent after Purchase Date, seller must reimburse the servicing-rights purchase price and pay a $1,500 EPD fee, wired within 7 business days of request. All thresholds and consequences align; differences are only that the duplicates were extracted as separate rules rather than combined in one.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: Under the Servicing Marketplace Mortgage Loan Servicing Purchase and Sale Agreement: (a) If a mortgage loan is paid in full within 120 days of the Purchase Date, Seller must refund 100% of the price paid by Servicer for the servicing rights, plus any applicable associated interest loss and any applicable fees due to Fannie Mae. (b) If the first, second, or third monthly payment due Servicer becomes 60 or more days delinquent after the Purchase Date (an EPD Mortgage Loan), Seller must reimburse Servicer for the original purchase price paid for that EPD Mortgage Loan and pay an early payment default fee of $1,500 per EPD Mortgage Loan; Seller must wire the applicable amount within seven (7) Business Days from Servicer’s request date. Failure to pay/refund as required constitutes a breach of the servicing rights purchase/sale agreement.
duplicate_groups.merged_references: Servicing Marketplace Mortgage Loan Servicing Purchase and Sale Agreement (as referenced)

deduplication_details.analysis_notes: Conservative deduplication applied: only clear, rationally identical restatements were merged. Notably, several near-overlaps were intentionally NOT merged because they add or omit material exceptions/conditions (e.g., the general 6-month flow-aging rule BR_DETERMINES_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_CONSTRAINT_002_001 lacks the explicit HomeStyle Renovation/SFC 279 exception present in the more detailed flow-aging rules; Phase I environmental screening rules overlap but differ on required question logic and stop/continue criteria). Where one rule was a strict superset of another (e.g., servicing rights payoff/EPD obligations), the superset rule was selected as primary and the partial extracts were merged into it to reduce redundancy while preserving full logic.

dependencies.strength_justification: STRONG dependency: the margin-call response deadline process is only operationally relevant when a price-differential margin call is actually triggered (i.e., thresholds/minimum transfer are met).
dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROCESS_001_002 governs the lender’s deadline to satisfy a price differential after Fannie Mae notice. In practice, Fannie Mae would only issue (or the lender would only treat) a margin call notice as actionable when the price differential meets the threshold and minimum transfer rules in BR_SELLER_SERVICER_CONSTRAINT_001_001 (unless modified/otherwise agreed).
dependencies.impact: If the threshold/minimum transfer logic in BR_SELLER_SERVICER_CONSTRAINT_001_001 is not satisfied (or is misapplied), the lender may either (a) fail to respond to a valid margin call (default risk) or (b) unnecessarily move funds for a non-actionable call (liquidity/operational error).
examples: Price differential is $40,000 (below $50,000 minimum transfer): lender should not treat a notice as requiring same-day funding; misapplying the rule could cause unnecessary cash movement.
Price differential is $3.2MM (over $3MM threshold): a notice before 10:00 a.m. creates a same-day COB deadline; missing it may be treated as nonperformance.
evidence_quotes: both Fannie Mae’s and the lender’s designated threshold amount are $3,000,000... and both parties’ minimum transfer amount is $50,000
When Fannie Mae provides notice to satisfy the price differential... the deadline depends on when notice is sent

dependencies.strength_justification: STRONG dependency: pool-structure constraints are a major component of being “error-free” for certification; violations commonly block or delay certification/delivery.
dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROCESS_001_004 requires Major deliveries be “error-free and certified” by a specific monthly deadline. BR_SELLER_SERVICER_CONSTRAINT_001_003 imposes pool-count limits for high pass-through rate trades; exceeding those limits creates a delivery defect that prevents an “error-free” certification state or forces restructuring before certification.
dependencies.impact: If the pool-count constraint is violated, certification may fail before the monthly cutoff, causing missed issue month/settlement eligibility and requiring pool restructuring and re-certification.
examples: A ≥8% pass-through trade of $450,000 is delivered as 5 pools (limit is 3): certification fails and must be restructured before the fourth-to-last business day cutoff.
A $1.2MM trade is structured exceeding allowed pools per million: delivery is rejected or kicked back for correction, missing the intended month-end cycle.
evidence_quotes: For MBS trades with pass-through rate ≥ 8%, the maximum number of pools permitted depends on original trade amount...
All Fannie Major deliveries must be error-free and certified by 6:00 p.m. Eastern time...

dependencies.strength_justification: CRITICAL mathematical dependency: monthly remittance calculation explicitly requires the annualized weighted-average guaranty fee factor as an input.
dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_CALCULATION_003_008 instructs the lender to multiply the “annualized weighted-average guaranty fee factor” by the pool balance and divide by 120,000. That factor is produced by BR_SELLER_SERVICER_CALCULATION_003_007; without it, BR_SELLER_SERVICER_CALCULATION_003_008 cannot be computed.
dependencies.impact: If the factor is not calculated (or is wrong), the remittance amount will be wrong, leading to drafting discrepancies, remittance shortfalls, reconciliations, and potential compensatory adjustments.
examples: Pool has mixed g-fee rates; lender skips remittance-cycle adjustments in the factor: monthly draft is understated and triggers a correction/true-up.
Scheduled balance changes in later months are not reflected in the factor inputs; remittance is miscomputed for subsequent periods.
evidence_quotes: To determine the monthly guaranty fee remittance amount... the lender multiplies the annualized weighted-average guaranty fee factor by the pool balance...

dependencies.strength_justification: STRONG process dependency: the draft event requires the lender to have computed/anticipated the correct draft amount and funded the draft account accordingly.
dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROCESS_003_009 states Fannie Mae drafts monthly guaranty fee remittances on the 7th (or preceding business day) and the lender must manage the custodial bank draft account so the draft can occur. To manage funding and reconciliation, the lender must compute the expected remittance amount via BR_SELLER_SERVICER_CALCULATION_003_008.
dependencies.impact: If the remittance amount is not computed correctly (or not computed in time), the draft account may be underfunded or overfunded, causing failed drafts, collections/escalations, or accounting breaks.
examples: The 7th falls on a Sunday; draft occurs Friday. If the lender doesn’t compute remittance early, funds are not available on the preceding business day.
Lender uses prior-month balance instead of current scheduled security balance; draft differs from expectation, creating a reconciliation exception.
evidence_quotes: Fannie Mae drafts monthly guaranty fee remittances on the 7th day of each month...
The lender must manage its designated custodial bank draft account so the draft can occur as scheduled.

dependencies.strength_justification: CRITICAL override: OFAC valid match requires servicing activities (including remittances) to cease, superseding normal remittance drafting operations.
dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROCESS_003_009 expects ongoing drafting of guaranty fee remittances. BR_SELLER_SERVICER_COMPLIANCE_005_010 states that upon a valid OFAC match, the servicer must block/segregate funds and ensure all servicing activities cease, including remittances and disbursements—this directly overrides normal remittance processing.
dependencies.impact: If OFAC cessation requirements are not applied, the servicer may transmit prohibited payments/remittances and mishandle blocked funds, creating sanctions exposure and severe enforcement risk.
examples: Serviced loan flags a valid OFAC match on the 5th; the system must prevent the scheduled 7th-day remittance-related activity for that loan/account and segregate escrow.
An OFAC match is identified after the draft file is prepared; lender must stop processing and notify Ethics within 24 hours, rather than letting the remittance proceed.
evidence_quotes: If a valid match is identified... block and segregate funds (including escrow), and ensure all servicing activities cease (including remittances and disbursements).

dependencies.strength_justification: CRITICAL override: OFAC valid match requires cessation of servicing disbursements, which would include tax payments from escrow.
dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROCESS_021_002 requires paying real estate taxes under certain escrow/transfer conditions. BR_SELLER_SERVICER_COMPLIANCE_005_010 requires blocking/segregating funds (including escrow) and ceasing servicing activities including disbursements upon a valid OFAC match. That creates an exception state overriding routine tax disbursement obligations until properly handled under OFAC controls.
dependencies.impact: If the lender continues tax disbursements from blocked escrow, it can violate OFAC requirements. If it ceases disbursements without appropriate OFAC handling/notification, it can create tax delinquency risk—requiring a controlled, documented resolution path.
examples: Tax bill due during a servicing transfer period; an OFAC match is validated—escrow funds must be segregated and disbursement stopped pending compliance handling.
System detects match and still auto-pays taxes from escrow: prohibited disbursement event and potential sanctions exposure.
evidence_quotes: block and segregate funds (including escrow), and ensure all servicing activities cease (including remittances and disbursements).
Seller must pay all real estate taxes that are due and payable...

dependencies.strength_justification: STRONG operational dependency: the reporting mechanism required by the target rule (Loan Quality Connect self-report) requires registration/access and supporting documentation practices.
dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_COMPLIANCE_005_009 requires reporting using “Loan Quality Connect self-report functionality.” BR_SELLER_SERVICER_DOCUMENTATION_011_009 requires Seller/Servicers to register for Loan Quality Connect and maintain complete loan files; without registration and file completeness, timely self-reporting and supporting evidence submission is severely compromised.
dependencies.impact: If not registered or unable to produce complete supporting files, self-reporting can be missed or incomplete, increasing remedies/repurchase exposure and governance findings.
examples: Compliance identifies 8 potentially affected delivered loans; lender is not registered in LQC—reporting cannot be submitted timely.
LQC report is filed but lender cannot upload requested supporting file due to unsupported format and no approval for alternate method—submission becomes non-compliant.
evidence_quotes: reporting must be done using Loan Quality Connect self-report functionality.
Seller/Servicers must register for Loan Quality Connect...

dependencies.strength_justification: STRONG dependency: target requires using LQC self-report functionality; source establishes the required registration and file readiness.
dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROCESS_013_007 mandates notice using the self-report functionality in Loan Quality Connect. BR_SELLER_SERVICER_DOCUMENTATION_011_009 mandates registration for Loan Quality Connect and maintaining a complete loan file needed to support the report.
dependencies.impact: If LQC registration/file readiness is missing, the lender may fail to notify within 30 days, increasing contractual remedy exposure.
examples: Post-closing review finds a likely selling warranty breach; lender cannot access LQC because registration was not completed—30-day clock is missed.
Lender submits a self-report but cannot furnish the complete file electronically when requested, causing an escalated defect.
evidence_quotes: must notify Fannie Mae within 30 days using the self-report functionality in Loan Quality Connect.
must register for Loan Quality Connect...

dependencies.strength_justification: STRONG dependency: examination/record production requires the underlying record retention and “complete loan file” discipline.
dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROCESS_008_007 requires delivering all requested mortgage records within the specified timeframe and reproducing non-paper records. BR_SELLER_SERVICER_DOCUMENTATION_011_009 requires maintaining a complete loan file and providing electronic copies upon request—this is foundational to meeting record examination requests.
dependencies.impact: If complete loan files are not maintained or cannot be produced in required formats, the seller/servicer may miss the examination deadline, leading to enforcement and potential loss liability.
examples: Fannie requests underwriting and closing documentation for an exam; lender’s file is incomplete—cannot respond by the required date.
Records are stored digitally but cannot be reproduced/exported; lender incurs expense/delay and must justify inability to comply timely.
evidence_quotes: must deliver all requested records... within the timeframe specified by Fannie Mae.
must maintain a complete loan file... and provide electronic copies of the complete loan file...

dependencies.strength_justification: STRONG dependency: delivering a “complete loan file” to the servicer/custodian depends on having compiled and retained the complete loan file in the first place.
dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROCESS_021_003 requires furnishing a complete loan file within 3 business days after purchase date and retaining a copy. BR_SELLER_SERVICER_DOCUMENTATION_011_009 defines the expectation that the lender maintains a complete loan file including all documents used to support the underwriting decision.
dependencies.impact: If the lender does not maintain a complete file, it cannot timely furnish a complete transfer file, leading to late fees and servicing transfer defects.
examples: After purchase, servicer requests the full file; lender discovers missing income documentation—cannot deliver a complete file within 3 business days and is assessed late fees.
File exists but not in a supported electronic format; lender must seek approval for alternate submission method, risking timeline failure.
evidence_quotes: must maintain a complete loan file including all documents used to support the underwriting decision.
Seller must furnish... a complete loan file...

dependencies.strength_justification: STRONG conditional dependency: the exception is only evaluated when the standard 2-year history requirement is not met.
dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROCESS_012_003 sets the general requirement: obtain a two-year history for self-employment income. BR_SELLER_SERVICER_EXCEPTION_012_004 defines the pathway when the borrower has less than a two-year history—therefore the exception is triggered by failing/being unable to satisfy the general rule.
dependencies.impact: If the 2-year history requirement is not assessed correctly, the lender may incorrectly apply the exception (using ineligible income) or incorrectly deny/underwrite (excluding eligible income).
examples: Borrower has 18 months self-employment: lender must move from the 2-year standard to the <2-year exception test (12 months returns + prior similar field income support).
Borrower has 30 months self-employment: exception should not be used; standard 2-year history should be documented.
evidence_quotes: generally requires the lender to obtain a two-year history...
A borrower with less than a two-year history... may be considered if...

dependencies.strength_justification: MODERATE dependency: tax returns/transcripts are a primary means to substantiate the required multi-year self-employment earnings history, but other documentation patterns can exist depending on program/DU messaging.
dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROCESS_012_003 requires demonstrating likelihood of continued self-employment income via a two-year prior earnings history. BR_SELLER_SERVICER_VALIDATION_012_005 provides the core verification mechanism (two years returns or IRS transcripts) that evidences that history.
dependencies.impact: If returns/transcripts are not obtained (or are incomplete/illegible), the lender may not be able to substantiate the two-year history, forcing exception analysis or exclusion of self-employment income.
examples: Underwriter needs two-year earnings trend; incomplete IRS transcripts missing schedules cannot support the required history.
Borrower provides only a draft return not filed with IRS; lender cannot use it as a substitute for filed returns/transcripts.
evidence_quotes: may verify... by obtaining signed federal income tax returns... for the past two years... Alternatively... IRS-issued transcripts for the most recent two years
requires... a two-year history of the borrower’s prior earnings...

dependencies.strength_justification: STRONG dependency: this rule explicitly modifies the standard two-year documentation approach by allowing one-year returns only if strict prerequisites are met.
dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_VALIDATION_012_005 describes two-year returns/transcripts as the baseline approach. BR_SELLER_SERVICER_EXCEPTION_012_006 permits a reduced documentation set (one year) only under defined longevity/ownership conditions; this is an exception/override to the baseline documentation requirement.
dependencies.impact: If the exception is misapplied, the file may be under-documented (QC findings, underwriting defects) or over-documented (unnecessary borrower friction).
examples: Borrower owns 30% of a business for 5 consecutive years and business exists 5 years: lender can document with one year of returns.
Borrower reached 25% ownership only 2 years ago: exception fails; lender must revert to two-year returns/transcripts.
