evidence_quotes: may provide one year of personal and business tax returns... if: (1) the business has been in existence for five years... and (2) the borrower has had an ownership share of 25% or more for the past five years consecutively.

dependencies.strength_justification: MODERATE dependency: VOE timing is a closing/delivery control that typically follows (and is driven by) the underwriting decision to use employment/self-employment income, which relies on income verification artifacts.
dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROCESS_012_009 applies when the borrower is using employment or self-employment income to qualify and imposes timing rules for verbal VOE. In most workflows, the lender first validates self-employment income via acceptable returns/transcripts (BR_SELLER_SERVICER_VALIDATION_012_005) and then completes/refreshes VOE within the required window before note date (or by delivery).
dependencies.impact: If sequencing breaks (VOE not refreshed within required window), the loan becomes ineligible at delivery even if income documentation was otherwise acceptable.
examples: Self-employed borrower: transcripts obtained early, but verbal VOE not completed within 120 days prior to note date—must redo VOE or risk delivery ineligibility.
Employment-income borrower: VOE done 20 business days prior to note date—outside the 10 business day window; must be re-verified.
evidence_quotes: A verbal verification of employment... must be obtained... within 10 business days prior to the note date... and within 120 calendar days... for self-employment income.

dependencies.strength_justification: MODERATE dependency: K-1 income evaluation commonly relies on the same tax return/transcript package used to validate self-employed income; not strictly mandatory but operationally interdependent.
dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_VALIDATION_012_002 requires documentation verifying K-1 distributions or business liquidity. BR_SELLER_SERVICER_VALIDATION_012_005 provides the standard tax documentation set (returns/transcripts with schedules) that frequently contains K-1s and supporting business financial context used to evaluate distributions/liquidity.
dependencies.impact: If tax returns/transcripts are missing/incomplete, K-1 income may be improperly included or must be excluded, changing qualifying income and underwriting outcomes.
examples: Borrower uses partnership K-1 income; lender obtains complete returns with schedules showing K-1 and distribution history to support use of income.
Transcripts are illegible/missing schedules; lender cannot evidence K-1 distribution and must exclude the income, increasing DTI.
evidence_quotes: Income reported on Schedule K-1 may be considered... only if the lender obtains documentation verifying...
may verify... by obtaining signed federal income tax returns... with all schedules attached.

dependencies.strength_justification: MODERATE conflict risk: both rules govern appraisal waivers but require different prerequisites and different SFCs at delivery; applying both simultaneously can create data/code conflicts.
dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROCESS_018_007 covers DU value acceptance without appraisal and requires SFC 801 at delivery. BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROCESS_018_008 covers value acceptance + property data, requires Property Data API submission and SFC 774. A loan should follow the path corresponding to DU’s eligibility message and whether property data collection was submitted; using the wrong path or coding both can create delivery defects.
dependencies.impact: If the lender misclassifies the waiver type, it may deliver with the wrong SFC and missing prerequisite artifacts (e.g., missing Property Data API submission), causing delivery findings, repurchase risk, or forced appraisal.
examples: DU returns value acceptance + property data option; lender uses SFC 801 instead of 774 and cannot evidence Property Data API submission—delivery defect.
DU returns value acceptance only; lender assumes + property data and codes SFC 774 without having submitted property data prior to note date—noncompliant.
evidence_quotes: must include Special Feature Code (SFC) 801 at delivery.
must include SFC 774 at delivery.
property data collection is submitted... prior to the note date

dependencies.strength_justification: STRONG dependency for eMortgages: eNote registration and controller assignment are core eligibility controls that must be satisfied before successful delivery.
dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROCESS_020_006 requires timely MERS eRegistry registration (no later than one business day after signing) and correct Controller designation; failure may make the loan ineligible for sale. BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROCESS_031_006 gates delivery of conventional loans on successful UCD submission—delivery readiness depends on underlying eMortgage eligibility controls being satisfied.
dependencies.impact: If eRegistry registration/controller is incorrect or late, the loan may be ineligible for sale even if UCD submission is successful, causing delivery suspension and corrective re-registration work.
examples: eNote signed Monday; registration not completed by Tuesday EOD—loan flagged as potentially ineligible at delivery review.
MERS record does not show originating lender as first Controller; lender must remediate before sale even though UCD status is Successful.
evidence_quotes: eNotes are registered... no later than one business day after signing.
Before delivering a conventional loan... must... receive a “Successful” status...

dependencies.strength_justification: CRITICAL compliance blocker: loans cannot be delivered if any borrower is on an OFAC sanctions list.
dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_COMPLIANCE_005_010 explicitly prohibits delivery of a loan to Fannie Mae if any borrower is on an OFAC sanctions list. Therefore, before completing delivery steps (including UCD submission success as a delivery gate) under BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROCESS_031_006, OFAC screening must clear.
dependencies.impact: If OFAC screening fails (valid match), delivery must stop; attempting delivery creates severe sanctions/compliance exposure and contractual violations.
examples: Borrower name screens as a valid match; loan cannot be delivered regardless of UCD Successful status.
Servicer identifies a match during pipeline checks; loan is removed from delivery batch and escalated to Ethics within 24 hours.
evidence_quotes: may not deliver a loan to Fannie Mae if any borrower is on an OFAC sanctions list

dependencies.strength_justification: STRONG dependency: flood/NFIP eligibility is a hard purchase eligibility rule; delivery must not proceed for ineligible collateral scenarios.
dependencies.rationale: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_ELIGIBILITY_004_002 makes loans ineligible where a principal/residential detached structure is in an SFHA and the community does not participate in NFIP (with a CBRS/OPA nuance). BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROCESS_031_006 governs delivery readiness; eligibility checks should gate delivery because an ineligible loan cannot be purchased.
dependencies.impact: If flood/NFIP status is not validated before delivery, the lender may deliver an ineligible loan leading to rejection, pipeline disruption, and potential contractual remedies.
examples: Property is in SFHA; community is non-NFIP: loan must be blocked from delivery despite otherwise complete UCD submission.
Property is in CBRS/OPA and non-participating community: eligible only if not in SFHA; misclassification causes improper delivery attempt.
evidence_quotes: the loan secured by the property is not eligible for purchase by Fannie Mae
Before delivering a conventional loan to Fannie Mae...

dependencies.strength_justification: CRITICAL gating dependency: UCD Successful status is required “before delivery” of conventional loans; whole-loan purchase submission packages are part of the delivery process.
dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROCESS_037_004 describes operational timing for whole-loan document package submission aligned to the day loan data is submitted for purchase. Since BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROCESS_031_006 requires UCD XML submission and Successful status before delivering a conventional loan, UCD success must occur before/with the delivery submission events that BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROCESS_037_004 supports.
dependencies.impact: If UCD Successful status is not obtained, delivery is blocked; document shipment and certification timelines become irrelevant or cause rework and missed purchase windows.
examples: Lender ships custodian docs same day as loan data submission, but UCD status is not Successful—delivery cannot proceed; certification is delayed.
UCD XML missing Closing Disclosure PDF; submission fails validation and loan cannot be delivered even though doc package was sent overnight.
evidence_quotes: Before delivering a conventional loan... must... receive a “Successful” status...
seller should submit the document package... the same day the seller submits loan data...

dependencies.strength_justification: CRITICAL delivery gating dependency: UCD Successful status is required before delivery of conventional loans; Major MBS deliveries are a delivery pathway for conventional collateral.
dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROCESS_001_004 imposes a monthly certification deadline for Major deliveries. BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROCESS_031_006 requires UCD Successful status before delivery of any conventional loan. Therefore, UCD completion must precede (or be satisfied before) the Major delivery certification process can be completed.
dependencies.impact: If UCD submission fails or is incomplete, Major delivery certification cannot be completed for affected loans/pools, risking missed issue month and settlement eligibility.
examples: Pool is ready for month-end Major certification, but several loans lack Successful UCD status—pool certification is blocked until corrected.
Non-owner-occupied loan still requires UCD even if Reg Z CD not required; missing UCD blocks delivery for that loan.
evidence_quotes: Before delivering a conventional loan... must... submit a Uniform Closing Dataset (UCD)...
All Fannie Major deliveries must be error-free and certified...

dependencies.strength_justification: STRONG dependency: certification/purchase workflow is materially affected by whether delivered volume is within commitment overdelivery limits; exceeding limits can cause rejection/adjustments that disrupt the document/certification timeline.
dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROCESS_037_004 anticipates next-business-day certification when documents are delivered properly and data is submitted. BR_SELLER_SERVICER_CONSTRAINT_037_003 limits the maximum delivery/overdelivery amounts; if delivery exceeds permitted limits without approval, delivery may be rejected or repriced, preventing smooth certification under the process timeline.
dependencies.impact: If overdelivery constraints are not met, the loan set may be rejected or require permission/price adjustments, delaying certification and increasing operational costs.
examples: Commitment is $1,000,000; lender delivers $1,050,000 without approval (exceeds greater of $10,000 or 2.5%): delivery is rejected despite timely doc shipment.
Lender receives approval to overdeliver but exceeds the approved 25% cap: certification is blocked and commitment must be corrected/pair-off may occur.
evidence_quotes: maximum delivery amount generally cannot exceed the original commitment amount...
Loan data delivered will be certified the next business day provided documents were delivered...

dependencies.strength_justification: MODERATE dependency: PERS-required projects drive how project approval is obtained; CPM monitoring/notification applies once a project is in an approved/CPM status, commonly downstream of formal review pathways like PERS.
dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROCESS_023_010 requires certain projects to be submitted to PERS as a required review method. BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROCESS_005_008 governs post-approval monitoring/notification for projects with eligibility status reflected in CPM and requires confirmation of approved status as of note date. Operationally, lenders move from initial required review (PERS) to ongoing CPM governance (notification/confirmation) once the project is approved and tracked.
dependencies.impact: If PERS-required review is skipped, the project may never be properly approved/tracked, making later CPM confirmation/notification moot and resulting in unit ineligibility for delivery.
examples: Newly converted attached condo in Florida: must go through required PERS; once approved and reflected in CPM, lender must monitor and notify CPM Management if litigation arises.
Lender relies on CPM approval status but fails to submit PERS for a PERS-required project type; unit loan becomes ineligible for sale.
evidence_quotes: Lenders must submit certain projects... to... PERS as a required review method.
If the lender becomes aware of information that could impact the project eligibility status reflected in CPM... it must notify...

dependencies.strength_justification: CRITICAL dependency: termination actions are directly triggered by failing the minimum loan sale requirement.
dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROCESS_025_003 states that if the seller fails the minimum loan sale requirement, Fannie Mae will terminate approval and technology access within 60 days. That minimum requirement is defined by BR_SELLER_SERVICER_CONSTRAINT_025_001 (≥12 loans sold in prior calendar year).
dependencies.impact: If the minimum sale requirement is not met, seller approval and technology access are terminated, blocking future deliveries and requiring reapplication for approval.
examples: Seller sold only 9 loans last year: approval termination workflow is initiated and system access is cut within 60 days.
Seller sold 12+ loans: termination process does not trigger; eligibility maintained.
evidence_quotes: must have sold at least 12 loans... during the prior calendar year.
If the seller fails the minimum loan sale requirement, Fannie Mae will terminate approval...

dependencies.strength_justification: CRITICAL dependency: termination actions are directly triggered by failing the minimum servicing requirement.
dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROCESS_025_003 states that if the servicer fails the minimum servicing requirement, Fannie Mae will terminate approval and technology access. The minimum requirement is defined by BR_SELLER_SERVICER_CONSTRAINT_025_002 (service at least one Fannie Mae loan as of Dec 31).
dependencies.impact: If the minimum servicing requirement is not met, servicer approval is terminated, restricting servicing activities and requiring reapproval.
examples: Servicer has zero Fannie Mae loans as of Dec 31: approval termination process triggers.
Servicer retains at least one loan at year-end: termination process does not apply.
evidence_quotes: must service at least one loan for Fannie Mae as of December 31...
If the servicer fails the minimum servicing requirement, Fannie Mae will terminate approval...

dependencies.strength_justification: STRONG dependency: the capital/liquidity plan must be designed to manage capital consistent with specific minimum adjusted net worth requirements.
dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROCESS_027_005 requires a plan describing how the lender will manage capital and liquidity consistent with Fannie Mae requirements. BR_SELLER_SERVICER_CONSTRAINT_027_001 defines a core minimum adjusted net worth requirement (base + UPB-based add-ons) that the plan must address and monitor.
dependencies.impact: If minimum adjusted net worth requirements are not incorporated into the plan, the plan may be noncompliant/inadequate, increasing the risk of eligibility actions and forced remediation during stress.
examples: Large non-depository’s plan ignores the 0.25% UPB add-on for Fannie/Freddie servicing: plan underestimates required net worth buffer.
Plan includes triggers and actions (capital injections, dividend restrictions) when adjusted net worth approaches the minimum.
evidence_quotes: must maintain Adjusted Net Worth at all times...
must submit a capital and liquidity plan... consistent with Fannie Mae requirements.

dependencies.strength_justification: STRONG dependency: the plan must account for the non-depository net worth-to-assets ratio requirement as a standing capital adequacy constraint.
dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_CONSTRAINT_027_002 imposes a minimum adjusted net worth to total assets ratio for non-depositories. A capital plan required by BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROCESS_027_005 must include monitoring and actions to remain above this ratio, especially in stress scenarios.
dependencies.impact: If the ratio requirement is not planned for, asset growth or valuation changes can cause a breach, triggering corrective action and potential eligibility restrictions.
examples: Non-depository expands assets rapidly; ratio falls below 6% without planned capital actions—breach occurs.
Plan includes asset shrink strategies or capital raising triggers to keep ratio ≥6%.
evidence_quotes: must maintain at all times a minimum Adjusted Net Worth to total assets ratio of 6%
must submit a capital and liquidity plan...

dependencies.strength_justification: STRONG dependency: allowable liquidity minimums must be incorporated into the liquidity plan and stress notifications.
dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_CONSTRAINT_027_003 defines the calculated minimum allowable liquidity for non-depositories based on servicing UPB/remittance types. BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROCESS_027_005 requires a liquidity plan and rapid notice of material deviations/changes, which necessarily depends on understanding/measuring this minimum.
dependencies.impact: If liquidity minima are not measured/embedded in the plan, the lender may breach liquidity requirements without timely mitigation or notice, increasing eligibility action risk.
examples: Servicing UPB shifts toward actual/actual remittance types; liquidity requirement changes—plan must capture the recalculation and funding actions.
During stress, liquidity drops below calculated minimum; one-business-day notice requirement in stress becomes relevant.
evidence_quotes: must maintain Allowable Liquidity at all times at or above a calculated minimum equal to...
must submit a capital and liquidity plan... and... written notice... within one business day... during times of stress.

dependencies.strength_justification: STRONG conditional dependency: if the >$1B origination threshold applies, the plan must incorporate the origination liquidity requirement and monitoring.
dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_CONSTRAINT_027_004 imposes an additional origination liquidity requirement for certain high-volume non-depository originators. BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROCESS_027_005 requires a plan consistent with Fannie requirements; when the threshold condition is met, this constraint becomes a required input into plan design and reporting triggers.
dependencies.impact: If the lender crosses the >$1B threshold and the plan does not adapt, the lender may become noncompliant with liquidity requirements and face restrictions or mandated liquidity increases.
examples: Originations rise to $1.2B over four quarters; lender must calculate and hold 0.5% origination liquidity against eligible HFS loans.
Originations fall below the threshold; plan can de-emphasize the origination liquidity component but must retain monitoring to detect re-qualification.
evidence_quotes: originate more than $1,000,000,000... must maintain origination liquidity equal to 0.5%...
must submit a capital and liquidity plan...

dependencies.strength_justification: MODERATE dependency: audited financial statements are a primary artifact used to evidence/validate adjusted net worth compliance, though calculations may also use interim reporting.
dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROCESS_022_001 requires submission of annual audited financial statements, which are commonly used to compute/validate adjusted net worth. BR_SELLER_SERVICER_CONSTRAINT_027_001 requires maintaining adjusted net worth at all times; annual AFS is a key validation checkpoint used by Fannie to assess compliance.
dependencies.impact: If AFS is late or missing, Fannie may treat it as a contract breach and may be unable to validate compliance, increasing oversight and potential eligibility actions.
examples: AFS not submitted within 90 days; Fannie declares breach and increases scrutiny of net worth compliance.
AFS reveals adjusted net worth below minimum; triggers remediation and possible restrictions.
evidence_quotes: must submit its annual Audited Financial Statement (AFS)...
must maintain Adjusted Net Worth at all times...

dependencies.strength_justification: MODERATE dependency: quarterly Form 1002 reporting supports periodic validation/monitoring of financial eligibility metrics including net worth.
dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROCESS_022_002 requires quarterly Form 1002 reporting for certain entity types. These financial data submissions are used to monitor financial condition between annual audits, supporting validation of ongoing compliance with BR_SELLER_SERVICER_CONSTRAINT_027_001.
dependencies.impact: If quarterly reports are missing or inaccurate, compliance monitoring degrades and can trigger eligibility actions or breach determinations.
examples: Quarterly 1002 is late; lender cannot demonstrate ongoing compliance with minimum net worth during the quarter.
1002 shows deteriorating net worth trend; triggers enhanced oversight before an actual breach occurs.
evidence_quotes: must submit Form 1002 at the end of each calendar quarter...
must maintain Adjusted Net Worth at all times...

dependencies.strength_justification: MODERATE dependency: monthly 1002A reporting is a monitoring mechanism that supports validation of liquidity compliance for large non-depositories.
dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROCESS_022_003 requires monthly Form 1002A for large non-depository seller/servicers. BR_SELLER_SERVICER_CONSTRAINT_027_003 requires allowable liquidity to be maintained at all times. Monthly reporting is a practical validation tool to monitor liquidity between annual and quarterly checkpoints.
dependencies.impact: If monthly reports are missing, liquidity compliance may not be detectable timely, increasing the probability of unreported breaches and eligibility actions.
examples: Large non-depository fails to submit 1002A for April; liquidity deterioration goes unreported until later, causing a delayed remediation.
1002A indicates allowable liquidity below the computed minimum; prompts immediate funding actions.
evidence_quotes: must electronically submit Form 1002A within 30 days of the end of each month...
must maintain Allowable Liquidity at all times...

dependencies.strength_justification: STRONG conditional dependency: a failure to meet eligibility requirements (including net worth) is a triggering event that activates the cash-wire prohibition.
dependencies.rationale: BR_FANNIE_MAE_PROHIBITION_001_005 applies when a positive price differential would otherwise require a cash wire, but a triggering event has occurred—explicitly including “failure to meet lender eligibility requirements” and net worth decline triggers. BR_SELLER_SERVICER_CONSTRAINT_027_001 is a core eligibility requirement; violation can place the lender in a triggering event state that activates BR_FANNIE_MAE_PROHIBITION_001_005.
dependencies.impact: If net worth requirements are not met, even favorable trading economics (positive price differential) may not result in a cash wire to the lender, creating liquidity stress and downstream operational impacts.
examples: Adjusted net worth falls below minimum: Fannie withholds positive price differential wire that would otherwise be paid.
Quarterly net worth decline exceeds 20%: triggers cash-wire withholding even if lender expects funds from positive price differential.
evidence_quotes: Fannie Mae will not wire cash... if... failure to meet lender eligibility requirements
must maintain Adjusted Net Worth at all times...

dependencies.strength_justification: STRONG conditional dependency: liquidity shortfalls are part of eligibility failure conditions that can trigger cash-wire restrictions.
dependencies.rationale: BR_FANNIE_MAE_PROHIBITION_001_005 includes “failure to meet lender eligibility requirements” as a triggering event. BR_SELLER_SERVICER_CONSTRAINT_027_003 is an eligibility liquidity requirement; a breach can place the lender in a triggering event posture where Fannie may withhold otherwise payable wires.
dependencies.impact: If liquidity falls below minimum, the lender may lose access to expected cash wires from positive price differentials, worsening liquidity and potentially accelerating default risk.
examples: Liquidity drops below computed minimum due to servicing advance stress; lender expects a positive price differential wire but Fannie withholds it due to eligibility failure.
Lender remediates liquidity (new line draw/capital injection); once eligibility restored, normal wire treatment may resume (subject to Fannie discretion).
evidence_quotes: failure to meet lender eligibility requirements
must maintain Allowable Liquidity at all times...

dependencies.strength_justification: MODERATE dependency: termination of seller/servicer approval is a strong indicator of failing eligibility requirements, which are listed as triggering events for cash-wire prohibition; linkage is practical but not stated as a direct chain.
dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROCESS_025_003 describes termination of approval and technology access when minimum activity requirements are not met. BR_FANNIE_MAE_PROHIBITION_001_005 triggers when lender eligibility requirements are not met. Approval termination and loss of approved status typically correspond to eligibility failure states relevant to the triggering-event logic.
dependencies.impact: If approval is terminated, the lender may also be subject to wire restrictions under triggering-event logic, affecting trading settlement cash flows.
examples: Seller approval terminated due to failing minimum sale requirement; lender anticipates cash wire from positive price differential but may be restricted due to eligibility failure.
Servicer approval terminated; lender’s operational status triggers enhanced controls including restricted cash movements.
evidence_quotes: terminate approval...
failure to meet lender eligibility requirements

dependencies.strength_justification: STRONG override: the triggering-event prohibition can eliminate/modify the practical effect of price differential cash movement thresholds by withholding wires and allowing threshold modification without notice.
dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_CONSTRAINT_001_001 sets threshold/minimum transfer for margin calls based on price differential. BR_FANNIE_MAE_PROHIBITION_001_005 states Fannie will not wire cash even with positive price differential if triggering events occur and may modify/eliminate thresholds without notice. That behavior supersedes normal threshold-based expectations for cash movement in those triggering-event conditions.
dependencies.impact: If triggering events occur, the lender cannot rely on threshold-based mechanics to receive cash wires; liquidity planning and settlement operations must shift to a restricted-cash posture.
examples: Lender expects a positive price differential cash wire because the amount exceeds threshold; a triggering event occurs (eligibility failure) and Fannie withholds the wire.
During a triggering event, Fannie modifies/eliminates threshold application without notice; lender’s automated margin/cash forecasting based on $3MM threshold becomes inaccurate.
evidence_quotes: Fannie Mae will not wire cash to a lender even when there is a positive price differential if any specified triggering event has occurred...
may modify/eliminate thresholds without notice
designated threshold amount are $3,000,000... minimum transfer amount is $50,000

dependencies.strength_justification: MODERATE relationship: both are mandatory sanctions/exclusion screening controls that together support a comprehensive compliance program for origination/servicing risk management.
dependencies.rationale: BR_SELLER_SERVICER_PROHIBITION_020_010 requires screening employees against exclusion lists (GSA EPL, HUD LDP, FHFA SCP) and removing listed individuals from origination/servicing functions. BR_SELLER_SERVICER_COMPLIANCE_005_010 requires OFAC program controls including borrower screening and cessation actions. Together they form layered sanctions/exclusion compliance controls across people (staff) and parties (borrowers/loans).
dependencies.impact: If either control is missing, the organization’s sanctions/exclusion compliance posture is incomplete, increasing regulatory risk and contractual noncompliance exposure.
examples: Company screens borrowers for OFAC but fails to screen new hires against FHFA SCP; a suspended counterparty employee works on servicing—noncompliance.
Employee screening removes an excluded individual; OFAC screening separately blocks delivery of a sanctioned borrower loan.
evidence_quotes: must establish and maintain an effective OFAC compliance program...
must follow a written procedure for checking all employees... against... GSA... HUD... FHFA...

dependencies.strength_justification: Strong dependency: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_CONSTRAINT_004_004 references a per-unit sufficiency comparison against the one- to four-unit flood insurance requirement, which is defined/calculated by BR_MORTGAGE_LOAN_CONSTRAINT_004_003.
dependencies.rationale: The condo HOA master flood insurance (RCBAP) rule includes a branch that compares per-unit coverage against the one- to four-unit minimum flood insurance requirement. The minimum required amount benchmark (lesser of replacement cost, NFIP max, or UPB) is the input used to determine whether per-unit coverage is sufficient when applying the condo building rule.
dependencies.impact: If the one- to four-unit minimum flood insurance requirement is not correctly determined, the lender may incorrectly conclude the HOA master policy is sufficient (or insufficient), leading to delivery defects, loan ineligibility, or post-purchase repurchase exposure for flood insurance noncompliance.
examples: Attached condo in SFHA: HOA RCBAP meets 80% replacement cost, but per-unit allocation must still be tested against the standard minimum requirement; lender calculates per-unit sufficiency using the lesser-of test from BR_MORTGAGE_LOAN_CONSTRAINT_004_003.
Loan UPB is $420,000 but NFIP maximum per unit is lower; the lender must use the lesser-of amount to determine if per-unit coverage is sufficient when applying BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_CONSTRAINT_004_004.
evidence_quotes: BR_MORTGAGE_LOAN_CONSTRAINT_004_003: "minimum amount of flood insurance required... must equal the lesser of (1) 100% of the replacement cost value... (2) the maximum coverage amount available from NFIP, or (3) the unpaid principal balance"
BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_CONSTRAINT_004_004: "If the master policy meets the 80% replacement-cost requirement but the per-unit amount is insufficient versus one- to four-unit req"

dependencies.strength_justification: Strong dependency: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_ELIGIBILITY_029_010 requires the dwelling to be 1–4 units; BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_ELIGIBILITY_004_005 determines whether a property remains classifiable as a one-unit dwelling (with an ADU) vs becoming 2–4 units (ineligible or differently underwritten).
dependencies.rationale: Unit-count eligibility is foundational for delivery eligibility. The ADU rule determines whether the subject is properly treated as 1-unit-with-ADU (still within 1–4) or is misclassified (e.g., multiple ADUs or ADU with 2–4 unit dwelling). That classification directly feeds the 1–4 unit eligibility gate.
dependencies.impact: If ADU classification fails or is misapplied, the lender can deliver a loan with an incorrect unit count (e.g., actually a 2-unit or illegal second unit), creating eligibility defects, appraisal/underwriting errors, and potential purchase/securitization rejection.
examples: Property has two accessory structures with kitchens; BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_ELIGIBILITY_004_005 fails (only one ADU allowed), so the property cannot be treated as 1-unit+ADU and may fail eligibility checks that rely on unit count.
Appraiser notes ADU kitchen lacks a stove/stove hookup; the ADU is not a qualifying ADU, which can change the dwelling classification used when testing 1–4 unit eligibility.
evidence_quotes: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_ELIGIBILITY_004_005: "only one ADU is permitted on the parcel and ADUs are not permitted with a two- to four-unit dwelling"
BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_ELIGIBILITY_029_010: "dwelling consists of one to four units"

dependencies.strength_justification: Strong dependency: Leasehold eligibility is limited to specific property types (1–4, condo, co-op, PUD, certain MH). The base property-type eligibility gate must be satisfied before leasehold eligibility can be determined.
dependencies.rationale: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_ELIGIBILITY_011_001 is a narrower collateral-structure rule that assumes the property is an otherwise eligible residential type for Fannie Mae delivery. Confirming the property type and (where applicable) geographic eligibility (U.S. / territories) prevents wasted processing on a collateral structure that can never be delivered.
dependencies.impact: If base property type/location eligibility is not satisfied, leasehold analysis becomes irrelevant and the loan will be ineligible for purchase/securitization regardless of leasehold terms or market acceptance.
examples: Leasehold property is a 5-unit dwelling: fails BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_ELIGIBILITY_029_010 (1–4 units), so BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_ELIGIBILITY_011_001 cannot be satisfied even if leasehold is common in the area.
Property is outside eligible geography: base eligibility fails; leasehold criteria do not cure geographic ineligibility.
evidence_quotes: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_ELIGIBILITY_011_001: "eligible... only... if the property is an eligible property type: one- to four-unit property, condo unit, co-op unit, PUD unit..."
BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_ELIGIBILITY_029_010: "dwelling consists of one to four units"

dependencies.strength_justification: Strong dependency: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_CONSTRAINT_020_008 applies specifically to "new or newly converted condo projects" undergoing Full Review, which requires classification as new/newly converted under BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_DEFINITION_037_002.
dependencies.rationale: The Full Review thresholds (50% conveyed/under contract to principal residence/second home, legal phase limits) are only triggered if the project is a new/newly converted condo. Correctly classifying the project determines whether this constraint must be enforced.
dependencies.impact: If the project is misclassified (new vs established), the lender may (a) apply the wrong review standards and incorrectly approve an ineligible project, or (b) over-restrict and unnecessarily decline an eligible project.
examples: Project is newly converted from apartments: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_DEFINITION_037_002 classifies as new condo; lender must then test the 50% conveyed/under contract requirement in BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_CONSTRAINT_020_008.
Project is 92% sold and HOA controlled by unit owners: not new under BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_DEFINITION_037_002; lender avoids incorrectly applying the new-project 50% conveyed constraint.
evidence_quotes: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_CONSTRAINT_020_008: "For units in new or newly converted condo projects undergoing Full Review..."
BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_DEFINITION_037_002: "classified as a new condo project when any of the following are true... newly converted... HOA still in developer control"

dependencies.strength_justification: Moderate dependency: while the reserve calculation applies to any condo under Full Review, classifying a project as new/newly converted commonly determines the review path and therefore whether Full Review (and this calculation) is invoked.
dependencies.rationale: In practice, lenders determine condo project classification (new vs established) early to route the file into the correct review workflow. That routing decision often precedes the 10% reserve allocation calculation required under Full Review.
dependencies.impact: If classification is skipped or wrong, the file may be routed to the wrong review process, and the lender may omit or misapply the 10% reserve calculation, creating project eligibility defects.
examples: Condo is still under developer control (new condo). System routes to Full Review workflow, then executes the 10% reserve calculation.
Project is incorrectly tagged as established and routed to a streamlined process; reserve calculation is not performed and project is later rejected in post-review.
evidence_quotes: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_CALCULATION_036_008: "For condo projects evaluated under the Full Review process... at least 10% of the budget"
BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_DEFINITION_037_002: "classified as a new condo project when... HOA still in developer control"

dependencies.strength_justification: Moderate dependency: both are core Full Review project acceptability checks; passing one does not eliminate the need to pass the other, but they are jointly required to approve a new/newly converted condo project under Full Review.
dependencies.rationale: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_CONSTRAINT_020_008 governs sales/conveyance and phasing constraints; BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_CALCULATION_036_008 governs HOA reserve funding adequacy. In an automated condo review workflow, both must be executed to reach an overall project eligibility result.
dependencies.impact: If conveyance/phasing is satisfied but reserves fail (or vice versa), the project still fails Full Review and the loan becomes ineligible for delivery based on project standards.
examples: Project meets 50% owner-occupant/second home conveyance requirement, but reserves are only 6% of budget: fails Full Review due to BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_CALCULATION_036_008.
Project has adequate 12% reserves, but multiple legal phases exist within one building: fails due to BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_CONSTRAINT_020_008.
evidence_quotes: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_CONSTRAINT_020_008: "at least 50%... conveyed or be under contract... There may not be more than one legal phase per building"
BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_CALCULATION_036_008: "must provide for funding replacement reserves... of at least 10% of the budget"

dependencies.strength_justification: Moderate dependency: remediation for excluded perils (stand-alone policy) is typically evaluated after confirming the master policy is fundamentally acceptable (replacement cost settlement).
dependencies.rationale: A stand-alone peril policy (BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_PROCESS_023_003) is a cure for coverage gaps, not a cure for an unacceptable settlement basis (actual cash value). Therefore, workflows generally confirm the master policy meets replacement-cost requirements first before deciding whether a stand-alone policy is needed for exclusions/limitations.
dependencies.impact: If replacement-cost acceptability is not confirmed first, lenders may incorrectly attempt to cure an unacceptable master policy with add-on coverage, delaying remediation and risking closing/delivery timelines.
examples: Master policy is ACV-based (fails BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_PROHIBITION_023_002): lender must replace the master policy, not just add a stand-alone wind policy.
Master policy is replacement cost compliant but excludes a required peril: lender proceeds to BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_PROCESS_023_003 and obtains a stand-alone policy.
evidence_quotes: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_PROHIBITION_023_002: "must settle claims on a replacement cost basis... actual cash value... are not acceptable"
BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_PROCESS_023_003: "If the master... excludes or limits coverage for any required peril... must obtain... stand-alone"

dependencies.strength_justification: Moderate dependency: borrower interior coverage (HO-6 or equivalent) is typically evaluated after confirming the project’s master policy structure is acceptable; otherwise the file may require a broader master policy fix first.
dependencies.rationale: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_VALIDATION_023_004 addresses an interior-coverage gap scenario. If the master policy is unacceptable overall (e.g., ACV settlement), the lender must resolve the master policy defect before relying on borrower-level interior policies for delivery acceptability.
dependencies.impact: If the master policy is unacceptable and this is not detected early, the lender may incorrectly focus on borrower HO-6 coverage while the project remains ineligible, causing rework and delays.
examples: Master policy replacement cost is verified first; then lender checks whether interiors are covered and requires borrower HO-6 if not.
If master policy is ACV-based, lender must replace master policy; verifying borrower interior coverage alone does not resolve delivery defect.
evidence_quotes: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_PROHIBITION_023_002: "Policies that settle on an actual cash value basis... are not acceptable"
BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_VALIDATION_023_004: "If the master policy does not cover the interior... borrower must maintain an individual property insurance policy"

dependencies.strength_justification: Moderate dependency: insurer rating validation is commonly performed as part of the master policy review package after identifying the policy and confirming it is the correct policy type (master policy for a project).
dependencies.rationale: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_VALIDATION_033_009 requires an identified insurer and policy context. BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_PROHIBITION_023_002 defines acceptability criteria for the master policy; the rating check is executed against that same master policy’s insurer as part of a complete acceptability decision.
dependencies.impact: If the master policy isn’t properly identified/validated first, the lender might validate the wrong insurer (e.g., borrower HO-6 carrier instead of HOA master carrier) and miss a noncompliant master insurer rating.
examples: HOA master policy is reviewed for replacement-cost settlement, then insurer AM Best rating is validated to satisfy BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_VALIDATION_033_009.
Policy document is incomplete and insurer name is unclear; rating validation cannot be completed and project approval is delayed.
evidence_quotes: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_VALIDATION_033_009: "including master policies for projects"
BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_PROHIBITION_023_002: "When a master property insurance policy is required..."

dependencies.strength_justification: Moderate dependency: if BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_VALIDATION_023_004 requires an individual unit policy, BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_VALIDATION_033_009 must be applied to that policy’s insurer to finalize acceptability.
dependencies.rationale: When the master policy lacks unit interior coverage, a borrower-level policy becomes required. Once that policy becomes required, it becomes subject to insurer acceptability validation (rating thresholds).
dependencies.impact: If insurer rating validation is skipped for the required borrower policy, the file may contain a required policy from a non-qualifying carrier, resulting in delivery defects and last-minute re-shopping of insurance.
examples: HOA master policy excludes interiors; borrower obtains HO-6. Lender must then validate that HO-6 carrier meets AM Best/Demotech/Kroll/S&P threshold.
Borrower provides HO-6 from unrated carrier; BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_VALIDATION_033_009 fails and closing must be delayed until replaced.
evidence_quotes: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_VALIDATION_023_004: "borrower must maintain an individual property insurance policy"
BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_VALIDATION_033_009: "For any first-mortgage property insurance policy... the insurer must meet... rating thresholds"

dependencies.strength_justification: Strong dependency: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_ELIGIBILITY_020_009’s acceptability decision is determined by the Phase I screening answers; BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_VALIDATION_038_002 provides the core Q2–Q7 decision logic used to reach that determination.
dependencies.rationale: The waste-site Phase I acceptability rule requires evaluating responses to screening questions (especially Q2–Q7). The validation rule operationalizes the accept/fail/Phase II trigger logic and is therefore required input to compute the eligibility outcome.
dependencies.impact: If the Q2–Q7 logic is not applied correctly, the lender may proceed without a required Phase II assessment (risking collateral contamination exposure) or incorrectly fail an acceptable property.
examples: All Q2–Q7 are 'no': BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_VALIDATION_038_002 yields acceptable, enabling BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_ELIGIBILITY_020_009 to conclude the property is acceptable for underwriting from waste-site standpoint.
Q4 is 'don't know': BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_VALIDATION_038_002 triggers Phase II/stop; BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_ELIGIBILITY_020_009 requires continued evaluation instead of approving.
evidence_quotes: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_ELIGIBILITY_020_009: "If the answer to any of questions 2 through 7 is 'yes' or 'don’t know,' then the property either fails or needs a Phase II"
BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_VALIDATION_038_002: "if any answer to questions 2 through 7 is 'yes' or 'don’t know,' the property either fails or requires a Phase II"

dependencies.strength_justification: Moderate dependency: PCB screening is an additional Phase I environmental screening module that is invoked when environmental hazard assessment is being performed and PCB screening is applicable.
dependencies.rationale: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_VALIDATION_038_003 is executed in the context of a Phase I environmental hazard screening workflow. When that workflow is initiated (BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_ELIGIBILITY_020_009 context), PCB-specific answers can independently force Phase II or failure and thus condition the overall environmental acceptability.
dependencies.impact: If PCB screening is omitted when applicable, the lender may fail to obtain a required Phase II assessment even when 'don’t know' responses exist, elevating environmental risk and delivery defensibility concerns.
examples: Phase I is underway; PCB screening includes a 'don't know' response: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_VALIDATION_038_003 requires Phase II (stop), even if waste-site Q2–Q7 are all 'no'.
PCB soil concentrations reported at 60 ppm: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_VALIDATION_038_003 triggers fail or Phase II requirement within the Phase I workflow.
evidence_quotes: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_VALIDATION_038_003: "if the answer to any question is 'don’t know,' a Phase II assessment is required (stop)"
BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_ELIGIBILITY_020_009: "Applies when completing Phase I environmental hazard assessment"

dependencies.strength_justification: Strong conditional dependency: when the subject is a conversion of an existing building, radon test results directly determine whether Phase II is required, materially affecting environmental acceptability.
dependencies.rationale: Within the Phase I environmental hazard assessment workflow, conversions introduce radon screening requirements. If radon test results are missing/unknown or above threshold, the rules require Phase II, which directly blocks environmental clearance for underwriting.
dependencies.impact: If this conditional screening is not executed for conversions, loans may proceed without required testing/Phase II, exposing lender to health/safety risk and potential ineligibility if discovered during QC or post-purchase review.
examples: Condo conversion: radon test is 5.2 pCi/L (or not within last six months). BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_VALIDATION_038_004 triggers Phase II requirement, preventing environmental clearance.
Conversion property: radon test within six months at 3.1 pCi/L. Screening passes and Phase I can proceed without Phase II on radon basis.
evidence_quotes: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_VALIDATION_038_004: "conversion... radon test... within the last six months must be at or below... 4 pCi/L... otherwise Phase II"
BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_ELIGIBILITY_020_009: "Applies when completing Phase I environmental hazard assessment"

dependencies.strength_justification: Strong dependency: unacceptable rights of parties in possession are a title acceptability blocker that must be cured/mitigated before the title insurance solution (meeting minimum coverage requirements) can be considered compliant for delivery.
dependencies.rationale: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_PROHIBITION_026_010 defines when possession rights create an unacceptable title condition that must be cleared. A title policy that meets the dollar-amount requirement (BR_MORTGAGE_LOAN_CONSTRAINT_005_001) does not resolve an unacceptable underlying title condition; therefore, curing the title defect is a prerequisite to a deliverable title insurance posture.
dependencies.impact: If possession-right issues are not resolved, the lender may be unable to deliver an insurable, acceptable title condition to Fannie Mae, blocking delivery or requiring negotiated sale/curative actions.
examples: Tenant has a 3-year lease (exceeds 2 years): title condition unacceptable under BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_PROHIBITION_026_010; even with a $400k title policy, delivery is blocked until cured.
Recorded right of first refusal exists: must be cleared; otherwise title remains unacceptable regardless of policy amount.
evidence_quotes: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_PROHIBITION_026_010: "If the possession rights include a right of first refusal or exceed two years... not acceptable... must be cleared/mitigated"
BR_MORTGAGE_LOAN_CONSTRAINT_005_001: "must obtain title insurance coverage... at least equal to the loan’s original principal amount"

dependencies.strength_justification: Moderate dependency: endorsement requirements are applied to a title policy that must first meet baseline coverage requirements (including minimum coverage amount).
dependencies.rationale: The environmental protection lien endorsement is a requirement within the title insurance package. In practice, lenders validate baseline title policy terms (including coverage amount) before validating required endorsements/forms, to avoid rework if the base policy is noncompliant.
dependencies.impact: If baseline policy requirements are not satisfied first, endorsement work may be wasted, and delivery can be delayed while reissuing the policy to correct coverage amount.
examples: Title policy issued at $350k for a $400k original principal amount: must be corrected per BR_MORTGAGE_LOAN_CONSTRAINT_005_001 before adding/confirming ALTA 8.1 endorsement per BR_MORTGAGE_LOAN_DOCUMENTATION_005_002.
Policy is reissued to correct amount; endorsement is then attached in the final package.
evidence_quotes: BR_MORTGAGE_LOAN_CONSTRAINT_005_001: "coverage amount must be >= original principal balance"
BR_MORTGAGE_LOAN_DOCUMENTATION_005_002: "must include... ALTA 8.1... endorsement (or equivalent)"

dependencies.strength_justification: Moderate dependency: policy-form prohibitions are checked as part of the same title policy review that begins with baseline policy requirements (including minimum amount).
dependencies.rationale: Both rules govern title policy acceptability; the minimum coverage amount check and prohibited language check are typically executed in a single title-policy compliance sequence.
dependencies.impact: If minimum requirements are not confirmed first, the prohibited language remediation may be duplicated when policy is reissued for amount corrections.
examples: Lender discovers ALTA 1990 creditors’ rights exclusion language in the draft policy; the final policy must be revised, but first the lender also ensures the policy amount meets BR_MORTGAGE_LOAN_CONSTRAINT_005_001.
Policy is reissued to fix amount and remove prohibited language in one revision cycle.
evidence_quotes: BR_MORTGAGE_LOAN_CONSTRAINT_005_001: "must obtain title insurance coverage... at least equal to the loan’s original principal amount"
BR_MORTGAGE_LOAN_PROHIBITION_005_003: "Title policies... must not include the creditors’ rights exclusion language"

dependencies.strength_justification: Moderate conditional dependency: ARM endorsement requirements are evaluated only after confirming a compliant title policy exists and meets baseline requirements.
dependencies.rationale: The ARM-specific endorsement is part of a compliant title policy package. Establishing that the title policy meets baseline minimum coverage is a practical gating step before checking loan-feature endorsements like ALTA 6.
dependencies.impact: If the baseline title policy is deficient, adding ALTA 6 does not cure the deficiency; delivery remains blocked and rework is required.
examples: ARM loan: after confirming title policy amount >= original principal, lender confirms ALTA Endorsement 6 is included.
ARM loan with deficient policy amount: lender must first reissue the policy; endorsement check is deferred until the base is corrected.
evidence_quotes: BR_MORTGAGE_LOAN_DOCUMENTATION_005_005: "For an ARM mortgage loan, the title insurance policy must include ALTA Endorsement 6"
BR_MORTGAGE_LOAN_CONSTRAINT_005_001: "coverage amount must be >= original principal balance"

dependencies.strength_justification: Moderate conditional dependency: manufactured home title endorsement requirements are evaluated as part of the title policy package after baseline policy compliance is established.
dependencies.rationale: Manufactured home endorsement requirements apply to the title policy. Confirming baseline title policy compliance (coverage amount) is a typical step before verifying product-specific endorsements.
dependencies.impact: If baseline title policy requirements are not met, endorsement compliance cannot make the title package deliverable; remediation must start with policy reissuance.
examples: Manufactured home loan: lender confirms policy amount is sufficient, then confirms ALTA Endorsement 7/7.1/7.2 is included.
Policy amount is insufficient; lender reissues policy and then adds the required manufactured home endorsement.
evidence_quotes: BR_MORTGAGE_LOAN_DOCUMENTATION_005_006: "manufactured home... must include ALTA Endorsement 7, 7.1, or 7.2"
BR_MORTGAGE_LOAN_CONSTRAINT_005_001: "coverage amount must be >= original principal balance"

dependencies.strength_justification: Moderate dependency: private transfer fee covenant screening is typically performed from the same title/land records materials obtained in the title insurance process.
dependencies.rationale: Private transfer fee covenants are discovered through title search, title commitment exceptions, and recorded covenant review. Obtaining and reviewing the title package (including policy/commitment) is a practical prerequisite to reliably determine whether the property is encumbered by a prohibited covenant.
dependencies.impact: If title materials are not obtained early, prohibited private transfer fee covenants may be discovered late, forcing contract/closing delays or making the loan undeliverable.
examples: Title commitment reveals a recorded private transfer fee covenant created in 2012; lender flags the loan as ineligible per BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_PROHIBITION_035_008.
Without a title search, lender misses the covenant until post-closing QC, creating delivery blockage and potential buyback exposure.
evidence_quotes: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_PROHIBITION_035_008: "properties encumbered by private transfer fee covenants... created on or after February 8, 2011"
BR_MORTGAGE_LOAN_CONSTRAINT_005_001: "must obtain title insurance coverage"

dependencies.strength_justification: Moderate dependency: the required recorded maintenance agreement/covenant is typically verified and sourced through the same land-record review performed during title work.
dependencies.rationale: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_DOCUMENTATION_010_005 requires a recorded agreement/covenant in land records. Title/closing workflows that produce title insurance materials are the standard mechanism for confirming recording, enforceability, and obtaining the recorded document for the file.
dependencies.impact: If the recorded maintenance agreement is not identified during title/land record review, the file may be missing required documentation at delivery, risking eligibility defects and post-purchase findings.
examples: Private road: title search shows recorded road maintenance covenant; lender includes it in the mortgage file to satisfy BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_DOCUMENTATION_010_005.
No recorded maintenance agreement is found in land records; lender must obtain/record an agreement or the loan becomes ineligible/at risk.
evidence_quotes: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_DOCUMENTATION_010_005: "maintenance agreement or covenant recorded in the appropriate land records"
BR_MORTGAGE_LOAN_CONSTRAINT_005_001: "must obtain title insurance coverage"

dependencies.strength_justification: Strong dependency: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_PROHIBITION_010_004 is an absolute purchase/securitization prohibition that can supersede a general zoning legality/marketability eligibility conclusion under BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_ELIGIBILITY_010_003 when triggered.
dependencies.rationale: A property may be a legal conforming or legal nonconforming use (and otherwise eligible under BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_ELIGIBILITY_010_003), yet still be prohibited if land-use regulations prevent reconstruction/maintenance after damage. In that trigger condition, the prohibition controls the final eligibility decision.
dependencies.impact: If the prohibition is not applied, the lender may deliver a loan on collateral Fannie Mae will not purchase, resulting in a hard eligibility failure and potential repurchase exposure.
examples: Coastal tideland setback line prevents rebuilding after storm damage: regardless of zoning conformity, BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_PROHIBITION_010_004 makes the loan ineligible.
Appraisal supports a legal nonconforming use with marketability analysis, but separate wetland restriction prevents reconstruction; prohibition overrides the general zoning eligibility conclusion.
evidence_quotes: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_PROHIBITION_010_004: "will not purchase... subject to specific land-use regulations... preventing reconstruction"
BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_ELIGIBILITY_010_003: "legal conforming use... legal nonconforming... eligible only if the appraisal analysis reflects..."

dependencies.strength_justification: Strong dependency: transaction-level Community Seconds constraints assume the subordinate financing is an eligible Community Seconds loan from an eligible provider (and not an interested party) as defined by BR_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROFILE_ELIGIBILITY_017_009.
dependencies.rationale: Before applying structural constraints on a first mortgage with Community Seconds (rate/CLTV caps/allowed transactions/resubordination requirements), the lender must first confirm the subordinate lien qualifies as Community Seconds (eligible provider and not an interested party). Otherwise, the transaction cannot be treated as Community Seconds and becomes ineligible.
dependencies.impact: If provider eligibility is not validated first, a lender may incorrectly allow CLTV up to 105% or permit a transaction structure based on Community Seconds rules, creating a delivery ineligibility.
examples: Subordinate lien funded by property seller (not a permitted exception): fails provider eligibility; lender cannot proceed with Community Seconds-specific CLTV allowances.
Subordinate lien funded by a government entity (eligible): provider eligibility passes, then lender applies rate/CLTV and resubordination requirements.
evidence_quotes: BR_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROFILE_ELIGIBILITY_017_009: "must be funded only by an eligible provider type... must not be the property seller or other interested party"
BR_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROFILE_CONSTRAINT_017_008: "When a first mortgage is originated with Community Seconds subordinate financing... maximum CLTV is 105%"

dependencies.strength_justification: Strong conditional dependency: BR_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROFILE_ELIGIBILITY_013_009 permits CLTV up to 105% only when the subordinate lien is a Community Seconds loan; the Community Seconds constraints must be satisfied to rely on that higher CLTV allowance.
dependencies.rationale: The high-LTV limited cash-out refinance band has strict product and collateral constraints and only allows CLTV above typical limits (up to 105%) when Community Seconds rules are met. Therefore, if the file uses the CLTV 105% allowance, it is dependent on satisfying Community Seconds constraints (eligible transaction type, terms, and resubordination recording for LCOR).
dependencies.impact: If Community Seconds constraints are not met, the loan may be ineligible for the 95.01–97% policy band or may require restructuring (reduce CLTV, change subordinate financing, or change transaction type).
examples: LCOR at 97% LTV with CLTV 104%: only permissible if subordinate lien is a compliant Community Seconds loan; otherwise must reduce CLTV below the applicable cap.

Community Seconds resubordination agreement is not recorded for LCOR: fails BR_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROFILE_CONSTRAINT_017_008; therefore, the CLTV 105% allowance in BR_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROFILE_ELIGIBILITY_013_009 cannot be used.
evidence_quotes: BR_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROFILE_ELIGIBILITY_013_009: "The CLTV may be up to 105% only if the subordinate lien is a Community Seconds loan"
BR_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROFILE_CONSTRAINT_017_008: "For limited cash-out refinance, the Community Seconds holder must execute a resubordination agreement and it must be recorded"

dependencies.strength_justification: Strong dependency: BR_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROFILE_ELIGIBILITY_013_009 requires a one-unit principal residence occupied by all borrowers; the ADU/unit-count rule ensures the property can be correctly treated as one-unit (with at most one ADU) rather than a 2–4 unit dwelling.
dependencies.rationale: If the property is misclassified due to multiple ADUs or an ADU combined with a 2–4 unit dwelling, the loan fails the 'one-unit principal residence' requirement for this specific high-LTV LCOR band. Therefore, unit classification is an input prerequisite for eligibility testing under BR_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROFILE_ELIGIBILITY_013_009.
dependencies.impact: If ADU classification fails, the loan cannot be delivered under this high-LTV LCOR policy band and may require program change, restructure, or denial.
examples: Borrower occupies main house; detached unit has full kitchen and is rented. If there are two ADUs, property cannot be 1-unit+ADU, so the transaction fails the one-unit requirement for BR_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROFILE_ELIGIBILITY_013_009.
ADU kitchen lacks stove hookup (not a qualifying ADU kitchen): property may be reclassified, affecting eligibility for the one-unit-only policy band.
evidence_quotes: BR_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROFILE_ELIGIBILITY_013_009: "must be secured by a one-unit principal residence that all borrowers occupy"
BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_ELIGIBILITY_004_005: "only one ADU is permitted... ADUs are not permitted with a two- to four-unit dwelling"

dependencies.strength_justification: Strong dependency: BR_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROFILE_ELIGIBILITY_013_009’s one-unit requirement presumes the property is otherwise within Fannie Mae’s eligible 1–4 unit residential collateral scope and eligible geography per BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_ELIGIBILITY_029_010.
dependencies.rationale: The high-LTV LCOR rule is a program overlay that still sits within Fannie Mae’s base collateral eligibility. Base unit/location eligibility must be satisfied before program-specific constraints can be applied.
dependencies.impact: If the property fails base eligibility (units/location), the high-LTV LCOR policy band cannot be used and the loan is ineligible for delivery.
examples: Property is located outside eligible geography: base eligibility fails, so the LCOR high-LTV policy band cannot apply.
Dwelling is 5 units: fails base 1–4 requirement, making the transaction ineligible regardless of LCOR band requirements.
evidence_quotes: BR_SUBJECT_PROPERTY_ELIGIBILITY_029_010: "dwelling consists of one to four units"
BR_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROFILE_ELIGIBILITY_013_009: "secured by a one-unit principal residence"

dependencies.strength_justification: Strong dependency: payment history requirements in BR_MORTGAGE_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_004_010 are evaluated only if the transaction is being considered for the high LTV refinance option whose base eligibility is defined by BR_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROFILE_ELIGIBILITY_007_009.
dependencies.rationale: BR_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROFILE_ELIGIBILITY_007_009 establishes the foundational program eligibility (existing loan owned by Fannie, note date threshold, and seasoning). If those gating criteria are not met, the payment history test is irrelevant to program eligibility and the file should exit the high LTV refi workflow.
dependencies.impact: If base eligibility is not checked first, lenders may spend time collecting/verifying 12-month payment history for a loan that is categorically ineligible for the high LTV refinance option.
examples: Existing loan is not owned/securitized by Fannie Mae: fails BR_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROFILE_ELIGIBILITY_007_009; system should not proceed to the 12-month delinquency pattern check.
Existing loan meets note date and 15-month seasoning: proceed to BR_MORTGAGE_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_004_010 to verify the 12-month payment history requirements.
evidence_quotes: BR_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROFILE_ELIGIBILITY_007_009: "existing loan... owned/securitized by Fannie Mae... seasoning of at least 15 months"
BR_MORTGAGE_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_004_010: "To be eligible for the high LTV refinance option... payment history..."

dependencies.strength_justification: Strong dependency: BR_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROFILE_CONSTRAINT_007_010 applies only to "high LTV refinance transactions"; therefore, the base program eligibility determination is a gating prerequisite for applying the modified cash-out/loan amount limits.
dependencies.rationale: The max new loan amount components and caps in BR_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROFILE_CONSTRAINT_007_010 are specific to high LTV refi option processing. If the transaction is not eligible for the option, standard limited cash-out requirements apply instead.
dependencies.impact: If the base program eligibility is not checked first, the lender may incorrectly apply high-LTV refi loan amount caps to a non-high-LTV refi transaction (causing incorrect denials or mis-disclosures), or fail to apply them to an eligible transaction (causing delivery defects).
examples: Borrower requests high LTV refinance but existing note date is before 10/1/2017: base eligibility fails, so BR_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROFILE_CONSTRAINT_007_010 should not govern loan amount calculation.
Eligible high LTV refi: lender enforces $5,000 cap for points/closing costs financed and $250 cash back limit per BR_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROFILE_CONSTRAINT_007_010.
evidence_quotes: BR_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROFILE_CONSTRAINT_007_010: "For high LTV refinance transactions..."
BR_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROFILE_ELIGIBILITY_007_009: "To be eligible for the high LTV refinance option..."

dependencies.strength_justification: Strong dependency: BR_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROFILE_CONSTRAINT_007_010 limits the maximum new loan amount; BR_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROFILE_CONSTRAINT_035_006 can require a further reduction of the otherwise-available maximum loan amount when special assessments are unpaid.
dependencies.rationale: To ensure the final new loan amount is compliant, the lender must incorporate mandatory adjustments that reduce the maximum available loan amount (unpaid special assessments), otherwise the calculated loan amount under the high-LTV refi caps may still exceed what is permissible after required reductions.
dependencies.impact: If special assessments are not accounted for, the delivered loan amount can exceed allowable limits, causing eligibility defects, required curtailments, or forced rebalancing of funds at closing.
examples: High LTV refi: borrower finances closing costs up to the allowed cap, but HOA special assessment of $2,000 is unpaid and not escrowed; lender must reduce max loan amount by $2,000 per BR_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROFILE_CONSTRAINT_035_006.
Special assessments will be paid via sufficient escrow deposits collected with the loan payment; reduction is not required if properly documented.
evidence_quotes: BR_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROFILE_CONSTRAINT_035_006: "maximum loan amount... must be reduced by the amount of the unpaid special assessments"
BR_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROFILE_CONSTRAINT_007_010: "new loan amount is limited to... payoff... financing of closing costs... cash back..."

dependencies.strength_justification: Moderate dependency: payment history eligibility is typically confirmed before performing final loan amount structuring under the high LTV refinance option.
dependencies.rationale: If the borrower fails the high-LTV payment history gate, there is no need to apply the option-specific loan amount caps and curtailment logic. Sequencing the payment history gate earlier reduces rework and prevents structuring a loan under a program the borrower cannot use.
dependencies.impact: If sequencing is not followed, the lender may spend time calculating compliant loan amount components for a borrower who is ineligible due to payment history, delaying requalification into another product.
examples: Borrower has a 60-day delinquency in the last 12 months: fails BR_MORTGAGE_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_004_010; lender should exit the high-LTV option and not proceed with BR_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROFILE_CONSTRAINT_007_010 structuring.
Borrower passes payment history check; then lender applies the $250 cash-back cap and $5,000 financed closing cost cap.
evidence_quotes: BR_MORTGAGE_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_004_010: "payment history... must show... no delinquencies..."
BR_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROFILE_CONSTRAINT_007_010: "For high LTV refinance transactions... new loan amount is limited..."

dependencies.strength_justification: Moderate conditional dependency: significant temporary buydowns (SFC 014) are stated to be subject to MBS pooling composition restrictions; the nonstandard-loan pool concentration limits rule can be implicated when SFC 014 loans are treated as nonstandard in TBA-eligible pools.
dependencies.rationale: BR_MORTGAGE_LOAN_DEFINITION_006_010 explicitly flags that significant buydown loans are subject to pooling composition restrictions. BR_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROFILE_CONSTRAINT_003_010 governs concentration limits for nonstandard categories in TBA UMBS pools. When a lender pools SFC 014 loans as a nonstandard category, the pooling limits rule becomes a required downstream check.
dependencies.impact: If the pooling concentration limits are not applied, a lender may create a noncompliant TBA-eligible UMBS pool, requiring re-pooling, delayed issuance, or moving loans to a different execution channel.
examples: Pool includes multiple loans with significant temporary buydowns (SFC 014). If treated as a nonstandard category, the pool must keep that category under 10% of issue-date UPB (and combined nonstandard categories under 15%, subject to exceptions).
A loan with >2% buydown for 3 years is tagged SFC 014; pooling desk must evaluate nonstandard concentration limits before finalizing the TBA pool.
evidence_quotes: BR_MORTGAGE_LOAN_DEFINITION_006_010: "require Special Feature Code (SFC) 014 at delivery (and are subject to... pooling composition restrictions)"
BR_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROFILE_CONSTRAINT_003_010: "each category of nonstandard loans is limited to 10%... combined... may not exceed 15%"

dependencies.strength_justification: CRITICAL: The approved-MI-form eligibility test is only relevant once primary MI is required (or present). If MI is required by LTV>80 at purchase/securitization, the loan cannot be delivered with a non-approved MI form.
dependencies.rationale: BR_008_010 establishes when primary MI is required for conventional first mortgages (LTV > 80% at purchase/securitization unless other charter-compliant enhancement is provided). BR_008_011 then constrains *which MI forms* are acceptable for any loan that requires MI (or is delivered with MI). Therefore, MI requirement/presence is the trigger and prerequisite context for the MI-form eligibility rule.
dependencies.impact: If BR_008_010 is not satisfied/assessed correctly (e.g., LTV computed incorrectly at purchase), the lender may fail to apply BR_008_011 and deliver a loan with MI that is required but not on an approved form, resulting in ineligibility/repurchase exposure.
examples: Conventional purchase delivered with LTV=82% (based on UPB at purchase). MI is required under BR_008_010, so the lender must ensure the policy uses a Fannie Mae-approved MI form per BR_008_011.

Lender assumes MI not required by using origination balance rather than UPB at purchase; delivers with a legacy MI agreement. Loan becomes ineligible/repurchaseable under BR_008_011 once MI requirement is identified.
evidence_quotes: BR_008_010: "...must obtain a primary mortgage insurance policy... with an LTV ratio greater than 80%... at the time it is purchased..."
BR_008_011: "Any mortgage loan... that requires primary mortgage insurance... must be insured under one of the Fannie Mae-approved mortgage insurance forms."

dependencies.strength_justification: CRITICAL: BR_009_007 explicitly references the 'maximum financing concession cap' which is defined by BR_009_006. Without the cap table (by occupancy and LTV/CLTV), BR_009_007 cannot be executed correctly.
dependencies.rationale: BR_009_006 sets the IPC maximum percentages by occupancy and LTV/CLTV and specifies the basis (lower of sales price or appraised value). BR_009_007 requires comparing IPCs to (a) the maximum cap and (b) closing costs, then treating excess as sales concessions and recalculating ratios. Therefore, BR_009_006 supplies the mandatory cap thresholds that BR_009_007 needs as an input.
dependencies.impact: If BR_009_006 is not applied, BR_009_007 may fail to identify excess IPCs, causing incorrect price adjustments and LTV/CLTV recalculations. This can result in delivery defects, incorrect eligibility decisions, or under-collateralization risk.
examples: Principal residence with CLTV=92%: cap is 3% per BR_009_006; BR_009_007 must treat any IPCs above 3% (or above closing costs) as sales concessions and reduce the sales price accordingly.
Investment property: cap is 2% regardless of CLTV per BR_009_006; BR_009_007 uses that 2% cap when testing excess concessions.
evidence_quotes: BR_009_006: "Interested party financing concessions are capped based on occupancy and LTV/CLTV..."
BR_009_007: "Any IPC financing concessions exceeding (a) the maximum financing concession cap... must be treated as sales concessions."

dependencies.strength_justification: STRONG: Properly reclassifying excess IPCs as sales concessions and reducing the sales price prevents (or surfaces) impermissible borrower cash back risk at settlement and forces ratio recalculation that affects whether cash back can exist.
dependencies.rationale: BR_009_007 defines how to handle IPCs that exceed caps or closing costs (treat as sales concessions, deduct from price, and recalc LTV/CLTV). BR_030_004 prohibits cash back on purchase transactions except narrow exceptions and requires confirming minimum borrower contribution if permissible cash back occurs. Correct application of BR_009_007 is a primary control to prevent excess credits (often a source of cash back) and to ensure final settlement economics are compliant before applying BR_030_004.
dependencies.impact: If BR_009_007 fails, the Closing Disclosure/settlement statement may show excess credits that turn into borrower cash back, triggering BR_030_004 violations and potential purchase ineligibility or post-closing cures/re-disclosures.
examples: IPC credits exceed total closing costs; without BR_009_007 reclassification and price reduction, the borrower receives cash at closing, violating BR_030_004 (unless it is a documented fee overpayment refund).
After BR_009_007 reduces the sales price, LTV increases and may require MI or fail program LTV caps—preventing a scenario where the borrower appears to have 'extra' credits paid out as cash.
evidence_quotes: BR_009_007: "...exceeding... closing costs must be treated as sales concessions... deducted from... sales price... ratios must be recalculated..."
BR_030_004: "...proceeds may not be used to give the borrower cash back except..."

dependencies.strength_justification: MODERATE: BR_009_008 determines whether an HOA payment is treated as an IPC (≤12 months) which then triggers application of the IPC cap tables in BR_009_006.
dependencies.rationale: BR_009_008 classifies certain HOA fee payments as IPCs when the payment extends 12 months or less. Once classified as an IPC, the IPC cap calculation rule (BR_009_006) applies to limit the concession based on occupancy and LTV/CLTV.
dependencies.impact: If BR_009_008 classification is missed, the lender may fail to cap HOA credits as IPCs, causing excessive concessions and potential ratio miscalculations and delivery defects.
examples: Seller pays 12 months of HOA dues: BR_009_008 says it is an IPC; BR_009_006 then caps the total IPCs based on occupancy and LTV/CLTV tier.
Seller pays 10 months of HOA dues plus other credits; without BR_009_008, staff may misclassify the HOA payment and incorrectly ignore it in the BR_009_006 IPC cap test.
evidence_quotes: BR_009_008: "HOA payments for 12 months or less are treated as IPCs."
BR_009_006: "Interested party financing concessions are capped based on occupancy and LTV/CLTV..."

dependencies.strength_justification: MODERATE: When an HOA payment is treated as an IPC (≤12 months), BR_009_007 governs how to test it against caps/closing costs and reclassify any excess.
dependencies.rationale: BR_009_008 provides the decision rule for whether an HOA payment is an abatement (prohibited) or an IPC (permitted but capped and subject to closing-cost limits). If it is an IPC, BR_009_007 governs treatment of any excess amounts as sales concessions and the resulting recalculation of LTV/CLTV.
dependencies.impact: If BR_009_008 classification is wrong, BR_009_007 may not be applied (or may be applied incorrectly), resulting in miscalculated ratios and potential ineligibility at delivery.
examples: Seller pays 12 months HOA: treated as IPC, included in the BR_009_007 test against closing costs; any excess becomes a sales concession and reduces sales price.
Processor incorrectly treats 8 months HOA as an abatement; loan is wrongly blocked rather than being evaluated under BR_009_007 IPC logic.
evidence_quotes: BR_009_008: "HOA payments for 12 months or less are treated as IPCs."
BR_009_007: "Financing concessions must be equal to or less than... closing costs... exceeding... cap... must be treated as sales concessions."

dependencies.strength_justification: STRONG: If a payment abatement exists (as defined), the loan is ineligible regardless of whether it could otherwise pass IPC caps; the abatement prohibition supersedes IPC cap evaluation.
dependencies.rationale: BR_009_008 states: 'Loans with any type of payment abatement are not eligible.' This creates an eligibility stop that renders IPC cap compliance (BR_009_006) irrelevant for the abatement portion because the structure is prohibited outright.
dependencies.impact: If override is not implemented, the system may incorrectly clear a loan by capping/converting abatements via IPC logic, when the correct outcome is delivery ineligibility.
